CLARA VAUGHAN 
 BOOK I. 
 CHAPTER I. 
 I do not mean to describe myself . already I feel that the personal pronoun will appear too often in these page . know the fault of my character almost as well as my good friend know they , I shall attempt to hide they no more than would those beloved one . enough of this : the story I have to tell be strange , and short as my own its preamble . 
 the day when I be ten year old begin my serious life . it be the 30th of December , 1842 ; and proud be the kiss my love father give I for spelling , writing , and pronounce the date in English , French , and italian . no very wonderful feat , it be true , for a clever child well - teach ; but I be by no mean a clever child ; and no one except my father could teach I a single letter . when , after several year of wedlock , my parent find new joy in I , their bliss be soon overhang with care . they fear , but durst not own the fear , lest the wilful , passionate , love creature , on whom their heart be wholly set , should be tear from their love to a distance great than the void of death ; in a word , should prove insane . at length they could no long hide this terror from each other . one look tell it all ; and I vaguely remember my hazy wonder at the scene that follow . like a thief , I come from the corner behind the curtain - loop , and tremble at my father 's knee , for he to say something to I . then frighten at his silence - a thing unknown to I - I pull his hand from before his eye , and find hot tear upon they . I coax he then , and pet he , and feel his sorrow through I ; then make believe to scold he for be so naughty as to cry . but I could not get his trouble from he , and he seem to watch I through his kiss . 
 before I have cease to ponder dreamily over this great wonder , a vast event   for a child of seven   divert I . Father , mother , and tooty - for so I then be call - be draw a long way by horse with yellow man upon they : from enlarged experience I infer that we must have post to London . here , among many marvel , I remember especially a long and mysterious interview with a kind , white - haired old gentleman , who wear most remarkable shoe . he take I upon his lap , which seem to I rather a liberty ; then he smooth down my hair , and feel my head so much that I ask if he want to comb it , have make up my mind to kick if he dare to try such a thing . then he put all sort of baby question to I which I be disposed to resent , have long discard Cock Robin and little red - riding - hood . unconsciously too , I be move by Nature 's strong hate of examination . but my father come up , and with tear in his eye beg I to answer everything . meanwhile my mother sit in a dark corner , as if her good doll be die . with its innate pugnacity , my hazy intellect rise to the situation , and I narrowly heed every thing . 
 " now go , my dear , " the old gentleman say at last ; " you be a very good little girl indeed . " 
 " that be a great lie , " I cry ; for I have learn bad word from a flighty girl , take rashly as under - nurse . 
 the old gentleman seem surprised , and my mother be dreadfully shocked . my father laugh first , then look at I sadly ; and I do what he expect , I jump into his arm . at one word from he , I run to the great physician , and humbly beg his pardon , and offer he my very dear toy . he come up warmly , and shake my father 's hand , and smile from his heart at my mother . 
 " allow I , Mrs. Vaughan - allow I , my dear sir - to congratulate you cordially . the head be a noble and honest one . it be the growth of the brain that cause these little commotion ; but the congestion will not be permanent . the fit , that have so alarmed you , be at this age a good symptom ; in fact , they be Nature 's remedy . they may last for seven year , or even for ten ; of course they will not depart at once . but the attack will be milder , and the interval long , when she have turn fourteen . for the intellect you need have no fear whatever . only keep she quiet , and never force she to learn . she must only learn when it come as it be with the wind . she will never forget what she do learn . " 
 Hereupon , unless I be much mistaken , my father and mother fall to and kiss and hug one another , and I hear a sound like sobbing ; then they catch I up , and devour I , as if I be bear anew ; and star round with great childish eye , I could not catch the old gentleman 's glance at all . 
 henceforth I learn very little , the wind , perhaps , be unfavourable ; and all the little I do learn come from my father 's lip . his patience with I be wonderful ; we spend most of the day together , and when he be force to leave I , I take no food until he return . whenever his horse be order , Miss Clara 's little grey pony begin to neigh and to fidget , and Miss Clara be off in a moment to get her blue riding - skirt . even when father go shoot or fishing , Tooty be sure to go too , except in the depth of winter ; and then she be up at the top of the house , watch all round for the gun - smoke . 
 ah , why do I linger so over these happy time - be it the pleasure of think how fondly we love one another , or be it the pain of know that we can do so no more ? 
 now , the 30th of December be my parent ' wedding - day , for I have be bear six year exact after their affectionate union . and now that I be ten year old - a notable hinge on the door of life - how much they make , to be sure , of each other and of I ! at dinner I sit in glory between they , upset all ceremony , please my father , and tease my mother , by many a childish sally . so genial a man my father be that he would talk to the servant , even on state occasion , quite as if they be human being . yet none of they ever take the small liberty with he , unless it be one to love he . before dessert , I inter my queen doll , with much respect and some heartache , under a marble flag by the door , which have be prepare for the purpose . my father be chief - mourner , but do not cry to my liking , until I have pinch he well . after this typical good - bye to childhood , I ride he back to the dining - table , and help he and my mother to the last of the West 's St. Peter grape , give he all the fat one . then we all drink health and love to one another , and I fall to in earnest at a child 's delight . dear father keep supply I with thing much nice than be now to be get , while my mother in vain pretend to guard the frontier . it be the first time I taste Guava jelly ; and now , even at the name , that scene be bright before I . the long high room oak - panel , the light and shadow flicker as on a dark bay horse , the crimson velvet curtain where the window be go to bed , the great black chair with damask cushion , but hard and sharp at the edge , the mantel - piece all carve in stone which I be forbid to kick , the massive lamp that never would let I eat without loose cloud of hair dance all over my plate , and then the great fire , its rival , shudder in blue flame at the thought of the frost outside ; all these thing , and even the ticking of the timepiece , be more palpable to I now than the desk on which I write . my father sit in his easy chair , laugh and joking , full of life and comfort , with his glass of old port beside he , his wife in front , and I , his " claricrop , " at his knee . more happy than a hundred king , he wish for nothing well . at one time , perhaps , he have long for a son to keep the ancient name , but now he be quite ashamed of the wish , as mutiny against I . after many an interchange , a drink for father , a sip for tooty , he begin to tell wondrous story of the shot he have make that day ; especially how he have kill a woodcock through a magpie 's nest . my mother listen with playful admiration ; I with breathless interest , and most profound belief . 
 then we play at draught , and fox and goose , and pretend even to play at chess , until it be nine o'clock , and my hour of grace expire . three time Ann Maples come to fetch I , but I would not go . at last I go submissively at one kind word from my father . my mother obtain but a pouting kiss , for I want to wreak some vengeance ; but my father I never kiss with less than all my heart and soul . I fling both arm around his neck , lay my little cheek to his , and whisper in his ear that I love he more than all the world . tenderly he clasp and kiss I , and now I be sure that through his smile he look at I with sadness . turn round at the doorway , I stretch my hand towards he , and meet once more his love , laugh eye . once more and only once . next I see he in his coffin , white and stark with death . by - and - by I will tell what I know ; at present I can only feel . the emotion - away with long word - the passion which sweep my little heart , with equal power rend it now . long I lay dumb and stun at the horror I could not grasp . then with a scream , as in my fit , I fling upon his body . what to I be shroud and shell , the rigid look and the world of awe ? such thing let step - child fear . not I , when it be my father . 
 CHAPTER II . 
 how that deed be do , I learn at once , and will tell . by whom and why it be do , I have give my life to learn . the evidence lay before the coroner be a cloud and fog of mystery . for day and day my mother lie insensible . then , for week and week , she would leap from her bed in fit of terror , stare , and shriek and faint . as for the servant , they know very little , but imagine a great deal . the only other witness be a medical man , a shoemaker , and two London policeman . the servant say that , between one and two in the morning , a clear , wild shriek ring through the house . large as the building be , this shriek unrepeate awake nearly all but I . rush anyhow forth , they hurry and huddle together at the head of the great staircase , doubt what to do . some say the cry come one way , some another . meanwhile Ann Maples , who sleep with I in an inner room at the end of a little passage , in the courage of terror go straight to her master and mistress . there , by the light of a dim night - lamp , use to visit I , she see my mother upright in the bed , and point towards my father 's breast . my father lie quite still ; the bed - clothe be smooth upon he . my mother do not speak . Ann Maples take the lamp , and look in her master 's face . his eye be open , wide open as in amazement , but the surprise be death . one arm be stiff around his wife , the other lax upon the pillow . as she describe it in west - country phrase , " he look all frore . " the woman rush from the room , and scream along the passage . the servant run to she , flurry and haggard , each afraid to be leave behind . none except the butler dare to enter . whispering and tremble they peer in after he , all ready to run away . Thomas Kenwood love his master dearly , be his foster - brother . he at once remove the bedclothe , and find the fatal wound . so strongly and truly be it deal , that it pierce the centre of my dear father 's heart . one spot of blood and a small three - cornered hole be all that could be see . the surgeon , who come soon after , say that the weapon must have be a very keen and finely - temper dagger , probably of foreign make . the murderer must have be quite cool , and well acquaint with the human frame . death follow the blow on the instant , without a motion or a groan . in my mother 's left hand strongly clutched be a lock of long , black , shin hair . a curl very like it , but rather finer , lie on my father 's bosom . in the room be no sign of disorder , no mark of forcible entrance . 
 one of the maid , a timid young thing , declare that soon after the stable - clock strike twelve , she have hear the front baluster creak ; but as she be know to hear this every night , little importance be attach to it . the coroner pay more attention to the page   a sharp youth from London , who , be first in the main corridor , after the cry , see , or think he see , a move figure , where the faint starlight come in at the oriel window . he be the more believe , because he own that he durst not follow it . but no way of escape could be discover there , and the eastern window be strongly bar betwixt the mullion . no door , no window be anywhere find open . 
 outside the house , the only trace be at one remarkable spot . the time have be choose well . it be a hard black frost , without , as yet , any snow . the ground be like iron , and an Indian could have spy no trail . but at this one spot , twenty - five yard from the east end of the house , and on the verge of a dense shrubbery , a small spring , scarcely visible , ooze among the moss . around its very head , it clear , and keep , a narrow space quite free from green , and here its margin be a thin coat of black mineral mud , which never froze . this space , at the broad , be but two foot and ten inch across from gravel to turf , yet now it hold two distinct footprint , not of some one crossing and re - crossing , but of two successive step lead from the house into the shrubbery . these footprint be remarkable ; the one nearest the house be of the left foot , the other of the right . each be the impression of a long , light , and pointed boot , very hollow at the instep . but they differ in this - the left footprint be plain and smooth , without mark of nail , or cue , or any other roughness ; while the right one be clearly stamp in the centre of the sole with a small rectangular cross . this mark seem to have be make by a cruciform piece of metal , or some other hard substance , inlay into the sole . at least , so say a shoemaker , who be employ to examine it ; and he add that the boot be not those of the present fashion , what he call " duck 's bill " be then in vogue . this man be ask to account for the fact of the footprint be so close together , do so very easily , and with much simplicity . it be evident , he say , that a man of average stature , walk rapidly , would take nearly twice that distance in every stride ; but here the verge of the shrubbery , and the branch strike he in the face , have suddenly curtail the step . and to this , most likely , and not to any hurry or triumph , be to be ascribe the fact that one so wily and steadfast do not turn back and erase the dangerous token . most likely , he do not feel what be beneath his foot , while he be battle with the tangle above . 
 be that as it may , there the mark remain , like the blot - paper of his crime . cast of they be take at once , and carefully have they be store by I . 
 the shoemaker , a shrewd but talkative man , say unasked that he have never see such boot as have leave those mark , since the " Young Squire "   he mean Mr. Edgar Vaughan   go upon his travel . for this gratuitous statement , he be strongly rebuke by the coroner . 
 for the rest , all that could be find out , after close inquiry , be , that a stranger darkly clothe have be see by the gamekeeper , in a copse some half - mile from the house , while the man be beat for woodcock on the previous day . he do not seem to be follow my father , and they think he have wander out of the forest road . he glide quickly away , before they could see his feature , but they know that he be tall and swarthy . no footprint be find in that ride like those by the shrubbery spring . 
 I need not say what verdict the coroner 's jury find . 
 CHAPTER III . 
 thus far , I have write in sore haste , to tell , as plainly and as briefly as possible , that which have darken all my life . though it never leave my wake thought , to dwell upon it before other be agony to I . henceforth my tale will flow perhaps more easily , until I fall again into a grief almost as dark , and be strike by storm of passion which childhood 's stature do not reach . 
 when the shock of the household , and the wonder of the county , and the hope of constable   raise by a thousand pound ' reward   have subside gradually , my mother continue to live in the old mansion , perhaps because none of her friend come forward to remove she . under my father 's will she be the sole executrix ; but all the estate   include house and park   be leave to my father 's near relative , as trustee for myself , with a large annuity to my mother charge upon they . there be many other provision and power in the will , which be of no consequence to my story . the chief estate be large and rich , extend three or four mile from the house , which stand in a beautiful part of Gloucestershire . the entire rental be about 12,000 l.   a year . my father   whose name be Henry Valentine Vaughan , be a very active man in the prime of life , have employ no steward , but manage everything himself . the park , and two or three hundred acre round it , have always be keep in hand ; the rest be let to thriving tenant , who love   as they express it   " every hair on the head of a Vaughan . " there be also a small farm near the sea , in a lonely part of Devonshire ; but this be my mother 's , have be leave to she by her father , a clergyman in that neighbourhood . 
 my father 's near relative be his half - brother , Edgar Vaughan , who have be educate for the Bar , and at one time seem likely to become eminent ; then suddenly he give up his practice , and reside   or rather rove   abroad , during several year . Sinister rumour about he reach our neighbourhood , not long before my father 's death . to these , however , the latter pay no attention , but always treat his brother Edgar with much cordiality and affection . but all admit that Edgar Vaughan have far outrun his income as a young son , which amount to about 600 l.   a year . of course , therefore , my father have often help he . 
 on the third day after that night , my guardian come to Vaughan Park . he be say to have hurry from London , upon learn there what have happen . 
 the servant and other have vainly and foolishly try to keep from I the nature of my loss . soon I find out all they know , and when the first tit and horror leave I , I pass my whole time , light or dark , in rove from passage to passage , from room to room , from closet to closet , search every chink and cranny for the murderer of my father . though heretofore a timid child , while so engaged I know not such a thing as fear ; but peer , and grope , and listen , feel every inch of wall and wainscot , crawl lest I should alarm my prey , spy through the slit of every door , and shake every empty garment . certain board there be near the east window which sound hollow ; at these I scoop until I break my nail . in vain nurse maple lock I in her room , hold I at her side , or even bind I to the bed . my raving force she soon to yield , and I would not allow she , or any one else , to follow I . the Gloucester physician say that since the disease of my mind have take that shape , it would be more dangerous to thwart than to indulge it . 
 it be the evening of the third day , and weary with but never of my search , I be grope down the great oak - staircase in the dusk , hand after hand , and foot by foot , when suddenly the main door - bell rang . the snow be fall heavily , and have deaden the sound of wheel . at once I slide   as my father have teach I to do   down the broad balustrade , run across the entrance - hall , and with my whole strength draw back the bolt of the lock . there I stand in the porch , unfrightened , but with a new kind of excitement on I . a tall dark man come up the step , and shake the snow from his boot . the carriage - lamp shone in my face . I would not let he cross the threshold , but stand there and confront he . he pretend to take I for some servant 's child , and hand I a parcel cover with snow . I fling it down , and say , look he full in the face , " I be Clara Vaughan , and you be the man who kill my father . " " carry she in , John , " he say to the servant - " carry she in , or the poor little thing will die . what eye ! " and he use some foreign oath - " what wonderful eye she have ! " 
 that burst of passion be the last conscious act of the young and over - labour brain . for three month I wander outside the gate of sorrow . my guardian , as they tell I , be most attentive throughout the whole course of the fever , and even in the press of business visit I three time every day . meanwhile , my mother be slowly shake off the stupor which lie upon she , and the new fear of lose I come through that thick heaviness , like the wind through a fog . Doubtless it help to restore her sens , and awake she to the work of life . then , as time go on , her former beauty and gentleness come back , and her reason too , as regard other subject . but as to that which all so long to know , not a spark of evidence could be have from she . the faint allusion to that crime , the name of her love husband , the mere word " murder " utter in her presence - and the consciousness would leave her eye , like a loan withdraw . upright she sit and rigid as when she be find that night , with the line of her face as calm and cold as moonlight . only two mean there be by which her sens could be restore : one be low sweet music , the other profound sleep . she be never throw into this cataleptic state by her own thought or word , nor even by those of other when in strict sequence upon her own . but any attempt to lead she to that one subject , no matter how craftily veil , be sure to end in this . the skilful physician , who have know she many year , judge , after special study of this disease , in which he feel deep interest , that it be always present in her brain , but wait for external aid to master she . I need not say that she be now unfit for any stranger 's converse , and even her most careful friend must touch sometimes the motive string . 
 as I recover slowly from long illness , the loss of my good friend and the search for my bad enemy revive and reign within I . sometimes my guardian would deign to reason with I upon what he call " my monomania . " when he do so , I would fix my eye upon he , but never try to answer . now and then , those eye seem to cause he some uneasiness ; at other time he would laugh and compare they pleasantly to the blue fire - damp in a coal - mine . his dislike of their scrutiny be well known to I , and incite I the more to urge it . but in spite of all , he be ever kind and gentle to I , and even try some grimly playful overture to my love , which flee from he with loathing , albeit a slow conviction form that I have wrong he by suspicion . 
 Edgar Malins Vaughan , then about thirty - seven year old , be   I suppose   a very handsome man , and perhaps of a more striking presence than my dear father . his face , when he be pleased , remind I strongly of the glance and smile I have lose , but never could it convey that soft sweet look , which still come through the cloud to I , now and then in dream . the outline of my guardian 's face be keener too and strong , and his complexion far more swarthy . his eye be of a hard steel - blue , and never seem to change . a slight lameness , perceptible only at time , do not impair his activity , but serve he as a pretext for decline all field - sport , for which   unlike my father   he have no real taste . 
 his enjoyment , if he have any - and I suppose all man have some - seem to consist in the management of the estate   which he take entirely upon himself , in satiric literature and the news of the day , or in lonely ride and sail upon the lake . it be hint too , by Thomas Kenwood , who dislike and fear he strangely , that he drink spirit or foreign cordial in his own room , late at night . there be nothing to confirm this charge ; he be always up betime , his hand be never tremulous , nor do his colour change . 
 CHAPTER IV . 
 my life - childhood I can scarcely call it - go quietly for several year . the eastern wing of the house be leave unused , and rarely traverse by any but myself . foolish tale , of course , be tell about it ; but my frequent visit find nothing to confirm they . at night , whenever I could slip from the care of good but matter - of - fact Ann Maples , I use to wander down the long corridor , and squeeze through the iron gate now set there , half in hope and half in fear of meet my father 's spirit . for such an occasion all my question be prepare , and all the answer canvass . my infant mind be struggle ever to pierce the mystery which so vaguely lead its life . year only quicken my resolve to be the due avenger , and harden the set resolve into a fatalist 's conviction . my mother , always full of religious feeling , teach I daily in the scripture , and try to make I pray . but I could not take the mild teaching of the Gospel as a little child . to I the Psalms of David , and those book of the Old Testament which recount and seem to applaud revenge , be sweet than all the balm of Gilead ; they supply a terse and vigorous form to my perpetual yearning . with a child 's impiety , I claim for myself the mission of the Jews against the enemy of the Lord . the form of prayer , which my mother teach I , I mumble through , while look in her gentle face , with anything but a prayerful gaze . for my own bedside I keep a widely different form , which even now I shudder to repeat . and yet I love dear mother truly , and pity she sometimes with tear ; but the shadow - love be far the deep . 
 my father 's grave be in the churchyard of the little village which cluster and nestle beyond our lodge . it be a real grave . the thought of lie in a vault have always be loathsome to he , and he say that it strike he cold . so fond be he of air and light and freedom , the change of season and weather , and the shifting of the sun and star , that he use to pray that they still might pass over his bury head ; that he might lie , not in the dark locker of death , but in the open hand of time . his friend use to think it strange that a man of so light and festive nature should ever talk of death ; yet so he often do , not morbidly , but with good cheer . in pursuance , therefore , of his well - know wish , the vault wherein there lie five century of Vaughan dust be not open for he ; neither be his grave build over with a hideous ash - bin ; but lie narrow , fair , and humble , with a plain , low headstone of the white marble , bear his initial deeply carve in grey . through our warm love and pity , and that of all the village , and not in mere compliance with an old usage of the western county , his simple bed be ever green and white with the fairest of low flower . though otherwise too moody and reckless to be a gardener , I love to rear from seed his favourite plant , and keep they in my room until they blossom ; then I would set they carefully along his grave , and lie down beside it , and wonder whether his spirit take pleasure in they . 
 but more often , it must be own , I lay a dark tribute there . the gloomy channel into which my young mind have be force be overhung , as might be expect , by a sombre growth . the legend of midnight spirit , and the tale of black crime , shed their poison on I . from the dust of the library I exhume all record of the most famous atrocity , and devour they at my father 's grave . as yet I be too young to know what grief it would cause to he who sleep there , could he but learn what his only child be do . that knowledge would at once have check I , for his presence be ever with I , and his memory cast my thought , as moonlight shape the shadow . 
 the view from the churchyard be a lovely english scene . what high praise can I give than this ? long time a wanderer in foreign part , nothing have I see that come from nature to the heart like a true english landscape . 
 the little church stand back on a quiet hill , which bent its wing in a gentle curve to shelter it from the north and east . these bend wing be feather , soft as down , with , larch , hawthorn , and the lightly - pencil birch , between which , here and there , the bluff rock stand their ground . southward , and beyond the glen , how fair a spread of wave country we could see ! to the left , our pretty lake , all clear and calm , give back the survey of the tree , until a bold gnoll , fringe with alder , lead it out of sight . far away upon the right , the severn steal along its silver road , leave many a reach and bend , which catch towards eventide the notice of the travelled sun . upon the horizon might be see at time , the blue distance of the Brecon hill . 
 often when I sit here all alone , and the evening dusk come on , although I hold those volume on my lap , I could not but forget the murder and the revenge of man , the motif , form , and evidence of crime , and nurse a vague desire to dream my life away . 
 sometimes also my mother would come here , to read her favourite Gospel of St. John . then I would lay the dark record on the turf , and sit with my injury hot upon I , wonder at her peaceful face . while , for her sake , I rejoice to see the tear of comfort and contentment dawn in her eye , I never grieve that the soft chastenment be not shed on I . for she I love and admire it ; for myself I scorn it utterly . 
 the same clear sunshine be upon we both : we both be look on the same fair scene - the gold of ripen corn , the emerald of wood and pasture , the crystal of the lake and stream ; above we both the peaceful heaven be shed , and the late distress be but a night go by - wherefore have it leave to one the dew of life , to the other a thunderbolt ? I know not the reason then , but now I know it well . 
 although my favourite style of literature be not likely to improve the mind , or yield that honey melancholy which some young lady woo , to I it do but little harm . my will be so bent upon one object , and the whole substance and shape of my thought so stanch in their sole ductility thereto , that other thing go idly by I , if they show no power to promote my end . but upon palpable life , and the doing of nature I become observant beyond my age . thing in growth or motion round I impress themselves on my sens , as if a nerve be touch . the uncoiling of a fern - frond , the shrinking of a bind - weed blossom , the escape of a cap - pinch bud , the projection of a seed , or the spark from a fade tuberose , in short , the light print of Nature 's sandalled foot , be trace and count by I . not that I derive a maiden pleasure from they , as happy person do , but that it seem my business narrowly to heed they . 
 as for the proud phenomenon of imperial man , so far as they yet survive the crucible of convention - the line where cunning crouch , the smile that be but a brain - flash , the veil let down across the wide mouth of greed , the guilt they try to make volatile in charity , - all these I be not old and poor enough to learn . yet I mark unconsciously the trait of individual , the mannerism , the gesture , and the mode of speech , the complex motive , and the underflow of thought . so all I do , and all I dream , have one colour and one aim . 
 my education , it be just to say , be neglect by no one but myself . my father 's love of air and heaven have descend to I , and nothing but my mother 's prayer or my own dark quest could keep I in the house . abstract principle and skeleton dogma I could never grasp ; but whatever be vivid and shrewd and native , whatever have point and purpose , be seize by I and make my own . my faculty be not large , but steadfast now , and concentrate . 
 though several master try their good , and my governess do all she could , I choose to learn but little . drawing and music   to soothe my mother   be my principal study . of poetry I take no heed , except in the fierce old drama . 
 enough of this . I have say so much , not for my sake , but for my story . 
 CHAPTER V. 
 on the fifth anniversary of my father 's death , when I be fifteen year of age , I go to visit   as I always do upon that day   the fatal room . although this chamber have be so long unused , the furniture be allow to remain ; and I insist passionately that it should be my charge . what have seem the petulance of a child be now the strong will of a thoughtful girl . 
 I take the key from my bosom , where I always keep it , and turn it in the lock . no mortal have enter that door since I pass it in my last paroxysm , three week and a day before . I see a cobweb reach from the black finger - plate to the third mould of the beading . the weather have be damp , and the door stick fast to the jamb , then yield with a crack . though I be bold that day , and in a mood of triumph , some awe fall on I as I enter . there hang the heavy curtain , last draw by the murderer 's hand ; there lie the bed - clothe , raise for the blow , and replace on death ; and there be the pillow where sleep have be so prolonged . all these I see with a forced and fearful glance , and my breath stand still as the wind in a grave . 
 presently a light cloud float off the sun , and a white glare from the snow of the morning burst across the room . my sight be not so dim with tear as it generally be when I stand there , for I have just read the history of a long - hide crime detect , and my eye be full of fierce hope . but stricken soon to the wonted depth of sadness , with the throb of my heart fall like the avenger 's step , I go minutely through my death - inspection . I feel all round the dusty wainscot , open the wardrobe and cupboard , raise the lid of the deep - bay window - seat , peer shudder down the dark closet , where I believe the assassin have lurk , start and star at myself in the mirror , to see how lone and wan I look , and then approach the bed , to finish my search in the usual place , by lie and sob where my father die . I have glance beneath it and round the pillar , and clutch the curtain as if to squeeze out the truth , and be just about to throw myself on the coverlet and indulge the fit so bitterly hold at bay , when something on the hanging above the head - board stop I suddenly . there I see a narrow line of deep and glow red . it grow so vivid on the faded damask , and in the white glare of the level sun , that I think it be on fire . hastily set a chair by the pillar , for I would not tread on that bed , I leap up , and closely examine the crimson vein . 
 without think , I know what it be - the heart - blood of my father . there be three distinct and several mark , trace by the reeking dagger . the first on the left , which have catch my glance , be the broad and clear to read . two line , meet at a right angle , rudely form a Roman L. rudely I say , for the poniard have be too rich in red ink , which have clot where the two stroke meet . the second letter be a roman d , form also by two bold stroke , the upright very distinct , the curve less easily trace at the top , but the low part deep and clear . the third letter be not so plain . it look like c at first , but upon further examination I feel convinced that it be mean for an o , leave incomplete through the want of more writing fluid ; or be it then that my mother have seize the dark author by the hair , as he stoop to incline his pen that the last drop might trickle down ? 
 decipher thus with finger and eye , I trace these letter of blood , one by one , over and over again , till they dance in my gaze like the northern light . I stand upon tiptoe and kiss they ; I care not what I be do : it be my own father 's blood , and I think of the heart it come from , not of the hand which shed it . when I turn away , the surprise , for which till then I have find no time , break full upon I . how could these letter , in spite of all my vigilance , so long have remain unseen ? why do the murderer peril his life yet more by stay to write the record , and seal perhaps the conviction of his deed ? and what do these character mean ? of these three question , the first be readily solve . the other two remain to I as new shadow of wonder . several cause have conspire to defer so long this discovery . in the first place , the damask have be of rich lilac , shoot with a pile of carmine , which , in the wave play of light , glossed at once and obscure the crimson stain , until the fade hue of art leave in strong contrast nature 's abide paint . secondly , my rapid growth and the clearness of my eye that day lessen the distance and favour perception . again - and this be perhaps the paramount cause - the winter sun , with ray unabsorbe by the snow , throw his sheer dint upon that very spot , keen , level , and uncoloure - a thing which could happen on few day in the year , and for few minute each day , and which never have happen during my previous search . perhaps there be also some chemical action of the ray of light which evoke as well as show the colour ; but of this I do not know enough to speak . suffice it that the letter be there , at first a great shock and terror , but soon a strong encouragement to I . 
 my course be at once to perpetuate the mark and speculate upon they at leisure , for I know not how fleeting they might be . I hurry downstairs , and speak to no one procure some clear tissue paper . apply this to the damask , and hold a card behind , I carefully trace with a pencil so much of the letter as could be perceive through the medium , and complete the sketch by copy most carefully the rest ; it be , however , beyond my power to keep my hand from tremble . a shade flit over my drawing - oh , how my heart leap ! 
 when I have finish the pencil - sketch , and before it be ink over   for I could not bring myself to paint it red , I kneel where my father die and thank God for this guidance to I . by the time I have dry my eye the sun be pass and the line of blood be go , even though I know where to seek they , have leave a pin in the damask . by measure I find that the letter be just three foot and a quarter above the spot where my father 's head have be . the large of they , the L , be three inch long and an eighth of an inch in width ; the other be nearly as long , but nothing like so wide . 
 tremble now , for the rush of passion which still the body be past , and step silently on the long silent floor , I go to the deep dark - mullioned window and try to look forth . after all my lone tumult , perhaps I want to see the world . but my jade eye and brain show only the same three letter burn on the snow and sky . evening , a winter evening , be flutter down . the sun be spend and stop by a grey mist , and the landscape full of dreariness and cold . for mile , the earth lie white and wan , with nothing to part life from death . no step be on the snow , no wind among the tree ; fence , shrub , and hillock be as wrinkle in a winding - sheet , and every stark branch have like I its own cold load to carry . 
 but on the left , just in sight from the gable - window , be a spot , black as midnight , in the billowy snow . it be the spring which have store for I the footprint . perhaps I be superstitious then ; the omen be accept . suddenly a last gleam from the dauntless sun come through the ancient glass , and fling a crimson spot upon my breast . it be the red heart , centre of our shield , win with Coeur de Lion . 
 oh scutcheon , blazonment , and other gewgaw , by which man think to ennoble daylight murder , how long shall fool account it honour to be tattooed with you ? mercy , fellow - feeling , truth , humility , virtue that never flap their wing , but shrink lest they should know they stoop , what have these win ? gaze sinister , and their crest a pillory . 
 with that red pride upon my breast , and that black heart within , and my young form stately with revenge , I be a true descendant of Crusaders . 
 CHAPTER VI . 
 to no one , not even to Thomas Kenwood   in whom I confide most , do I impart the discovery just describe . again and again I go to examine those letter , jealous at once of my secret , and fearful lest they should vanish . but though they remain perhaps unaltered , they never appear so vivid as on that day . 
 with keener interest I begin once more to track , from page to page , from volume to volume , the chronicled step of limping but sure - footed justice . 
 not long after this I be provide with a companion . " Clara , " say my guardian one day at breakfast , " you live too much alone . have you any friend in the neighbourhood ? " 
 " none in the world , except my mother . " 
 " well , I must try to survive the exclusion . I have do my good . but your mother have succeed in find a colleague . there be a cousin of yours come here very soon . " 
 " mother dear , " I cry in some surprise , " you never tell I that you have any niece . " 
 " neither have I , my darling , " she reply , " nor any nephews either ; but your uncle have ; and I hope you will like your visitor . " 
 " now remember , Clara , " resume my guardian , " it be no wish of mine that you should do so . to I it be a matter of perfect indifference ; but your mother and myself agree that a little society would do you good . " 
 " when be she to come ? " I ask , in high displeasure that no one have consult I . 
 " he be likely to be here to - morrow . " 
 " oh , " I exclaim , " the plot be to humanize I through a young gentleman , be it ? and how long be he to stay in my house ? " 
 " in your house ! I suppose that will depend upon your mother 's wish . " 
 " more likely upon your , " I cry ; " but it matter little to I . " 
 he say nothing , but look displeased ; my mother do the same , I be silent , and the subject drop . but of course I see that he wish I to like his new importation , while he dissemble the wish from knowledge of my character . 
 two year after my father 's birth , his father have marry again . of the second wedlock the only offspring be my guardian , Edgar Vaughan . he be a posthumous son , and his mother in turn contract a second marriage . her new husband be one Stephen Daldy , a merchant of some wealth . by he she leave one son , name Lawrence , and several daughter . this Lawrence Daldy , my guardian 's half - brother , prove a spendthrift , and , while scatter the old merchant 's treasure marry a fashionable adventuress . as might be expect , no retrenchment ensue , and he die in poverty , leave an only child . 
 this boy , Clement Daldy , be of my own age , or thereabout , and , in pursuance of my guardian 's plan , be to live henceforth with we . 
 he arrive under the wing of his mother , and his character consist in the absence of any . if he have any quality at all by which one could know he from a doll , it be perhaps vanity ; and if his vanity be singular enough to have any foundation , it could be only in his good look . he be , I believe , as pretty a youth as ever talk without mind , or smile without meaning . need it be say that I despise he at once unfathomably ? 
 his mother be of a very different order . long - endure , astute , and plausible , with truth no more than the pith of a straw , she add thereto an imperious spirit , embody just now in an odious meekness . whatever she say or do , in her large contempt of the world , her lady - abbess walk , and the chastened droop of her brilliant eye , she convey through it all the impression of her humble superiority . though profoundly convinced that all be vanity , she be reluctant to force this conviction on mind of a narrow scope , and dissemble with conscious grace her knowledge of human nature . 
 to a blunt , outspoken child , what could be more disgusting ? but when upon this be assume an air of deep pity for my ignorance , and interest in my littleness , it become no long bearable . 
 this Christian Jezebel nearly succeed in estrange my mother from I . the latter feel all that kindness towards she which people of true religion , when over - charitable , conceive towards all who hoist and salute the holy flag . our sweet pirate know well how to make the most of this . 
 for myself , though I feel that a hypocrite be below the level of hate , I could not keep my composure when with affectionate blandness our visitor dare to " discharge her sacred duty of impress on I the guilt of harbouring thought of revenge . " of course , she do not attempt it in the presence of my mother ; but my guardian be there , and doubtless know her intention . 
 it be on a Sunday after the service , and she have stay for the sacrament . 
 " my sweet child , " she begin , " you will excuse what I be about to say , as I only speak for your good , and from a humble sense that it be the path of duty . it have please God , in his infinite wisdom , to afflict your dear mother with a melancholy so sensitive , that she can not bear any allusion to your deeply - lament father . you have therefore no female guidance upon a subject which justly occupy so much of your thought . your uncle Edgar , in his true affection for you , have think it right that you should associate more with person calculate to develop your mind . " 
 now I hate that word " develop ; " and I feel my passion rise , but let she go on : - 
 " under these circumstance , it grieve I deeply , my poor dear child , to find you still display a perversity , and a wilful neglect of the blessed mean of grace , which must   humanly speak   draw down a judgment upon you . now , open your heart to I , the whole of your little unregenerate heart , you mysterious but   I firmly believe   not ill - dispose lambkin . tell I all your thought , your brooding , your dream - in fact , your entire experience . Uncle Edgar will leave the room , if you wish it . " 
 " certainly not , " I say . 
 " quite right , my dear ; have no secret from one who have be your second father . now tell I all your little trouble . make I your mother - confessor . I take the deep interest in you . true , I be only a weak and sinful woman , but my chastisement have work together for my edification , and God have be graciously pleased to grant I peace of mind . " 
 " you do not   look as if you have much , " I cry . 
 her large eye flash a quick start from their depth , like the stir of a newly - fathomed sea . my guardian 's face gleam with a smile of sly amusement . recover at once her calm objective superiority , she proceed : 
 " I have be trouble and chasten severely , but now I perceive that it be all for the good . but perhaps it be not very graceful to remind I of that . yet , since all my trial have work together for my good , on that account I be , under Providence , well qualified to advise you , in your dark and perilous state . I have see much of what thoughtless people call ' life . ' but in help you , I wish to proceed on high principle than those of the world . you possess , beyond question , a strong and resolute will , but in your present benighted course it can lead only to misery . now , what be the principal aim of your life , my love ? " 
 " the death of my father 's murderer . " 
 " exactly so . my unhappy child , I know it too well . though a dark sin be your lead star , I feel too painfully my own shortcoming , and old unregenerate tendency , to refuse you my carnal sympathy . you know my feeling , Edgar . " 
 " indeed , Eleanor , " reply my guardian , with an impenetrable smile , " how should I ? you have always be such a model of every virtue . " 
 she give he a glance , and again address I . " now suppose , Clara Vaughan , that , after year of brood and lonely anguish , you obtain your revenge at last , who will be any the well for it ? " 
 " my father and I. " 
 " your father indeed ! how you wrong his sweet and most forgiving nature ! " 
 this be the first thing she have say that touch I ; and that because I have often think of it before . but I would not let she see it . 
 " though his nature be an angel 's , " I cry , " as I believe it be , never could he forgive that be who tear he from I and my mother . I know that he watch I now , and must be cold and a wanderer , until I have do my duty to he and myself . " 
 " you awful child . why , you will frighten we all . but you make it the more my duty . come with I now , and let I inculcate the doctrine of a high and holier style . " 
 " thank you , Mrs. Daldy , I want no teaching , except my mother 's . " 
 " you be too wilful and headstrong for she . come to I , my poor stray lamb . " 
 " I would soon go to a butcher , Mrs. Daldy . " 
 " be it possible ? be you so lose to all sense of right ? " 
 " yes , if you be right , " I reply ; and leave the room . 
 Thenceforth she pursue tactic of another kind . she try I with flattery and fictitious confidence , likely from a woman of her maturity to win a young girl , by inflate self - esteem : she even feign a warm interest in my search , and wish to partake in my reading and secret musing . indeed , I could seldom escape she . I be ready to own that , by her suggestion and quick apprehension , she gain some ascendancy over I , but not a tenth part of what she think she have win ; and I still continue to long for her departure . of this , however , no symptom appear : she make herself quite at home , and do she good to become indispensable to my mother . 
 Clement Daldy have full opportunity to commend himself to my favour . we be constantly throw together , in the presence of his mother , and the absence of mine . for a long time , I be too young , and too much engross by the object for which I live , to have any inkling of their scheme ; but suddenly a suspicion break upon I . my guardian and his sister - in - law have form , as I think , a deliberate plot for marry I , when old enough , to that tailor 's block . the one have be so long accustom to the lordship of the property , to some county influence , and great command of money , that it be not likely he would forego the whole without a struggle . but he know quite well that the moment I should be of age I would dispense with his wardship , and even with his residence there , and devote all I have to the pursuit of my " monomania . " all his endeavour to make I his thrall have fail , partly from my suspicion , partly from a repugnance which could not be conquer . of course , I intend to give he an ample return for his stewardship , which have be wise and unwearying . but this be not what he want . the motif of his accomplice require no explanation . if once this neat little scheme should succeed , I must remain in their hand , clement be nobody , until they should happen to quarrel for I . 
 to show what Clement Daldy be , a brief anecdote be enough . when we be about sixteen year old , we sit in the park one morning , at the corner of the lake ; Clement 's little curl spaniel , which he love as much as he could love anything , be gambol round we . as the boy lounge along , half asleep , on the rustic chair , with his silky face shade by a broad hat , and his bright curl glisten like daffodil play , I think what a pretty peep - show he make , and wonder whether he could anyhow be the owner of a soul . 
 " oh , Clara , " he lisp , as he chance to look up - " Couthin Clara , I wish you would not   look at I tho . " 
 " and do it look fierce at its dolly ? " I say ; for I be always good - natured to he . " dolly know I would not   hurt it , for it be house full of sugar - plum . " 
 " then do let I go to thleep ; you be such a howwid girl . " 
 so I hush he off with a cradle song . but before the long lash sink flat on his cheek , like the ermine tip on my muff , and while his red lip yet tremble like cherry in the wind , my attention be suddenly draw to the lake . there be a plashing , and barking , and hiss , and napping of snow - white wing - poor Juan engage in unequal combat with two fierce swan who have a nest on the island . the poor little dog , though he fight most gallantly , be soon drive into deep water , and the swan keep knock he under with rapid and powerful stroke . see he almost drown , I call Clement to save he at once . 
 " I can not , " say the brave youth ; " you go if you like . they will kill I , and I can not   bear it ; and the water ith tho cold . " 
 in a moment I push off the boat which be near , jump into it , and , seize an oar , contrive to beat back the swan , and lift the poor little dog on board , gasp , half - drown , and woefully beat . meanwhile my lord elect have leap on the seat for safety , and be wring his white little hand , and dancing and cry , " oh , Clara'll be throwne , and they will say it be I . oh , what thall I do ! what thall I do ! " 
 even when I bring he his little pet safe , he would not touch he , because he be wet ; so I lay he full on his lap . 
 CHAPTER VII . 
 the spring of the year 1849 be remarkable , throughout the western county , for long drought . I know not how it may be in the east of England , but I have observe that in the west long drought occur only in the spring and early summer . in the autumn we have sometimes as much as six week without rain , and in the summer a month at most , but all the real drought   so far as my experience go   commence in February or March ; these be , however , so rare , and April have win such poetic fame for shower , and July for heat and dryness , that what I state be at variance with the popular impression . 
 be that as it may , about Valentine's - day , 1849 , and after a length of very changeable weather , the wind fix its home in the east , and the sky for a week be grey and monotonous . brilliant weather ensue ; white frost at night , and strong sun by day . the frost become less biting as the year go on , and the sun more powerful ; there be two or three overcast day , and people hop for rain . but no rain fall , except one poor drizzle , more like dew than rain . 
 with habit now so ingrained as to become true pleasure , I mark the effect of the drought on all the scene around I . the meadow take the colour of russian leather , the cornland that of a knife - board . the young leaf of the wood hung pinch and crisp , unable to shake off their tunic , and more like catkin than leaf . the pool go low and dark and thick with a coppery scum   in autumn it would have be green , and little bubble come up and pop where the earth crack round the side . the tap - root plant look comely and brave in the morning , after their drink of dew , but flag and flop in the afternoon , as a club cabbage do . as for those which have only the surface to suck , they dry by the acre , and powder away like the base of a bonfire . 
 the ground be hard as horn , and fissure in star , and angle , and jagged gap crack , like a dissect map or a badly - plaster wall . it amuse I sometimes to see a beetle suddenly cut off from his home by that which to he be an earthquake . how he would run to and fro , look doubtfully into the dark abyss , then , rise to the occasion , bridge his road with a straw . the snail shrink close in their shell , and resign themselves to a spongy distance of slime . the bird might be see in the morning , hop over the hollow of the shrunken pond , pry for worm , which have shut themselves up like caddise deep in the thirsty ground . our lake , which be very deep at the low end , become a refuge for all the widgeon and coot and moorhen of the neighbourhood , and the quick - diving grebe , and even the summer snipe , with his wild and lonely " cheep . " the brink of the water be feather , and dabble with countless impression of foot of all sort - dibber , and wader , and wagtail , and weasel , and otter , and fox , and the bore of a thousand bill , and muscle lay high and dry . 
 for my own pet robin I use to fill pan with water along the edge of the grass , for I know their dislike of the mineral spring   which never go dry , and to these they would fly down and drink , and perk up their impudent head , and sluice their poor little dusty wing ; and then , as they could not sing now , they would give I a chirp of gratitude . 
 when the drought have last about three month , the east wind , which till then have be cold and creep , become suddenly parch hot . Arid and heavy , and choke , it pant along the glade , like a dog on a dusty road . it come down the water - meadow , where the crowsfoot grow , and wild celery , and it lick up the dreg of the stream , and powder the flood - gate , all skeleton now , with grey dust . it come through the copse , and the young leaf shrunk before it , like a child from the hiss of a snake . the blast push the door of our house , and its dry wrinkle hand be lay on the wall and the staircase and woodwork ; a hot grime track its step , and a taint fall on all that be fresh . as it fold its baleful wing , and lie down like a desert dragon , vegetation , so long a time sick , give way at last to despair , and flag off flabbed and dead . the clammy grey dust , like hot sand throw from rampart , eat to the core of everything , choke the shrivelled pore and stifle the languid breath . old gaffer be talk of murrain in cattle , and famine and plague among man , and farmer be too badly off to grumble . 
 but the change even now be at hand . the sky which have long present a hard and cloudless blue , but trail a light haze round its rim in the morning , be bedim more every day with a white scudding vapour across it . the sun grow large and pale , and lean more on the heaven , which soon become rib with white skeleton - cloud ; and these in their turn grow soft and deep , then furry and ravel and wispe . one night the hot east wind drop , and , next morning   though the vane have not change , the cloud drive heavily from the south - west . but these sign of rain grow for several day before a single drop fall ; as be always the case after discontinuance , it be hard to begin again . indeed , the sky be amass with black cloud , and the dust go swirl like a mat beat over the tree , and the air become cold , and the wind moan three day and three night , and yet no rain fall . as old Whitehead , the man at the lodge , well observe , it have " forget the way to rain . " then it suddenly clear one morning   the 28th of May , and the west be streak with red cloud , that come up to crow at the sun , and the wind for the time be lull , and the hill look close to my hand . so I go to my father 's grave without the little green watering - pot or a trowel to fill the chink , for I know it would rain that very day . 
 in the eastern shrubbery there be a pond , which my father have take much trouble to make and adorn ; it be not feed by the mineral spring , for that be think likely to injure the fish , but by a large and purer stream , call the " Witches ' brook , " which , however , be now quite dry . this pond have be plant around and through with silver - weed , thrumwort and sun - clew , water - lily , arrow - head , and the rare double frog - bit , and other aquatic plant , some of they bring from a long distance . at one end there be a grotto , case with fantastic porous stone , and inside it a small fountain play . but now the fountain be silent , and the pond shrink almost to its centre . the silver eel which once have abound here , find their element likely to fail , make a migration , one dewy night , overland to the lake below . the fish , in vain envy of that great enterprise , huddle together in the small wet space which remain , with their back - fin here and there above water . when any one come near , they dash away , as I have see grey mullet do in the shallow sea - side pool . several time I have water pour in for their benefit , but it be go again directly . the mud round the edge of the remnant puddle be bake and crack , and foul with an oozy green sludge , the relic of water - weed . 
 this little lake , once so clear and pretty , and full of bright dimple and crystal shadow , now look so forlorn and wasted and old , like a bright eye wear dim with year , and the tree stand round it so fade and wan , the poplar unkempt of its silver and green , the willow without wherewithal to weep , and the spray of the birch lay dead at its foot ; altogether it look so empty and sad and piteous , that I have be deeply grieve for the sake of he who have love it . 
 so , when the sky cloud up again , in the afternoon of that day , I hasten thither to mark the first effect of the rain . 
 as I reach the white shell - walk , which loosely girt the pond , the lead - colour sky take a greyer and woollier cast , and overhead become blurred and pulpy ; while round the horizon it lift in fray festoon . as I take my seat in the grotto , the big drop begin to patter among the dry leaf , and the globule roll in the dust , like parch pea . a long hiss sound ensue , and a cloud of powder go up , and the tree move their bough with a heavy dull sway . then break from the laurel the song of the long - silent thrush , and reptile , and insect , and all that could move , dart forth to rejoice in the freshness . the earth send forth that smell of sweet newness , the breath of young nature awake , which remind we of milk , and of clover , of balm , and the smile of a child . 
 but , most of all , it be in and around the pool that the sign of new life be stir . as the circle begin to jostle , and the bubble sail close together , the water , the slime , and the bank , dance , flickered , and darken , with a whirl of living creature . the surface be brush , as green corn be flaw by the wind , with the quivering dip of swallow ' wing ; and the ripple that race to the land splash over the foot of the wagtail . 
 here , as I mark all narrowly , and seem to rejoice in their gladness , a sudden new wonder befell I . I be watch a monster frog emerge from his penthouse of ooze , and lift with some pride his brown spot and his bright golden throat from the matted green cake of dry weed , when a quick gleam shoot through the fibre . with a listless curiosity , wonder whether the frog , like his cousin the toad , be a jeweller , I advance to the brim of the pool . the poor frog look timidly at I with his large start eye ; then , shoulder off the green coil , make one rapid spring , and be safe in the water . but his movement have far disclose some glitter object below . determine to know what it be , despite the rain , I place some large pebble for step , run lightly , and lift the weed . before I lay , as bright as if polish that day , with the jewel hilt towards I , a long narrow dagger . with a haste too rapid for thought to keep up , I snatch it , and rush to the grotto . 
 there , in the drought of my long revenge , with eye on fire , and tooth set hard and dry , and every root of my heart cleave and cry to heaven for blood , I pore on that weapon , whose last sheath have be - how well I know what . I do not lift it towards God , nor fall on my knee and make a theatrical vow ; for that there be no necessity . but for the moment my life and my soul seem to pass along that cold blade , just as my father 's have do . a treacherous , blue , three - cornered blade , with a point as keen as a viper 's fang , sublustrous like ice in the moonlight , sleuth as hate , and tenacious as death . to my curdled and fury - strike vision it seem to writhe in the gleam of the storm which play along it like a corpse - candle . I fancy how it have quiver and rung to find itself deep in that heart . 
 my passion at length overpower I , and I lay , how long I know not , utterly insensible . when I come to myself again , the storm have pass over , the calm pool cover my stepping stone , the shrub and tree weep joy in the moonlight , the nightingale sing in the elm , healing and beauty be in the air , peace and content walk abroad on the earth . the May moon sleep on the water before I , and stream through the grotto arch ; but there it fall cold and ghost - like upon the tool of murder . over this I hastily fling my scarf ; coward , perhaps I be , for I could not handle it then , but flee to the house and dream in my lonely bed . 
 when I examine the dagger next day , I find it to be of foreign fabric . " Ferrati , Bologna , " the name and abode of the maker , as I suppose , be damascene on the hilt . a cross , like that on the footprint , but small , and make of gold , be inlay on the blade , just above the handle . the hilt itself be wreathe with a snake of green enamel , have garnet eye . from the fine temper of the metal , or some annealing process , it show not a stain of rust , and the blood which remain after write the letter before describe have probably be wash off by the water . I lay it most carefully by , along with my other relic , in a box which I always keep lock . 
 so God , as I think , by his sun , and his season , and weather , and the mind he have so prepare , be hold the clue for I , and shake it clear from time to time , along my dark and many - wind path . 
 CHAPTER VIII . 
 soon after this , a ridiculous thing occur , the consequence of which be grave enough . the summer and autumn after that weary drought be rather wet and stormy . one night towards the end of October , it blow a heavy gale after torrent of rain . go to the churchyard next day , I find , as I have expect , that the flower so carefully keep through the summer be shatter and strew by the temp ; and so I return to the garden for other to plant in their stead . my cousin Clement   as he be tell to call himself   come saunter towards I among the bed . his usual look of shallow brightness and empty self - esteem have fail he for the moment , and he look like a fan - tail pigeon who have tumble down the horse - rack . he follow I to and fro , with a sort of stutter walk , as I choose the plant I like well ; but I take little notice of he , for such have be my course since I first discover their scheme . 
 at last , as I stoop to dig up a white verbena , he come behind I , and begin his errand with more than his usual lisp . this I shall not copy , as it be not worth the trouble . 
 " oh , Clara , " he say , " I want to tell you something , if you will only be good - natured ! " 
 " do not   you see I be busy now ? " I reply , without turn to look . " will not   it do when you have take your curl - paper off ? " 
 " now , Clara , you know that I never use curl - paper . my hair do not   want it . you know it be much pretty than your long wave black stuff , and it curl of its own accord , if mamma only brush it . but I want to tell you something particular . " 
 " well , then , be quick , for I be go away . " and with that I stand up and confront he . he be scarcely so tall as myself , and his light showy dress and pink rise of a face , which seem make to be wear in the hair , be throw into bright relief by my sombre apparel and earnest twilight look . some lurk sense of this contrast seem to add to his hesitation . at last he begin again : 
 " you know , Cousin Clara , you must not be angry with I , because it be not   my fault . " 
 " what be not your fault ? " 
 " why , that I should fall - what do they call it ? - fall in love , I suppose . " 
 " you fall in love , you dissolute doll ! how dare you fall in love , sir , without my leave ? " 
 " well , I be afraid to ask you , Clara . I could not   tell what you would say . " 
 " oh , that must depend , of course , on who Mrs. Doll be to be ! if it be a good little thing with blue satin arm , and a sash and a slip , and pretty blue eye that go with a string , perhaps I will forgive you , poor child , and set you up with a house , and a tea - set , and a mother - of - pearl perambulator . " 
 " now , do not   talk nonsense , " he answer . " before long I shall be a man , and then you will be afraid of I , and put up your hand , and shriek , and want I to kiss you . " 
 I have indulge he too much , and his tongue be take liberty . I soon stop he . 
 " how dare you bark at I , you wretche little white - woolled nursery dog ? " 
 I leave he , and go with my basket of flower along the path to the churchyard . for a while he stand there frighten , till his mother look forth from the drawing - room window . between the two fear he choose the less , and follow I to my father 's grave . I stand there and angrily wave he back , but he still persist , though tremble . 
 " Cousin Clara , " he say - and his lisp be quite go , and he try to be in a passion - " Cousin Clara , you shall hear what I have get to say . you have live with I now a long time , and I be sure we have agree very well , and I - I - no , I do not   see why we should not be marry . " 
 " do not   you indeed , sir ? " 
 " perhaps , " he continue , " you be afraid that I do not   care about you . really now , I often think that you would be very good - look , if you would only laugh now and then , and leave off those nasty black gown ; and then if you would only leave off be so grand , and mysterious , and stately , and get up so early , I would let you do as you like , and you might paint I and have a lock of my hair . " 
 " Clement Daldy , " I ask , " do you see that lake ? " 
 " yes , " he reply , turn pale , and incline to fly . 
 " there be water enough there now . if you ever dare again to say one word like this to I , or even to show by your look that you think it , I will take you and drown you there , as sure as my father lie here . " 
 he slunk away quickly without a word , and could eat no lunch that day . in the afternoon , as I sit in my favourite bow - window seat , Mrs. Daldy glide in . she have put on with care her cling smile , as she would an indian shawl . I think how much well her face would have look with its natural , bold , haughty gaze . 
 " my dear Clara , " begin this pious tidewaiter , " what have you do to vex so your poor cousin clement ? " 
 " only this , Mrs. Daldy : he be foolish or mad , and I give he advice in a truly christian spirit , entirely for his own good . " 
 " I hope , my dear , that some day it may be his duty as well as his privilege to advise you . but , of course , you need not take his advice . my Clara love her own way as much as any girl I ever know ; and with poor Clement she will be safe to have it . " 
 " no doubt of that , " I reply . 
 " and then , my pet , you will be in a far well position than you could attain as an unmarried girl to pursue the great aim of your life ; so far , I mean , as be not inconsistent with the spirit of christian forgiveness . your guardian have think of that , in effect this arrangement ; and I trust that I be not wrong in allow so fair a prospect , under Providence , of your ultimate peace of mind to influence I considerably when he seek my consent . " 
 " I be sure I be much oblige to you . " 
 " I can not conceal from you , so clear - sighted as you be - and if I could , I object to concealment of any kind , on principle - that there be also certain worldly advantage , which be not without weight , however the heart be wean by trial and chasten from transient thing . and your guardian have this arrangement so very much at heart . my own dear child , I have feel for you so long that I love you as a daughter . how thankful I ought to be to the Giver of all good thing to have you really my own dear child . " 
 " be thankful , madam , when you have get it . this be a good thing which under Providence you must learn to do without . " 
 it be coarse of I to hint at my rich . but what could I do with she ? 
 " why , Clara , " she ask , in great amazement , " you can not be so foolish and wilful as to throw away this chance of revenge ? if only for your dear mother 's sake , as well as your father 's , it be the path of duty . let I tell you , both she and yourself be very much more in your guardian 's power than you have any idea . and what would be your poor father 's wish , who have leave you so entirely to his brother 's care and discretion ? will you put off for ever the discovery of his murderer ? " 
 " my father , " I say , proudly , " would scorn I for do a thing below he and myself . the last of the Vaughans to be plot away to a grocer 's doll ! " 
 it have be a trial of temper ; and contempt be too much for hypocrisy . through the rouge of the world , and the pearl - powder of religion , nature flush forth on her cheek ; for she really love her son . she know where to wound I the deep . 
 " be it no condescension in we that my beautiful boy should stoop to the maniac - child of a man who be stab - stab in his midnight bed - to atone , no doubt , for some low act of his own ? " 
 I spring up , and ring the bell . Thomas Kenwood , who make a point of attend I , come at once . I say to he , calmly and slowly : 
 " allow this person one hour to pack her thing . get a fly from the Walnut Tree Inn , and see she beyond the Lodge . " 
 if I have tell he to drag she away by the hair , I believe that man would have do it . she shrink away from I ; for the moment her spirit be quell , and she tremble into a chair . 
 " I assure you , Clara , I do not mean what I say . you provoke I so . " 
 " not one word more . leave the room and the house . " 
 " Miss Vaughan , I will not leave this house until your guardian return . " 
 " Thomas , " I say , without look towards she , " if Mrs. Daldy be not go in an hour , you quit my service . " 
 how Thomas Kenwood manage it , I never ask . he be a resolute man , and all the servant obey he . she turn round once , as she cross the threshold , and give I a look which I shall never forget . be such the look that have glare on my father before the blow ? she lift the white arm of which she be proud , and throw back her head , like the Fecial hurl his dart . 
 " Clara Vaughan , you shall bitterly grieve for this . it shall throw you and your mother at the foot of your father 's murderer , and you shall crave meat bad than your enemy 's blood . " 
 until she have quit the house , I could not sit down ; but go to my father 's bedroom , where I often take refuge when strongly excited and unable to fly to his grave . the thought and the memory hover and sigh around that fatal chamber be enough to calm and allay the sensation of trivial wrong . 
 but now this be not the case . the outrage offer have be , not to I , but to he who seem present there . the suggestion , too , of an injury do by my father , though scorn at first , be work and ruffle within I , as child put bearded corn - ear in another 's sleeve , which by - and - by work their own way to the breast . till now , I have always believe that some worldly advantage or gain have impel my foe to the deed which leave I an orphan . but that woman 's dark word have start a new train of reasoning , whose very first motion be doubt of the man I worship . among all I have ever meet , there exist but one opinion as to what he have be - a true gentleman , who have injure not one of God 's creature , whose life have be guide mainly by the wish and welfare of other . moreover , I have my own clear recollection - his voice , his eye , and his smile , his manner and whole expression ; these , it be true , be but outward thing , yet a child 's intuition be strong and hard to refute . 
 again , during my remembrance , he have never be absent from we , except for a day or two , now and then , among his county neighbour ; and any ill will which he might have incur from they must , from his position , have become notorious . 
 and yet , in the tooth of this reasoning , and in spite of my own warm feeling , that horrible suspicion clave to my heart and chill it like the black spot of mildew . and what if the charge be true ? in that case , how be I well than he who have always be to my mind a fiend in special commission ? his be vengeance , and mine revenge ; he have suffer perhaps a wanton wrong , as deep to his honour as mine to my love . 
 while I be brood thus miserably , my eye fall upon the bed . there be the red streak , grain and fibre like the cross - cut of a fern - stalk ; frame and look down on I , the sampler of my life . draw near , I tremble with an unknown awe , to find myself in that lonely presence , not indeed think , but inkle such thing of my father , my own darling father , whose blood be look at I . in a storm of self - loathing and sorrow , I kneel there and sob my atonement ; but never thenceforth could I wholly bar out the idea . foul idea when once admit will ever return on their track , as the cholera walk in the trail of its former pall . 
 but instead of abate my dogged pursuit , I now have a new incentive - to dispel the aspersion cast on my father 's shadow . 
 CHAPTER IX . 
 at this particular time of my life , many thing begin to puzzle I , but nothing be a great puzzle than the character of my guardian . Morose or moody he be not , though a stranger might have think he so ; nor could I end with the conviction that his heart be cold . it rather seem to I as if he feel that it ought to be so , and try his good to settle down as the inmate of an icehouse . but any casual flush of love , any glow of native warmth from the heart around he , and take by surprise he waver for one traitor moment , and in his eye gleam some remembrance , like firelight upon frozen window . but let any one attempt to approach he then with softness , to stir kind interest and feel into benevolent expression , and Mr. Vaughan would promptly shut himself in again , with a bar of irony , or a bolt of sarcasm . only to my mother be his behaviour different ; towards she his manner be so gentle , and his tone so kind , that but for my conviction that remorse lie under it , I must have come to like he . true , they do not often meet , for dear mother confine herself   in spite of Mrs. Daldy   more and more closely to her own part of the house , and rarely have the spirit now to share in the meal of the family . therefore , I begin at once to take her place , and would not listen to Mrs. Daldy 's kind offer to relieve I . this have lead quite recently to a little outbreak . one day I have be rather late for dinner , and , enter the room with a proud apology , find to my amazement Mrs. Daldy at the head of the table . for I a seat be place , as for a good little girl , by the side of Master Clement . at first I have not the presence of mind to speak , but stand by my rival 's chair , wait for she to rise . she affect not to understand I , and begin , with her hand on the ladle , and look I full in the face : " I fear , darle Clara , the soup be cold ; but your uncle can give you a very nice slice of salmon . have you offer thank for these mercy ? " 
 " thank you , I will take soup . allow I to help myself . I be sorry to have trouble you . " 
 and I place my hand on the back of her chair , presume that she would get up ; but she never stir one inch , and actually call for a plate to help I . my guardian be look at both of we , with a dry smile of amusement , and Clement begin to simper and play with his fork . - now for it , or never , think I. " Mrs. Daldy , you quite mistake I , or pretend to do so . have the goodness to quit my chair . " 
 she have presume on my dread of an altercation before the servant , but only Thomas Henwood happen to be in the room . have there be a dozen present , I would still have assert my right . at last she rise in her stateliest manner , but with an awkward smile , and a still more awkward sneer . 
 " your use , my poor child , of the possessive pronoun be far more emphatic than your good breeding be . " 
 " who care for your opinion ? " not a hospitable inquiry ; but then she be not my visitor . 
 in grand style she march to the door , but soon think well of it , and come to her proper place with the sigh of a contrite spirit . 
 " poor creature ! it be a rebuke to I , for my want of true faith in the efficacy of prayer . " 
 and after all this , she make a most excellent dinner .
