 about that woman there be something of a slimy pride , no more like to upright prickly self - respect than macerate bird - lime be to the stiff bright holly . yet no one I ever know possess such wiry power of irritation . whenever my mother and my guardian meet , she take care to be in the way , and watch they both , and appeal to I with all her odious pantomime of sorrow , sympathy , wonder , love superiority , and spiritual yearning . and all the time her noisome smile , like the smell of a snake , come over we . she know , and rejoice in the knowledge , how hard set I be to endure it , and every quick flash of my eye only light up her unctuous glory . 
 for all I know , it be natural that my antipathy to that woman should , by reaction , thaw sometimes my coldness towards my uncle . though self - respect have at length compel he to abandon his overture to my friendship , now and then I detect he look at I with a pitying regard . in self - defence , I begin to pity he , and cease to make face or sneer when the maid - those romantic being - declare that he must have be cross in love . at this conclusion , long ago , all the servant ' hall have arrive ; and even little Tilly Jenkins , not admit as yet to that high conclave , remarkable only for live in dust - bin , and too dirty to cause uneasiness to the under - shoeboy 's mother - even that Tilly , I say , run up to I one morning   when I go to see my dear pony   and beat out her dust , and then whisper : 
 " oh , please , Miss Clara , to give my very good wish to Master . what a terrible blight to the heart be unrequited love ! " and Tilly sigh a great cloud of brick - dust . 
 " terrible , Tilly : I hope you have not fall in love with the weed boy ! " - a smart young lad , ten stair at least above she . 
 " I , miss ? do you think I would so demean myself ? " and Tilly catch up her dust - pan arrogantly . 
 this little anecdote prove a fact which I never could explain , viz . that none of the servant be ever afraid of I . 
 to return to the straight line of history . my guardian come home rather late that evening , and some hour after the hasty exit of Mrs. and Master Daldy . while I be wait in some uneasiness , it strike I that he have keep out of the way on purpose , lest he should seem too anxious about the plot . Mrs. Daldy , as I find afterwards , have write to he from the inn , describe my " frenzied violence , and foam satanic fury " - perhaps I turn pale , no more - and announce her intention to remain at Malvern , until she should be apprise whether uncle or niece be the master . in the latter case she demand - not that she care for mammon , but as a humble mean for the advancement of the Kingdom - the sum of 300 l. ; that be the low salary conscience allow she to specify for tread the furnace of affliction , to save the lost sheep of the house of Israel . I forget to say that , before she leave the house , she have try to obtain an interview with my mother , hop , no doubt , to leave she in the cataleptic state . but this have be sternly prevent by Thomas Kenwood , who perform quite a labour of love in minister the expulsion . all the servant hat she as a canting sneak and a spy . 
 that night when I receive Mr. Edgar Vaughan 's short missive - " Clara , I wish to see you immediately in my study , " my heart begin to flutter provokingly , and the long speech I have prepare fly away in shred of rhetoric . not that I mean for an instant to bate one tittle of what I have do and would do : but I have never assert my right as yet in direct opposition to he , nor take upon my own shoulder the guardianship of myself . but the dreary year of dark preparation and silent welding of character have brace a sensitive , nervous nature with some little self - reliance . 
 with all the indifference I could muster , I enter the gloomy room , and find he lean upon the high desk where he keep the account of his stewardship . the position be choose well . it serve at once to remind I of his official relation , and to appeal to the feeling as betoken an onerous wardship . of late his health have be fail he , and after every long absence from home , he return more jaded and melancholy . now a few silver hair - no more than a wife would have quickly pull out - be glisten among his black lock ; but though he be weary and lonesome , he seem to want none to love he , and his face wear the wonte sarcastic and travelled look . 
 as our glance meet , we both see that the issue be join which should settle for life the mastery . he begin in a light and jocund manner , as if I be quite a small thing . 
 " well do , Miss Clara , you be assert yourself . why , you have dismiss our visitor with very scant ceremony . " 
 " to be sure I have ; and will again , if they dare to come back . " 
 " and do not   you think that you might have consult your mother or I ? ' 
 " most likely I should have do so , in an ordinary case . " 
 " then your guardian be mean for small matter ! but what be the wonder to - day ? " 
 " no wonder at all . Mrs. Daldy insult my father , and I send she out of his house . " 
 " what make her insult my brother ? " 
 " my refusal to marry her puppet and puppy . " 
 " clement daldy ! do she propose such a thing ? she must think very highly of you ! ' 
 " then I think very lowly . " 
 " and you decline , do you , Clara ? " 
 " no . I refuse . " 
 " very good . no one shall force you ; there be plenty of time to consider the subject . " 
 " one moment be too much . " 
 " Clara , I have long notice in you a rude , disrespectful , and I will say   in spite of your birth   a low and vulgar manner towards I , your uncle and guardian . once for all , I will not permit it , child . " 
 " child you call I , do you ? Me , who be just seventeen , and have live seven such year as I have , and no one else ! " 
 he answer quite calmly , and look coldly at I : 
 " I never argue with woman . much less with girl . Mrs. Daldy come back to - morrow . you will beg her pardon , as become a young lady who have forget herself . the other question may wait . " 
 " I think , sir , that you have travel far , and in many country . " 
 the abrupt inquiry startle he , and his thought seem to follow the memory . 
 " what if I have ? " he ask , at length , and with a painful effort . 
 " have you always find woman do just what you choose ? " 
 he seem not to listen to I ; as if he be out of hearing : then laugh because I be look at he . 
 " Clara , " he say , " you be an odd girl , and a Vaughan all over . I would rather be your friend than your enemy . if you can not like I , at least forget your dislike of I , and remember that I be your uncle , and have try to make you love I . " 
 " and what if I do not ? " 
 " then I must keep you awhile from the management of this property . my dear brother would have wish it , until you recover your sens ; and not an acre of it be legally your . " 
 this he say so slowly , and distinctly , and entirely without menace , that , know his manner , I see it be the truth , at least in his opinion . strange as it may seem , I begin at once to revolve , not the result of dispossession and poverty on myself , or even on my mother , but the influence which the knowledge of this new fact must have on my old suspicion , surmise , and belief . 
 " will the property pass to you ? " I ask . 
 " yes , if I choose : or at any rate the bulk of it . " 
 " what part will be yours ? do you mean to say the house ? - " 
 " never mind now . I would rather leave thing as they be , if you will only be more sensible . " 
 " I will not disguise my opinion for a hundred Vaughan Parks , or a thousand Vaughan Palaces ; no , nor even to be near my father 's bone . " 
 " very well , " he say , " just as you like . but for your mother 's sake , I give you till Christmas to consider . " 
 " if you bring back Mrs. Daldy , I shall leave the door as she enter it . " 
 " I have no wish to hurry you , " he reply , " and therefore she shall not return at present . now take these paper with you . you may lay they before any lawyer you please . they be only copy , but may be compare with the original , which I have . they will quickly prove how totally you be at my discretion . " 
 " the money and the land may be so , but not i. before I go , answer I one question . do you know of these thing , whatever they may be , before my father 's death ? " 
 he look at I clearly and calmly , with no withdrawal , or conscious depth in his eye , and answer : 
 " no . as a gentleman , I do not . " 
 I feel myself more at a loss than ever , and for the moment could not think . 
 CHAPTER X. 
 thus be I , and , what matter much more , my mother , reduce quite suddenly from a position of rank and luxury , and a prospective income of L15,000 a - year   so much have the land increase in value   to a revenue of nothing , and no home . even to I it be a heavy blow , but what could my poor mother do ? 
 we be assure by counsel that a legal struggle could end in expense alone , and advise by the family lawyer to throw ourselves on the good feeling and appeal to the honour of Mr. Edgar Vaughan . Mr. Vaughan he must henceforth be call . I can not well understand , still less can I explain , small and threadbare technicality   mote , which too often be the beam of Justice , but the circumstance which rob I of my father 's home be somewhat as follow : - 
 by the will of my father 's grandfather , Hubert Vaughan , who die in the year 1782 , the whole of the family property be devise to his son , Vaughan Powis Vaughan , for life , and after his decease , to his son successively in tail male , fail these to his right heir in general . this will be say to have be prepare in haste : it be , in fact , draw by a country attorney , when the testator be rapidly sink . it be very brief , and by no mean accurately word ; neither do it contain those power to meet family exigency , which I be tell a proper practitioner would have insert . 
 there be no reason to suppose that the testator have contemplate anything more than a strict settlement of the usual kind , i.e. a common estate entail , expectant upon a life - interest ; and under which I should have succeed my father , as his heiress , in the ordinary course . but it be the chief fault of smatterer in the law   and country attorney at that time be no well   that they will attempt to be too definite . the country lawyer in this case , grossly ignorant of his profession , and catch by the jangle of the word tail male , have insert they at hazard , possibly not without some idea that they would insure a strict succession than a common entail would do . 
 when my father become of age , measure be take for bar the entail create by the will of Hubert Vaughan ; and at the time it be believe that these be quite effectual , and therefore that my father be now entitle in fee - simple , and could dispose of the property . 
 upon his marriage with my mother , she , with worthy pride , refuse most firmly to accept a jointure charge on his estate , allege that as she bring no fortune into the family , she would not incumber the family property , which have but recently be relieve of incumbrance . More than this - she have even insist upon expressly abandon , by her marriage settlement , all claim to dower . this unusual course she have adopt , because of some discontent express by relative of my father at his marriage with a portionless bride , whereby her self - respect have be deeply wound . so nothing be settle upon she , except her own little estate in Devonshire , which be secure to her separate use . 
 my father have never permit this excess of generosity on her part , but that he be by nature careless upon such subject , and hop to provide amply for her interest by his will : moreover he be hot to remove all obstacle to their marriage . but it be now discover that he have no power to charge the real estate for her benefit , in the manner his will import ; that he have never be more than a tenant in tail , and that entail such that I could not inherit . neither , of course , could I take under his will , as he possess no power of disposition . one quarter of all that have be write upon the subject I never could understand ; and even as to the simple point , sometimes I seem to apprehend they clearly , and then I feel that I do not . my account of the matter be compress from what I remember of the legal opinion . 
 the lead fact , at any rate , and the key to all the mischief , be , that the entail have never be bar at all : the legal process   call a " recovery "   which be to have have that effect , be null and void through some absurd informality . they tell I something about a tenant to a precipice , but they must have make a mistake , for there be no precipice on the estate , unless some cliff near the church could be call so , and they be never let . 
 be that as it may , my father 's will be declare to be waste paper , except as regard what they call the personalty , or , in good English , the money he have to bequeath . and of this there be very little , for , shortly before his death , he have spend large sum in drainage , farm - building , and other improvement . furthermore , he have always maintain a profuse hospitality , and his charity be most lavish . the lawyer tell we that , under the circumstance   a favourite expression of theirs when they mean some big robbery , a court of equity would perhaps consider our application to be " recuppe , " as they call it , out of the estate , for the money lay out in improvement under a false impression . but we have be cup enough already . grossly plunder by legal jargon , rob by statute , and scourge by scrivener ' tradition , we fling away in disgust the lint the bandit offer , and leave they " all estate , right , title , interest , and claim , whether at law or in equity , in to or out of " the licking of our blood . 
 but now my long suspicion , and never - discard conviction of my guardian 's guilt , be , by summary process , not only revive , but redouble . this arise partly from the discovery of the stake he have on my father 's life , and partly , perhaps , from a feeling of hatred towards our supplanter . that he know not till now the flaw in our title , and his own superior claim , be more than I could believe . I feel sure that he have gain this knowledge while in needy circumstance and sharp legal practice , bring , as he then most probably be , into frequent contact with the London agent who have the custody of the document . 
 to be in the same room with he , be now more than I could bear , and it become impossible that we should live any long in the same house . he , indeed , wish , or feign to wish , that we should remain there , and even show some reluctance to urge his unrighteous right . but neither my mother   who bear the shock with strange resignation   nor myself would hear of any compromise , or take a farthing at his hand , and he be too proud and stern to press upon we his compunction . 
 statement of our case have be prepare and submit to three most eminent conveyancer , and the three opinion have be find to agree , except upon some trivial point . More than two month have be thus consume , and it be now once more the anniversary of my father 's death . I have spend the time in narrowly watch my ex - guardian 's conduct , though keep aloof , as much as possible , from any intercourse with he . 
 one night , I steal into the room which he call his study , and where   with a child 's simplicity   I believe he to keep his private document . through Thomas Kenwood , to whom I now confide almost everything , and whose suspicion be even strong than mine , I obtain clandestine possession of the key of the large bureau . as I stand before that massive repository in the dead of night , the struggle within I be intense and long . what letter , what journal , document , or momentous relic of a thousand kind , might be lurk here , wait only for a daughter 's hand to turn the lock , and cast the light she bear on the death - warrant of her father ! how easy then to snatch away the proof , clutch it , though it should burn the hand or bosom , to wave it , with a triumph wilfully prolong , before the eye of justice 's dull - visioned minister ; and then to see , without a shudder or a thrill of joy , but with the whole soul gaze , the slow , struggle , ghastly expiation . as this thought come crawl through my heart , light up its depth as would a snake of fire , the buhl before I grow streak of blood , and the heavy crossbar a gallow . I lift my hand to open the outer lock . already the old cruciform key be tremble in the silver scutcheon . I raise the lamp in my left hand to show the lunette guard which curve above the hole , when a heavy mass all cold and dark fall across my eye . I start , and think for the moment , in my strong excitement , that it be my father 's hand . one instant more , and , through the trembling of my sens , I see that it be only a thick fold of my long black hair , shake down on the face by my bending and quiver posture . but the check be enough . a Vaughan , and that the last one of so proud and frank a race , to be prowl meanly , with a steal tool , to violate confidence , and pry through letter ! no suspicion , however strong , nothing short of certainty   if even that   could warrant it . drive away by shame combine with superstition , I glide from the cold silent room , and restore the key to my faithful friend , whom I have leave in the passage , order he at once to replace they , and never touch they again . 
 " well , miss , " he whisper , with a smile , " I know you could not   do it , because I seem , somehow , it be not   like a Vaughan . " 
 we be already prepare to quit the house , no long ours , when our dismissal become abrupt , through another act of mine . what drive I to such a wild deed I can scarcely tell . Shame , perhaps , for the furtive nature of my last attempt hurry I into the other extreme ; and now I be so shake by conflict impulse , that nothing be too mad for I . 
 on the seventh anniversary of my father 's death , and the last which I be likely ever to spend beneath that roof , I pass the whole day in alternate sadness and passion , in the bedroom where he die . all the relic I possess , both of his love and of his death , I bring thither ; and spread they out , and weep upon the one , and pray upon the other . I also bring my choice history of murder and revenge , and pore over they by the wan daylight and the dull lamp , and so on through the night , until my mind become the soul 's jetsam . 
 then I procure four very large wax candle , and light they at the head of the bed , two on each side , and spread a long white cloth between , as if my father be lie in state ; and hang a row of short light above , to illuminate the letter of blood . then I take a small alarum clock , give I by dear father , that I might rise for early walk with he , and set it upon a chest by the door , and fix it so as to ring five minute before the hour at which the murder befell . a cold presentiment crawl through I that , at the fatal time , I should see the assassin . after all these arrangement I take my volume again , and sit in the shade of the curtain , with a strong light on the page . I be deep in some horrible record , and creep with terror and hope , when the clear bell ring a long and startling peal . I leap up , like one shot through the heart , and what I do be without design or purpose . my glance fall on the dagger ; I catch it up , and snatch the lamp , and hurry down corridor and staircase , straight to my guardian 's private room . 
 he be sit at the table , for he never pass that night in bed . at the sound of the lock he leap up , and point a pistol , then hide it . straight up to he I go , as swiftly and quietly as a spirit , and speak : 
 " seven year ago , at this very moment , my father be kill . do you know this dagger ? " he start back , as if I have stab he with it , then cover his eye with both hand . 
 " you know it , then ? " I say , with a triumph chill all over I . " it be your hand that use it . " 
 another moment , and I should have strike he with it . I lift it in my frenzy ; when he look at I by some wonderful effort , calmly , steadily , even coldly . " yes , " he say , " I have see that weapon before . alas my poor dear brother ! " 
 whether it be true feeling that make his voice so low and deep , or only fierce self - control , I know not then , nor try to think . 
 " you know who own it ? " I ask , with my life upon his answer . 
 " yes . I know who own it once ; but many year ago . and I know not in the least what be become of he now . " 
 the baffled fury and prostrate hope - for at the moment I fully believe he - be too much for my reel brain and fast body . for one minute 's command of my faculty , I would have sell they for ever ; but I feel they ebb from I , as the life do from a wound . the hemisphere of my brain be part one from the other , and a grey void spread between they . I try to think , but could not . I strove to say anything , but fail . fainter and fainter grow the room , the lamp , the ceiling , the face at which I try to look . thing go to and fro with a quick quiver , like flame in the wind , then , round and round like whirling water ; my mouth grow stiff , and the tongue between my tooth feel like a glove ; and with a rush of sound in my brain and throat , and a scream pen up , yet burst , I fall , as I think , through the earth . I be only on the floor , in a fit . 
 when I come to myself , I be in my own bed , and my own dear mother bend over I , pale , and haggard , and full of tear . the broad daylight be around we , and the faint sunshine on her face . she have be with I ever since . in my weakness , I look up at she with a pang of self - reproach , to think how little I have value her love ; and I vow to myself to make up for it by future care and devotion . 
 that violent convulsion , and the illness after it , change I not a little both in mind and body . 
 CHAPTER XI . 
 it be indeed high time for I to cherish my mother . her pain at leave the place where she have know she little all of happiness - for her childhood have be overcast with trouble - her pain be so acute and overpower that all my deep impassione feeling sink reprove before it . 
 my guardian now seem much embitter against I , and anxious for our departure . he come once or twice , in my illness , to ask for and to see I ; and he bring back , unperceive by any one , the weapon for which I rave . but ere I be quite recover , he write , request to see I on business in his study . I could not speak yet without pain , have bite my tongue severely . 
 " your mother shall have a home here , " he say , " as long as ever she want one ; but as for you , malignant or mad , I will try no more to soften you . when first I see you in your early childhood , you fly at I as a murderer . soon after you ransack my cupboard and steal my boot , to compare they with some impression or cast you keep . yes , you look astonish . I never tell you of it , but I know it for all that . of those absurdity I think little , for I regard they as the folly of a mad child , and I pity you deeply , and even like you for your filial devotion . but now I find that you have grow up in the same belief , and you dare even now to avow it . you know that I have no fear of you . " 
 " then why have you get that pistol ? " 
 I see that he be vex and surprise at my having perceive it . 
 " in a house like this , where such deed have be do , I think it right to be arm . do you think if I have fear you , or your evidence , I would have restore that dagger ? " 
 " whose be it ? " 
 " I tell you the other night that I once see a weapon like it , for which at first I mistook it , but close examination convince I of the difference . " 
 " how do it differ ? " 
 " in this . there be no snake on the handle of the other , though there be the cross on the blade . " 
 " and where do you see the other ? " 
 " some day I will tell you . it be not right to do so now . " 
 " not convenient to you , I suppose you mean . " 
 " I have also show you that the lock of hair find in your poor mother 's hand be much fine and more silky than mine ; and you know that I can not draw on my foot a boot so small as the one whose impression you have . but I be ashamed of myself for have stoop to such proof as these . dare you to look at I and suppose that I with my own hand could have stab my brother , a brother so kind and good to I , and for whose sake alone I have bear so long with you ? " 
 he try to look I down . I have meet but one whose gaze could master mine ; and he be not that one . 
 " so , you doubt I still ? be your thing pack ? ' 
 " yes , and my mother 's . " 
 " then if your mother be well enough , and will not let you leave she , you have well go next week . " 
 " no , " I reply , " we will go to - morrow . " 
 " wilful to the last . so be it . take this ; you can not refuse it in duty to your mother . " 
 he put in my hand an order for a large sum of money . I throw it into the fire . 
 " there have be criminal , " I exclaim , " who have suffer from a life - long fear , lest the widow and orphan , starve through their crime , should compass their die bed . though we starve in a garret , we touch no bread of your . " 
 " Bravo , Miss Melodrame . you need never starve in the present state of the stage . " 
 " that I do not   understand ; but this I do . it be perhaps the last time I shall ever see you live . whether you do that deed or not be know to God , and you , and possibly one other . but whether you do it or not , I know it be on your soul . your day be wretche , your night be troubled . you shall die as your brother die , but not so prepared for death . " 
 " good bye , Clara . my lunch be come up . " 
 God have much to forgive I , but nothing bad than the dark thought of that speech . in my fury at weakness in such a cause , I have dare sometimes to imagine that my mother know he to be the murderer , but conceal it for the sake of the family honour ! 
 CHAPTER XII . 
 no need to recount my bitter farewell to all the scene and object I have love so long , to all which possess a dark yet tender interest , and most of all to my father 's grave . that some attention might still be pay to this , I entrust it to the care of an old housekeeper of our , who be live in the village . my last visit be in the moonlight , and dear mother be there . I carry rather than lead she away . slight as my knowledge have be of lightsome and happy love , I be sure that a sombre affection be far the strong and sweeter . 
 as we begin our journey , a crowd of the villager meet we beyond the lodge , and line the Gloucester road as far as the old oak - tree . while our hire conveyance pass between they , the man stand mute with their hat in their hand , the woman sob and curtsey , and bless we , and hold up their child to look at we . 
 our refuge be the small estate or farm in Devonshire , which I have mention as my mother 's property . this , which produce L45 a - year , be all that now remain to we , except a sum of L1,000 leave to I by a godfather , and of which I could not touch the principal . the residue of the personalty , and the balance at the banker 's , we have refuse to take , be assure that legally we be responsible to Mr. Vaughan , even for the back rent of the Gloucestershire estate . of course we have plenty of jewellery , some of it rather valuable , but the part most precious be heirloom , and that we have leave behind . Most of our own have be my father 's gift , and therefore we could not bear to sell it . 
 as regard myself , this comparative poverty be not of very great moment , except as impair my mean of search ; but for my mother 's sake I be cut to the heart , and lose in perplexity . she have so long be accustom to much attention and many luxury , which her weak health have make indispensable to she . Thomas Henwood and poor Ann maple insist on follow our fortune , at one third of their previous wage . my mother think it beyond our mean to keep they even so ; but for her sake I resolve to try . I need not say that I carry all my relic , difficult as it be to hide they from my mother . 
 when we reach our new home , late in the evening of the second day , a full sense of our privation for the first time break upon we . it be mid - winter , and in the gloom of a foggy night , and after the weariness of a long journey , our impression be truly dismal . jolt endlessly up and down by rut a foot deep and slaty stone the size of coal - scuttle , entomb alive betwixt grisly hedge which meet above we like the wing of night , then oblige to walk up treadmill hill while the rickety fly crawl up behind ; then again plunge and lurch down some corkscrew steep to the perpetual wood and rush stream at the bottom ; at length and at last along a lane so narrow that it scrap we on both side as we pass , a lane which zig - zagge every thirty yard with a tree - bole jutting at every corner , at length and at last we come to the farmyard gate . it be not far from the lonely village of Trentisoe , which lie some six mile to the west of Lynmouth . this part be little know to London tourist , though it possess scenery of a rare kind than Lynmouth itself can show . 
 pass through an outer court , with a saw - pit on one side and what they call a " linhay " on the other , and where a slop of straw and " muck " quelche under the wheel , we come next to the farmyard proper , and so   as the flyman express it   " home to ouze . " the " ouze " be a low straggle cottage , jag - thatch , and heavy - eaved , and remind I strongly of ragged wet horse - cloth on a rack . the farmer be not come home from Ilfracombe market , but his wife , Mrs. Honor Huxtable , soon appear in the porch , with a bucket in one hand and a candle stick in a turnip in the other . in the cross - light , we see a stout short woman , brisk and comely , with an amazing cap , and cheek like the apple which they call in Devonshire " hoary morning . " 
 " a massy on we , Zuke , " she call into the house , " if here bain't   the genelvolk coom , and we be arl of a muck ! Hum , cheel , hum for thee laife to the calf ' ouze , and toorn out both the peg , and take the pick to the strah , and gie un a veed o ' wet . " 
 have thus provide for our horse , she advance to we . 
 " so , ye be coom at last ! I be crule glad to zee e , zure enough . baint e starve amost ! an unkid place it be for the laike of you . " 
 so say , she hurry we into the house , and set we before a wood - fire all glow upon the ground , beneath an enormous chimney pod with great pot and crock hang on thing like saw . these pot , like Devonshire hospitality , be always boil and chirping . the kitchen be low , and floor with lime and sand , which be wear into pit such as boy use for marble ; but the great feature be the ceiling . this be divide by deep rafter into four compartment lengthwise . across some of these , batten of wood be nail , form a series of rack , wherein repose at least a stye - ful of bacon . herb and store of many kind , and rope of onion dangle between . 
 Mrs. Huxtable go to the dresser , and get a large dish , and then turn round to have a good look at we . 
 " poor leddy , " she say gently , " I sim she 's turble weist and low . but look e zee , there be a plenty of bakken yanner , and us'll cut a peg 's drort to - morrow , and Varmer Badcock will zend we a ship , by rason ourn be all a'lambe . " then she turn to I . 
 " Whai , Miss , you look crule unkid tu . do e love zider ? " 
 " no , Mrs. Huxtable . not very much . I would rather have water . " 
 " oh drat that wash , e shall not   have none of thiccy . we have get a brown gearge of beer , and more nor a dizzen pan of mulk and crame . " 
 her chatter warmth soon put we at our ease ; and as soon as the parlour fire burn up , she show we with many apology , and " hop no offence " the room which be thenceforth to be ours . 
 after tea , I put my dear mother to bed as soon as possible , and sit by the die fire to muse upon our prospect . not the strangeness of the place , the new idea around I , not even my weariness after railroad , coach , and chaise , could keep my mind from its one subject . in fact , its colour have now become its form . 
 to other indeed , all hope of ever detect and bring to justice the man , for whose death I live , might seem to grow fainter and fainter . expel from that place , and banish from those recollection , where , and by which alone , I could well expect ever to wind up my clue , rob of all mean of move indifferent person and retain strong one ; and , more than this , engross   as I must henceforth be   in keep debt at bay , and shield my mother from care - what prospect be there , nay what possibility , that I a weak unaided girl , lead only by set will and fatalism , should ever overtake and grasp a man of craft , and power , and desperation ? 
 it matter not : let other thing be doubtful , unlikely , or impossible ; let the hand of man be clench against I , and the ear of heaven be stop ; let the earth be spread with thick darkness , as the water be spread with earth , and the murderer set Sahara between we , or turn hermit on the Andes ; happen what would , so God be still above we , and the world beneath our foot - I be as sure that I should send that man from the one to the throne of the other , as he be sure to be drag away thence , to fire , and chain , and gnashing of tooth . 
 CHAPTER XIII . 
 so impulsive , kind - hearte , and honest be Mrs. Huxtable , that we could always tell what be the next thing she be go to say or do . even at her meal she contrive to be in a bustle , except on Sundays ; but she get through a great deal of work . on Sundays she put on , with her good gown , an air of calm dignity which make she unhappy until it be off , which it be directly after the evening service . she seem a very sensible woman , and whatever the merit of the case she side always with the weak . the next morning we ask how it be she appear not to expect we , as I have write and post the letter myself on the previous Saturday . 
 " for sure now , " she reply , " and the papper scrawl coom'd on Monday ; but we bain't   girt scholard , and Varmer say most like ' twa the Queen 's taxis , for there be her head upon it ; so we put un in the big mortar till Beany Dawe should come over , or we should go to church next Zunday , and passon would discoorse it for we . but " - and off she run - " but she belong to you now , Miss Clerer , see as how you have   coom after un . " 
 so they have only a general idea that we be come , and know not when it would be . the following day , Thomas Henwood arrive , bring our box in a vehicle call a " butt , " which be a short and rudely make cart , use chiefly for carry lime . 
 after unpack our few embellishment , we set up a clumsy but comfortable sofa for my mother , and try to divert her sadness a little by many a shift and device to garnish our narrow realm . we remove the horrible print of " Death and the Lady , " which be hang above the chimneypiece , and sundry daub of our Lord and the apostle , and a woman of Samaria with a french parasol , and Eli fall from a turnpike gate over the great western steamer . but these alteration be not make without some wistful glance from poor Mrs. Huxtable . at last , when I begin to nail up a simple sketch of the church at Vaughan St. Mary instead of a noble representation of the Prodigal Son , wear a white hat with a pipe stick under the riband , and weep into a handkerchief with some horse upon it , the good dame could no long repress her feeling . 
 " Whai , Miss Clerer , Miss , dear art alaive , cheel , what be 'bout ? they be the smart picter anywhere this saide of Coorn . Varmer gi a pan of hog ' pudden for they , and a Chainey taypot and a Zunday pair of corderahoy . why them'll shaine with the zun on ' um , laike a vield of poppy and charlock . but thic smarl pokey papper of yourn ha'ant no more colour nor the track of a marly scrarly . a massy on we if I could not   walk a well picter than thic , with my patten on in the zider squash . " 
 to argue with such a connoisseur would have be bad than useless ; so I pacify she by hang the reject gem in her own little summer room by the dairy . our parlour begin before long to look neat and even comfortable . of course the furniture be rough , but I care not much for upholstery , and be quite rude of french polish . my only fear be lest the damp from the lime - ash floor should strike to my dear mother 's foot , through the scanty drugget which cover it . the fire - place be bright and quaint , line with old dutch tile , and the grey - wash wall be less offensive to the eye than would have be a paper choose by good Mrs. Huxtable . the pretty lattice window , bud even now with woodbine , and impudent to the wind with myrtle , would have make amend for the mean room in England . before it lie a simple garden with sparry walk and bright - thatched hive , and down a dingle rich with tree and a crystal stream , it catch a glimpse of the Bristol Channel . 
 CHAPTER XIV . 
 when our thing be nearly settle , and I be sit by myself , with dirty hand and cover with dust , there come a little timid tap at the door , follow by a shuffle outside , as if some one contemplated flight , yet fear to fly . open the door , I be surprised to find the child whom I expect a massive figure , some six foot and a quarter high , and I know not how many foot in width , but wide enough to fill the entire passage . he make a doubtful step in advance , till his great open - hearted face hang sheepishly above my head . 
 " have I the pleasure of see Mr. Huxtable ? " I ask . 
 " Ees ' um , " he stammer , blush like a beet - root , " leastway Miss , I ort to zay , no plasure ' um to the laike of thee , but a honour to ai . Varmer Uxtable they karl I round about these ' ere part , and some on them Varmer Jan , and Beany Dawe , he karl I ' Varmer Brak - plew - harnish , as toss arl they garnish , ' and a dale he think of his potry as it please God to give ' un : but Maister , may be , be the riglar thing , leastway you know well , Miss. " " dange if I can coom to discourse with girt folk nohow , no more nor a sto - un . " this be an " aside , " but audible a long way off , as they always be on the stage . 
 " but I be a very small folk , Mr. Huxtable , compare at least with you . " 
 " I humbly ax your parding , Miss , but ai do not   goo for to be zuch a beg , nockety , sprarle zort of a chap . I do not   goo for to do it nohow . Reckon ' twar my moother 's valt , she be always drain of hayrick . " this also be an " aside . " 
 " come in , " I say , " I be very glad to see you , and so will my mother be . " 
 " Noo ! be e now ? be e though undade , my dear ? " he ask with the true and fine smile I ever see : and I feel ashamed in front of the strong simplicity which take my conventional word for heart 's truth . 
 " they be the good word , " he continue " as ai ' ve   ' eere this many a dai ; for ai'll be dange if ever a loi could coom from unner such eye as yourn . " 
 and thereupon he take my puny weak hand in his rough iron palm , like an almond in the nut - cracker , and examine it with pity wonder . 
 " wull , wull ! some hand be make for mulke coo , and some be make of the crame itself . now there could not   be such a purty thing as this ere , unless it wor to snow war'rm . but she bain't   no kaind of gude for rarstle ? and ai be aveare thee'll have to rarstle a rare bout wi the world , my dearie : one down , tother coom on , that be the wai of ' un . " 
 " oh , I be not afraid , Mr. Huxtable . " 
 he take some time to meditate upon this , and shake his head when he have finish . 
 " Noo , thee bain't   aveard yet I will warr'ne . gude art alaive , if e bain't   a spurrity maid . but if ere a chap zays the black word on e - and thiccy 's the taime when a maid can not   help herzell , then ony you karl Jan Uxtable that be arl my dear , and if so be it be in the dead hoor of the naight , and thee beest to tother zaide of Hexymoor , ai'll be by the zaide of thee zooner nor ai could thraw a vorehip . " 
 before I could thank he for his honest championship my mother enter the room , and all his bashfulness   lose for the moment in the pride of strength   come over he again like an extinguisher . although he do not tremble - his nerve be too firm for that - he stand fumble with his hat , and redden , and look vaguely about , at a loss where to put his eye or anything else . 
 my mother , quite wear out with her morning 's walk , surprised at her uncouth visitor , and frighten perhaps at his bulk , sink on our new - fangle sofa , in a stupor of weakness . then it be strange and fine to see the strong man 's sense of her feeble state . all his embarrassment vanish at once ; he see there be something to do ; and a look of deep interest quicken his great blue eye . poise his heavy frame with the lightness of a bird , he step to her side as if the floor have be holy , and , scarcely touch she , contrive to arrange the rude cushion , and to lay her delicate head in an easy position , as a nurse compose a child . all the while , his look and manner express so much feeling and gentleness , that he must have know what it be to lose a daughter or mother . 
 " poor dear leddy , " he whisper to I , " she be use to zummut more plum nor thiccy , I reckon . her zimth crule weist and low laike . hath she be long in that there wai ? " 
 " yes , she have long be weak and poorly ; but I fear that her health have be grow bad for the last few month . " I could not   help cry a little ; and I could not   help his see it . 
 " dang thee , Jan Uxtable , for a doilish girt zinny . now doon e tak on so , Miss ; doon e , that be a dear . Avore she be be here a wake , her'll be as peart as a gladdy . there bain't   in arl they furren part no place the laike of this ere to make a body ston upraight . the braze coom off o hexymoor as frash as a young coolt , and up from the zay as swate as the breath of a coo on the clover , and he will zit on your chake the zame as a dove on her nestie ; and ye'll be so hearty the both on ye , that ye'll karl for tatie and heggs and crame and inyon avore e be hout of bed . Ee 's fai ye wull . " with this homely comfort he depart , after a cheer glance at my mother . 
 before I proceed , the homeric epithet " break - plough - harness , " apply by the poet to Mr. Huxtable , need some explanation . it appear that the farmer , in some convivial hour   for at other time he detest vaunt , have lay a wager that he and Timothy Badcock , his farm - labourer , would plough half an acre of land , " wiout no beastessy in the falde . " now , it happen that the Parracombe blacksmith have lately be at Barnstaple , and there have see a man who have hear of plough by steam . so when the farmer 's undertaking get noise abroad and magnify , all Exmoor assemble to witness the exploit , wonder , trembling , and wrathful . bench and table be set in the " high Barton , " a nice piece of mealy land , just at the back of the house , while Suke and Mrs. Huxtable ply the cider - barrel for the yeoman of the neighbourhood . the farmer himself be not visible - no plough or ploughing tackle of any description appear , and a rumour begin to spread that the whole affair be a hoax , and the contriver afraid to show himself . but as people begin to talk of ' send for the constable "   who , of course be there all the time , and as cart - whip and knob - stick begin to vibrate ominously , Mrs. Huxtable make a signal to Mr. Dawe , who lead off the grumbling throng to the further end of the field , where an old rick - cloth lie along against the hedge . while the tilting be move aside , the bold son of Exmoor shrunk back , expect some horrible monster , whose smoke be already puff . all they see be a one - horse plough with the farmer , in full harness , sit upon it and smoke his pipe , and Timothy Badcock patiently stand at the plough - tail . amid a loud hurrah from his friend , Mr. Huxtable leap to the fore , and cast his pipe over the hedge ; then settle the breast - band across the wrestling - pad on his chest , and draw tight both the chain - trace . " Gee wugg now , if e wull , " cry stout Tim Badcock cheerily , and off sail the good ship of husbandly , cleave a deep bright furrow . but when they reach the corner , the farmer turn too sharply , and snap the off - side trace . that accident impress the multitude with a deep sense of his prowess than even the striking success which attend his primitive method of speed the plough . 
 to return to my mother . as spring come on , and the beautiful country around we freshen and take green life from the balmy air , I even venture to hope that the good yeoman 's word would be true . he have become , by this time , a great friend of ours , do his utmost that we might not feel the loss of our faithful Thomas Henwood . 
 Poor Thomas have be very loth to depart ; but I find , as we get settle , that my mother cease to want he , and it would have be wrong as well as foolish to keep he any long . he invest his saving in a public - house at Gloucester , which he call the " Vaughan Arms , " and soon afterwards marry Jane Hiatt , a daughter of our head game - keeper ; or I ought to say , Mr. Vaughan 's . 
 Ann Maples remain with we still . we live , as may be suppose , in the most retired manner . my time be chiefly occupy in attendance upon dear mother , and in attempt to create for she some of those countless comfort , whose value we know not until they be lose . after breakfast , my mother would read for an hour her favourite part of Scripture , and vainly endeavour to lead I into the path of peace . her soul discard more and more the travel garb and wayfare trouble of this low existence , as , day by day , it win a near view of the golden gate , and the glory beyond ; with which I have see her eye suffuse , like the lucid heaven with sunrise . it have be say , and I believe , that there be nothing , in all our material world , so lovely as a fair woman look on high for the angel she know to be wait for she . 
 even I , though look in an opposite direction , and for an opposite being , could not but admire that gentle meekness , whose absence form the main fault of my character . not that I be hard - hearted , or cross ,   unless self - love deceive I , but restless yearning and hatred be ever at work within I ; and these repel thing of a milder nature , as a bullet cry tush to the zephyr . 
 CHAPTER XV . 
 one cold day in March , when winter have come to say " good - bye " with a roar , after wheel the sofa with my mother upon it towards the parlour fire , I go out to refresh my spirit in the kitchen with Mrs. Huxtable , and to " yat myself "   for the sofa take all the parlour fire   by the fragrant hearth of wood and furze . the farmer 's wife be " larn " I some strange word of her native dialect , which I be now desirous to " discoorse , " and which she declare to be " the only vitty talk . arl the lave of thiccy stoof , zame as the Carnishers and the Zummersets and the lunnoner tull up , arl thiccy be no more nor a passel of gibbersh , Miss Clerer , and not vitty atarl ; noo , nor english nother . instead of zaye ' ai ' laike a Kirsten , zome on em zays ' oi , ' and zome on em ' i. ' " in the middle of her lecture , and just as I have learn that to " quilty " be the proper English for to ' swallow , " and that the passage down which we quilty be , correctly speak , not the throat , but the " ezelpipe , " a strange - look individual darken the " draxtool "   corruptly call the threshold   and cross the " planch , " or floor , to the fireplace where we sit . 
 turn round , I behold a man about fifty year old , of moderate stature , gauntly bodied , and loosely build , and utterly reckless of his attire . his face be long and thin , the profile keenly aquiline ; and the angle make yet sharp , by a continual twitching and tension of the muscle . the skin of his cheek be draw , from his solemn brow to his lipless and down - curve mouth , tight and hollow , like the bladder on a jam - pot . his eye , of a very pale blue , seem always to stand on tip - toe , and never to know what he be go to say . a long , straight , melancholy chin , grisly with patch of hair , be mean by nature to keep his mouth shut , and come back sullenly when it fail . over his shoulder be fling a patch potato - sack , fasten in front with a wooden skewer , and his nether clothe be as rag as poetry . in his air and manner , self - satisfaction strove hard with solemn reserve . upon the whole he remind I of an owl who have lose his heart to a bantam hen . I can not express he justly ; but those who have see may recognise Beany Dawe , the sawyer , acknowledge the bard of the north of Devon . 
 Mr. Ebenezer Dawe , without any hesitation or salute , take a three - legged stool , and set it between our chair , then look from Mrs. Huxtable to I , and introduce himself . 
 " wull , here be we three , 
 and I hopp we shall agree . " 
 
 " Agray indeed , " cry Mrs. Huxtable , " doon ' e zee the quarlity be here , ye aul vule ? " then turn to I . " doon'e be skeare , Miss Clerer , it be oney that there aul mazed ramscallion , Beany Dawe . her makth what girt scholard , laike you , karl potry , or zum such stoof . her casn ' oppen the drort of he nohow , but what she must spake potry . pote     indeed ! no tino , I would pote un out of ouze if I be the waife of un . ' Zee zaw , Beany Dawe ! ' that be arl the name he hath airne vor his rhaiming and rubbish , and too good for ' un too ! rhaime , rhaime , drash , drash , like two girt gawk in a barn ! oh fai , oh fai ; and a maight have aim two zhilling a dai and his zider ! " 
     " pote . " danmonic for to " kick . " 
 the subject of these elegant stricture regard she all the time , with that pleased pity which none but a great poet so place can feel . then swing slowly on his tripod , and address the back of the chimney , he respond : 
 " poor vule ! her dunno what a saight ' tis haigher 
 to be a pout , nor a hunderzawyer ! " 
 
 perhaps his lofty couplet charm her savage ear ; at any rate she make a peaceful overture . 
 " Coom now , Mr. Dawe , wull e have a few broth ? " 
 he assent with an alacrity much below his dignity ; 
 " tatie , and zider , maat , and broth a few , 
 " Wull , zin you ax ai , ai'll not answer noo . " 
 
 " E shall not   have no cider , " reply his hostess , " without e'll spake , for wance , laike a Kirsten , maind that , without no moor of thiccy jingle jangle , the very zame for arl the world as e be use to droon in the zawpit , ' Zee , zaw , Margery Daw , ' with the arm of e a gwayn up and doon , up and doon , and your oyes and maouth most chokke with pilm     and the vace of e a hurne laike a taypot , and never a drop of out to aise the crickle of your barck . that be the steet you pot be in , and zawyer . " 
     Pilm , Londinice , " dust . " 
 as she deliver this comment , she swing to and fro on her chair , in weak imitation of the impressive roll , with which he enforce his rhyme . this plagiarism annoy he much more than her word : but he vindicate his cause , like a true son of song . 
 " and if zo hap , I be a pout grand , 
 Thee need not   jah , 'cos thee doon't   understand . 
 a pout , laike a ' ooman , or a bell , 
 must have his clack out , and can not   help hiszell . " 
 
 a mighty " ha ha " from the door , like a jocund earthquake , prove that this last hit have find an echo in some ample bosom . 
 " thee shall have as much vittel as ever thee can let down , " say the farmer , as he enter , " dange if thee bain't   a wunnerful foine chap , zure enough . Ai'd as lieve a'most to be a pote , plase God , as I wud to be a ooman : zimth to ai , there bain't   much differ atwixt them . but they vaind out a saight of thing we tak no heed on . I reckon now , Beany , thee cas'n drink beer ? " 
 this be a home thrust , for Mr. Dawe be a notorious drinker . he reply with a heavy sigh and profoundly solemn look : 
 " Ah noo ! a noo ! unless when I be vorce , 
 by rason , Dactor zaith , my stommirk ba'in exhaust . " 
 
 " and what be it the doctor say to you , Mr. Dawe ? " I ask , perceive that he court inquiry . he fix his eye upon I , with a search look ; eager , as it seem , yet fear to believe that he have find at last a generous sympathy . 
 " ' twas more nor dree month zince ai titche a drap , 
 when ai be compel to consult the Dactor chap ; 
 he zaith , zaith he , " tain't   no good now this here , 
 oh , Ebenezer Dawe , you must tak beer . ' " 
 
 these word he repeat with impressive earnestness , shake his head and sighing , as if in deprecation of so sad a remedy . yet the subject possess perhaps a melancholy charm , and his voice relent to a pensive unctuousness , as he conclude . 
 " ' tak beer ! ' I zays , ' Lor , I dunnow the way ! ' 
 ' then you must larn , ' zays he , ' this blessed day : 
 You'm get , ' he zays , ' a daungerous zinking here , 
 your constitooshun do requaire beer . ' " 
 
 " Thee wasn ' long avore thee try it , I will warr'n , " say the farmer , " tache the calf the wai to the coo ! " 
 scorn this vile insinuation , Mr. Dawe continue thus : 
 " Wull , after that , mayhap a month or zo , 
 I be gooin home , the zame as maight be noo : 
 I have zawe a hellum up for Varmer Yeo , 
 and a velt my stommick gooin turble low , 
 she cry and skoone , like a chield leave in the dark , 
 and a maze laike in my head , and a maunder in my barck . 
 Zo whun ai coom to the voot of Breakneck hill , 
 I zeed the public keep by Pewter Will : 
 the virelight show the glass in the bar , 
 and ' um dance and twinkle like the avening star . " 
 
 here he pause , overcome by his own description . 
 " wull , " say the farmer , brighten with fellow - feeling , for he like his glass , " wull , thee toorne in and have a drap , laike a man , and not be sham of it nother . and how do she tast ? a must have be nation good , after so long a drouth ! " 
 " Coom'd down my drort , like the Quane and Princess Royal , 
 the very sa - be as a drap of oi - al ! " 
 
 " the very sa - am , the very sa - am , " he repeat with an extrametrical smack of his lip , which he wipe with the back of his hand , and cast a meaning glance towards the cellar . the farmer rise , and take from the dresser a heavy quart cup make of pewter . with this he go to the cellar , whence issue presently a trickling and froth sound , which thrill to the sensitive heart of Mr. Dawe . the tankard of ale , with a crown of white foam , be present to the thirsty bard by his host , who do not , however , relinquish his grasp upon the vessel ; but impose   like Pluto to Orpheus   a stern condition . " now , Beany Dawe , thee shall not   have none , unless thee can zay zummut without no poetry in it . " 
 at this barbarous restriction , poor Ebenezer roll his eye in a most tragic manner ; he thrust his tongue into his cheek , and swing himself , not to and fro as usual , but sideways , and clutch one hand on the tatter of his sack , while he cling with the other to the handle of the cup . then with a great effort , and very slowly , he speak - 
 " if my poor vasse only mak you frown , 
 I will try , ee fai I wull , to keep them - 
 
 a rhyme come over he , the twitching of his face show the violence of the struggle ; he attempt to say " in , " but nature triumph , and he utter the fatal " down . " in a moment the farmer compress his mighty finger , and crush the thick metal like silver paper . the forfeit liquor fly over the poet 's knee , and hiss at his foot in the ash . foresee a storm of verse from he , and of prose from Mrs. Huxtable at the fate of the pride of her dresser , I make a hasty retreat . 
 Thenceforth I take a kind interest in our conceited but harmless bard . his neighbour seem not to know , how long it be since he have first yield to his unfortunate ailment ; which probably owe its birth to the sound of the saw . during our first interview , his rhythm and rhyme have be unusually fluent and finish , from pride perhaps at have find a new audience , or from some casual inspiration . Candour compel I to admit that his subsequent work be little , if at all , well than those of his more famous contemporary ; and I be not so proud , as he expect I to be , of his connexion with my sad history . 
 CHAPTER XVI . 
 about half a mile from Tossil 's Barton   the farmhouse where we live   there be a valley , or rather a vast ravine , of a very uncommon formation . a narrow winding rocky combe , where slab , and tor , and boulder stone , seem pasture on the velvet grass , or look into the bright trout - stream , which leap down a flight of step without a tree to shade its flash and foam ; this narrow , but glad dingle , as it near the sea , burst suddenly back into a desert gorge , cleave the height that front the Bristol Channel . the mountain side from right and left , straight as if strike by rule , steeply converge , like a high - pitch roof turn upside down ; so steep indeed that none can climb they . along the deep bottom gleam a silver chord , where the cramp stream chafe its way , bedded and bank in stone , without a blade of green . from top to bottom of this huge ravine there be no growth , no rock , no cliff , no place to stay the foot , but all a barren , hard , grey stretch of shingle , slate , and gliddery stone : as if the ballast of ten million fleet have be shoot in two enormous pile , and be always on the slip . look at it we forget that there be such a thing as life : the desolation be not painful , because it be so grand . the brief noon glare of the sun on these titanic dry wall , where even a lichen die ; the gaunt desert shade steal back to its lair in the early afternoon ; the solemn step of evening stoop to her cloak below - I know not which of these be the most impressive and mournful . no stir of any sort , no voice of man or beast , no flow of tide , ever come to visit here ; the little river , after a course of battle , win no peaceful union with the sea , but pond against a shingle bar , and gurgle away in slow whirlpool . only a fitful moaning wind draw up and down the melancholy chasm . the famous " Valley of Rocks , " some four mile to the east , seem to I common - place and tame compare to this grand defile . yet how many man I know who would smoke their pipe throughout it ! 
 think so much of this place , I long wish my mother to see it ; and find she rather strong one lovely April morning , I persuade she forth , embark on Mrs. Huxtable 's donkey . we go , down a small tributary glen , towards the head of the great defile . the little glen be bright , and green , and laugh into bud , and banter a swift brook , which could hardly stop to answer , but leave the ousel as it pass to talk at leisure about their nest , and the trout to make those musical leap that sound so crisp through the alder . another stream meet it among the bush below , and now they be entitle to the dignity of a bridge whereon grow the maidenhair fern , and which , with its rude and pointed arch , look like an old pack - saddle upon the stream . 
 from this point we follow a lane , lead obliquely up the ascent , before the impassable steep begin . have tether our quiet donkey to a broken gate , I take my mother along a narrow path through the thicket to the view of the great ravine . stand at the end of this path , she be astonish at the scene before she . we have gain a height of about two hundred foot , the hill - top stretch a thousand foot above we . we stand on the very limit of vegetation , a straight line pass clown the hill where the quarry - like steep begin . 
 my dear mother be tired , and I have call she to come home , lest the view should make she giddy ; when suddenly she step forward to gather a harebell straggling among the stone . the shingle beneath her foot give way , then below she , and around , and above her head , begin in a great mass to glide . bury to the knee and fall sideways , she be sink slowly at first , then quickly and quick yet , with a hoarse roar of move ton of stone , gather and whelm upon she , down the rugged abyss . scream , I leap into the avalanche after she , never think that I could only do harm . strong , and swifter , and louder , and surging , and berge with shoulder stone the solid cascade rush on . I see dear mother below I try to clasp her hand in prayer , and to give I her last word . with a desperate effort drag my shawl from the gulfing crash , I throw it towards she , but she do not try to grasp it . a heavy stone leap over I , and strike she on the head ; her head drop back , she lie senseless , and nearly bury . we be dash more headlong and headlong , in the rush of the mountain side , to the precipice over the river , and my sens have all but fail , and revenge be prone before judgment , when I hear through the din a shout . on the brink of firm ground stand a man , and sign I to throw my shawl . with all my remain strength I do so , but not as he mean , for I cast it entirely to he , and point to my mother below . one instant the avalanche pause , he leap about twenty foot down , through the heather and gorse , and stay his descent by clutch a stout ash sapling . to this in a moment he fasten my shawl ,   a long and strong plaid , and just as my mother be be sweep by , he plunge with the other end into the shingle tide . I see he leap and struggle towards she , and lift she out of the gliding tomb , glide himself the while , and sway himself and his burden , by mean of the shawl , not back   for that be impossible , but obliquely downwards ; I see the strong sapling bow to the strain like a fishing - rod , while hope and terror fight hard within I ; I see he , by a desperate effort , which bent the ash - tree to the ground , leap from the whirling havoc , and lie my mother on the dead fern and heath . of the rest , I know nothing , have become quite unconscious , before he save I , in the same manner . 
 we must have be take home in Farmer Huxtable 's butt , for I remember well that , amidst the stir and fright of our return , and while my mother be still insensible , Mrs. Huxtable fall savagely upon poor Suke , for have despatch that elegant vehicle without clean it from the lime dust ; whereby , as she declare , our dress   so rent and tattere by the jagged stone   be " muxe up to shord . " Poor Suke would have be likely to fare much bad , if , at such a time , she have stop to dust the cart . 
 when the farmer come home , his countenance , rich in capacity for express astonishment , far outdo his word . " wull , wull , for sure ! wuther ye do or no ? " be all the vent he could find for his idea during the rest of the day ; though it be plain to all who know he that he be think profoundly upon the subject , and wholly occupy with it . in the course of the follow week he advise I very impressively never to do it again ; and nothing could ever persuade he but that I jump in , and my mother come to rescue I . 
 but his wife very soon have all her wit about she . she send to " Coom " for the doctor   I beg that it might not be Mr. Dawe 's physician , she put dear mother to bed , and dress her wound with simple worth ten druggist ' shop , and bath her temple with rosemary , and run down the glen for " fathery ham "   Valerian , which she declare " would kill nine sort of infermation ; " then she hush the entire household , permit no tongue to move except her own , and beat her eld boy   a fine young huxtable   for crying , whereupon he roar ; she even conquer her strong desire to know much more than all could tell ; and show my mother such true kindness and pity that I love she for it at once , and ever since . 
 breathing slowly and heavily , my poor mother lie in the bed which have long be the pride of Tossil 's Barton . the bedstead be make of carved oak , as many of they be in North Devon , and would have be handsome and striking , if some ancestral Huxtable have not adorn it with whitewash . but the quilt be what they be proud of . it be form of patch of diamond shape and most incongruous colour , with a death 's head in the centre and crossbone underneath . 
 when first I behold it , I toss it down the stair , but my mother would have it bring back and use , because she know how the family glory in it , and she could not bear to hurt their feeling . 
 one taper white hand lie on it now , with the tender skin bruise and discolour by blow . she have close the finger which bear her wedding ring , and it still remain curved and rigid . in an agony of tear , I kneel by the side of the bed , watch she placid and deathlike face . till then I have never know how strongly and deeply I love she . 
 I firmly believe that she be revive in some degree by the glare of the patched quilt upon her eye . the antagonism of nature be rouse , and bring home her wander power . feebly glance away , she come suddenly to herself , and exclaim : 
 " be she safe ? be she safe ? ' 
 " yes , mother ; here I be , with my own dear mother . " 
 she open her arm , and hold I in a nervous cold embrace , and thank God , and weep . 
 CHAPTER XVII . 
 when the surgeon come , he pronounce that none of her limb be break , but that the shock to the brain , and the whole system , have be so severe , that the only chance of recovery consist in perfect quiet . she herself say that the question be , whether Providence want she still to watch over her child . 
 after some day she come down stair , not without my support , and be prop once more upon her poor sofa . Calm she appear , and content , and happy in such sort as of old ; but whenever she turn her glance from I , she observe with start eye every little thing that move . especially she would lie and gaze through the open window , at a certain large spider , who work very hard among the woodbine blossom . one day , in make too bold a cast , he fall ; some chord of remembrance be touch , and she swoon away on the couch . 
 in spite of these symptom I fondly hop that she be recover strength . she even walk out with I twice , in the sunny afternoon . but this only last a very short time ; it soon become manifest , even to I , that ere long she would be with my father . 
 unable to fight any more with this dark perception , I embrace it with a sort of savage despair , an utter sinking of the heart , which defy God as it sink . this she soon discover , and I fear that it sadden her end . 
 she be much disappointed , too , that we could not find or thank he who have perille his life for we . none could tell who he be , or what have become of he ; though the farmer , at our entreaty , search all the village round . we be tell , indeed , by the landlady of the " red - deer Inn "   a lonely public - house near the scene of the accident   that a stranger have come to she in very great haste , and , have learn who we be , for she have see we pass half an hour before , have send her boy to the farm for some kind of conveyance , while he return at full speed to attend those whom he have rescue . it far appear that this stranger have help to place we in the cart , and show the kind anxiety to lessen the roughness of its motion , himself even lead old " Smiler , " to thwart his propensity to the deep and hard rut . by the time our slow vehicle reach the farm , Mrs. Huxtable be return from the Lower Cleve orchard , where she have be smoke the fernwebs , in ignorance of our mishap ; and our conductor , see we safe in her hand , depart without a word , while she be too flurried and frighten to take much notice of he . 
 neither could the woman of the inn describe he ; she be so " mazed , " when she hear of the " vail arl down the girt goyal , " as she call our slide of about fifty foot ; and for this she quote the stranger as her authority , " they be the very word as he use ; " though , just before this , she have state that he be a foreigner and could not speak English . know that in Devonshire any stranger be call a foreigner , and English mean the brogue of the countryside , I do not attach much weight to this declaration . the only remain witness , the lad who have come with the butt , be too stupid to describe anything , except three round O 's , with his mouth and eye . 
 but it matter little about description ; I have see that stranger under such circumstance , that I could not fail to know he again . 
 on the morrow , and once in the follow week , some kind inquiry be make as to our condition , by mean of slip of paper convey by country lad . no name be attach to these , and no information give about the inquirer . the bearer of the first missive come from Lynmouth , and of the second from Ilfracombe . neither lad know anything   though submit by Mrs. Huxtable to keen cross - examination , except that he be pay for his errand , but would like some cider , and that the answer be to be write upon the paper he bring . 
 whether any motive for concealment exist , beside an excess of delicacy , or whether there even be any intentional secresy , or merely indifference to our gratitude , be more than we could pretend to say . I be not at all inquisitive - not more so , I mean , than other woman - but I need not confess that my curiosity   to say nothing of well feeling   be pique a little by this uncommon reserve . 
 so now , beside the engrossing search for my deadly enemy , I have to seek out another , my brave and noble friend . 
 CHAPTER XVIII . 
 but for the present , curiosity , gratitude , hate , all feeling indeed and passion , except from the bleed vein of love , and the heart - root fibre of sorrow , be to be crush within I . evening after evening , my dear mother 's presence seem more and more dreamy and shadowy ; and night after night she go feebler and feebler to bed . in the morning indeed she have gather some fragile strength , such strength as so waste a form could exert , and the breeze and the fresh May sun make believe of health on her cheek . but no more be I tempt to lay my arm round her waist , and rally she on its delicate girlish span , nor could I now look gaily into her eye , and tell she how much she excel her child . those little liberty , which with less than a matron 's dignity , and more than a mother 's fondness she have so long allow I , become as she still expect , and I could not bear to take they , so many great distress . even at night , when I twin in its simple mode her soft brown hair , as I think how few the time my old task would be need again , it cost I many a shift to prevent her descry my tear in the glass , or suspect they in my voice . for herself , she know well what be come ; she have learn how soon she must be my sweet angel instead of my mother , and her last trouble be that she could not bring I to think the difference small . so calmly she speak of her end , not look at I the while for fear we both should weep , so gently and sweetly she talk of the time when I should hearken no more , as if she be go to visit a garden and hand I the flower outside . then , if I break forth in an anguish of sob , she would beg my forgiveness , as if she could have do wrong , and mourn for my loneliness after she , as though she could help forsake I . 
 look back , even now , on that time , how I condemn and yet pardon myself , reflect how little I try to dissemble my child - like woe . 
 when all thing rejoice in their young summer strength , and scarcely the breeze turn the leaf for the song of the bird , and the pure white hawthorn be calm as the death of the good , and the soul of gladness be sad , we talk for the last time together , mother and child , look forth on the farewell of sunset . the room under the thatch smell musty in summer , and I have make up a bed on the sofa downstairs . the waste low fever be past , and the wearisome cough exhaust , and the flush have ebb from her cheek   as the world from her heart , and of all human passion , and wish , and care , not one leave a trace in her bosom , except a mother 's love . this and only this retard her flight to heaven , as the sight of his nest delay the rise of the lark . 
 " my child , " she begin , and her voice be low , but very distinct , " my only and darling child , who have mind I so long , and lay her youth , and beauty , and high courageous spirit , at the foot of her weak mother ; my child , who foster in wealth and love , will be to - morrow an orphan , cast upon the wide world " - here she fairly break down , in spite of religion , and heaven , and turn her head to the pillow , a true daughter and mother of earth . I would fain have give that fortune , whose loss to I she lament , for leave to cry freely with she , without add to her distress . 
 in a minute or two , she be able to proceed ; with her thin hand she part the hair shake purposely over my eye . 
 " I be sure that my pet will listen , with kindness and patience , while I try to say what have lie so long at my heart . you know how painfully I have always be move by any allusion to the death of your dear father . it have be a weakness no doubt on my part , but one which I vainly strove against ; and for which I trust to be pardon where all be pardon and peace . " 
 her voice begin to tremble , and her eye become fix , and I fear a return of the old disorder ; but she shake it off , and speak again distinctly , though with great labour : 
 " this be a bitter subject , and I never could bring myself to it , till now , when it seem too late . but , my poor love , I be so anxious about it . for the rest - that Providence which have never forsake we , repine as I would , I can trust that Providence still to protect my darling child . there be one thing , and only one , by promise which you will make my departure quite happy . then I shall go to rejoin your father , and carry such tiding of you , as will enable we both to wait , in the fulness of time , your come . " 
 " oh , that the fulness of time be come ! " I cry in my selfish loneliness ; " for I it be empty enough . " 
 " my precious , my own darling Clara , you sob so , you make I most wretched . " 
 " Mother , I will not cry any more ; " neither do I , while she could see I . 
 " I need not tell you , " she say , " what be that promise which I crave for your own dear sake . " 
 " no , ma'am , " I reply , " I know quite well what it be . " 
 I see that I have grieve she . how could I call she then anything else than " mother " ? 
 " my mother dear , you wish I to promise this - that I will forego my revenge upon he who slay my father . " 
 she bow her head , with a look I can not describe . in the harsh way I have put it , it seem as if she be injure both my father and I . 
 " have you ask I anything else , although it be sin against God and man   if you could ask such a thing   - I would have pledge myself to it , as gladly as I would die - die , at least , if my task be do . but this , this one thing only - to abandon what I live for , what I be bear to do , to be a traitor to my own father and you - I implore you , mother , by he whose glory be on you now , do not ask I this . " 
 her face in its sadness and purity make I bury my eye and forget thing . 
 " then I must die , and leave my only child possess with a murderer 's spirit ! " 
 the depth of her last agony , and which I believe would cling to she even in heaven , be more than I could bear . I kneel on the floor and put my hand to her side . she wear out heart be throb again , with the pang of her disappointment . 
 " Mother , " I cry , " I will promise you this . when I have discover , as I must do , that man who have make you a widow and I an orphan , if I find any plea whatever to lessen his crime , or penitence to atone for it , as I hope to see my father and mother in heaven , I will try to spare and forgive he . can you wish I to rest in ignorance , and forget that deed ? " 
 " Clara , " she answer weakly , and she speak more slowly and feebly every time , " you have promise I all I can hope for . how you love your father ! I too you have love I can not say how much . for my sake , you have bear poverty , trouble , and illness , without a complain word . by day , and by night , through my countless want , and long fretfulness . " 
 I put my finger upon her pale lip . how could she tell such a story then ? her tear come now and then , and would not be stop , as she lay her weak hand on my head . 
 " may the God of the fatherless and the poor , who know and comfort the widow 's grief , the God who be take I now to his bosom , bless with all blessing of earth and heaven , and restore to I this my child . " 
 a sudden happiness fall upon she , as if she have see her prayer 's acceptance . she let her arm fall round I , and lay my cheek by the side of her bright flow smile . it be the last conscious stir of the mind ; all the rest seem the flush of the soul . in the window the night - scented heath be bloom ; outside it , the jessamine cross in a milky way of white star , and the lush honeysuckle have fling down her lap in cluster . the fragrance of flower lie heavy upon we , and we be sore weary with the burden of sorrow and joy . so tranquil and kind be the face of death , that sleep , his half - brother , still hold his hand . 
 the voice of the thrush , from the corner laurel , break the holy stillness . like dream of home that break our slumber , his melody be its own excuse . my mother awake , and say faintly , with no gleam in her eye : 
 " raise I upon the pillow , my love , that I may hear he once more . he sing like one your father and I use to listen to every evening , in the day when we watch your cradle . " 
 I lift she gently . the voice of nature make way for her pass spirit . 
 " now kiss I , my child ; once more , my own love child , my heart be with you for ever . light of my eye , you be grow dim . " 
 she clasp her hand in prayer , with one of mine between they . my other be round her neck . 
 then she speak slowly , and with a wan voice ; but firmly , as if it have be her marriage - response . 
 " thou art my guide , and my staff . I have no fear , neither shadow of trembling . make no long tarrying , oh my God ! " 
 the bird go home to his nest , and she to that refuge where all be home . though the hand that hold mine grow cold as ice , and her lip reply to no kiss , and the smile on her face sleep off into stillness , and a grey shade creep on her feature ; - I could not believe that all this be death . 
 CLARA VAUGHAN 
 BOOK II . 
 CHAPTER I. 
 " long - shadow death , " some poet say . how well I know and feel it ! the gloom before he deepen as he come , and the world of darkness stretch many year behind . 
 I once dare to believe that no earthly blow could ever subdue , or even bend my resolute will . I now find my mistake , and care not even to think about it .
