 on the morning after my mother 's death I wander about , and could not tell where to go . the passionate clinging which would not allow I , during that blank and sleepless night , to quit what remain of her presence , and the jealous despair which feel it a wrong that any one else should approach , have now settle down to a languid heaviness , and all that I care for be to be let alone . all the place where we have be together I visit now , without know why , perhaps it be to see if she be there . then vaguely disappointed , I think there must be some mistake , and wearily go the dreary round again . 
 I can not clearly call to mind , but think it must have be that day , when I be in the corner of the room , look at the place whence they have take dear mother . Ann Maples and Mrs. Huxtable come in , follow by the farmer , who have leave his shoe at the door . they do not see I , so I suppose it must have be in the evening . they be come to remove the sofa . I have not the heart to follow their brogue . 
 " yes to be sure , " say Mrs. Huxtable , look at it with a short sigh . it be odd that it should strike I then , but all she do be short . 
 " get it out of her sight , poor dear , " say Ann Maples . 
 " to see she sit and look at it ! " exclaim the farmer 's wife . 
 " with her eye so dry and stupid like ! " return the other . " poor child , she must have cry herself out . I have know she sit by the hour , and stare at the bed where her father be kill , but it be a different sort of look to this . " 
 " ah well , she have lose a good mother , " say Dame Huxtable . " God grant my poor little chick may never be leave like she . " 
 " what be your child to talk of along with Miss Clara ? " ask my nurse . 
 Mrs. Huxtable be about to answer sharply , but check herself , and only say : 
 " all child be much of a muchness to their mother . " 
 " do not   tell I , " cry Ann Maples , who have never have any . 
 the farmer come between they , walk on tip - toe . 
 " for good , now , do not   ye fall out at such a time as this here . what be our affair to speak of now ? " 
 " what be any folk , " ask Mrs. Huxtable , " that have the breath of life ? " 
 " and go forth in the morning , and be cast into the oven , ma'am , " continue her antagonist . 
 " ah , bless thee , yes ! " the farmer reply , " I will take my gospel oath of it . it be not much good I be at parsone , and maybe I like a drop of drink when the weather be fitty ; but that young chestnut filly that be just come home from break , I would sell she to a gipsey , and trust he for the money , if so be ' twould make the young lady turn her face to the Lord . can not   ye speak to she now about it , either of you woman ? Doo'e now , doo'e . " 
 " how could I possible ? " his wife exclaim ; " why , farmer , you must be maze . a high young lady like that , and the tear still hot in her eye ! " 
 " the very reason , wife , the very time and reason . but likely Mrs. Maples would be the proper person . " 
 " thank you , sir , " my nurse reply , " Mrs. Maples know good manner a little . thank you , sir ; Mrs. Maples be not   bear in Devonshire . " 
 " I ask your pardon , ma'am , " say the farmer , much abash , " I humbly ask your pardon ; I be not   teach no well . I can only go by what I have see , and what seem to come inside of I . and I know , in our way of business , when a calf be wean from the mother , the poor beastess hath a call for some one else to feed it . maybe it be no harm to let she have the refusal . " Therewith he open my mother 's Bible , and place it reverently on the window - seat . " Waife , do'e mind the time as poor Aunt Betsy die , over there to Rowley Mires ? " 
 " for sure I do , but what have she get to do with it ? we must not   talk of she , I reckon , any more than of the chiller , though we be so unlucky as to be bear in Devonshire . Fie , fie , thee ought to know well than to talk of poor Aunt Betsy along of a lady , and before our better . " here she curtsy to Ann Maples , with a flash of light in her eye , and rub they hard with her apron . 
 " well , well , " reply the farmer , sadly , " mayhap so I do . and who be I to gainsay ? mayhap so I do ; " he drop his voice , but add , after some reflection , " it be hard to tell the right of it ; but sure she be a woman . " 
 " who say she be a man , thee zany ? " Mrs. Huxtable be disappoint that the case would not be argue . the farmer discreetly change the subject . 
 " now , if it be I , " he continue , " I would not   think of take this here settle - bed away from the poor thing . " 
 " why not , farmer ? " ask Mrs. Huxtable , sharply . " give I a reason for leave it , and I will give you ten for take it . " 
 " I can not   give no reason . but maybe it comfort she a little . " 
 " Comfort indeed ! " say his wife ; " break her heart with , cry , more likely . come , lend a hand , old heavy - strap ; what can a great dromedary like thee know about young wench ? " 
 at any rate he know more than she do . the moment they touch it I burst forth from my corner , and fling myself upon it , roll as if I would bury myself in the ecstasy of anguish . what they do I can not tell ; they might say what they like , I have not cry till then . 
 the next day I be sit stupifie and heavy , try once more to meet the necessity of think about my mother 's funeral ; but again and again , the weakness of sorrow fall away from the subject . the people of the house keep from I . Mrs. Huxtable have do she good , but they know I would rather be alone . 
 the door be open quietly , and some one enter in a stealthy manner . regard it as an intrusion , I would not look that way . 
 " Miss Clara dear , " begin the farmer , stand behind I , and whispering , " I humbly ask your pardon , Miss , for call you that same . but we have have a wonderful fine season , sure enough . " 
 I make he no answer , be angry at his ill - timed common - place . 
 " if you please , Miss , such a many lamb be never know afore , and turnip fine last winter , and corn , and hay , and every kind of stock , a fetching of such price . the farmer about here have make their fortune mainly . " 
 " I be glad to hear that you be so prosperous , Mr. Huxtable , " I answer , very coldly . 
 " yes fie , good time , Miss , wonderful good time , we do not   know what to do with our money a'rnost . " 
 " buy education and good taste , " I say , " instead of thrust your happiness upon such as I. " 
 how little I know he ! shall I ever forgive myself that speech ? 
 " ah , I wish I could , " he answer , sadly , " I wish with all my heart I could . but we must be bear to the like of that , I be afeare , Miss Vaughan . " 
 poor fellow ! he know nothing of irony , as we do , who be bear to good taste , otherwise I might have suspect he of it then . 
 he suddenly wish I " good evening , " although it be middle - day , and then he make off for the door , but come back again with a desperate resolve , and speak , for he , very quickly , look all the time at his foot . 
 " there , I can not   make head or tail of it , Miss Clara , but wife say I be to do it so . take the dange money , that be a dear , and for good now do not   be offend , for I cas'n help it . " 
 he open his great hand , which be actually shake , and hurriedly place on the sofa a small packet tie in the leaf of a copy book ; then suddenly put in mind of something , he make a dive , and snatch it up , fling it upon a Windsor chair . it fall with a chink , the string slip off , and out roll at least forty sovereign and guineas , and a number of crown - piece . 
 Peremptorily I call he back , for he be run out of the door . 
 " Mr. Huxtable , what be the meaning of this ? " 
 " mean , Miss ! Lord bless you , Miss Clara , there bain't   no meaning of it ; only it corn into my head last night , as I be lay awake , humbly ask your pardon , Miss , for that same , that if so be you should desire , that the dear good lady herself might like , if I may make so bold , mean that it be not   fitly like , that she should lay nowhere else , but alongside of her own husband , till death do they part , Mr. Henry Valentine Vaughan , Esquire , Vaughan Park , in the county of Gloucestershire . there I be as bad as Beany Dawe . " 
 he repeat his rhyme , with some relief , hop to change the subject . I catch he by both hand , and burst into tear . 
 " do not   ye now , " he say , with a thickness in his voice , " do not   ye now , my dearie , leastway unless it do you good . " 
 " it do I good , indeed , " I sob , " to find still in the world so kind a heart as your . " 
 though I long to look he in the face , I know that I must not do so . oh why be man so ashamed of manly tear ? perceive that I could not speak , he begin to talk for both of we , make a hundred blunder apology , try to hide his knowledge of my poverty , and to prove that he be only pay a debt which extend over many year of tenancy . he be not at all an imaginative man , but delicacy supply he with invention . so deep a sense pervade all class in this english country , that want of money be an indictment , which none but the culprit may sign . poor or rich , I should not be worth despise , if I have show the paltry pride of decline such a loan . 
 the tear come anew to my eye when I find that what have be bring so freely be the saving of year of honest toil , a truth which the owner have try to conceal by polish the old coin . but not be skilled , dear soul , in plate - cleaning , they have leave some rotten - stone adhering to the George and Dragons . 
 CHAPTER II . 
 although I find a sad pleasure in linger over these time , with such a history still impend , I can not afford the indulgence . 
 dear mother 's simple funeral take I once more to my native place . even without Mr. Huxtable 's generous and noble assistance , I should have lay she to rest by the side of the husband she love so well . but difficulty , sore to encounter at such a time , would have meet I on every side . moreover the kind act cheer and lead I through despondency , like the hand and face of God . 
 care little what people might say or think , I could not stay at a distance . nature tell I that it be my duty to go , and duty or not , I could not stay away . 
 and now for the last time I look on the face and form of my mother . that which I have play , and talk , and laugh with , though lately not much of laughter , that which have feed and care for I , till it need my care in turn ; that which I have toddle beside , or proudly run in front of ; whose arm have be round I whenever I weep , and whose bosom the haven of childhood 's storm ; first to greet I with smile in the morning , and last to bless I with tear at night ; ever love , and never complain - in one word for a thousand , my mother . so far away now , so hopelessly far away ! there it lie indeed , I can touch it , kiss it , and embrace it ; but oh how small a part of mother ! and even that part be not mine . so holy and calm it lie , such love kindness still upon its feature , so near I , but in mystery so hopelessly far away ! I can see it , but it never will know I again ; I may die beside it , and it can not weep . the last last look of all on earth - they must have carry I away . 
 I remember totter down the hill , support by a stalwart arm . the approach to the house prevent - or something . two child run before I , stop now and then to wonder , and straggle to pick hedge - flower . one of they bring I a bunch , then star , and be afraid to offer they . " Nancy , I will be the death of thee , " whisper a woman 's voice . the little girl shrink to I for shelter , with timid tear in her great blue eye . so I take her hand , and lead she on , and somehow it do I good . 
 at interval , the funeral hymn , which they sing on the road to the grave , fall solemnly on our ear . some one from time to time give out the word of a verse and then it be sing to a simple impressive tune . that ancient hymn , which have drown so many sob , I do not hear , but feel it . 
 we arrive at Vaughan St. Mary late in the afternoon of the second day . the whole of the journey be to I a long and tearful dream . Mr. Huxtable come with we . he have never before be far from home than Exeter ; and his single visit to that city have form the landmark of his life . he never try to comfort I as the other do . the ignorant man know well . 
 alone I sit by my father 's grave , with my mother 's ready before my foot . they have cast the mould on the other side , so as not to move my father 's coverlet . the poor old pensioner have be true to her promise , and man 's last garden be bloom like his first flower - bed . 
 my mind   if any I have   seem to have undergo some change . defiance , and pride , and savage delight in misery , be entirely go ; and depression have take the place of dejection . death now seem to I the usual and proper condition of thing , and I feel it an impertinence that I should still be alive . so I wait , with heavy composure , till she should be bring , who so often have walk there with I . at length she be come for good and all , and a space be leave for I . but I must not repose there yet ; I have still my task before I . 
 the bell be toll fast , and the shadow grow long , and the child who have be play at hide - and - seek , where soon themselves shall be seek in vain , have flit away from sight , perhaps scar at my presence , perhaps go home to tea , to enjoy the funeral afterwards . the evening wind have cease from trouble the yew , and the short - live song of the bird be do . the place be as sad as I could wish . the smell of new earth inspire , as it always do , some unsearchable everlaste sympathy between the material and the creature . 
 the sun be set behind I : suddenly a shadow eclipse my own upon the red loam across the open grave . without a start , and dreamily   as I do all thing now , I turn to see whence it come . within a yard of I stand Mr. Edgar Vaughan . in a moment the old feeling be at my heart , and my wit be all awake . 
 I observe that he be pale than when I have see he last , and the rigid look be waver on his face , like steel reflect by water . he lift his hat to I . I neither rise nor speak , but turn and watch he . 
 " Clara , " he say in a low , earnest voice , " I see you be still the same . will no depth of grief , no length of time , no visitation from he who be over we all , ever bend your adamant and implacable will ? " 
 I hear , with some surprise , his allusion to the Great Being , whom he be not will not to recognise ; but I make he no reply . 
 " very well , " he resume , with the ancient chill harden over his feature ; ' so then let it be . I be not come to offer you condolence , which you would despise ; nor do I mean to be present when you would account the sight of I an insult . and yet I love your mother , Clara ; I love she very truly . " 
 this he say with such emotion , that a new thought break upon I . 
 Quick as the thought , he ask , " would you know who kill your father ? " 
 " and my mother , too , " I answer , " whose coffin I see come . " 
 the funeral turn the corner of the lane , and the dust rise from the bearer ' foot . he take his hat off , and the perspiration stand upon his forehead . betwixt suspense and terror , and the wildness of grief , I be oblige to lean on the headstone for support , and a giddiness come over I . when I raise my eye again , there be no one near I . in vain I wipe they hurriedly and look again . Mr. Vaughan be go ; but on the grass at my foot lie a fold letter . I seize it quickly , and break the seal . that moment a white figure appear between the yew - tree by the porch . it be the aged minister lead my mother the last path of all . the book be in his hand , and his form be tall and stately , and his step so slow , that the white hair fall unruffled , while the grand word on his lip call majesty into his gaze . thrust aside the letter , I follow into the Church , and stand behind the old font where I have be baptize ; a dark and gloomy nook , fit for such an entrance . she who have carry I there be carry past it now , and the pall wave in the damp cold air , and all the world seem stone and mould . 
 but afterwards , on the fair hill - side , while the faint moon gather power from the deepening sky , and glance on that hoary brow seal the immortal promise and smooth the edge of the grave , around which bent the uncovered head of many who have mourn before , and after a few bound of mirth should bend again in mourning , until in earth 's fair turn and turn , other should bend and they lie down - behold this , and feel something high than " dust to dust , " I grow content to bide my time with the other child of man , and remember that no wave can break until it reach the shore . 
 CHAPTER III . 
 when a long and heavy sleep   my first sleep since dear mother 's death   have bring I down to the dull plain of life , I read for the first time the letter so strangely deliver . even then it seem unkind to my mother that I should think about it . Mr. Vaughan have place it in a new envelope , which he have seal with his own ring , the original cover   if any there be   have be remove . the few word , of which it consist , be write in a clear round hand , upon a sheet of thin tough paper , such as we use for foreign postage , and fold in a peculiar manner . there be nothing remarkable in the writing , except this , that the word as well as the letter be join . it be as follow : 
 " the one who slay your brother be at 19 Grove Street London . you will come in danger of it why you know . " 
 no date , no signature , no stop , except as show above . in short , it be so dark and vague , that I return to Devonshire , with a resolution to disregard it wholly . when we reach the foot of the hill , at the corner of the narrow lane which lead to Tossil 's Barton , and where the white gate stand of which the neighbourhood be so proud , a sudden scream be hear , and a rush make upon we from behind the furze - bush . the farmer receive the full brunt of a most vigorous onset , and the number and courage of the enemy make up for their want of size , his strong bastion be almost carry by storm . to the cry of " daddy ! daddy 's come home ! " half a dozen urchin and more , without distinction of sex , jump and tug and fling and clung around he , with no respect whatever for his Sunday coat , or brass - button gaiter . take advantage of his laughing , they pull his leg this way and that , as if he be skate for the first time , and little Sally   his favourite   swarm up , make a base foot - rope of the great ancestral silver watch - chain whose mysterious awe sometimes suffice to keep her eye half open in church . Betwixt delight and shame , the poor father be so dreadfully take aback , that he could not tell what to do , till fatherly love suggest the only escape . he lift they one by one to his lip , and after some hearty smack send all   except the baby   slide down his back . 
 while all this be go forward , the good dame , with a clean apron on , keep herself in the background , curtsey and try to look sad at I , but too much carry away to succeed . her plump cheek leave but little room for tear , yet I think one try to find a road from either eye . when the burst be nearly do , she feel   like a true woman   for I so lonely in all this love , though I could not help enjoy it ; and so she try to laugh at it . 
 for a long time after this , the farmer be admire and consult by all the neighbour parish , as a man who have see the world . his labourer , also , one man and a boy , for a fortnight call he " Sir , " a great discomfort to he ; more than this , some letter be bring for he to interpret , and Beany Dawe become unduly jealous . but in this , as in most other matter , thing come to their level , and when it be slowly discover that the farmer be just the same , his neighbour show much disappointment , and even some contempt . 
 it be not long before the thought of that letter , which have be lay by so scornfully , begin to work within I . again and again , as time wear on , and the deep barb of sorrow darkly rust away , it come home to I as a sin , that I be neglect a special guidance . moreover , my reason for stay in Devonshire be go , and as my spirit recover its tone , it could not put up with inaction . 
 three month after our return , one breezy afternoon in August , when the heath have long succeed the gorse and broom upon the cleve , and the child be search for " wut " and half - kernele nut , I sit on a fallen tree , where a break in the copse make a frame for one of our favourite view . of late I have be try to take some sketch in water - colour of what my mother and I have so often admire together , and this have be keep for the last . wild as the scheme may appear to all who know the world and its high contempt for woman 's skill , I have some hope of earn money in London by the pencil , and be do my utmost to advance in art . also , I wish to take away with I some memorial of a time comparatively happy . 
 little Sally Huxtable , a dear little child , now my chief companion , have stray into the wood to string more strawberry bead on her spike of grass , for the wood strawberry here last almost to the equinox ; and I have just rough in my outline , and be correct the bold stroke , by nature 's soft gradation ; when suddenly through a cobnut bush , and down the steep bank at my side , come , in a slide canter , a magnificent red deer . he pass so close before I , with antler , like a varnished crabstick , russet in the sun , that I could have touch his brown flank with my pencil . be in no hurry or fright whatever , he regard I from his large deep eye with a look of courteous interest , a dignified curiosity too well breed for word ; and then , as if with an evening of pleasant business before he , trot away through the pod wild broom on the left . 
 before I have time to call he back , which , with a childish impulse , I be about to do , the nutbush where he have enter move again , and , laugh at his own predicament on the steep descent , a young man leap and land in the bramble at my foot . before I stand the one whom we have so often long to thank . but at sight of I , his countenance change entirely . the face , so playful just before , suddenly grow dark and sad , and , with a distant salutation , he be hurry away , when I spring forward and catch he by the hand . every nerve in my body thrill , as I feel the grasp that have save my mother and I . 
 " excuse I , " he say coldly , " I will lose my prey . " 
 but I would not let he go so curtly . what I say I can not tell , only that it be very foolish , and clumsy , and cold by the side of what I feel . whom but God and he have I to thank for my mother 's peaceful end , and all her treasured word , each worth a dozen life of mine ? he answer not at all , nor look at I ; but listen with a cold constraint , and , as I think , contemptuous pity , at which my pride begin to take alarm . 
 " Sir , " I exclaim , when still he answer not , " Sir , I will detain you no long from murder that poor stag . " 
 he answer very haughtily , " I be not of the Devonshire hunter , who toil to exterminate this noble race . " 
 as he speak he point down the valley , where the red deer , my late friend , be cross , for his evening browse , to a gnoll of juicy grass . then why be he pursue he , and why do he call he his prey ? the latter , probably a pretext to escape I , but the former question I could not answer , and do not choose to ask . he go his way , and I feel discharge of half my obligation . 
 CHAPTER IV . 
 the farmer , his wife , and little Sally be now all I have to love . poor Ann maple , though thoroughly honest and faithful , be of a nature so dry and precise that I respect rather than love she . I be bear to love and hate with all my heart and soul , although a certain pride prevent I from exhibit the well passion , except when strongly move . that other feeling , sow by Satan , he never allow I to disguise . 
 to leave the only three I love be a bitter grief , to tell they of my intention , a sore puzzle . but , after search long for a good way to manage it , the only way I find be to tell they bluntly , and not to cry if it could be help . so when Mrs. Huxtable come in full glory to try upon I a pair of stocking of the bright blue ever see , which she have long be knit on the sly , for winter wear , I thank she warmly , and say : 
 " dear I , Mrs. Huxtable , how they will admire these in London . " 
 " in Lonnon , cheel ! " she always call I her child , since I have lose my mother - " they will never see the like of they in Lonnon , without they git one of they there long glaskie , same as preventive chap have , and then I reckon there will be Hexymoor between , and Dartmoor too , for out I know , and ever so many church - tower and milestone . " 
 " oh yes , they will . I shall be there in a week . " 
 " in Lonnon in a wake ! dear heart alaive , cheel , dont'e tell on so ! " 
 she think my wit be wander , as she have often fancy of late , and set off for the larder , which be the usual course of her prescription . but I stop she so calmly that she could not doubt my sanity . 
 " yes , dear Mrs. Huxtable , I must leave my quiet home , where all of you have be so good and kind to I ; and I have already write to take lodging in London . " 
 " oh , Miss Clerer , dear , I can not   belave it nohow ! come and discoorse with farmer about it . he know a power more than I do , though I say it as should not . but if so be he hearken to the like of that , I will comb he with the toasting iron . " 
 give I no time to answer , she lead I to the kitchen . the farmer , who have finish his morning 's work , be stamp about outside the threshold , wipe his boot most carefully with a pitchfork and a rope of twisted straw . this process , to his great discomfort , Mrs. Huxtable have at length enforce by many scolding ; but now she snatch the pitchfork from he , and send it fly into the court . 
 " Wun't   thee never larn , thee girt drummedary , not to ston there an hour , muck arl the place ? " 
 " wull , wull , " say the farmer , look at the pitchfork first , and then at I , " Reckon the old mare 's dead at last . " 
 " Cas'n thee drame of nothing but boss and ass , thee girt mule ? here be Miss Clerer , as be like a cheel of my own , and now she'm gooin awai , and us'll niver zee she no more . " 
 " what dost thee mane , ' ooman ? " ask the farmer , sternly , " hast thee darr'd to goo a jahing of she , zame as thee do Zuke ? " 
 " oh , no , farmer ! " I answer , quickly , " Mrs. Huxtable never give I an unkind word in her life . but I must leave you all , and go to live in London . " 
 the farmer look as if he have lose something , and begin feel for it in all his pocket . then , without a word , he go to the fire , and unhung the crock which be boil for the family dinner . this do , he rake out the ember on the hearthstone , and sit down heavily on the settle with his back towards we . presently we hear he say to himself , " if any cheel of mine ate ever a bit of bakkon to - day , I will bile he in that there pot . and to zee the copy our Sally write this very morning ! " 
 " wonnerful ! wonnerful ! " cry Mrs. Huxtable , " and now her'll not know a p from a pothook . and little Jack can spell zider , zame as them do in Lonnon town ! " 
 " Dang Lonnon town , " say the farmer , savagely , " and arl as live there , lave out the Duke of Wellington . it be where the devil live , and them catch his braath in lantern . my faather tould I that , and her niver spak a loi . but it hain't   for the larning I be vex to lose my dearie . " 
 that last word he dwelt upon so tenderly and sadly , that I could stop no long , but run up to he bravely with the tear upon my face . as I sit low before he , on little Sally 's stool , he lay his great hand on my head , with his face turn toward the settle , and ask if I have any one to see I right in the world but he . 
 I tell he , " none whatever ; " and the answer seem at once to please and frighten he . 
 " then do not   e be a - gooin ' , my dear heart , do not   e think no more of gooin . if it be for the bit and drap thee ate and drink , do not   thee know by this time , our own flash and blood bain't   no more welcome to it ! and we have a plenty here , and more nor a plenty . and if we have not , Jan Huxtable hisself , and Honor Huxtable his waife , wud live on pegmale   well nor they desarve   and gie it arl to thee , and bless thee for ate of it . " 
 " ay , that we wud , ee fai , " answer Mrs. Huxtable , come forward . 
 " and if it be for channge , and plaisure , and zeee of the warld , I have   zeen a dale in my time , ax your pardon , Miss , for convarse so to you . and what hath it be even at Coom market , with the varmer I have   a - knowed from little chiller up ? no well nor a harrow dill for a little coolt to zuck . I would liefer know thee be a - gooin ' to Trentisoe churchyard , where little Jane and Winny be , than let thee goo to Lonnon town , zame as this here be . and what wud thy poor moother zay , if so be she could hear tell of it ? " 
 at this moment , when I could say nothing , be thoroughly convict of ingratitude , and ashamed before nature far well than my own , dear little Sally , who have be roll on the dairy floor , recover from the burst of childish grief enough to ask whether it have any cause . up to I she run , with great pearl tear on the veining of her cheek , and peep through the lash of her violet - blue eye , she give I one long reproachful look , as if she begin to understand the world , and to find it disappointment ; then she bury her flaxen head in the homespun apron I have lately take to wear , and sob as if she have spoil a dozen copy . what happen afterwards I can not tell . cry I hate , but there be time when nothing else be any good . I only know that , as the farmer leave the house to get , as he say , " a little braze , " these ominous word come back from the court : 
 " ' Twud be a bad job for Tom Grundy , if she coom'd acrass I now . " 
 CHAPTER V. 
 that same evening , as I be sit in my lonely room , yet not quite alone , - for little Sally , who always do as I bade she , be scratch and blot her good copy - book , under my auspex , - in burst Mrs. Huxtable , without stop to knock as usual . 
 " oh Miss Clerer , what have e be and do ? Varmer 's in crule trouble . Us'll arl have to goo to gaol to - morrow , chiller and arl . " 
 she be greatly flurry and out of breath , and yet seem proud of what she have to tell . she do not require much asking , nor beat about the bush , as many woman do ; but tell I the story shortly , and then ask I to come and hear all particular from Tim Badcock the farm - labourer , who have see the whole . 
 Tim sit by the kitchen fire with a pint of cider by he on the little round table ; strong evidence that his tiding , after all , be not so very unwelcome . 
 " Wull , you zee , Miss , " say Tim , after get up , and pull his rough forelock , " you zee , Miss , the Maister coom out this arternoon , in a weist zort of a wai , as if she have not   have no dinner . " here he give a sly look at " the Missus , " who have the credit of stop the supply , when the farmer have be too much on the cruise . 
 " what odd to thee , Tim , " she reply , " what odd to thee , what thee better have for dinner ? " 
 " Noo fai , " say Tim , " zo long as ai git maine , and my missus arlway have un raddy . Zo I ze to Bill , zays I , ' well maind what thee be at boy , there be a starm a coomin , zure as my name 's Timothy Badcock . ' Howsomever she do not   tak on atarl wi we , but kitche up a shivel , and work awai without niver a ward . ' twur the tap of the clave , ' langside of the beg fuzz , where the braidle road coomth along ' twixt that and the double hadge ; and we be arl a stubbing up the bush as plaisant as could be , to plough thiccy plat for clover , coom some rain , plase God . " 
 " Git on , Tim , wull e , " cry his impatient mistress , " we know arl about that . Cas'n thee tull it no quick ? " 
 " Wull , Miss , " continue Tim , in no hurry whatever , " prasently us zee a girt beg chap on a zort of a brown cob , a coomin in our diraction " - Tim be proud of this word , and afraid that we should fail to appreciate it - " they be a coomin , as you might zay , in our diraction this beg chap , and anither chap langside on he . Wull , when ' um coom'd within spake room of we , beg chap a ' horsebarck holler out , ' can ' e tell , my man , where Jan Uxtable live ? ' Avore I have taime to spake , Maister lift hissell up , and zaith , ' what doo ' e want to know for , my faine feller ? ' every bit the zame as ai be a tullin of it to you . ' what be the odd to thee , ' zays tother chap , ' thee d'st well kape a zivil tongue in thee head . I be Tom Gundry from Carnwall . ' and with that he stand up in his starrup , as beg a feller as iver you zee , Miss. Wull , Maister know all about Tom Gundry and what a be a coom for , and zo do I , and the boy , and arl the country round ; for Maister have get a turble name for rarstle ; maybe , Miss , you have   a heer'd on he in Lunnon town ? " 
 " I have never be in London , Tim , since I be a child ; and I know nothing at all about wrestling . " 
 " Wull , Miss , that be nayther here nor there . but there have be a dale of brag after Maister have throw arl they carnisher to barnstable vair , last year , about vetche this here Tom Gundry , who wor the good man in Cornwall , to throw our Maister . Howsomever , it be time for ai to crack on a bit . ' ah , ' zays the man avoot , who zimth have coom to back un , ' ah , ' twor arl mighty faine for uxtable to play skittle with our zecond rate man . Chappell or Ellicombe cud have do as much as that . Rackon Jan Uxtable wud vind a different game with Tom Gundry here . ' ' Rackon he wud , ' zaith Gundry , ' a have well jine a burial club , if her've   get ere a waife and vamily . ' " 
 " Noo . do a zay that though ? " inquire Mrs. Huxtable , much excited . 
 " ' Coom now , ' my maister zaith , try to look smarl behaind the fuzz , ' thee must throw I , my lad , avore thee can throw Jan Uxtable . he be a well man mainly nor ai be this dai . but ai baint in no oomer for playin ' much jist now , and rackon ai should hoort any man ai kitche on . ' ' she that be a good un , Zam , baint it now ? ' zaith Gundry to little chap , the very zame as ai be a tullin it now , ' doth the fule s'pose ai be ratten ? Ai've   half a maind to kick un over this hadge ; jist thee hold the nag ! ' ' Sober now , ' zaith varmer , and ai zeed a be gettin ' rad in the chake , ' God know ai do not   feel no carl to hoort ' e. Ai'll gie thee wan chance more , Tom Gundry , as thee'st a coom arl this wai fram Carnwall . can ' e trod a path in thiccy country , zame as this here be ? ' and wi ' that , a walk into the beg fuzz , twaice so haigh as this here room , and the stock begger round nor my body , and hard nor wrought hiern . a jist stratche his two hon , raight and leave , and twitch un up , wan by wan , vor ten gude lanyard , as asily as ai wud pull spring inyon . ' now , wull e let I lone ? ' zaith he , zo zoon as a coom barck , wi his brath a little quick by rason of the exarcise , ' wull ' e let I lone ? ' ' Ee 's fai , wull I , ' zaith the man avoot . ' hor , ' zaith Tom Gundry , who have be a     shopping zumwhere , ' thee cans't do a gude dai 's work , my man , tak that vor thee 's wage . ' and wi ' that a let fly at Maister 's vace wi ' a light hash stick a carr'd , man to raide off avore Maister cud coom to 's brath again . in a crack Jan Uxtable zet both his hon under the stommick of the nag , one avore the starrup and one behaind , zame as I maight to this here little tabble , and have un , harse and man , clane over hadge into Muster Yeo 's turmot falde . then with wan heft , a kitche up tother chap , and zent un sprarling after un , zame as if ' twor this here stule after the tabble . " 
     i.e. deal commercially where the staple be liquid . 
 I think poor Tim , in the excitement of his story , would have throw table and stool over the settle to illustrate it ; and if he have , Mrs. Huxtable would have forgive he . 
 " ' Thar , ' zaith our Maister , as plaisant as cud be , and ai think us shud have die of laffing , ' thar now , if zo be the owner of thiccy falde zummon e for traspash , you zay Jan Uxtable zent e on a little arrand , to vaind a carnisher as can do the laike to he . ' and wi ' that , a waipe his hon with a slip of vern , and tuk a little drap of zider , and full to 's wark again . " 
 " wull , but Tim , " ask the farmer 's wife , to lose no part of the effect , " what zort of a hadge wor it now ? twor a little hadge maybe , no haigher nor the zettle barck . " 
 " Wor it though ? " say Tim , " thee know well nor that , Missus . it be the begg hadge on arl the varm , wi ' a double row of saplin hash atap . her mak the boundary betwixt the two parish , and be not   be trim these vaive year , ai can swear . " 
 " and how be the both on them now , Tim ? a must have go haigh enough to channge the mune . 
 " Wull , Miss , " say Tim , address I , for he have tell his mistress all the story twice , " Tom Gundry brak his collar boun , and zarve ' un raight , for a brak Phil Dascombe 's a puppose whun a get ' un in a trap , that taime down to Bodmin thar ; and harse gat a rick of his taial ; but the little chap , he vell upon his hat , and that zave he kindly . but I heer'd down to Pewter Will 's , whur I gooe for a drap of zumthin for my waife 's stommick , ai heer'd zay there , as how Constable be a coomin to Maister this very naight , if Carnishers cud have perswadde un . but Constable zaith , zaith he , ' twor all along o you garnish chap , fust battery be mad , and fust blow gien , and wi'out you can zhow I Squaire Drake 's warrant , I wunt have nout to do wi ' it , not ai ; and that be law and gospel in Davonsheer and in Cornwall . ' " 
 " Tim , " say Mrs. Huxtable , " i'se warrant thee 's niver tould so long a spin up in thee 's laife avore . and thee 's tould it wonnerful well too ; hathn't   un Miss Clerer ? Zuke , here be the kay of zellar , gie Tim a half a paint more zider ; and thee mai'st have a drap theesell , gall . waipe thee mouth fust . " 
 " ah , " say Tim , favour I with a wink , in the excess of his glory , " rackon they Carnishers will know the wai off Tossil 's Barton varm next taime , wi'out no saign postesse . "   
     every word of Tim 's story be true , except as regard the name . 
 CHAPTER VI . 
 two or three day after this , I be keep school in the dairy , the parlour be too small for that purpose , and the kitchen and " wash - up "   as they call the back - kitchen   too open to inroad from Suke and Tim . my class consist of ten , or rather be eight strong , the two weames   big baby and little baby , only attend for the sake of example , and because they would have roar , if part from the other child . so those two be allow to spraddle on the floor , where sometimes they make little roller of themselves , with much indecorum , and between while sit gravely suck their fat red finger , and then point they in a glistening state at I or my audience , and giggle with a large contempt . the eight , who make believe to learn something , be the six eld Huxtables , and two of Tim Badcock 's " young un . " I marshal they , four on each side , against the low lime - whitened wall , which bear the pan of cream and milk . Little Sally , my head scholar , be very proud of measure her height , by the horizontal line on the milk - pan where the glazing end ; which Tabitha Badcock , even on tiptoe , could not reach . they be all well " clane , " and have white pinny on , and their ruddy cheek rub up to the high possible polish , with yellow soap and the jack - towel behind the wash - up door . hence , I never could relieve they from the idea that Sunday now come every day in the week . 
 I maintain strict discipline , and allow no nonsense ; but two sad drawback constantly perplex I . in the first place , their way be so ridiculous , and they labour so much hard to make I laugh , than they do to learn , that I could not always keep my countenance , and when the spelling - book go up before my face , they know , as well as possible , what be go on behind it , and peep round or below , and burst out all together . the second drawback be , that Mrs. Huxtable , in spite of all my protest , would be always rush in , upon errand purely fictitious ; and the farmer himself always find some special business in the yard , close to the wired and unglazed window , whence every now and then his loud haw - haw , and too audible soliloquy , " Dang I ! wull do , zally , that wor a good un ; zay un again , cheel ! zay un again , wull ' e ? " utterly overthrow my most solemn institution . 
 " Coom now , smarl chiller " - I address they in my unclassical Devonshire dialect , for it keep their attention alive to criticise I when I ' spak unvitty " - " coom now , e've   a be spulle lang enough : ston round I now , and tull I what I ax you . " 
 already , I have make one great mistake , by say " round " instead of " raound , " and Billy , the genius of the family , be upon the giggle . 
 " now thun , wutt be a quadripade ? " 
 " ai knoo ! " say Sally , with her hand hold out . 
 " Zo do ai , " say Jack , thrust forth his stomach . 
 " who wur ax of you ? " I inquire in a stately manner . " you bain't   the smarl chiller , be ' e ? Bill know , " I continue , but wax doubtful from the expression of Bill 's face . 
 " Ees fai , " cry Bill , suddenly clear up , " she be wutt moother zit on vor to mulk the coo . bain't   she now ? " 
 " Thee bee'st ony wan leg out , Bill . now Tabby Badcock ? " 
 while Tabby be splash in her memory   for I tell they all last week , the farmer much excite , and have no idea what the answer should be , but hop that one of his own child may discover it first , boldly show his face at the wire window , but be quite resolve to allow fair play . not so Mrs. Huxtable , who , in full possession of the case , suddenly appear behind I , and shake her fist at poor puzzle Tabby . " thee'dst good pretend to know more than thy better . " she try to make Tabby hear , without my catch her word . but the farmer hotly shout , " Lat un alo - un , waife . tak thee hon from thee mouth , I tull ' e. Spak up now , little wanch . " 
 thus encourage , Tabby make reply , look cross - wise at Mrs. Huxtable . 
 " Plase , Miss , it be a beastie wi vour taial . " 
 " raight , " cry the farmer , with admiration conquer his disappointment ; " raight this taime , ai'll tak my oath on it . I zeed wan to Barnstaple vair last year , and her wor karle , ' Phanominy Quadripade , ' her Kirsten name and her zurname , now ai coom to racollack . " 
 Tabby look elated , and Mrs. Huxtable chagrin . before I can redress the situation , a sound of heavy blow , deliver on some leathery substance , cause a new stir . all recognise the arrival of her Majesty 's mail , a boy from Martinhoe , who come upon a donkey twice a week , if there happen to be any letter for the village below . 
 out rush Mrs. Huxtable and Suke   who once receive an epistle , and the child long to go , but know well . the boy , however , have only a letter for I , which be from Mrs. Shelfer   a cousin of Ann Maples , to whom I write a few day since , ask whether she have any room to let . Mrs. Shelfer reply that ' she have apartment , and they be splendid , and the rent quite trifle ; " so the mail be bribe with a pint of cider , while I write to secure a new home . 
 my departure be now fix and inevitable , the woman naturally begin to remonstrate more than over . it have be settle that Ann Maples should go with I , not to continue as my servant , but to find a place for herself in London . 
 my few arrangement , which cost I far more pain than trouble , be not long in make ; and after say good - bye to all the dear little child and weanie , and kiss their pretty face in their little bed , amid an agony of tear from Sally , I be surprised , on enter the kitchen , to find there Mr. Beany Dawe . there be little time for talk , and much less for poetry . we be to start at three in the morning , the farmer have promise to drive we to meet the coach in Barnstaple , whence there would be more than thirty mile of hilly road to Tiverton , the near railway station . the journey to London could thus be make in a day , though no one in the parish could be bring to believe it . 
 the poet have be suborn , no doubt , by Mrs. Huxtable , and now detain I to listen to an elegy upon the metropolis of England . I can not stop to repeat it , neither do it deserve the trouble ; but it begin thus : - 
 " Fayther be wance to Lonnon town , 
 and a zed , ze he , whan a coom down , 
 ' do not   e niver goo there , Ebenezer my son , 
 for they mulk a coo , when she be not   gat none . 
 they kape up sich a hollerin , naight and day , 
 and a Devonsheer man dunno the impudence they zay . 
 their head and their hat wag regular , like the 
 scratcher of a harrow , 
 and they bile their tatie peel , and ate they 
 in a barrow . 
 they raid on a waggon top with their wife squaze 
 up inside she , 
 and they drink black dose and yesty pop in the 
 place of wholesome zider . 
 they want take back anything they have   zelle , 
 and the bed can bite , and the cat can speak : 
 and a well - dress'd man be a most compel 
 to channge his shirt in the middle of the week ! ' " 
 
 " Lor , " cry Mrs. Huxtable , " however could they do their washing ? Thee vayther must a be as big a liar as thee , Beany . they gift always run in the family . " 
 when , with remarkable patience , I have hear out his elegant effusion , the author , who have conceive much good will towards I , because I listen to his lay and call he Mr. Dawe , the author dive with a deep - draw sigh into a hole in his sack , and produce in a mysterious manner something wrap in greasy silver paper , and well tie up . he beg I to accept , and carry it about I most carefully and secretly , as long as I should live . to no other person in the world would he have give this , but I have earn it , as a true lover of poetry , and require it as a castaway among the peril of London . in vain I decline the present ; refusal only confirm his resolution . as the matter be of so little importance , I soon yield upon condition that I should first examine the gift . he give I leave with much reluctance , and I be surprised at the beauty and novelty of the thing . it be about the size of a Geneva watch , but rather thick , jet black and shin , and of the exact shape of a human heart . around the edge run a moulding line or cord of brilliant red , of the same material as the rest . in the centre be a white spot like a siphuncle . what it be I could not guess , but it look like some mineral substance . where the two lobe meet , a small hole have be drill to receive a narrow riband . after put I through many guess , Mr. Dawe inform I that it be a pixie 's heart , a charm of unequalled power against witchcraft and assassination , and to enthral the affection of a love one . he only smile , and rub his nose , on hear that I should never want it in the last capacity . be greatly pleased with it , I ask he many question , which he be very loth to answer . nevertheless I extort from he nearly all he know . 
 as he be saw into board a very large oak - tree , something fall from the very heart of it almost into his mouth , for poor Ebenezer be only an undersawyer . as he could not stop the saw without his partner concurrence , and do not wish to share his prize , he kick some sawdust over it until he could stoop to pick it up unobserved . in all his long experience of the wood , he have see but two of these rare and beautiful thing , and now assure I that any sawyer be consider lucky who find only one in the course of his career . the legend on the subject be rather quaint and graceful , and deserve a well garb than he or I can furnish . 
 " all in the olden time , there live 
 a little Pixie king , 
 so lovely and so light of foot 
 that when he dance the ring , 
 the moonlight always shift , to gaze upon his face , 
 and the cowslip - bell uplifted , rang time with every pace . 
 
 there come a dozen maiden , 
 almost as tall as bluebell ; 
 the cowslip hush their cadence , 
 and bow before the true belle : 
 the maiden shyly glance , betwixt the cummer dart , 
 espy the monarch dancing , and lose a dozen heart . 
 
 he be fit up so neatly , 
 with dewdrop for his crown , 
 and he foot it so featly 
 he never shake they down . 
 the maid begin advance , along a lily stem , 
 not to stop the monarch 's dancing , but to make he look at they . 
 
 the king could not afford they 
 the proper time to gaze , 
 but sweetly bow toward they , 
 at the turn of every maze : 
 till full of pretty face , and his sandal get wear , 
 he be puzzle in his pace , and fall upon a thorn . 
 
 the maiden break the magic ring , 
 and leap the cummer dart ; 
 ' alas , our little Pixie king , 
 the thorn be in his heart ! ' 
 they lay he in a molehill , and piteously they cry : 
 yet this be not the whole ill , for all the maiden die . 
 
 each take a spindled acorn , find 
 below a squirrel 's nest , 
 and set the butt against the ground , 
 the barb beneath her breast : 
 so truly she address the stroke unto her love part , 
 that when the acorn grow an oak , it hold her little heart . ' 
 
 by no mean a " little heart , " it seem to I , for a fairy to have own , but as large as it be love . I assure Mr. Dawe that he be quite untaught in fairy lore , or he never would have confound fairy with pixy , a different class of society . but he treat my learning with utter contempt , and reasonably enough declare that he who spend all his time in the wood must know more than any book could tell . 
 he also inform I , that the proper name for the lignify fairy heart , be a " gordit : " but he do not choose to tell I what have become of the other , which be not so large or handsome as this , yet it have save he a month 's sawing , and earn he " a rare time , " which mean , I fear , that the proceed have be spend in a very long cruise . 
 after refuse all compensation , Mr. Dawe make his farewell in several couplet of uncouth but hearty blessing , beg I only to shake hand with he once , and venture as a poet to prophesy that we should meet again . the " gordit " be probably nothing more than a rare accretion , or ganglion , in the centre of an aged oak . however , it be very pretty ; and of course I observe the condition upon which I have receive it , value it moreover as a token of true friend . 
 but how can I think of such trifle , while sit for the last time in the room where my mother die ? to - morrow all the form and colour of my life shall change ; even now I feel once more my step on the dark track of justice , which be to I revenge . how long have I be saunter on the dreary moor of listlessness and hollow weariness , which spread , for so many dead league , below the precipice of grief ? how long have I be saunter , not care to ask where , and conscious of existence only through the nerve and fibre of the memory . the thing I have be do , the duty I have discharge , the vague unlinked idea , startle I by their buffoonery to grief - might not these have all pass through I , every whit as well , if I have be set against a wall , and wound up for three month , and fit with the mind express in the chuckle of a clock ? Nay , bad than all - have I not allow soft thought to steal throughout my heart , the love of child , the warmth of kindness , the pleasure of do good in however small a way ? much more of this , and I shall learn forgiveness of my wrong ! 
 but now I see a clear road before I . return health renew my gall . death recede , and lift his train from the sword that fall before he . once more my pulse beat high with hatred , with scorn of meanness , treachery , and lie , with admiration of truth and manhood , not after the fashion of fool . 
 but dare I mount the Judge 's throne ? shall the stir of one frail heart , however fresh from its maker 's hand , be take for his voice pronounce right and wrong ? 
 these thought give I pause , and I dwell again with my mother . but in all the strength of youth and stern will , I tread they down ; and be once more that Clara Vaughan whose life shall right her father 's death . 
 CHAPTER VII . 
 at last we get through our parting with the good of people   far worthy than myself to interest any reader , and after it the dark ride over the moor , and the farmer 's vain attempt at talk to relieve both himself and we . the honest eye be bright with tear , tear of pity for my weakness , which now he scarcely care to hide , but would not show by wipe away ; and how many time he beg for frequent tiding of we , which Sally could now interpret , if write in large round hand . how many time he consult , command , and threaten the coachman , and promise he a goose at Michaelmas , if he take good care of we and our luggage ! these great kindness , and all the trifling care which strew the gap of long farewell , be more to think of than to tell . but I ought to mention , that much against the farmer 's will , I insist on pay he half the sum , which he have lend I in a manner never to be forget . moreover , with the same presentiment which he have always feel , he make I promise once more to send for he , if I fall into any dreadful strait . 
 it be late at night when our cabman , the most polite , and   if his word may be trust   the most honourable of mankind , ring the bell of Mrs. Shelfer 's house . the house be in a by - street near a large unfinished square , in the northern part of London . Mrs. Shelfer come out at once , sharp and quick and short , and wonderfully queer . at first she take no notice at all of either of as , but begin pull with all her strength at the strap of the heavy box , which , by mean know to herself alone , she contrive to drag through the narrow passage , and down three low step into the little kitchen . then she hurry back , talk all the time to herself , re - open the door of the fly , jump in , and feel under both the seat , and round the lining . find nothing there , she climb upon the driver 's box , and thoroughly examine both that and the roof . be satisfied now that none of our chattel be leave in the vehicle , she shake her little fist at two or three boy , who stand at the corner near the mew , and set both hand to the farmer 's great hamper or " maun "   as he call it , she drag it inside the front door , and turn point blanc upon I . 
 " pray , my good friend , how many be there ? " 
 " I be sure I do not   know , Mrs. Shelfer , your cousin know well . " 
 " ah , they be terrible fellow they cabby , terrible ! " the cabman stand by all the time , beat his hand together . " ' twas only last time I go to Barbican , one of them come up to I , ' Mrs. Shelfer , ' say he , ' Mrs. Shelfer ! ' say I , ' pray my good friend , how do you know my name ? ' ' ho , I know Charley well enough , ' say he , ' and there be not   a well fellow living . ' ' a deal too good for you , ' say I , ' and now pray what be your business with I ? ' ' why , old lady , ' he say , as impudent as the man with the wooden leg , ' you have   be and leave your second good umbrella under the seat of the Botany Bay Bus . ' ' catch I ! ' say I. ' it be Bible truth , ' say he , ' and my old woman 's get it now . ' ' if you never get drunk , ' say I , ' till that umbrella run in your shoe , your old woman need not   steal her light , ' and with that I run between the leg of a sheep , hang up with my tuscan bonnet on trim with white - nothing like it , my good friend , the same as I have   have these two and twenty year . " 
 " what for , Mrs. Shelfer ? " I ask in great surprise . 
 " why , for the butcher to see I , to be sure , Miss. you see he want to get I down the mew , and murder I with my little wash - leather bag , as I be go to pay the interest on Shelfer 's double - barrel gun . ah yes , " with a short sigh , " and there will be four and ninepence again , next Tuesday . " 
 talk at this rate , and stop for no reply , she lead we into her kitchen , say that she would not light a fire upstairs , it be so bootiful , the trimming of the grate , because she be not   certain that we would come , but she have get supper for we , excuse I , my good friend , in her own snug little room , and bootiful they be sure enough , the wind last week have make they so fat . 
 she point in triumph to a large dish on the table pile up with blue shell . 
 " why , Mrs. Shelfer , they be muscle , " I exclaim with some disgust . 
 " ah I see you know them , that they be , Miss , and as bootiful as ever you ate . Charley and I sit down to a peck of they . but the man as come round with the catsmeat 's brother the man with the truck and his eye cross , he tell I there be such a demand for they in Grosvenor Square , and they be so cunning this weather when they get fat , he have not   more than half a peck left , but they be the good of the lot . now I will have they all bootiful hot , bootiful , boil my good friend , if you will just run upstairs , and a teaspoon and a half of salt , and Cousin Ann know the way , and the apartment be splendid , splendid , Miss Vaughan ! " 
 she draw herself up , at the end of the sentence , with an air of the great dignity ; then suddenly drop it again , and begin bustle in and out . now for the first time , I have leisure to examine she , for while she speak , the short jump of her idea unsettle my observation . 
 she be a little body , rather thin , with a face not strongly peculiar , but odd enough to second the oddity of her mind . no doubt she have once be pretty , and her expression be pleasant now , especially when a glimpse be afford of her quick grey eye , which generally avoid the gaze , and drop beneath a fringe of close - set lash . but the loss of the front tooth , and the sharpening and wrinkling of the face , with the straggling neglect of the thick black hair fray out from the black cap , and the habit she have of shut her mouth with a snap , all these interfere with her credit for pristine good look . like Mrs. Huxtable , she be generally in a bustle , but a bustle of word more often than of deed . she have no deception about she , yet she never know the difference between the truth and a lie , and could not understand that any one else should do so . therefore she suspect everything and everybody , till one of her vein of opinion be touch , and then she would swallow anything . 
 tire out with the long day 's travel , the dazing of railway speed , and the many scene and face which have flash across I , I could not appreciate the beauty of Mrs. Shelfer 's furniture ; but leave Ann maple to eat the muscle , if she could , and to gossip with her cousin , I be not slow to revisit the old farmhouse , and even the home of my childhood , in the wing cradle of sleep . 
 CHAPTER VIII . 
 Ann Maples have do she good to persuade I to call on my godmother , Lady Cranberry , but I be quite resolve to do nothing of the sort . in the first place , Lady Cranberry be a person of great wealth , live in a very large house , and keep up such state as gay widow love , who have forget old affection and be look out for new . in I , therefore , to whose fix estimate fidelity seem the very pith of honour , there could be no love towards such a changeling . and even if I have like she , my circumstance would not admit of our visit upon equal term , and it be not likely that I would endure to be patronize by any one . in the second place , the same most amiable lady have write letter of beautiful condolence , and take a tender interest in our change of fortune , so long as there be any novelty in it ; but soon flag off , and have not even reply to my announcement of dearest mother 's death . finally , I hat she without any compromise , from what I have see of she , and what she have do to I at Vaughan Park . 
 so my good Ann set off all alone , for she hop to obtain some recommendation there , and I be leave to receive Mrs. Shelfer 's morning visit . 
 her queer episodical conversation , and strange biography of every table , chair , and cushion - her ' stick , " as she delight to call they - I shall not try to repeat , for my history be not a comic one ; neither will she appear , unless the connexion require it . one vein of sympathy between we be open at once , by she come into the room with a lame blackbird on her finger ; and I be quite surprised at the number of her pet . as for the ' splendid apartment , " they be two little room on the first floor , adjoin one another , and form , together with the landing outside and a coal - closet , the entirety of that storey . the room above be occupy by a young dress - maker . Mr. and Mrs. Shelfer , who have no child keep the ground - floor   consist of a parlour and kitchen   and the two attic , one of which be always full of onion and carrot seed . upon the whole , though the ' stick " be very old , and not over clean , until I scour they , and the drawing - room   as my landlady love to call it   be low and small , and look through the rail of a narrow balcony upon a cheese - monger 's shop across the road   instead of a woode dingle , I be very well satisfied with they ; and above all the rent be within my mean . 
 in the afternoon , when thing be grow tidy , a carriage drive up rapidly , and a violent ringing of the bell ensue . it be Lady Cranberry , who , under the pretext of bring Ann Maples home , be come to gratify her own sweet curiosity . she run upstairs in her most charming manner , catch I by both hand , and would have kiss I desperately , if I have show any tendency that way . then she stop to admire I . 
 " oh , you lovely creature ! how you be grow to be sure ! I should never have know you . how delicious all this be ! " 
 of course I be pleased with her admiration ; but only for a moment , because I dislike she . 
 " I be glad you find it delicious , " I reply quite coldly ; " perhaps I shall by - and - by . " 
 " what would I give to be enter life under such sweetly romantic circumstance ? dear I ! I must introduce you . what a sensation you will cause ! with such a face and figure and such a delightful story , we shall all rave about you . and how well you be dress from that outlandish place ! what a piece of luck ! it be the great marvel on earth that you find I in London now . " 
 " excuse I , " I say , " I neither find , nor mean to find you . " 
 " oh , of course you be cross with I . I forget about that . but who make your dress , in the name of all woodland grace ? " 
 " I always make my own dress . " 
 " then you shall make mine . say no more about it . you shall live with I , and make my dress by day ; and by night you shall go with I everywhere , and I will not   be jealous . I will introduce you everywhere . ' this be my ward , Miss Vaughan , whose father - ah , I see , you know that romantic occurrence in Gloucestershire . ' do you think it will be a your - and the Great Exhibition season - before you be mistress of a property ten time the size of Vaughan Park ? if you doubt it , look in the glass . ah I ! you know nothing of the world , I forget , I be so warm - hearted . but you may take my word for it . will you cry a bargain ? " 
 she hold out her hand , as she have see the fast man do , whose society she affect . I notice it not , but lead she on ; my fury have long be gather . I almost choke when she speak in that way of my father , utterly as I despise she . but I make it a trial of self - control , which might be demand against more worthy object . 
 " be you sure that I shall be useful ? sure that I shall earn my board ? " 
 " oh , you Vaughans be always so conscientious . I want an eider - down petticoat quilt at once for the winter , and I dare not trust it to Biggs , I know she will pucker it so . that shall be the first little job for my Clara . " 
 her cup be full . she have use dearest mother 's fond appellative , and , as I think , in mockery . I do not lower myself by any sarcastic language . she would not have understand it .  poor godmother , she be so frightened that I be sorry for she . they help she into the carriage , and she have just strength to draw down the blind .

" I could not attend to she , but lock myself in , and wrestle with my passionate nature , at one time indulge , then spurn and freeze it . yet I could not master it , as I fancy I have do . 
 CHAPTER IX . 
 soon afterwards , Ann Maples go to the place which she have obtain in Lady Cranberry 's household ; and I determine to begin my search .

 presently a nice old lady , with snow - white hair , come in .

 despite her kindness and good breeding , the lady star a little .

 she look at I in great surprise , examine I closely , and then reply :

 she mention a name of good repute in the world of letter , as even I be aware .

 all this I learn from Mr. and Mrs. Elton during my second visit , for Mrs. Elton be too good a wife to dispense with her husband 's judgment . also I see their daughter , a pleasing delicate girl ; they learn of course some part of my story , and be most kind and affectionate to I ; and I be proud to have preserve their friendship to the present time . but as they take no prominent share in the drama of my life , henceforth they will not be present upon its stage .

 CHAPTER X. 
 although Ann Maples be not so very talkative , it would be romantic to suppose that Mrs. Shelfer have fail to learn my entire history , so far at least as her cousin know it .

 she bring up my dinner and tea , without a single word , but with many sly glance at I from her quick grey eye . once or twice she be at the point of speak , and the dry smile she always speak with flutter upon her face ; but she close her lip firmly and even bit they to keep herself in .  late in the evening , she come to say that Inspector Cutting be there , and would come up if I wish it . upon my request he come , and one look be enough to show that his niece have not misdescribe he . an elderly man , but active looking and wiry , with nothing remarkable in his feature , except the clear cast of his forehead and the firm set of his mouth . but the quick intelligence that shoot from his eye make it seem waste of time to finish tell he anything . for this reason , polite though he be , it become unpleasant to talk to he . it be something like shoot at diver - as my father use to describe it - for whom the flash of the gun be enough . 
 yet he never once stop or hurry I , until my tale be do , and all my thought lay bare . then he ask to see all my relic and vestige of the deed ; even my gordit do not escape he .

 " I can read it , but not speak it . " 
 " be it commoner for italian surname to begin with an o , or with a c ? "

 when all my particular have be tell , and all my evidence show , I ask with breathless interest - for my confidence in he grow fast - what his opinion be .

 I promise to answer without reserve . 
 " what be your mother 's personal appearance ? " 
 " most win and delicate . " 
 " how old be she at the time of her marriage ? " 
 " twenty - one , I believe . " 
 " how old be your father then ? " 
 " twenty - five . " 
 " how many year be they marry ? " 
 " Sixteen , exactly . " 
 " when do your guardian first leave England ? " 
 " in the course of a year or two after the marriage . " 
 " have there be any misunderstanding between he and your father ? " 
 " none , that I ever hear of . " 
 " do your father , at any time , travel on the continent ? " 
 " only in Switzerland , and part of Italy , during his wedding tour . " 
 " your guardian return , I believe , at interval to England ? " I have never tell he this . 
 " yes . at least I suppose so , or he would not have be in London . "

 " not once within my memory . " 
 " thank you . I will ask no more . it be a strange story ; but I have know several much more strange . of one thing be assure . I shall catch the criminal . I need not tell you that I hear much of this case at the time . "

 " no . if I have be - well , I will not say . but I be not then in my present position . have I be so , it would have become my special department . "

 " change my name , Inspector Cutting ! do you think I be ashamed of my name ? "

 " excuse I , Miss , it would not be strange at all , in a female . and now I will wish you ' good night . ' you shall hear from I when needful . meanwhile , I will take charge of these article . " 
 he begin , in the cool manner , to pack up my sacred relic , dagger , cast , and all .

 when I come to myself , Mrs. Shelfer be kneel before I , with her foot in a basin of water , while two other basin , and numberless towel , be round . " and with that he disappear , and I have time to pity myself . the hand the poor farmer use so to admire , and which I be proud of no doubt , in my way , lie in a dishcloth cover and ooze with blood . but my relic be on the table , all safe . a quick step be hear on the stair , and Inspector Cutting come in , carry a small phial . 
 " out of the way , Patty , " he cry , " you be do more harm than good . " 
 he take up a basin of cold water , and pour half the content of the little phial into it . 
 " now hold her arm up , Patty , as high as you can . I never know arnica fail . " 
 my hand be put into the water , and the bleeding be stanch in a minute or two . however he keep it there for a quarter of an hour , till it be quite benumb .

 the last word seem to move he . they all like big word , however clear - head they be . 
 " Miss Vaughan , under these special circumstance , I will promise what you require ; upon condition that you give I accurate drawing , for I see that you can make they . " 
 " certainly , when my hand be well enough . "

" 
 I do so with all my heart . I like he much well since I have defeat he ; and I see that it be well worth the pain , for he would do his utmost to make amend . he wish I good night with a most respectful bow . " I will come and inquire how you be to - morrow , Miss Vaughan . Patty , quiet , and coolness , and change the lard frequently . no doctor , if you please ; and above all hold your queer little tongue . " 
 " never fear I , Uncle John ; you be right , my good friend , it be a little tongue , but no queerer than my neighbour . " 
 Inspector Cutting would have form a far low opinion of my spirit , if he have see how I cry that night ; not from the pain of the wound , I be sure , but to think of the fuss dear mother would have make about they . 
 CHAPTER XI . 
 in spite of the arnica , my cut be not heal for a month ; not enough , I mean , for I to handle a pencil . Mr. Cutting , when he come , accord to promise , tell I something to quiet I , because I be so feverish . whether he believe it , or only act medically , be more than I could decide . the opinion he give I , or the substance of it , be this . 
 that the deed be do , not for money , or worldly advantage in any way , but for revenge . here I think of Mrs. Daldy . what wrong the revenge be wreak for , he could not even guess , or at any rate would not hint to I . 
 that the straight clue to the mystery be to be seek in Italy , where my guardian 's track should be follow carefully . the idea of force , or worming , the truth from he be reject at once through my description of his character ; although the Inspector quite agree with I , that , even if guiltless of the crime , Mr. Edgar Vaughan know all about it now . 
 that no importance should be attach to the anonymous letter from London ; in accordance with my promise to Mrs. Elton , I do not mention the Polish lady 's name ; and Mr. Cutting do not press I to do so , for he firmly believe from what I say that she have make a mistake in the address she give , and would not help we now , even if we could find she . that nevertheless a strict watch should be keep in London , whither flock nine - tenth of the foreigner who ever set foot in this country . London moreover be likely , ere long , to draw nearly all the migratory stranger to the business or pleasure of next year 's " great exhibition , " provide only that it should prove successful , as the Inspector think it would . 
 as for my enemy be attract by work of industry , it seem to I quite against nature that a base assassin should care for art or science , or any national progress . but the remembrance of several case , among the dark annal I use to delight in , soon prove to I my error ; while the long experience of a man , verse from his youth in criminal way , convict I of presumption . 
 to put myself more on a level with fraud , and stealth , and mystery , I do a thing for which I feel guilty to myself and my mother . I change my name . but , in spite of Inspector Cutting , I do not travel out of the family . my father 's second name be " Valentine , " take from his mother . this name I assume in a short form , become " Clara valence ; " it save change of initial and a world of trouble , and I feel warm in it , because it seem to have be my father 's . in the neighbourhood I know no one except Mrs. Elton , to whom   as I grow intimate with her   I partly explain my reason . as for Mrs. Shelfer , she be delighted at the change . she say that her Uncle John have christen I , that it sound much pretty , and would always remind she of valentine . nevertheless I long for the day when I might call myself " Clara Vaughan " once more . 
 by the time I be able to go about freely again and use my hand as of old , it be the middle of November . the first use I make of my pencil be to copy most carefully all that Inspector Cutting require . he promise to keep these drawing , and indeed the whole matter , most jealously to himself ; by which term he mean , as I afterwards find , Inspector Cutting and those to whom he be bind to report . 
 what I now want be money , to send an adroit inquirer throughout the North of Italy , and other part where my guardian 's shift abode have be . I know that he dwell awhile at Pisa , Genoa , and Milan , also at an obscure little village name " Calva , " which I could not find in the map . all I have learn of his roving be from the lesson my father would give I sometimes , when he use to say , " now , Tooty , put your finger on Uncle Edgar . " to every one , but myself , it seem a strange thing that after so many wandering , Mr. Edgar Vaughan have bring no valet , major domo , or courier , no dependant or retainer of any kind , and not even a foreign friend to England , or at any rate to Vaughan Park . 
 but now for the needful resource - the only chance of procure they lie in my young and partly self - tutor art . I brace myself with the remembrance , that while none of my family ever lay claim to genius , the limner 's faculty have never be want among they . inferior gift be often as heirloom in the blood , though high original power follow no vein except its own . the latter none of we ever possess ; but taste and the knack of adaptation have seldom be alienate . observation too , in a small way , and the love of nature seem inborn in we all . my father 's drawing be perfect , but for the one thing want ; and in sketch from outdoor nature that want be less perceive . my grandfather have be know among the few amateur of the day as a skilful colourist . as to habit of observation , a little tale hand down in our family will show that they have exist in one of its member seven generation ago . 
 in the autumn of 1651 , when King Charles be steal along from Colonel Wyndham 's house to the coast of Hampshire and Sussex , the little band be overtake by nightfall , somewhere near the New Forest . it be shortly after the narrow escape of the King from that observant blacksmith , who see that his horse be shod with north - country iron . though he be take it easily , his three trusty friend know well that a Roundhead Squadron be near , and that his last chance depend on speed and night travel . what could they do now in the tempestuous darkness ? they be in a tract thinly inhabit , half woodland , half heather , and the road be hopelessly lose . no rain fall as yet it be true , and the wind be wait for rain , but the lightning come fitfully from the horizon all round . the King alone be on horseback , his three companion afoot . they stand still in doubt and terror , for they could not tell north from south . suddenly Major Cecil Vaughan espy a faint gleam familiar to he of old in the waste land round Vaughan Park . to an accurate eye there could be little doubt as to the source of the lambent light - flame it could not be call . it play in a pale yet constant stream on a certain kind of moss , know to botanist , not to I , for the waste land have be reclaim . this light be to be see at no time , except when the air be surcharge with electricity . 
 " follow I all ; I know the way ! " cry Major Vaughan , right cheerily . 
 " and if you do , man , " say the King , " your eye be make of dasher . " 
  what this meant , I use as a child to wonder ; but now I know . 
 for six dark mile the Major lead they without default , until they come to a lonely heathman 's house , where they sleep in safety . he never tell they how he do it ; be apt , I suppose , as man of the second order be , to hug superior knowledge . but it be a most simple thing . that strangely sensitive moss follow the course of the sun , and therefore the lambent light can only be see from the west . so all the time he could see it - the other never see it at all - he know that they be wend from west to east , which be their proper course . 
 to return to myself . I put the finishing touch to a view of rock and woodland scenery , north - west of Tossil 's Barton , and set off to try my fortune with it . some young lady , bear to my position , would have think this errand one of much degradation , but it do not appear so to I . so I walk briskly - for I hate an omnibus , and could ill afford a cab - to the shop of a well - know dealer in picture , not far from the haymarket . it be my first venture into the heart of London , but I find the way very easily , have jot it down from a map . the day be dark and drizzly ; the pavement grimy and slimy , and hillocke with mud at the joint of the flag . it be like walk on a peeled kneading - trough with dollop of paste leave in it . along the far reach of the street , and the garden in the square , wisps of fog be crawl , and almost every one be cough . 
 the dealer receive I politely . too politely in fact : for it seem to savour of kindness , which I do not want from he . what I want be business , and nothing else . he take my poor drawing , do only in water - colour , and set it up in a square place make perhaps for the purpose , where the brown flaw fall upon it from a skylight form like a Devonshire chimney . then he draw back and clasp his hand , then shade his eye with they , as if the light be too strong , whereas the whole place be like a well turn upside down . he seem uneasy because I do not care to follow he throughout all this little performance .
