 " and now , " I say , for my foolish pride be up , and I speak as I would have do to the porter at our lodge , not with the least contempt - I be never so low as that - but with a long perspective , " now , Mr. Oxgall , it will soon be dark . what will you give I for it ? " 
 " allow I , Miss ; allow I one moment . the light be a leetle too strong . ah , the mark of the brush come out . strong touch , but indiscreet . a year of study require . Shade too broad and massive . a want of tone in the background . great feeling of nature , but inexperienced rendering . More mellowness desiderate . full however of promise . all the fault on the right side . Most energetic handling ; no weak stippling here . but water - colour be down just now ; a deal depend on the weather and time of year . " 
 " how so , Mr. Oxgall ? " 
 " hot sun , and off they go . fog and murk and frost , and the cry be all for oil . excuse I , Miss - a thousand pardon , your name escape I , you do not pronounce it strongly . " 
 " Miss Valence ! " I say , with an emphasis that startle he out of his mincing . 
 " Miss Valence , you think I very long . all young lady do . but my object be to do they justice , and if they show any power , to encourage they . " 
 " thank you , I want no encouragement . I know I can draw a little ; and there it be . the fog be thicken . I have far to go . your price , if you please ? " 
 I go up many step in his opinion , by reason of my curtness and independence . 
 " Miss Valence , I will give you three guineas , although no doubt I shall be a loser . " 
 " then do not   give it , " say I in pure simplicity . 
 I go up several step more . how utterly man of the world be puzzle by plain truth ! 
 " Miss Valence , if you will forgive the observation , I would beg to remark that your conversation as well as your painting be crisp . I will take this little piece at all hazard , because it be full of character . will you forgive I for one word of advice ? " 
 " there be nothing to forgive . I shall thank you heartily for it . " 
 " it be simply this : - the bad part of your work be the perspective . and figure - drawing will be of service to you . study at a school of design , if you have one near you ; and be not above draw stiff and unsightly object . house be the true guide to perspective . I can not paint or even draw ; but I be so much with great artist , that I know well how to advise . " 
 " thank you . can you kindly suggest anything more ? " 
 " yes . your touch be here and there too harsh . keep your hand light though bold , and your brush just a leetle wetter . but you have the grand thing quite unattainable , when not in the grain . I mean , of course , freedom of handling and an artist 's eye . " 
 " do you think I could do any good in oil ? " 
 " I have no doubt you could , but not for a long time . if fame be your object , take to oil . if speedy return , stick to water - colour . leave I your address , if you have no objection ; and bring I your next work . if I do well with this , I will try to give you more . " 
 he take from a desk three new sovereign and three new shilling , wrap they neatly in silver paper , and hand they to I . I never imagine I could be so proud of money . 
 light of heart I leave the shop , not that I have make my fortune yet , but what be great happiness , I think myself likely to make it . 
 soon I perceive , with some alarm , how thick and murky the air have grow . the fog be stoop heavily down , and be now become like a wash of gamboge and lamp - black . all the street - lamp be light , though they could not see one another , and every shop - keeper have his little jet . the pavement be no long slippery , but sticky and dry ; and a cold , that pierce to the bone , be steal along . already it have begin to freeze ; and I , so familiar both with white and black frost , observe with no small interest the grey or fog - frost , which be new to I . how different from the pure whiteness when the star be sparkle , and the earth be gleam , and the spirit of man so buoyant ! this grey fog - frost be rather depressing to most nature , and a chilly damp creep to the core of all thing . thick encrust rime come with it , and sometimes a freezing rain . 
 before I reach the New Road , the fog have grow so dense and dark , that I be much inclined to take a cab , for fear of lose my way . but I could not see one , and find myself at last in a main thoroughfare call the Hampstead Road , I walk on briskly and bravely till I reach Camden Town , when I know what course to pursue . 
 slowly wend up College Street , for I be get tired and the fog thick than ever , indeed every step seem a thrust into an ochre wall , I hear a plaintive , and rather musical , voice chant , much as follow : - 
 " christian friend , and sister in the Lord , all who own a heart that feel for undeserved distress , aid , I implore you , a bereaved wife and mother , who have this very moment seven small lovely child , starve in a garret , three of they upon a bed of sickness , and the inhuman landlord , for the sake of a few shilling about to turn they this bitter night into the flinty street . christian friend , may you never know what it be to be famish as I and my seven darling be this very night , in the midst of plenty . from Plymouth in Devonshire , I walk two hundred and fifty mile afoot all the way to join my beloved husband in London . when I come to this christian city - Georgiana , pick up that halfpenny - he have be order off in the transport ship Hippopotamus , to shed his blood for his queen and country ; and I who have know the smile of plenty in my happy rustic home , I be compel for the sake of my child to the degradation of publicly solicit alm . the small trifle , even an old pair of shoe or a leave off garment will be receive with the heartfelt gratitude of the widow and orphan . my eld child , ma'am , the old of seven , bad in the whooping cough . Georgiana , curtsey to the pretty lady , and show she your broken chilblain . " 
 " no thank you , " I say : I could just see she through the fog . she look like one who have see well day , and the thought of my own vicissitude open my heart towards she . how could I show my gratitude well for the money I have just earn , than by bestow a share in charity upon worthy object ? so I take out my purse , an elegant little french one give I by dear mother , and place my three new shilling in the poor creature 's hand , as she stand in the gutter . she be overpower with gratitude , and could not speak for a moment . then she come nearer , to bless I . 
 " sweet lady , in the name of seven famish innocent , whom you have save from death this night , may he who guard the fatherless and the widow from his mercy - seat above , may he shower his rich blessing - " 
 Snap - she have get my purse and be out of sight in the fog . Georgiana 's red heel be the last thing I see . for an instant I could not believe it ; but think that the fog have affect my sight . then I dart across the road , almost under the foot of a horse , and down a place call " Pratt Street . " it be hopeless , utterly hopeless ; and not only my three pound be go , but half besides of all I have in the world . I have take that money with I , because I mean , if fortunate with my landscape , to buy a large box of colour in rathbone - place ; but the fog have deter I . she have snatch my purse while I try to clasp it , for my glove have first get in the way . all be go , dear mother 's gift , my first earning , and all . More than all I feel sore at heart from the baseness of the robbery . nothing be so bitterly grievous to youth as a blow to faith in one 's specie . 
 I be not ashamed to confess that feel all alone in the fog , I lean against some iron railing and cry away like a child . child I be still at heart , despite all my trial and spirit ; and more so perhaps than girl who have play out their childhood . in the full flow of my passion , for I be actually sob aloud , ashamed of myself all the while , I feel an arm steal round my waist , and start in fear of another thief , confront the lovely face that human eye ever look on . with soft caress , and sweetest smile , it draw close to my own stormy and bitter countenance . 
 " be you well now , dear ? oh do not   cry so . you will break your poor little heart . do tell I what it be , that be a dear . I will do anything to help you . " 
 " you can not   help I : " I exclaim through my sob : " nobody can help I ! I be bear to ill luck , and shall have nothing else till I die . " 
 " do not   say so dear . you must not   think of it . my father , who never be wrong , say there be no such thing as luck . " 
 " I know that well enough . People always say that who have it on their side . " 
 " ah , I never think of that . but I hope you be wrong . but tell I , dear , what be the matter with you . I be sure you have do no harm , and dear papa say no one can be unhappy who have not injure any one . " 
 " can not   they though ? your papa be a moralist . now I will just tell you fact . " and to prove my point , I tell she of this new trouble , hint at previous one and my many great loss , of which money be the least . even without the controversial spirit , I must have tell she all . there be no deny anything to such a win love face . 
 " dear I ! " she cry very thoughtfully , with her mite of hand out of her muff - she have the prettiest set of fur I ever beheld , and how it become she ! - " dear I ! she could not   have mean it , I feel quite sure she could not . you will come to my opinion when you have time to consider , dear " - this be say so sagely that I could have kiss she all over like a duck of a baby . " to steal from you who have just give she more than you could afford ! now come with I , dear , you shall have all the money I have get ; though I do not   think it be anything like the nine pound you have lose , and I be sure it be not new money . only I have not   get it with I . I never carry money . do you know why , dear ? " 
 " no . how should I ? " 
 " well , I do not   mind tell you . because then I can not   spend it , or give it away . I do not   care a bit about money . what good be it to I ? why , I can never keep it , somehow or other . but papa say if I can show five pound on Christmas - day , he will put five more on the top of it , and then do you know what I will do ? I will give away five , and spend the rest for Pappy and Conrad . " and the lively little thing clap her hand at the prospect , quite forget that she have just offer I all her store . presently this occur to she . 
 " no . now I come to think of it , I will not   have the five pound on Christmas - day . as the girl at the College say , I will just sell the old Pappy . that will be well fun still . he will find a good reason for it . he always do for everything . you shall have every bit of it . come home with I now , that be a dear . you be well now , you know . come , that be a love . I be sure I shall love you with all my heart , and you be so terribly unlucky . " 
 I yield at once . she be so loving and natural , I could not resist she . she break upon I like soft sunshine through the fog , laughing , smiling , dancing , her face all light and warmth , yet not a shallow light , but one that play up from the fount of tear . her deep rich violet eye seldom use their dark lash , except when she be asleep . she be life itself , quick , playful , love life , feel for and with all life around ; pitying , trust , admire all thing ; yet true as the hearth to household tie . I never find another such nature : it be the perfection of maiden womanhood , even in its unreason . and therefore nobody could resist she . with I , of ten time her strength of will , and power of mind - small though it be - she could do in a moment exactly as she like ; I mean of course in trivial matter . it be impossible to be offend with she . 
 when she have lead I a few step towards her home - for I go with she   not , of course , to take her money , but to see she safe , she turn round suddenly : - 
 " oh I forgot , dear ; I must not take you to our house . we have have new order . but where do you live ? I will bring you my little bag to - morrow . they will not   let I out again to - night . now I know you will oblige I . I be so sorry that I must not   see you safe home , dear . " this she say with the fine air of protection imaginable . 
 I give she my name and address , and ask for her . 
 " my name be Isola Ross , I be seventeen and a half , and my papa be Professor at the College . I run away from old Cora . it seem such fun to be all alone in the fog . what trouble I shall get into ! but they can not   be angry with I long . kiss I , darling . mind , to - morrow ! " 
 off she dance through the fog ; and I go sadly home , yet think more of she , than of my serious and vexatious loss . 
 CHAPTER XII . 
 Inspector Cutting , upon the first tiding of the robbery , come at once , and assure I that he know the " party " well , and want she for several other plant , and crafty as she be   " leary " be the elegant word he use   he be sure to be down upon she in the course of a very short time . 
 Isola Ross , to my great surprise , do not come the next day , nor even the day after ; so I set out to look for she , at the same time wonder at myself for do so . know that College Street must take its name from some academic building in or near it , I conclude of course that there I should find Professor Ross and my lovely new friend . so without consult Mrs. Shelfer , who would have chatter for an hour , away I go one tine frosty morning to ask about the College . 
 I find that a low unsightly building , which I have often pass , near the bottom of the street , be the only College there ; so I enter a small quadrangle , to make further inquiry . 
 the first person I see be a young man dress like one of my father 's groom , and crack a long whip and whistling . he have a brilliant scarlet neckcloth , green sporting coat , and black boot up to his knee . I study he for a moment because it strike I that he would look well in a foreground , when tone down a little , as water colour would render he . he appreciate my attention , and seem proud of it . 
 " now , Polly , what can I do for you , dear ? " 
 he must have be three part drunk , or he would never have dare to address I so . of course I make no answer , but walk on . he crack his whip like a pistol , to startle I . 
 " splendid filly , " I hear he mutter , " but cuss high action . " what he mean I do not know or care . 
 the next I meet be a fussy little man , dress all in brown , who smell of musty hay . 
 " will you kindly tell I , " I ask , " where to find Professor Ross ? ' 
 " Ross , Ross ! do not   know the name . no Ross about here . what be he Professor of ? " 
 " that I be not tell . but it be something the young lady study . " 
 " no young lady about here . but I see you have bring your dear mamma 's lapdog . take it out of the bag . let I look at it . " 
 " be not this the College ? " 
 " yes to be sure . the good College in London . quick , let I see the dog . " 
 " I have no dog , sir . I have make some mistake . " 
 " then you have get a pony . pet over - feed . shetland breed . " 
 " no indeed . nothing except myself ; and I be look for Miss Ross . " 
 " young lady , you have make a very great mistake . you have keep I five minute from a lecture on the navicular disease . and my practice be controvert by an upstart youth from the country . I be in search of authority . " and off he dart , I suppose to the library . 
 it be clear that I have make some mistake , so I find my way back to the street , and ask in the near shop what building it be that I have just leave . 
 " oh , they be the weteran , " say the woman , " and a precious set they be ! " 
 " why , they do not look like soldier . " 
 " no , no , Miss. Weterans , where they take in all the sick horse and dog . and very clever they be , I have hear say . " 
 " and where be the College where the young lady be ? " 
 " I do not   know of no other College nearer than High Street , where the boy wear flat cap . but there be a girl ' school down the road . " 
 " I do not   want a school . I want a College where young lady go . " 
 " then I can not help you , Miss. " and back I go to consult Mrs. Shelfer . 
 " bless my soul , Miss Valence , " cry the little woman , out of breath with amazement , " have you be among they nigger ? it be a mercy they do not   skin and stuff you . what do you think now they do to my old Tom ? " 
 " how can I guess , Mrs. Shelfer ? " 
 " no , no , to be sure not . I forget , my good friend . why , they know he well it seem , because he have be there in dear Miss Minto 's time , for a salmon bone that have get crossway in his oesop , so they say at least , but they be the big liar - so only a year ago come next boxing - day , here come to the door half a dozen of they , bus - cad and coachman all in one , all look as grave as judge . when I go to the door they all pull their hat off , as if I have be the Queen at the very least . ' what can I do for you , my good friend ? ' say I ; for Shelfer be out of the way , and catch I let they in for all their politeness . no , no , thank you . ' Mrs. Shelfer , ' say the big of they , a lantern - jaw young fellow with cover over his pocket , ' Mrs. Shelfer , you be possess of a most remarkable cat . an animal , ma'am , of unparalleled cemetery and organic dewelopment . our Professor , ma'am , be deliver a course of lecture on the Canonical Heapatightness of the Hirumbillycuss . " 
 " well do , Mrs. Shelfer ! what a memory you must have ! " 
 " pretty well , Miss , pretty well . Particular for long word , when I like the sound of they . ' well sir , ' I say , feel rather take aback , ' thank God I have not   get it . ' ' no , ma'am , ' say he , ' your bloom countenance entirely negative any such dyingnose . but the Professor , in pass the other morning , observe some symptom of it in your magnificent cat , for whom he entertain the most sincere attachment , and whom he will cure for our advancement and edification upon the lecture table . and now , ma'am , Professor Sallenders desire his most respectful compliment , and will you allow we to take that dear good cat to be cure . the Professor be instrumental once in preserve his honoured existence , therefore he feel assure that you will not now refuse he . ' well you see , Miss , I do not   half like to let he go , but I be afraid to offend the Professor , because of all my animal , for I know that he could put a blight upon they , bird and all , if he choose . Old Tom be lie roast his back again the fender , the same as you see he now , poor soul ; so I catch he up and put he in a double cover basket , with a bit of flannel over he , because the weather be cold ; and he be so clever , would you believe it , he put up his old paw to fight I , he know he be go to mischief , and that turn I rather . ' now will you promise to bring he back safe ? ' I say . ' Ma'am , ' say the lantern - jaw young man , bow over his heart , and as serious as a pulpit , ' Ma'am , in less than an hour . rely upon the honour of Weteran Arian Gent . " 
 " well , Mrs. Shelfer , I be astonish . even I should never have be so silly . poor old Tom among the Philistines ! " 
 " well , Miss , I begin to feel very uneasy directly they be go . I think they look back so queerly , and old Tom be mew so dreadful in the basket . presently I begin to hear a mewing out of the cupboard , and a mewing out of the clock , and even out of the dripping - pan . so I put on my bonnet as quick as I could , and run right away to the College , and somehow or other by the time I get there , I be in a fright all over . as good luck would have it , the man be at the gate ; a nice respectable married man , and a friend of Charley 's . ' curb , ' I say , ' where be Professor Sallenders ? ' ' down in the country , ' say he , ' since last Friday . he never stop here at Christmas , Mrs. Shelfer , he be a deal too know for that . ' my heart go pop , Miss , like an oyster shell in the fire . I hold on by the door , and I think it be all up with I . ' do not   take on so , Missus , ' say Curbs , ' if any of your museum be ill , there be half a dozen clever young cove in the operating room over there , only they be busy just now , cut up a big black cat . my eye , how he do squeal ! ' I scream out and run - Curbs think I be mad , and he be not far out - bang go the door before I , and there on the table , with the lantern - jaw young man flourish a big knife over he , there lie my precious old Tom strap down on his back , with his mouth tie up in white tape , and leather glove over his foot , and stick truss across he the same as a roasting rabbit , and a streak of white all along his bless stomach - you know , Miss , he have not   get one white hair by right - where the nigger have shave and flour he , to see what they be about . he turn up his dear old eye when he see I ; it would have make you cry , and he try to speak . oh you precious old soul , do not   I scatter they right and leave ? I scratch that lantern - jaw hypocrite 's face till I give he the hirumbillycuss and hirumtommycuss too , I expect . I call a policeman in , and there be not   one of they finish his Christmas in London . but the poor old soul have never be the same cat since . the anxiety he be in , turn his hair white on both side of his heart and all round the back of his ear . he would not   come to the door , he shake so , at the call of the cat's - meat man for well than a month , and he will not   look at it now , while there be a skewer in it . " 
 the poor little woman be cry with pity and rage . Old Tom look up all the time as if he know all she say , and then jump on her lap , and show his paw , and purr . 
 meanwhile , a change have come over my intention . perhaps all the rudeness I have meet with that day have call my pride into arm . at any rate , much as I like pretty Isola , and much as I long for her fresh warm kindness , I now resolve to wait until she should choose to seek I . so I do not even ask Mrs. Shelfer whether she know the College where the Professor lecture . what be love and warm young heart to I ? I deserve such a rebuff for swerve so from my duty . now I would give all my thought to the art , whence only could spring any hope of attain my end , and the very next day I would follow the picture - dealer 's advice . 
 CHAPTER XIII . 
 there be a school of design not very far from my lodging , and thither I go the next morning . my landlady offer to come with I and see I safe in the room ; and of course her Charley , who seem to know everybody , know some one even there , to whom she kindly promise to recommend I . so I gladly accept her offer . 
 in some respect , Mr. Shelfer be more remarkable than even his wife . he be so shy , that on the rare occasion when we meet , I never could get he to look at I , except once when he be drunk ; yet by some mysterious process he seem to know everything about I - the colour of my eye , the arrangement of my hair , the dress I put on , the spirit I be in - a great deal more , in fact , than I ever care to know . so that sometimes my self - knowledge be largely increase , through his observation repeat by his wife . but I be not allow to flatter myself that this result from any especial interest ; for he seem to possess an equal acquaintance with the affair of all his neighbour . mention any one anywhere around , and he , without seem to mean it , would describe he or her unmistakably in half a dozen word . he never praise or blame , he simply identify . he must have see more with a blink of his eye , than most people see in five minute of gazing . he seldom bring any one home with he , though he often promise to do so ; he never seem to indulge in gossip , at any rate not with his wife . " cut it short , old ' ooman , " be all the encouragement he ever give she in that way . when he be at home - a thing of rare occurrence - he sit with his head down and a long pipe in his mouth ; he walk in the street with his head down , and never accost any one . where do he get all his knowledge ? I doubt if there be a public - house in London , but what Shelfer know at the furth a cousin of the landlord , and a brother of one of the potboy . " Charley Shelfer " everybody call he , and everybody speak of he , not with distinguished respect , but with a kindly feeling . his luck be proverbial ; he have a room full of thing which he have win at raffle , and he be in constant requisition to throw for less fortunate people . as for his occupation - he call himself a nurseryman , but he have no nursery that I could discover . he receive a pound a week for look after the garden in the great square ; but when any one come for he , he be never to be find there . I think he spend most of his time in job about , and ' swap "   as Mrs. Shelfer call it   among his brother gardener . sometimes , he bring home beautiful plant , perfectly lovely flower , unknown to I even by name , and many of these he present to I by Mrs. Shelfer 's hand . every Sunday morning he be up before the daylight , and away for an excursion , or rather an incursion , through the Hampstead , Highgate , and Holloway district . from these raid he use to return as I come home from the morning service . by the way , if I have want to puzzle he and find a blank in his universal acquaintance , the good chance would have be to ask he about the clergyman . he never give the pew - opener any trouble , neither indeed do Mrs. Shelfer , who call herself a Catholic ; but the lively little woman 's chief terror be death , and a parson to she be always an undertaker . if Mr. Shelfer have not spend the Sunday morning quite so well as I have , at any rate he have not waste his time . I think he must have rob hen - roost and allotment ground ; and yet he be too respectable for that . but whence and how could he ever have come by the gipsey collection he always produce from his hat , from his countless pocket , from his red cotton handkerchief , every Sunday at 1 P.M. ? egg , chicken , mushroom , stick of horseradish and celery , misletoe - thrush , cucumber , cabbage red and white , rabbit , watercress , Aylesbury duck - I can not remember one quarter of his manifold forage . all I can say be , that if these thing be to be find by the side of the road near London , Middlesex be a far well field for the student of natural history than Gloucestershire , or even beloved Devon . Mrs. Shelfer say it be all his luck ; but I hardly think it could have rain Aylesbury duck , even for Mr. Shelfer . 
 all the time he be extract from his recess this multifarious store , he never once smile , or show any symptom of triumph , but gravely go through the whole , as if a simple duty . 
 how be it such a man have not make his fortune ? because he have an incurable habit of " back bill " for any one who ask he ; and hence he be always in trouble . 
 Mrs. Shelfer and I be admit readily into the school of design . it be a long low room , very badly light , and fit up for the time until a well could be provide . it look very cold and comfortless ; form instead of chair , and desk like a parish school . the whitewashed wall be hang with diagram , section , tracing , reduction , most of they stiff and ugly , but no doubt instructive . at one end be a raise platform , reserve for lecturer and the high power . shelf round the wall be fill with cast and model , and book of instruction be to be have out of cupboard . of course we be expect to bring our own material , and a code of rule be exhibit . the more advanced student be permit to tender any work of their own which might be of service to the neophyte . from no one there do I ever receive any insolence . at first , the young artist use to look at I rather hard , but my reserved and distant air be quite enough to discourage they . 
 after the introduction , which Mrs. Shelfer accomplish in very great style , I dismiss she , and set to in earnest to pore once more over the rudiment of perspective . one simple truth as to the vanish point strike I at once . I be amazed that I have never perceive it before . it be not set forth in the book I be study ; but it be the sole key to all my error of distance . at once I close the book ; upon that one subject I want no more instruction , I have catch the focus of truth . book , like bad glass , would only refract my perception . all I want now be practice and adaptation of the eye . 
 strange as it seem to I then , I could draw no more that day . I be so overcome at first sight by the simple beauty of truth , mathematical yet poetical truth , that error and obscurity   for there be a balance in all thing   have their revenge for a while on my brain . but the truth , once see , could never be lose again . Thenceforth there be few high penance for I , in a small way , than to look at one of my early drawing . 
 when my brain be clear , I return to do a real day 's work . for the cup , and vas , and plate , and thing of " asthetic art "   as they choose to call it , I do not care at all ; but the copy and model and figure be most useful to I . unless I be much mistaken , I make more advance in a fortnight there , than I have in any year of my life before . 
 with my usual perseverance - if I have no other virtue , I have that - I work away to correct my many shortcoming ; not even indulge   much as I want the money   in any attempt at a finished drawing , until I feel sure that all my foundation be thoroughly lay and set . " and now , " I cry towards Christmas , " now for Mr. Oxgall ; if I do not   astonish he this time , my name be not Clara Vaughan ! " it do I good when I be alone , to call myself by my own name , and my right to be my father 's daughter . 
 CHAPTER XIV . 
 meanwhile old Christmas be come , and all I be worth in the world be change for half a sovereign . true , my lodging be pay for , a fortnight in advance , because good Mrs. Shelfer want to treat all her pet to a Christmas dinner ; but as for my own Christmas dinner - though I can not   say I care much for it - if I get one at all , it must be upon credit , since my drawing would not be finish for another week . credit , of course , I would not think of . any day in the week or year , I would rather starve than owe money . however , I be not go to cry about plum - pudding , though once or twice it make I hungry to think of the dinner in the great hall at Vaughan Park on the Christmas eve ; a much more elaborate matter in the old time , than the meal serve in the dining - room next day . 
 now I sit in my little room this dreary Christmas eve ; and do what I would , I could not help think a little . it be a gusty evening , cold and damp , with scud of sleet and snow , as yet it have not make up its mind whether to freeze or thaw . nevertheless , the street be full of merry laugh party , proud of their bargain for the Christmas cheer ; and as they go by , the misletoe and the holly glisten in the flickering gaslight . 
 for old recollection 's sake , I have make believe to dress my little room with some few sprig of laurel and unberrie holly ; the sceptre branch , all cobbe with coral bead , be too expensive for I . Misletoe I want not . who be there now to kiss I ? 
 from the sheer craving of human nature for a word of kindness , I have call , that afternoon , upon Mrs. Elton . but good as she be and sweet to I , she have near relative come ; and I see or fancy , that I should be in the way . yet I think that her mother heart yearn toward I as she say " good bye , " and show I out by the Christmas tree , all tremble to be light . 
 now I sit alone and lonely by the flickering of three pennyworth of wood which I have buy recklessly for the sake of the big ash - tree that use to glow with the lichen peel round it on the old Christmas hearth , where I be believe the heiress . the little spark and sputter of my sallow billet   chop by the poor old people at St. Pancras workhouse   lead I back through eight sad year to the last merry time when my father be keep his late Christmas , and I his pride and hope be prouder than all , at be just ten year old . 
 how he carve and ladle the gravy ; how he flourish his knife and fork with a joke all hot for every one ; how he smile when the thrice - help farmer send for another slice , and laugh when the crow - boy be nearly choke with plum - pudding ; how he pat I on the head and catch I for a kiss , when I , dress up as head - waitress , with my long hair all tie back , pull his right arm and point to widow Hiatt 's plate - the speech he make after dinner , when I be amazed at his eloquence and clap my little hand , and the way he make I stand up on a chair and drink the Queen 's health first - then the hurrah of the tenant and servant , and how they kiss I outside - all this go through my memory as the smoke of the billet go up the chimney , and the tear steal under my eyelid . 
 then I see the long hall afterwards , with the table clear away and the light hang round the tapestry , and the yule log roaring afresh ; my father   a type of the true english gentleman , not of the past but the present century , hold the hand of his wife   a lady of no condescending air , but true womanly warmth and love   - both dress for the tenant ' ball as if for the lord - lieutenant 's ; both eager to lead off the country dance , and beat their foot to the music . next they , a laugh child in a little white frock and pink slip   scarce to be know for myself , hand - in - hand with my brave chevalier , Master Roderick Blount , account by Cooky and both lady' - maid , and most of all by himself , my duly affiance lord . 
 then the housekeeper , starch beyond measure , yet not too stiff to smile , and open for the nonce even to joke about courtship , yield her gracious hand for the dance to the senior tenant , a man with great calf , red face , and snow - white hair . after they come - 
 Hark ! a loud knock and a ring . it be just in time before I begin the palinode . who can want I to - night ? I want no one but those I can not have , whom the fire have now restore I , though the earth have hide they . 
 Mrs. Shelfer be hard at work in the kitchen , prepare a wonderful supper for Charley , who have promise to come home . she have canvass the chance of his keep this promise fifty time in the day . hope cry " yes ; " experience whisper " no . " at any rate the knock be not his , for he always carry a latch - key . 
 she call up the stair " Miss Valence ! " before she go to the door , for who know but she might be murder in the midst of her Christmas pudding ? I come out to prove my existence and stand in the dark on the landing . she draw back the bolt ; I hear a gruff voice as if it come through a hat . 
 " Young ' ooman by the name of Clara Waun live here ? " 
 " yes to be sure ; Miss Valence you mean , my good friend . " 
 " the name on this here ticket be not   Walence , but Waun . " 
 " all right , my good friend . all right . it be just the same . " 
 " Hor , I do not   know that though . Jim , the name of the party here be not   waun after all . it be Walence . and three bless day we have be all over London ! " 
 Jim , from the top of the van , suggest that , after all , Walence and Waun be much of a muchness . for his part , he will be bless if he will go any far with it . let he and Ben look at the young lady , and see if she be like the card . meanwhile , of course , I come forward and claim the parcel , whatever it be . Mrs. Shelfer redouble her assurance , and call the man a great oaf , which have more effect than anything . 
 " why , Jim , this must be Charley 's missus ; Charley Shelfer 's missus ! he as beat you so at skittle last week , you know . " 
 " ah , he do so . and I would like to back he again you , Ben , for a quart all round . " 
 this fact be decisive . who can doubt any more ? but for all that , the book must be sign in the name of " Waun , " with which of course I comply . when the two strong man have , with much difficulty   of which they make much more , lower the enormous package from the van , Ben stand wipe his forehead . " Lor , how hot it be to - night to be sure ! and the job we have have with this big lump sure ly ! both the handle come off long ago . I wish my missus have get a featherbe half the weight of that . five - and - twenty year I have   be along of this company , man and boy , but I never see such a direction as that there in all my bear day . do ever you , Jim ? " 
 " well , " reply Jim , " I have   seed a many queer one , but none as could come up to that . and who have a thought after all their trouble - for I be bless if they write that there under a week - who have a ' thought they have a put ' waun ' on it when they mean ' Walence . ' but the young lady be await for we to drink her health , Ben , and a merry Christmas to she . " 
 " how much be the carriage ? " I ask , tremble for my change of the half - sovereign . 
 " nothing , miss . only eightpence for delivery . it be pay to Paddington , and if ever our company airne eightpence , I be bless if they have not   airne it now . thank you , Miss , and werry handsome on you , and we hop the content will prove to your liking , Miss , and make you a merry Christmas . " 
 away they go with the smoking horse , after carry into the little kitchen the mighty maun , which Mrs. Shelfer , with my assistance , could not stir . 
 " bless I , Miss Valence , what a direction ! " cry Mrs. Shelfer , when the full light fall upon it . 
 the direction be write in round hand upon a strip of parchment , about four inch wide and at least eight foot in length . it come from the bottom all up over the cover and down upon the other side , so that no one could open the basket without break it asunder . it be as follow : - 
 " Miss Clara Vaughan lodge at number seven in Prince Albert Street in London town near Windsor Castle in Gloucestershire the daughter of Mr. Henry Valentine Vaughan Esquire a nice tall young lady she always wear black thing and walk very peart pale with a little red on her cheek when they let she alone can not   be no mistake without it be do a purpose if so be this here little maun hain't   bring to her safe and sweet and wholesome will be prosecute with the utmost rigour of the law sign John Huxtable his mark x witness Timothy Badcock his'n x. " 
 I wonder much whether Mr. Beany Dawe have be call in to achieve this masterpiece of manuscript , which be all in large round hand , but without any stop . it seem beyond poor Sally 's art , yet be some loop and downstroke that must be dear little Sally 's . I take it off with much trouble - the parchment be join in four place - and I have it now . 
 meanwhile Mrs. Shelfer be dance around it , neglect her supper in the wonder of this gigantic hamper . " let I get a chopper , Miss , you will never get it open . why it be sew as tight as an oyster . " 
 however , I do get it open at last , and never shall I forget the content . there be a month 's food for a family of twelve . first come hay , such as I never smell out of Devonshire ; then eighteen roll of butter , each with a snowy cloth around it ; the butter so golden even at that time of year , that Mrs. Shelfer compare it to the yolk of an egg look out of the white . then a storey of clot cream and beautiful lard and laver , which they know I love . then a floor of hay . below it a pair of guinea fowl , two large turkey , and most carefully wrap from the rest a fine hare fill with dry sweet herb . below these a flitch of bacon , two wood - smoke ham , a pair of tongue , a leg of Exmoor mutton , and three bottle of good elder wine . then a brown paper parcel contain Sally 's last copy - book   I have set her copy for half a year to come   and a long letter , the first I have ever receive from Tossil 's Barton . 
 when all be out at last , after the great delight and laughter as each thing appear , I fall back in utter dismay at the spectacle before I . Mrs. Shelfer sit on the floor unable to find her way out , she be so flounced and tippete with good thing . when I come to her relief , she do nothing but go round and round what be leave of the little room , hum a catholic hymn , and press both hand to her side . 
 but something must be do at once . Waste be wickedness ; how could we stave it off ? everything would depend upon the weather . at present all be beautifully fresh , thank to the skilful packing and the frost , albeit the mighty package have make the round of all the Albert Streets in London . Mrs. Shelfer would have look at it for a month , and at interval exclaim , " bless I , my good friend , that beat Charley 's pocket . how they must eat in Devonshire ! " 
 " come , Mrs. Shelfer , what good be you at housekeeping ? you do not   help I at all . let we put most of it out of door at once . you have no cellar , and I suppose they have none in London . at least we can give it the chance of the open air , and it be not snow now . " 
 " oh , but the cat , Miss ! " 
 " well , I must find some plan for they before we go to bed . now come and help , that be a good little creature , and I will give you some eld wine when we have do . " 
 so we get all that be taintable into the little yard , while Tom , who never steal , except when quite sure of impunity , look on very sagely . there we fix it all up to the wall secure , except from cat , of whom a roving band serenade I every night . I present Mrs. Shelfer at once with a turkey - a speciman of natural history not find by the roadside , even on Mr. Shelfer 's Sabbath journey - also a ham , and three roll of butter . as to the rest , I would think what to do with it afterwards . 
 Mrs. Shelfer keep off the cat until midnight , after which I hold they at bay by the follow mean . with one of my mineral paint mingle with some phosphorus , I draw upon a black board a ferocious terrier , the size of life , with fang unsheathe , bristle erect , and eye start out of his head . we try the effect in the dark on poor Tom , who arch his back , and sputter with the strong execration , then turn and flee ignobly , amid roar of laughter from Mr. Shelfer , who by this time be come home . this one - head Cerberus be hang so as to oscillate in the wind , right across the cat - leap , I feel quite safe , so long as my chemical mixture should continue luminous . 
 CHAPTER XV . 
 dear little Sally 's letter give I the great delight . it be all in round hand , and have take at least a week to write , and she must have wash her hand almost every time . there be no stop in it , but I have put some . the spelling be wonderfully good for she , but here and there I have shape it to the present fashion . 
 " please Miss Clara dear , father and mother and I beg their most respectable duty and love and they hop no offence and will you be so kind as to have this here little hamper and wish it be ten time as much but hop you will excuse it and please to eat it all yourself Miss. all the pegmate be our own doctrine , and very wholesome , and we have take all the hair off , please Miss , because you say one time you do not   like it . likely you will remember , Miss , the young black sow as twist her tail to the left , she as Tim be ring the day as I write first copy , and the other chiller run out , well most of it be she , Miss. Father say as he do not   think they ever see butter in London town , but Beany Dawe say yes for they make a plenty out of red herring and train oil . 
 please Miss , Tabby Badcock would go on the ice in the old saw - pit last Sunday , by the upper linhay when I tell she it would not bear , and so she fall through and would have be drownde at last , only our little Jack crawl over the postesse and give she his heel to hold on by , and please miss it would have do your heart good , mother say , to see how Tim Badcock dress she when he come home from church for get she good frock all of a muck . 
 please Miss , Beany Dawe come when you be go , and make a poem about you , and father like it so much he give he free of the cider and as he be go home he fall into a bit of a ditch down Breakneck hill , and when he come to himself the road have take to run the wrong way beany do not   know how for the life of he , so he come back here ' nolus wolus ' he saith and that be the way to spell it and no mistake , and here he have be ever since a - making of poem and saw up hellum out of the low cleeve , and he sleepth in the onion loft and Suke can not   have no rest of night for the noise he make make verse . Mother tell Suke to pote he down stair and too good for he , but father say no , he be a fine chap for sure and airneth his meat and drink , let alone all the poetry . 
 please Miss he want to larn I to write , but father say no I have get well learn than hisn , and I say he may learn Tabby Badcock if he will , but he shall not   learn I . no tino . " 
 how she toss her pretty curl when she write this I will be bind . I wish that I could see she . 
 " please Miss I be force to write this when he be away , or he have a make it all in poetry ; and Tim Badcock tell I to be sure to tell you as how at the wrastling to Barnstaple fair , week after you be go , father be so crule unkid that in play off the tie he heave a Cornisher up through the chandelier , and a come down with a candle stick so fast down his throat doctor be force to set it a - fire and blow with a pair of bellis afore he could put he to right . cornisher be all right again now , Tim saith , but he have a make up his mind not to wrastle no more in Devonshire . 
 please Miss , father saith before this here go he will shoot the old hare as sit in the top of the cleeve if Queen Victoria transport he for it with hard labour . Tim have make four pop at she , but he say the powder be crook . 
 please Miss Clara , all the egg as my little black hen have lay , since the last of the barley be house , be to be sew up inside the Turkey with the black comb ; he be strut about in the court and look at I now as peart as a gladdy ; but she have not lay more than a dozen to now , though I have be up and whistle to she in the tall at every morning and evening same as we use to do when you be in good spirit . but the other hen have not lay none at all . 
 please Miss , father say as how he have sell such a many beastie , he be afeared to keep all the money in the house , and he have tell mother to sew up the rent for next Ladyday in the turkey with the white comb when he be kill and he humbly hope no offence . 
 please Miss Clara , we have have three letter from you , and I read they all to father and mother every Sunday evening , and Joe the Queen 's boy do not   know but what he lose another one in leather the jackass across the brook after the rain . Joe tell as he can not   say for certain , because why he baint no scholar the same as we be , and Joe only know the letter by the pin they stick in his sleeve afore he leave Martinhoe . whoever ' twas for he think there be crockery in it by reason it sink so quick . anyhow mother give he a little tap with a mop on the side of his head , to make he mind the Queen 's business , and do not   he holler a bit , and he fling down the parson 's letter all in the muck , but we wash they in a bucket and let parson have they on Sunday . Joe Queen 's boy have not   be nigh we since , and they do say to Martinhoe we should not   have no more letter , but father say if he do not   he will show the man there what a forehip mean pretty smart . 
 please Miss Clara , we would have write afore , but mother say no , not till I finish twelve copybook one every week , that the folk to London town might see the way as they ought to write and spell . Father say London be in Gloucestershire , but I be most sure it baint , and Beany Dawe shake his head and will not   tell , and mother believe he do not   know . 
 please Miss , there be a new babby come a month agone and well , and mother find out as how it be a girl , and please if you have no objection Miss , and if you do not   think as it would be a liberty , we have all make up our mind upon have it christen Clara , and please to say Miss if it be too high , or any way unfitty . Father be ' most afeared that it sound too grand for the like of we , but mother say as the Huxtables be think brave thing on , to Coom and Parracombe a hundred year agone . 
 please Miss , father hear to Coom market last week , as there be go to be a french invasion , and they be sure to go to London first , and he beg you to let he know as soon as ever there be one , and he come up at once with the big ash - stick and the ivy on it as grow in challacombe wood , and see as they do not   hurt you , Miss. 
 please Miss , the young chap as save you from the great goyal come here to ask for you , day after you be go , and mother believe he baint after no good , by token he would not come in nor drink a drop of cider . 
 please Miss , father say it make his heart ache every night , to think of you all to yourself in the wicked London town , and he go down the lane to the white gate every evening in the hope to see you acome , and mother say if you be a sell red and blue pictur her hope you will send for they as father give the hog 's pudden for , and we will not miss they at all . 
 and Miss Clara dear , I expect you will be mazed to see how I write and spell , father say it must be in the family , and I will not   write no more till I have finish another dozen of copy book ; and oh dear how I do wish that you be come back again , but father say to I to say no more about it for fear to make you cry , Miss. all the little childer except the new babby who have not see you yet , send their heart ' love and duty and a hundred kiss , and father and mother the same , and Timothy Badcock , and Tabby , and Suke , and Beany Dawe , now he know it . 
 I remain , Miss Clara dear , your thankful and love scholar to command , 
 sarah huxtable . 
 sign all this here papper scrawl in the settle by the fire . 
 JOHN HUXTABLE his mark X 
 HONOR huxtable hern X. " 
 CHAPTER XVI . 
 I be much grieve at the loss of my last letter to Tossil 's Barton , because it contain my little Christmas present for all the family . it be register for security , but I suppose they " take no count " of that where the delivery of letter depend so much upon luck . of their Christmas present to I I resolve to give the surplus to those who would be the well for it , and not   accord to the usual law of such thing   to those who do not want it , and would make return with interest . so on the Christmas morning Mrs. Shelfer and myself , each carry a large basket , go to the mew round the corner , and distribute among the poor lodger there , more Christmas dinner than have ever enter those door before ; and how grateful the poor thing be , only they all want the good . 
 now the school of design be close for a while , and I work hard for several day at the landscape for Mr. Oxgall , though the store of provision send I and the rent enclose in the turkey have save I from present necessity . 
 on the day of all day in the year the sad and darkest to I , I could not keep to my task , but go for a change of thought to the school , now open again . 
 it be the 30th of December , 1850 , and , though I crouch not to the mumming of prig scollop out at the throat , who block out with a patchwork screen the simple hearth of religion , and kneel at an ashbin to warm themselves ; though I do not   care a herring for small anniversary dot all over the calendar , and make by some Murphy of old ; yet I reverence deeply the true feast of Church and Chapel , the refreshing of faith and charity , whereupon we forgive and be sorry for those who work hard to mar they . neither do it seem to I - so far as my timid and waver judgment extend - to be superstition or vanity , if we dare to set mark by those date in our own little span which God have scar on our memory . 
 in the long dark room so bare and comfortless , and , to - day , so lonely and cold , I get my usual book and study , and try , all in vain , to fix my attention on they . find the effort so fruitless , I pack up my thing in the little black bag and rise to depart . turn round , I see on the table , where student ' work be exhibit , a small object newly place there . it be a statuette in white marble of a magnificent red deer , such as I have see once or twice in the north of Devon . the listening attitude , the turn of the neck , the light poise of the massive head , even the mild , yet spirit eye , and the quiver sensitive lip , I could answer for they all , they be do to the very life . truth , power , and elegance triumph in every vein of it . for a minute I stand overcome with wonder . if this be the work of a youthful sculptor , England might hope at last for something beyond the grotesque . 
 before I rise at once all the woodland scenery , the hill - side garb with every shade of green , the bramble quarry stand forth , the tree , the wind vale embosome the light , the haze that hover above the watersmeet , bold crest of amaranth heath behind , and far away the russet wold of Exmoor . the stag in the foreground of my landscape , I feel so grateful to he for this expanse of vision that I stoop down and kiss he , while no one can see I . as I bend , the gordit drop from its warm home in my breast . by some impulse undefined I lift the ribbon from my neck , and hang the little fairy 's heart on the antler of the Devonshire deer . out spring from behind a chest full of cast and model - what model can compare with she ? - the lovely of all lovely being , my little Isola Ross . 
 I hide the tear in my eye , and try to look cold and reserved . what use be it ? one smile of hers would have disarm Belial . 
 " it be not   my fault , dear . it be not   indeed . oh , please give I that cordetto . no do not . that be why I love you so at first sight . and here be all my money dear . I have carry it about ever since , though I sew up the purse not to spend it , and only once cut it open . they make I promise , and I would not eat for three day , and I try to be sulky with Pappy because he do not care ; they make I promise with all my honour not to go and see you , and Cora come about with I so that I have no chance of break it . and I would not tell they where you live , dear ; but I lead old Cora a dance through your street on the side you live , till she begin to suspect . but I could never see you , though I look in at all the window till I be quite ashamed , and the people kiss their hand to I . " 
 poor little dear ! I live upstairs , and could not have see she without stand out on the balcony , which be about the size of a chess - board . if she have not be so simple as to walk on my side of the street , she must have see I ere long , for I sit all day near the window to draw , when I be not away at my school . 
 I forgive she most graciously for have do I no wrong , and kiss she with all my heart . her breath be as sweet as violet in Spring clover , and her lip warm and soft as a wren 's nest . on receive my forgiveness , away she go dance down the long room , with her cloak throw off , and her hair toss all out of braid , and her exquisite buoyant figure float as if on a cloud . of course there be no one there , or even impulsive Isola would hardly have take her frolic ; and yet I be not sure . she never think harm of any one , and never imagine that any one could think harm of she . 
 after a dozen flit of some rapid elegant dance quite unknown to I   who have never have much of dancing , but which I suppose to be Scotch , back she come out of breath , and kiss I ever so many time , and kiss my gordit too , and tell I never to part with it . one thing she be sure of , that her Papa could not resist I now , and when he be tell of it I should come to their house the next day . and she know I be dreadfully proud , but would I , for her sake , forgive her Pappy ? of course , he know nothing about I , and she have never tell he my name , though she could not help tell my story , at least all she know of it ; but he be so dreadfully jealous of she , he do not want any one to have a touch of her glove but himself . 
 look at her pure sweet face , I could well believe it ; but how could he bear to see that dear little thing go three day without food ? most likely she have exaggerate . although she be truthful as light , sometimes her quick fancy and warmth , like the sunshine itself , would bring out some point too strongly . however , I be prepare , without that , to dislike the Professor , for , as a general rule , I do not   like man who moralise ; at least if their philosophy be frigid . nevertheless , I promise very readily to forgive her Papa , for I do so love that Isola . her nature be so different to mine , so light and airy , elastic and soft ; in short   if I must forsake my language , the complement of my own . we chat , or rather she do , for at least half an hour ; and then she tell I old Cora be come to fetch she at three o'clock . once more I rise to depart , for I fear she might get into trouble , if the old nurse should find she so intimate with a stranger . 
 but Isola tell I that she do not care for she a bit , and she have quite set her heart on my meeting her brother Conrad , the sculptor of that magnificent stag . perhaps he would come with Cora , but he be so altered now , she could never tell what he would do . since the time she first see I , Conrad have come of age , and she could not guess what it be all about , but there have be a dreadful disturbance between he and his father , and he have actually go to live away from the family . she think it must be about money , or some such nasty thing ; but even Cora do not know , or if she do , the old thing would not tell . it have make poor Isola cry till her eye be sore , but now she suppose she must make up her mind to it all . but she would tell the truth , she do hate be treat like a baby when she be a full - grow woman ; how much tall do they expect she to be ? and what be much bad , she do want so to comfort they both , and how could she do it without know what be the matter ? it be too bad , and she wish she be a boy , with all her heart she do . 
 she go on talk like this till her gentle breast flutter , and her coral lip quiver , and the tear steal down her long lash , and she creep to I close for comfort . 
 I be clasp her round little waist , and kiss the bright drop away , when in burst a dark , scraggy woman , who must , of course , be old Cora . she tear the poor child from my arm , and scowl at I fiercely enough to frighten a girl unacquainted with real terror . 
 I meet her dark gaze with a calm contempt , beneath which it quail and fall . she mumble some word in a language or patois , which I suppose to be Gaelic , and lead off her charge towards the door . 
 she have mistake her adversary . be I to be push aside , like a gingerbread woman tempt a weak - stomach child ? I pass they ; then turn and confront the hag . 
 " have the goodness , old woman , to walk behind this young lady and I . when we want your society , we will ask for it . Isola Ross , come with I , unless you prefer a rude menial 's tyranny to a lady 's affection . " 
 Isola be too frightened to speak . I know not what would have be the result , if the old hag , who be glare about , rather take aback , but still clutch that delicate arm , have not suddenly spy my fairy 's heart , as yet unrestored to its sanctuary . 
 she star , for a moment , in wide amazement ; then her whole demeanour be alter . she cringe , and fawn , and curtsey , as if I have wear a tiara . she drop my dear Isola 's arm , and fall behind like a negress . my poor little pet be tremble and cold with fright , for   as she tell I afterwards   she have never see old Cora in such a passion before , and the superstitious darling dread the evil eye . 
 as we go towards Isola 's home , I could not help think how fine the interview would be between Mrs. Shelfer and Cora , if I only choose to carry that vanquished beldame thither ; but sage discretion   be I not now eighteen ? , and the thought of that solemn day prevent I . so I take they straight home , lead Isola while she guide I , and turn sometimes , with complacency , to encourage old Cora behind we . 
 the house they live in be a high but narrow one , dull - looking and dark , with area rail in front . some little maiden come to the door , and I take my leave on the step . Dear Isola , now in high spirit again , kiss I , like a peach quite warm in the sun , and promise to come the next day , about which there could now be no difficulty . 
 Old Cora bent low as she wish I good evening and beg leave to kiss my cordetto . this I grant , but take good care not to let it pass out of my hand ; she admire it so much , especially when allow to examine it , and there be such a greedy light in her eye , that I be quite sure she would steal it upon the first chance ; and therefore I go straightway and buy a guard of thick silk cord , as a substitute for the black riband , which be get wear . 
 and so I come home before dark , full of wonder , but feel rather triumphant , and greatly delighted at have recover dear Isola . 
 END of VOL . I. 
 BOOK II .   continue . 
 CHAPTER XVII . 
 late in the evening of that same day , I sit in my room by the firelight only   for I could not work   and try to look into myself , and find out the cause of my strange attraction or rather impulsion towards Isola . somehow or other I do not wonder so much that she should be draw quite as strongly towards I , although an impartial observer would perhaps have wonder far more . Alter puzzle myself in vain with this inquiry , my thought begin to move , in their usual gloomy train . 
 eight year have now elapse , and what have I discover ? nothing ; but at long dark interval some impress of the deed itself , more than of the doer . have I halt in pursuit , or have my vengeance cool ? to the former question my conscience answer " yes , " to the latter " no . " gentle influence have be shed around I , sorrow have bedew the track of hate , intercourse with happy harmless people , and gratitude for unmerited kindness ; it be not in human nature , however finely constitute for evil , entirely to repulse these power . 
 I could not deny , that the religion of my heart , during the last twelvemonth , have be somewhat neglect . for my devotion to dear mother , no plea be require . but the time since that , what business have I with laugh child , and snug fireside , with dickybird , and Sandy the squirrel ? even sweet Isola cause I a pang of remorse ; but no , I could not quite abandon she . but now , thank God , I be in the right road again , and plod resolutely as my father could expect . to his spirit , ever present with I , I kneel down and pour out my remorse ; and swear to make amend , whatever it might cost I . yet even then , a gentle shadow seem to come as well , and whisper the word that calm the face of death . 
 my musing , if so mild a word may suit they , be roughly interrupt by a loud step on the stair . Inspector Cutting , who could walk when needful like a cat , love to redress this injury to the Goddess Echo , by make double noise when not on business . farmer Huxtable , a man of twice the weight , would have come up those stair at half the expense in sound . 
 when he enter the room , he find himself in a semi - official state again , and I see that he be not come for nothing . in a few brief word , he tell I what he have do , which be not very much ; or perhaps my suspicion be right , that he only tell I a part of it . then he say abruptly , 
 " Miss Valence , I know pluck when I see it . " 
 " what do you mean , Mr. Cutting ? " 
 " excuse I , I forget that you have be rear in the country . what I mean be , that I believe you possess an unusual share of courage . " 
 " as to that , I can not say , have never be severely try ; but in such a cause as mine , I could go through a good deal . " 
 " and not lose your presence of mind , even in real danger ? " 
 " that again I can not say , and for the same reason . but I be quite ready to make the trial . " 
 I feel the colour mount in my cheek . how glad I should be to prove to myself that I be not ignoble . he observe I closely , and appear quite satisfied . 
 " what I have to propose to you , be attend with no little danger . " 
 " I will do my utmost not to be afraid . I be more impulsive perhaps than brave , but what be life worth to I ? I will try to think of that all the time . no doubt you have a good reason for expose I to danger . " 
 " certainly I have , Miss Valence . for your own purpose it be most important that you should be able to identify certain person , whom I shall show you to - night ; that be , unless I be misinformed . " 
 " to - night ! so late as this ? " and I begin to tremble already . 
 " yes , we must go to - night , or wait for another fortnight ; and then it would be no early , even if we get such a chance again . and for your sake it be well than to be in a fright for a fortnight . " 
 " Inspector Cutting , I be in no fright whatever . at least I mean no more so than any other girl would be , who feel a vague danger impend . I hope and trust that my father 's memory and the justice of God will be with I . " 
 " young lady , I see that I may safely venture it . if you have boast , I should have hesitate , though I have have some proof already of your determination . the chief , and indeed the only danger , be lest you lose your presence of mind , and that most female would do , if place as you will be . now I wish you to make deliberate choice , and not to be carry away by impulse vindictiveness , or the love of adventure ; which , when the spirit be high like your , too often lead young female into trouble , from which it be not always possible even for the most capable member of the force to extricate they . " 
 " of course I know all that . how much long be we to talk ? must I disguise myself ? when be I to be ready ? and where be we go ? " 
 " now you be grow impatient . that be not a good sign . remember , I can easily procure another witness ; but for your own sake I wish to give you the chance . probably you will see to - night the man who kill your father . " 
 as he speak my flesh be creep , and my blood run cold , then suddenly flush through my system like electric fluid . he begin again as coolly as if he be report a case of some one discover " drunk and incapable . " from force of habit , he touch his forehead , and stand at attention , as he speak . " in consequence of information which I have receive , I have be induce to make certain inquiry , which have result in the conviction that the criminal I be in search of will be present at a certain place this night , at a certain hour . it be therefore my intention to embrace the opportunity of - " 
 " catch he ! " cry I in a breathless hurry . 
 " to embrace the opportunity , " continue the Inspector , like a talk oak , " of conduct my investigation personally , and in the presence of a witness . the effect thereby produce upon my mind shall be enter duly , the moral effect I should have say , and the cause of justice will be promote as rapidly as be consistent with the principle of our glorious constitution . " 
 " do you mean to say that you will let he go ? " 
 " no , I shall not let he go , Miss Valence , for the simple reason that I shall not apprehend he . I see that you be inclined to take the law into your own hand . that will never do for I . " 
 " oh no , I be not . a year ago I would have do so . but I be old and wise now . " 
 I be think of dear mother ; and begin to feel already that my character be change . 
 CHAPTER XVIII . 
 Inspector Cutting give I some minute instruction , and in less than half an hour we set forth upon our enterprise . I be wrap in a loose grey cloak have a hooded cape ; and carefully hide I carry for self - defence a very keen stiletto . I have procure it indirectly from the good cutler in London , but neither workmanship nor material could be compare to that of Italy . 
 the night be dark and cold , the street be almost deserted , and all the shop except the chemist ' and the public - house close . we walk straightway to the near cabstand , where Mr. Cutting order a vehicle , and put I inside , himself rid with the driver . so little do I know as yet of London , that after the first turn or two , I could not even guess what direction we be take . I have such confidence in my guide , a staid respectable man with a grow up family , that I never think there could be harm in my journeying with he at night . and even have I think so , most likely I should have do it all the same . ever since the time he wound I , or allow I to wound myself , his manner towards I have be most kind , considerate , and respectful ; though he find it his duty now and then to repress my impetuosity . 
 with all my perception alert , I keep a sharp look - out from the window , but vainly strove to find anything that might serve for a landmark . once we stop for about five minute , at a police - station somewhere in Clerkenwell , where , by the light of a lamp , I read , without leave the cab , the ghastly description of all the dead body recently find in London and wait identification . Hereupon my courage begin to ooze , and the weather seem much colder . the type be hard to read at that distance , and the imagination have fair play , as it do when word come slowly . 
 Anon the inspector reappear , so alter in dress and countenance , that I do not know he until he make I a bow . with a glance of encouragement , and a little grin of dry humour , he mount the box again . after another long drive , in the course of which we run silently over a wooden road , - probably High Holborn , - we stop in a broad but deserted thoroughfare , very badly light . here Mr. Cutting open the door , help I out , and discharge the cab , but whisper something to the driver before he let he go . 
 " now take my arm , Miss Valence , if you please . I have escort many a lady of high birth than your . " 
 " of high title perhaps , Mr. Cutting ; and their grandfather money - lender , or perhaps far bad . " 
 " I be sure I do not   know ; we must take thing as we find they . I think you despise such nonsense . but the cabbage that run to seed be the tall in the field . no Englishman see the nonsense of it , unless he happen to be a detective or a grave - digger . " 
 " do you mean to say that those of lofty birth be bad than those of low birth ? " 
 " no , I mean nothing of the sort . but I do mean that they ought to be well , and on the whole be not so . nature hold the balance , and temptation and education chuck into the opposite scale , and I think the first chuck fast . at any rate I would rather have a good drunken navvy than a lord to take to the station . I mean of course when my own rank be not what it be . " 
 this little dissertation be mean to divert my thought . I make no reply , be ignorant of such matter ; neither do I care to talk about they then . nevertheless , I believe Inspector Cutting be wrong . as we enter a narrow street he suddenly turn and look at I . 
 " poor child ! how you tremble ! draw your cape more forward ; the bitter cold require it . be you tremble from fear ? " 
 " no ; only from cold . " but I try in vain to think so . 
 " a steady hand and steadfast nerve be want for your task . if you can not rely on they , say so at once . in five minute you will have no retreat . " 
 " I shall be well directly . but I be so cold . Inspector Cutting , it must be freeze hard - ten degree , I should think . " 
 " it do not freeze at all . I see we must warm you a little . but no more ' Inspector Cutting , ' if you please , until to - morrow . " 
 Hereupon he lead I into a little room , fence off from the bar of some refreshment - house . a glorious fire be burn , by which he set and leave I . presently he return , with a small glass in his hand . 
 " drink this , young lady . it will warm you , and brace your nerve . " 
 I see by the firelight that it be brandy , or some dark - coloured spirit . 
 " no , I thank you . do you suppose that I require dutch courage ? " 
 I throw such emphasis on the personal pronoun , and look at he so indignantly , that he laugh outright . 
 " I thank you in turn . you suppose that I do . I will justify your discernment . " and with that he tip it off , and then return to business , all the graver for the interlude . 
 " now , if you be really warm , we will start again . stop one moment . I have hear you cough two or three time . can you keep it under ? " 
 I assure he that I could very easily do so , and that it be nothing but the sudden effect of the cold . forth we go again into the winter night , after I have learn from he that we be now in Whitechapel , not far from Goodman 's Fields . 
 after another short walk , we come to the end of a narrow by - street , where there be an archway . pass through this archway , we descend some steep and broken step . then the Inspector produce a small lamp brightly burning , which he must have light at the public - house . it be not what be call a bull's - eye , but a reflector - lamp . by its light I see that the chief entrance to the house must be round the corner , and perhaps in another street . with a small key which he take from his pocket , Mr. Cutting unlock a little iron gate , and we enter a narrow passage . at the end of it be a massive door stud with great nail . here my guide give a gentle knock , and hide the lamp as before . 
 presently we hear a shrill sound from the keyhole , like a dryad 's voice . the Inspector stoop thereto , and pronounce the password . not without some difficulty the lock be turn and the bolt withdraw , and we stand inside . a child , under - sized and unnaturally sharp , star at we for a moment , then dodge away from the lamp , as if more accustomed to darkness . Mr. Cutting close the door and refastene it , then lead I through some basement room unpaved and unfurnished , until we come to an iron step - ladder . this he ascend , and help I up , and we find ourselves in a small dark lobby , contain no furniture , except a high three - legged stool . when he close his lamp all around be dark , but on the rafter overhead a faint patch of light appear - ceiling there be none . 
 " do you see that light ? " he whisper to I , point , as I could just perceive , to a narrow glaze opening high in the wall , whence the faint gleam proceed . 
 " then jump upon this stool , and do your good to see through . " 
 he cast the light of his lamp upon the stool for a moment , while I do as he bid I . stand there , I find that I be tall enough to look through ; but the narrow pane which form the window be thickly cover with size , or some opaque integument . all I could tell be , that the space beyond be light . 
 " I know you can not   see now , " he say , as I come down despairing , " but you shall see by and by . the fool who be here before sized the glass on the wrong side , and this lot , though much sharp , have not correct the error . they keep that window for escape in the last resort . now take this bottle and this camel's - hair brush ; it will make the glass transparent without the small noise . the man be not there yet . we could easily rub it clear now , but they will examine it . when the time come , use the liquid most carefully and lightly , and do not   spread it high than an inch from the bottom of the frame . the light be at this end ; the shadow of the sill will allow you just an inch . " 
 " and how far may I go horizontally ? " 
 " the whole length of the glass , to command as much view as possible . the effect will pass in three or four minute , but you must not do it again . if you do , the glass will fly , and you will be in their hand . desperate man they be , and though I shall be near , I might be too late to save you . see all you can , to be able to swear to they all . " 
 " how shall I know the one ? " 
 " I can not tell you . I must leave it to your instinct , or your intuition . I only know myself that he be one of the four . my information , such as it be , be obtain very oddly , and I trust to this night 's work to make it more precise . one thing more : no noise , if you value your life . keep the bottle stop . do not   let the stuff drop on you ; do not   put your eye to it , or it will blind you for ever . there be very little of it , because it be so deadly . " 
 " when shall I do it ? " 
 " in one hour from this time . take this repeater . I have show you how to use it . look well at it now , while you have the light . " 
 I look at the watch ; it be nearly midnight . 
 " be I to be leave in the dark - all in the dark here , by myself ? " 
 " yes . I must be see elsewhere , or the whole thing fail . they know I even in this dress , and they watch I as I do they . but for to - night I believe I have mislead they . when it be over , wait here till I come for you , or the little girl you see . " 
 " oh ! I wish I have never come ; and all so vague and indecisive ! " 
 " you can go back now , if you please ; though ever that would be dangerous . " 
 " I will not go back . no doubt I shall know he . when will you secure he ? " 
 " when my evidence be complete . now , remember , you have to deal with man keen as hawk , and stealthy as tiger . but there be no real danger , if you keep your self - command . observe all four as narrowly as you can , both for your own sake and for mine . be careful to stand on the centre of the stool . but you have well not get upon it until they have search the room . now , good - bye . I trust to your courage . if any harm come , I will avenge you . " 
 " a comfort that ! what good will it be to I ? " 
 " if vengeance be no good , what be you do here ? " 
 " thank you . that be no business of your . do not   let I detain you . " 
 he tell I afterwards that he have vex I on purpose to arouse my mettle . and I be sure I need it . 
 " ah ! now you be all right . if your caution fail you , the man who slay your father will be sure to escape we . " 
 " if it fail I , ' twill be from anger , not from terror . " 
 " I know it . let I look at you . " 
 he throw the full light on my face . the burnished concave be not bright or firm than my eye . 
 " pale as death , and quite as resolute . rely only upon yourself . " 
 " God and myself , " I whisper , as he glide out of sight along the vault below . I could see no other entrance to the place in which I sit ; but how could I tell ? 
 for a minute excitement keep I hot ; but as the last gleam of the light die upon the wall below , my heart begin to throb heavily , and a chill come over I . the pulse thump in my ear , like a knocking in the cellar . " be it fear ? " I ask myself , in scorn that I should ask . no , it be not fear , but horrible suspense . the balance of life and death , of triumph and disgrace , swing there before I in the dark , as if my breath would turn it . no dream of a child , no vagary of the brain - the clear perception of strong will and soul poise upon this moment .
