 " why a nasty stink hussy , that would steal the feather out of my good bed again , the same as they do before . my very good bed , Miss Idols , as dear Miss Minto leave I by her will , not a well bed in London , unless it be the Queen 's , and so I use to tell she when I help to shake it up . my mouth water over it so , that she say one day , and the knife - boy hear she on the stair , ' Patty , you have   be a good girl to I , and you deserve it , and you shall have it , when I be tuck up for good and all . ' and so I do , very honourable , and all above board . yes , yes ; I have a commercial gent one time , a wonderful heavy man to be sure , and he stay with I three year for the sake of that same bed . and he know what bed be , and no mistake . it be bootiful to see when he be a getting up . it begin to rise up , up , the same as Tom 's back , when he see your dog , Miss Idols . " 
 " come , Mrs. Shelfer , I fear we can hardly wait . " 
 " ' twas like dough put afore the fire , Miss. there be no such Dantzic now . you could not   put your fist into one side of it , but out it would come the other . oh Lor , I could cry ; that nasty sly minx , she be soft than parsnip , you would say , and one leg more than the other . I could not   think why it be she would always make her own bed . ' thank you , Mrs. Shelfer ' - with her lip suck in like a button - hole - ' thank you , you be too kind . it do not   at all fatigue I , and my doctor pronounce the exercise good for my chest and arm . ' thank God , she get some exercise good for her leg as well . six month on the treadmill . Charley get I an order , and it do my heart good to see she . but my twenty pound of good feather never come back again , and that be not   the bad of it neither . " 
 " oh dear no , " say Idols , " the bad of it be the sin , Mrs. Shelfer . " 
 " the bad of it be that she stuff it with sawdust , and oakum , and jovanna , I do believe , by the smell of it . " 
 " what do you mean , Mrs. Shelfer ? " 
 " Lor , Miss Valence , do not   you know jovanna that the kingfisher lay on the top of the sea , and the gardener make water with it ? " 
 " and what do she do with your feather ? " 
 " sneak they out of the house in the crown of her bonnet , and sell they at eightpence a pound , and they worth three and sixpence , every flue of they . but the rag and bottleman get two month , thank God for it . ah , it will never be a bed again under 5 l.   at least . " 
 " be it the one I sleep on , Mrs. Shelfer ? " 
 " yes , my good friend , the very same . " 
 " and you have put I to sleep on guano ! well , I think it smell very odd . " 
 " no , no , my good friend , wait a bit . we get most of that out again , and give it to our geranium . she steal it out of a sack as Charley keep in the washhouse . there be feather in it . that put it into her head , I suppose . but as for your going , Miss Valence , that will never do . never , never . will it now , Miss Idols ? and to see her dress , to be sure , that baggage ! why , my good tarlatan , as dear Miss Minto give I to be marry in , be not   good enough for she to sweep the stair in . sweep the stair - yes , yes , she do sweep the stair when I see she last ; and she have afore , I know ; she be so clever at it ; and that be why one leg be so much more than the other . " 
 " Mrs. Shelfer , do you expect we to listen to you all night ? " 
 " true , my good friend , quite true . but when I think of my feather , something come over I , I must out with my trouble , or burst . but you musn't   go , Miss Valence . that will never do , never ; ask Miss Idols now . " and she turn to Isola , who be quite ready to be turn to . 
 " of course it will not , Mrs. Shelfer . you be quite right , my good friend . I will not   hear of it for a moment . why Mr. Shelfer be drunk . I know it by the way he hold his pipe . quite ' drunk and incapable , ' you know . and he will be so sorry , and he will never do it again . and he do not mean to be drunk at all , but the frost be very hard , and the cold get into his head . I be sure it would into mine , if I have stay much long ; and he do not   understand brandy - ball , as we do at College - you could not expect it , you know . " 
 the pure good faith of this last be too much for I . I laugh outright , have no husband concern in it . as for the dry little woman , she actually cry . I have never see a tear in her quick , shy eye before , though the feather - bed nearly bring they , and so do the death of the eld Sandy , the squirrel . she turn away . she be always ashamed of emotion . 
 " bless your innocent heart , Miss Idols , if you do not   marry a king ! not one of we be good enough to tie your shoe as you talk of , you be that simple and good of heart . " 
 be there any goodness more touching to a veteran than a soft young nature 's disbelief in evil ? but for bitter experience , I might have be sweet as Isola . thank God , that in spite of all vinegar , the ailment be still infectious . Isola could not make it all out . 
 " to - morrow morning , Miss Valence , " begin Mrs. Shelfer again , " to - morrow morning , after I have wig he well all night , and then give he a good breakfast , he will come and beg your pardon like a child , and be ashamed to look any high than your flounce ; and I know you will forgive he . " 
 " Mrs. Shelfer , I have forgive he long ago . I can not bear enmity against such people " - these last three word have well be away - " for such little wrong . and I owe you a great deal for all your kindness to I . the only question be , whether self - respect and prudence allow I to stay here . I will leave the decision to Miss Isola . young as she be , and innocent and confide , she can not be wrong on a question of delicacy . as for prudence , she know more of London than I do . " 
 Hereon I sit down with a womanly air . but I could hardly help laugh when the senior sophist jump up , proud to deliver judgment . to look tall , she shake her flounce down , throw back her plump white shoulder - her bonnet and cloak be off - draw her rich flow hair down the pearly curve of her ear and , scarcely satisfied yet , think of mount a stool , then take her foot off the too convictive bema . after all these anabolism , she begin with much solemnity . she be think of the College , and her father in the rostrum . 
 " Miss Valence and Mrs. Shelfer , since you have honour my weak judgment by appoint I umpire , and as I be lead to believe without any right of appeal , I will do my utmost to be discreet and impartial . in the first place I award that Miss Valence remain in this house , forget and forgive her wrong . in the second place I recommend   in such a matter I will not presume to command   that till Mr. Shelfer have make a humble apology and promise faithfully never to be intoxicate again , however cold the weather be , Mrs. Shelfer shall not permit he to have a single kiss , nor a single bit of hot dinner . now I have deliver my decree . " 
 " Lor , Miss Idols , you be too soft for the Old Bailey . he never kiss I , unless it be when he know I have get some money . but he do like a good hot dinner . right enough there , my good friend . " 
 so this knotty point be settle ; and Giudice , who be very loth to leave I , escort Miss Idols home , before go , he make another solemn deposit of his great jowl in my hand , and look at I with an air so tutelary and encouraging , that I could not help laugh ; at which he feel hurt , but condone it . Isola tell I that when he be put in charge of she , he feel the responsibility so strongly that he would not stir from her side , not even to speak to the most colloquially gifted dog ; though at other time he would stay gossip near a lamp - post for five minute together . one evening when he be thus commission , a rude fellow push between they , and say something to Isola . Giudice have he down in an instant , and stand over he , like a tawny thundercloud , with growling so fearful and such flash eye , that two policeman feel it wise not to act as conductor . idol herself be oblige , at the entreaty of her prostrate foe , to coax the great dog off ; but when the ungrateful man get up , he insist on give Giudice into charge , and have he drag to the Station . " very good , Sir , " say the policeman , " we will enter the charge when you bring he there ; let he go , Miss , for the Gent to collar he . " the " Gent " be away in no time , and Giudice and his mistress walk off amid loud hurrah from all the boy of the neighbourhood . 
 Conrad call with his sister the day after Mr. Shelfer 's ducking , to reassure himself as to my nerve , which be never well . he look over some of my drawing , and without seem to give , but rather to seek information , afford I many a hint , which I afterwards find most useful . I now learn what his profession be ; and it give I pleasure to find that he be not , as I have fear , a mere lounger upon town . instead of that , he be work very hard , be   as he tell I   nothing more or less than a journeyman sculptor . though , as himself admit , by no mean a novice , he be go through the regular course of study and hand - labour under an eminent artist . but Isola tell I , and no doubt it be true , that he could beat his master out and out , and that for any choice design , where original power and taste be need , they always come to he . of late the frost have lighten his task ; for warm the room as they would , the weather always affect the material ; and they fear to attempt the more delicate part of the work during the rigour of winter . so when the thaw come , he must lose the pleasure of see I for a while , unless dear Isola wish to be escort home on a Sunday ; if , indeed , I allow she to come on that day . why , that be the very day when I could well indulge in a walk with my gentle friend , after go to church ; and I be sure her society do I more good than the sermon . on her part , Isola find that the service always make she so nervous   her nerve be as good as mine , and that she do not much like walk about with a big dog on Sundays , and Cora be always cross all the day after mass , so Conrad must promise upon his honour always to come for she , rain , hail , or shine , on a Sunday . this he promise so readily , that , for a moment , I fancy it have all be preconcerte . then I despise myself for the suspicion . the trick would have be not out of the compass of Isola , but very unworthy of Conrad . 
 CHAPTER X. 
 soon as ever my sight be fully restore , and I have Dr. Frank 's permission , I take to my drawing again , and work at it till my eye ache . this be the symptom upon which I have promise immediately to leave off . then out I would rush , towards dusk , and away into the great square , full of the pure air of heaven , round by the church at the top , and six time round it till my breath be short . the senior sophist remind I that round a square be impossible . after square the circle , extract the square root , dear idol , by the binomial theorem . you do learn so much at college : but I write simple and often foolish English . never mind ; I would rather write bad English , than the good French ever write . one be the tongue of power and multitude : the other the language of nicety and demarcation . which of the two be the more expansive , even a woman may guess . 
 high time it be for I to recruit my exchequer . Dr. Franks have charge I far less than I even dare to hope . how I tremble when I open the envelope ! what quick terror be half so bad as the slow fear of gather debt ? I be accustomed to medical charge of the time when I be an heiress : but his appear to I now to be even below reason . the sum could hardly have pay he for his numerous walk to and fro . then a wretched idea shoot through I : have he charge I so little , because he know I be poor ? I take Mrs. Shelfer into my confidence ; she be likely to know what the London scale should be . the little thing soon reassure I : it be quite enough , she declare ; if she be in my place , she would demand a discount for ready money ! 
 " oh you dreadfully mean little woman ! I should lose my sight , and deserve it , if I do . " 
 however , in spite of all this , money be scarce and scarcer every day , and none of my grand revenue would fall due for ever so long . so another visit must be pay to Mr. Oxgall . Isola insist on come with I ; to my surprise I find that , with all her soft simplicity she have much more idea of make a market than I have . the reason probably be that she have much less pride . no pocket would hold mine , when a tradesman attempt any familiarity . and whoso stand on a pedestal to sell , be like to find the buyer 's arm too short . 
 whether it be that , or the golden charm of her manner , or of something else , let Mr. Oxgall say ; certain it be that the man of crackly canvas   for whom , by - the - bye , I have a sincere respect , because he cheat I so little and so neatly   - this man , I say , regard she with a wide - mouthed , brooch - eye , admiration , which he hardly ever expend on anything out of oil . for the king of painter himself she be a vision sweet than dream of heaven . such a tint in her lustrous eye , such tone in her dainty cheek , such perfection of line in her feature , and every curve of her exquisite shape . and bound and sparkle through all , from the ripple wealth of her hair to the light - curve arch of her foot , the full play of her innocent , joyous , love life . 
 no wonder the picture - dealer shade his eye and gaze , and rub they and gaze again . I have frequently see respectable elderly gentleman , whose rakishness have never be more than find vent in the cock of a hat , magisterial man I mean , who would no more think of insult a girl in London or anywhere else , than of give their daughter as prize for competitive skill in poach , such good man and true , also simple - hearted clergyman   for some there still be from the country   these and the like , I Clara Vaughan have see , when they meet my Isola , stop short , wink frequently , and without much presence of mind , until she be go by ; then shumble hotly across the street , with hand in their tail - coat pocket   for these gentleman always expect most to be rob when there be least chance of it   pretend to look at a shop , then march at top speed , fumble all the while for their spectacle , until they get well a - head of we . then I have see they cross again , some thirty yard in front , with spectacle nicely adjust , and become again wholly absorb by the beauty of metropolitan good . but when the light foot sound , from a fair gaze distance , these same gentleman have   by some strange coincidence   alway turn full upon we , in an absent and yet nervous manner , and focusse their green or pale blue eye upon the rich violet orb of Isola . I have even know they to look at I   when they could see she no more , to find some sympathy for their vague emotion . idol know it : of course she do . and she rather glory in it . she have much respect for a fine old gentleman ; and I know not how it be , but nobody ever think of insult she when she could be clearly see . 
 a " pretty girl " you would never call she - though Mr. Shelfer do - the term would be quite unworthy ; even a " beautiful girl , " sweetly beautiful though she be , would hardly be your expression , at least for a while . but a " lovely girl , " and the lovely one ever see , that be what she would be call at once , if you could take your eye off , to analyse your idea . 
 Isola know it of course , as I say before , she know all her wondrous gift ; but as for be conceite , a trull with a splay foot and a crop of short - horn carrot have often thrice her conceit . a certain pretty graceful pride she have , which throw a rosy playful halo round she , but never make other woman look plain in her eye . she will not value her beauty much , until she fall in love ; and bless be he who shall be the object , if she be allow to abide with he . 
 meanwhile Mr. Oxgall wish for nothing but to hear and see her talk ; and this she do to some purpose . I like a man who at the age of sixty be still impressible to the gay vein of youth . I know at once by his eye whether his admiration be abstract and admissible . if it be , I reciprocate it . what clear proof can we find , that his heart have not wither with his body ; that he be not a man of mammon , tinsel , or phylactery , - in a word , no mummy ? 
 shall I ever finish this bargain ? I have never be so reflective before ; and all the time no less a sum than five pound hang upon it . five guineas   which sound well   be the amount at which dear idol let off Mr. Oxgall . I believe she might have get ten , but she have an excellent conscience . it work like a patent chronometer , with compensation balance . mine be still more sensitive . I could hardly think my landscape , perspective mare 's nest and all , worth that amount of money , and I wish to throw off a guinea , but idol would not hear of it . 
 " Miss Valence , I be your factor for this beautiful landscape , which have cost you so much labour . either accept my term , inadequate as they be , or take the agency from I , and recommence with Mr. Oxgall ' de novo , ' as we say at College . " 
 betwixt her beauty and my stately integrity , poor Mr. Oxgall know not where he stand . I hear he mutter that he would rather go through fifty auction , even if it be George Robins . but if she have come to sell he a picture the very next day , he would have go through it all again with the same infatuation . so I take the money ; and now my evil demon , who have chafe beneath all this trampling , have his turn again . we have foolishly bring the great dog Giudice , for our delight and the expansion of his mind . in Mr. Oxgall 's shop he behave to admiration . with the air of a connoisseur he walk from picture to picture , close one eye , and faintly wag his tail . then he find a Scotch terrier scarcely worth a sniff , and a mastiff whom he salute with a contemptuous growl . the only work of high art he could discover be an interior , with a flitch of bacon in the foreground uncommonly well draw . before this he sit down , and receive no invitation , bedew the board with a stalactite from either side of his mouth . the dog be so well behave , he never take anything without leave and say t a long grace . 
 unluckily Mr. Oxgall , mainly I believe to prolong his interview with Idols , insist upon take we to the shop of a carver and gilder close by ; where my first drawing   which have be sell   be to be see in its frame . he declare that we could not tell what a painting be like , until we have see it frame . observe several large mirror in this shop , I beg that Giudice might be leave outside . and so he be , but he do not stay there . Scarcely have we begin to discuss the effect of the frame on my drawing , when Giudice push his way in , and look about with a truly judicial air . the shop be long , and the owner be with we at the further end . I see what would follow , and dash off to stop he , but it be too late . Giudice have see the very fine dog he ever behold in his life - a dog really worth fight . up go his crest and his tail , one savage growl , and he spring at he . Crash , - and the large mirror there be a wreck , and Giudice the rock beneath it . for a time he lie quite stunned ; then to my great delight he stagger to I , not Isola , lay his cut paw in my hand and his bleed nose in my lap , and explain it all to I with much entreaty for sympathy . this I give he readily , even to tear and kiss . Isola want to scold and even to beat he , but I would not hear of it . he have see another great dog between himself and we , how could he help attack he ? I order a sponge and some water at once , and bath his fore paw , which be terribly cut ; then remember the inspector , I send idol for some arnica . but the blood be not stanch by it as I expect ; perhaps the drug be not pure , or the hair obstruct its action . so I hold his paw in the basin , and he whinge , and lick I , and make my face all bloody . 
 meanwhile the poor carver and gilder think much more of his look - glass than of noble flesh and blood . the picture - dealer as well be in a great predicament . 
 " Mr. Oxgall , " I cry , still sponge the wound dog 's nose , " let we hear no more about it . tell I the full value of the mirror , and I will pay for it . what be glass and quicksilver , or even gold , compare to a noble dog like this ? not worth a wag of your tail , be they , my duck of diamond ? give I another kiss , you delicious pet of a dog . " 
 the delicious dog be entirely of my opinion . his beautiful eye be unhurt . his nose taste wholesomely salt . but Isola be not half so romantic . little she care about money for herself ; yet she have no idea of see a friend disburse . empower by nature to wind all man round her finger , she now call art to her aid , and Mr. Oxgall , who be half - way round already , have no chance of escape . 
 she settle it thus : the carver and gilder , in consideration of his dealing with Mr. Oxgall and his own " careless exposure " of the mirror , should accept cost price for the article . that amount should be pay in equal share by all three of we : by Mr. Oxgall because he would drag we thither , by herself as the mistress of the dog , and by I as the cause of the expedition . she have attend a course of lecture upon jurisprudence , and her decision be well than that of a judge , because she have see the whole of it , and because the dog be her - at least her brother 's , which be all the same . as for the owner of the mirror , he must think himself wonderfully lucky in have meet with such honest people , and in have sell his glass , and have not   he get all the piece , and she must have the large one for Judy to dress his hair by . and so indeed she do . 
 after our dear Portia have finish , and the whole thing be settle , it strike I that no lecture upon jurisprudence could turn wrong into right . Mr. Oxgall be quite blameless , so be I , so be Idols , except in bring unlucky Giudice with she , which , from the outset , I have discourage . she , as the temporary owner of the dog , should have bear all the loss ; and so she would have do gladly , only she do not see it in that light . as it be , she try afterwards to force upon I her last three guineas   that be the sum which I have pay , as my third of the whole , but of course I would not accept they . she have no money with she , so I pay her contribution , but allow she to repay I . Mr. Oxgall 's third I make good to he   without consult she   when he pay I for my next drawing . so I have earn five guineas , and lose six . be it always to be so when I labour to make a little money ? 
 at my earn entreaty - Idols could refuse I nothing , when I be in earnest - darling Giudice be bring home in a cab to my lodging . I know that he would not be care for at the stable where he be board ; and his wound be very serious . as for home , Professor Ross , who detest dog in general , would not admit he into the house . he even think it a great stretch of grace to allow old Cora to watch the dog back to the stable , after he have be patrol all the afternoon with his mistress . how I hate such low ingratitude ! an animal be to serve we , body and soul , to crouch and fawn for our notice - not that Giudice ever fawn to he , but growl awfully - and we be to think it well off with a curse or a kick , which we durst not give it but for its loyalty to we . 
 what pleasure I have in nursing that poor Giudice , and how grateful he be ! when we get home , I wash his wound again , with warm water this time , as the bleeding be stanch ; and then I " exhibit "   as the doctor absurdly say   a little friar 's balsam . " oh , it do smart so ! " Giudice exclaim with his eye , " but I know it be for the good , and you will not   see I give one wince . " neither do I. then a nice soft bandage over his lovely paw , and a plaister across his nose , and he lie snugly , at the proper distance from the fire , as proud as possible of be nurse , and with an interesting air of pallid refinement on his feature . he will hardly notice idol , but exclaim , at length , with the petulance of an invalid , " Isola , can not   you let I alone ? Clara understand a dog , and I like she much the good . " so he follow I all round the room with his eye , and beg I to come and talk to he , which I would not do , because he need quiet and composure . 
 CHAPTER XI . 
 Beloved Giudice remain many day under my care , until he become convinced that he be my dog absolutely , and have no claim on any other human being . he more than pay for his board and medical attendance , by sit repeatedly for his portrait ; in which at last I succeed to his and my own satisfaction . though by no mean a conceited dog , there be nothing he love well than have his likeness take ; and directly after breakfast he always assume the most become attitude , and watch intently for the appearance of the pencil with his massive head a little on one side , and his dark brown eye full of dignified interest , and his great ear curve down through russet tuft , like tawny cascade in autumn , he seem fit study for a real artist , who should quicken as well as copy he . however , he be too much of a gentleman to sneer at my weak effort , for he see that I do my good . oftentime he would gaze steadfastly at the portrait and then at I , and hobble up , and nudge I , and whine , a little , and then sigh in self - abasement at his want of speech . whenever he do this , I know that he wish to have something alter ; but it be long before I could discover what that something be . I try every change of line or colour that I could think of - all to no purpose . at length it strike I that as he criticise more with nose than eye , the defect must be in the smell . happy idea ! I satisfy my Giudice at last , and do it thus . after shade around the nose and mouth , before lay on the colour , I take a clean dry brush , and pass it lightly round the hollow of his own sweet saltish nostril , carefully avoid the cut ; then one turn of the brush , not on the palette , but on a dry square of colour , and with that I express the dear dog 's nose so well , that he would have spoil it in a sniff ecstasy , if I have not pull it away . his portrait now possess the life which he require . 
 meanwhile I receive almost daily visit from Isola and her brother ; the latter be , of course , very anxious about his poor dog , and could only relieve that anxiety by long interview with he . it happen strangely enough , yet more and more often as time go on , that Isola during these interview feel an especial desire for Mrs. Shelfer 's society , which she could only enjoy by betake herself to the kitchen . there , with all the pet , except old Tom , who be constancy itself , and the lame blackbird who be all gratitude , her influence begin to supersede mine , and even Mrs. Shelfer 's ; for this I care but little , so long as Giudice keep to I . 
 over that great dog , as he turn upon his side , and lift one hind leg   the canine mode of show submission to the will of God , over he we bent , Conrad and I , in most interesting diagnosis , until it seem the proper thing that our hair should flow together , and our breath make one soft breeze . from this position we would rise with a conscious colour in our cheek , and a flutter at the heart , and a certain awe of one another . then it would be ever so long before either of we dare to seek the other 's eye . haply when those eye be meet - unwitte yet inevitably - they would drop , or turn away , or find some new attraction in the dog or cloud . 
 then some weak remark would follow , for which the hearer care no whit , yet feign deep interest therein . 
 why labour thus to cheat ourselves - each other we can not cheat - why feel we so confused and guilty , why long so heartily to be a hundred league away , yet know thoroughly that , if it be so , all the space between be void and heartache ? the reason neither we nor other mortal know ; the cause be this , that we love one another . 
 I have feel that it must be so , at least on my part , ever since the day he come with Isola , and know I not , though I know he so well . do he know I now as the Clara Vaughan whom he once avoid ? these eyelash be as long and dark as ever ; the large eye , shade by they , be as deep a gray as twilight in a grove of willow . my cheek have regain their curve , my hair be never injure ; let I hie to the glass now he be go , and see if I be like myself , and whether I have face and form likely to win Conrad 's love . 
 no , I be not like myself . no wonder he do not know I . the gloom habitual to my face be go . it be the difference betwixt a cavern well and a sunny fountain . I see a laugh graceful girl , with high birth mark in every vein , and self - respect in every motion ; her clear cheek glow with soft wonder , her red lip part with delight , her arch neck and shoulder curve gleam through a night of tress , her forehead calm and thoughtful still , half - belie the bright eye where love and pleasure sparkle . for a moment self - approval heighten the expression . at my silly self my foolish self be smile ; but the smile have warm source than maiden 's light conceit . I smile because I see that , as regard exterior , he who slight I must be hard to please ; and some one , whom I think of , be not hard to please . straight upon the thought of he - ah well . 
 my father use to quote from the " Hero and Leander " a beautiful verse , which neither he nor any other could in English render duly ,

 " shower from her cheek the flow carmine of her shame . " 
 CHAPTER XII . 
 but when Conrad should have learn who it be that nurse his dog , would he feel the tender gratitude and delight which he now display so freely ? would he say , as in his fervour he now say every day , " Miss Valence , I do believe there be no one like you in the world ! " would he not rather say , " Miss Vaughan , how basely you have deceive I ! Giudice , come away ! " a whistle and the last sound of the foot , for which I listen now by the hour . 
 this thought be continually with I . it poison half the flavour and ruin all the digestion of my happy moment . but what could I do ? how unmaidenly , how presumptuous of I to imagine that he be likely to break his heart for I ! and if he do - why then he should break my own as well . I be not one of the drawing - room young lady , who receive a modify proposal every Sunday afternoon , and think much more about the sermon afterwards . I can not play with the daffodil upon the brink of love , sleepily thrust my admirer in , and lounge with half - open breast , which neither love - knot may secure , nor flutter sigh unzone . no , here I be , such as I be , such as God have make I . no usury , no auction for my heart : once for all I give it , and my life go with it . 
 so it must always be with a girl of any feeling , who have train her own existence . but for my wild ignorance , I would dare to say - so it must be always with a girl of feeling , twist and warp she as you will . yet I be tell , by those who know the world , that it be not so with nine girl out of ten among the lady caste . if , beneath the roc of fashion , they prefer the diamond to the meat , let they have it , and starve thereon . the choice be of their own young crop . no parent bird can force the bauble down . but what have I to do with this ? all I know be that neither I , nor any child of mine , will or shall be gullete thus for life . 
 after every little burst of thought , every feeble sally of imagination , come   as always be the case with I   come the slow pusillanimous reaction . all that I have any right to do be to paint , earn money , and be off for Italy . 
 little as I know about the expense of travel , I feel sure that it would be vain to start with less than a hundred pound . enormous sum ! how could I ever hope to win it , though I paint day and night , and live on bread and water . to this diet , or what in London be quite synonymous , bread and milk , I have already reduce myself , in my stern resolve to lay by two pound every week . farewell to meat , so soon as my Devonshire " pegmate " be go , and farewell to what I care much more about , a glass of good London stout . I suppose there be something horribly " vulgar " in my taste , for I will confess that the liquid call " black draught " by Mr. Dawe have much charm for I . however , I abjure it with all other luxury , and throve no whit the bad . the kindly little woman , whose summum bonum   next to her ' stick "   be plenty of good fare , take it much to heart that I should live so plainly . 
 " why , Miss Valence , you be the queer young lady as ever I set eye on . all as ever I see , and I have   see'd a many , they pick a little bit so dainty , like a canary crack a hemp seed when the gentleman be by : then off they go when there be nobody look , and munch like so many pig in a potato bury . Miss Violante you know . but as for you , why bless I and keep I , you feed that great horse of a dog with all the fat of the land , and you live on a crust yourself . now do come down , that be a good soul ; there be a clod of beef a - bile with suet dumpling , and such lovely parsnip , you can smell it all up the stair , gallop , gallop , my good friend , and that rogue of a Charley will not   come home I know , he be get along with that thief Bob Ridley ; and I expect the boy every minute with a little drop of stout , and the good pewter pot for you . now if you will not   come down , Miss Valence , my dinner will all stick in my throat , and I be so hungry . " 
 " so be I , Mrs. Shelfer , you have make I so . " 
 in her excitement , she slip from the edge of the chair , whereon she always balance herself when I make she sit down . she think it disrespectful to occupy too much room , and cuddle herself in the small compass possible . 
 let no ill be think of Giudice . who think ill of I I care not , for I can defend myself , if it be worth while . so can giudice with his tooth - the fine set in London - but he have no tongue , no merop tongue , I mean . it be true that Giudice have good fare , and thoroughly he enjoy it . that dog know a juicy bit of meat , short of staple , crisp , yet melt , quite as well as I do . true , he have a love of bone , transparent gristle , and white fibre , which I , from inferior structure , can not quite appreciate . yet all this be no part of his mind , much less do it affect the greatness of his soul . he keep , as all of we do who be good for anything , a certain alter ego , a high voice , a purer sense , a vein which fashion can not leech , or false shame tourniquet . so the good dog use to come to I , before he touch his breakfast , lunch , or dinner , and entreat I to devour all I could , there would be lot still leave for he . 
 in my hurry to get start of time , to spin a little fast the revolving moon , I do a thing which I could ill - approve to myself , even at the moment . I write to Sally Huxtable to obtain Mr. Dawe 's permission for I to sell my gordit . Professor Ross have offer I no less than ten guineas for it . as a gentleman he should not have make the offer , after what I have tell he . but the love of science - falsely so call by collector - drive man to discern propriety " by the wire - draw line of their longing . "     however , I be not quite so blind upon right and wrong , as to mean to keep all the money . I offer Mr. Dawe half , if the plaything should be sell . 
     " Exiguo fine libidinum . " 
 I know not why , but I could not bear the idea of a bargain and sale with Conrad 's father , wide apart as the two always be in my mind . I rather hop that Beany Dawe , though sorely tempt , would refuse . 
 and now the time be almost come for news from Tossil 's Barton . Dear Sally must have fill the twelve copybook , at the rate of one a week . Ere I quite expect it , the letter come ; but before its tiding be impart , I must in few word describe the visit of Inspector Cutting 's son . George Cutting come one evening to see his good Aunt Patty , for so he call Mrs. Shelfer , who be in truth his cousin . though I have be so assured that my enemy could not escape , I be not equally convinced , and at time a deep anxiety and despair possess I . 
 therefore I go to the kitchen to see the Inspector 's son , and request Mrs. Shelfer to allow I five minute of conversation with he . he stand all the while , and seem rather shy and confused . he have not hear from his father , since the ship sail ; but he have see in the paper that she have be speak somewhere . " the party as I know of " be still safe in London - my blood run like lava at the thought - or I should have hear of it . he , George Cutting , have his eye upon he , and so have two of the detective force ; but what be they in comparison with his father ? this he ask , despite his shyness , with so large a contempt , that I begin to think the cut family admire the cutting only . 
 upon I , who be no cutting , he leave the simple impression that the quality , so laud by his father , lie as yet beneath a bushel . however , his Aunt Patty declare that he could eat three time as much as Charley . not unlikely , if he only drink one - third of Charley 's allowance . 
 Mrs. Shelfer , who know that I be lay by a fix sum every week , begin to look upon I as a fine young miser . of course she quite fall in with what she suppose to be my idea , for she never contradict any one , unless it be a cabman . 
 " oh , I do love money , my good friend ; gold , gold , it be so bootiful . do you ever hear tell of the marrow bone I have ? oh dear ! " 
 " what marrow bone , Mrs. Shelfer ? " 
 " why a big beef marrow bone , that long , full of sovereign and guineas after dear Miss Minto . I stop it with a bung and a piece of bladder , and for well than a twelvemonth , while they be execute her will , I sleep with that beneath my pillow for fear the priest should get it . Lord , how they do fight over the poor old lady 's rag and bone , that leathery priest and three yellow kite of cousin , they say they be , as come from Portugal . at last they get a ministration     with the testament and text , and they rob I shameful , shameful , my good friend . never catch I go to mass again , or you may tell I of it . " 
     ? letter of Administration cum testamento annexo . 
 " and what become of the marrow bone , Mrs. Shelfer ? " 
 at this inquiry , she wink both eye rapidly , and screw up her little mouth . 
 " oh what a thief that Father Banger be , to be sure ! you see , Miss , I have strict order to shut he out , when Miss Minto be near her end , because he have kick her dear cat Filippina from the top of the stair to the bottom , after he have give her unction . what a pretty sight it be to see they seven dear cat , all sit round the fire , each one on his proper stool with his name do on it in different coloured worsted . I have so much a year leave I on the Bank of England , honourable to the day , for each one of those cat , and change of diet every week , and now there be only one of they leave , and that be my dear old Tom . " 
 " but , Mrs. Shelfer , about the marrow bone - " 
 " well , my good friend , I be go to tell you . the way that Father Banger get into the house again to steal the poor old lady 's money , for build a school or some such villany . he know how fond the poor soul be of cat , so he borrow a cat somewhere , and he get two boy to let it down the area with a whipcord round its stomach , and to jerk , jerk , jerk away at it , and the poor thing do squeal sure enough . ' pain , Patty , ' say my poor mistress , and she could hardly speak - ' oh , Patty , there be some cruel Englishman torture a cat again . ' so out I run into the area , and in pop Father Banger , who have his back to the wall , with a great sheet of paper ; and he begin to make a list of all the thing in the house . I take the cat to dear Miss Minto , and how pleased she be ! ' please God , ' say she , ' to let I live a few day more till I make a Catholic of this poor heretic ' - she always convert her cat the first thing - ' and then it shall have a stool and a good annuity . ' but next day the poor thing go . " 
 Little Mrs. Shelfer have so great a fear of death , that like some ancient nation she shun all mention of his name , by euphemistic periphrase . she have never know real illness , and even a stitch or a spasm would frighten she for day . when I speak calmly , as I sometimes do , of our great inevitable friend , whom we so labour to estrange , up would jump Mrs. Shelfer with a shudder and a little scream . 
 " oh do not , my good soul , oh do not ! how can you ? let we live , Miss Valence , let we live while we can , and not think of such dreadful thing . you make my blood run cold . " 
 " but , Mrs. Shelfer , surely you know that we all must die . " 
 " of course , my good friend , of course . but then you need not   remind one of it . I meet Doctor Franks to - day , and he say , ' why , Mrs. Shelfer , I do declare , you look young than ever , ' and a very clever man he be , yes , yes ; and not a gray hair in my head , and my father live to eighty - eight . " 
 " and how old be you , Mrs. Shelfer , now ? " 
 " oh I be sure I do not   know , Miss Valence , I do not   keep no account . let we talk of something else . do you hear what Tom do to your Judy to - day ? " 
 ah , poor little thing ! but I be not go to moralise . shall I ever know the history of that marrow bone ?   
     I have now ascertain that a rove dog pop in and away with the marrow bone , sovereign , guineas , and all . - C.V. 1864 . 
 CHAPTER XIII . 
 Tossil 's Barton , estimate the British Post by the standard of Joe Queen 's boy , place but little confidence in that institution . moreover , Tossil 's Barton hold that a " papper scrawl , " as it term a letter , be certain to be lose for want of size , unless it be secure in something large , ' something as a man can zee and hold on to , " as the farmer himself express it . 
 therefore I be not surprised at receive , instead of a letter by post , a packet deliver by the parcel van . this packet be bind round like the handle of a whip . and stick at either end with a mass of cobbler 's wax . bear the vivid impress of a mighty thumb . within the wrapping first appear an ominous crumpled scroll . ye star , where angel so buffoone by eminent painter dwell ! once more I behold Eli on the turnpike gate , the great western steamer , Job with a potsherd of willow - pattern plate , the Prodigal Son , and bad than all , that hideous Death and the Lady . recklessly I tumble out all the rest of the packet . three great bolt with silver clasp , three apostle spoon , two old silver salt - cellar mark W.H.J.H. , a child 's christening cup , a horn tobacco - stopper with a silver tip , an agate from the beach , a tortoise - shell knife with a silver blade , half a dozen coin and a bronze fibula find upon the farm , an infant 's coral , a neck - pin garnish with a Bristol diamond , a number of mother - of - pearl button and blue bead , and a mass of mock jewelry buy by the farmer from the Cheapjacks at Barum fair with the produce of his wrestling triumph . separate from the rest , and pack most carefully , be all but two of the trinket I have send as Christmas gift for the family . 
 touch to the heart by all this love kindness , I feel so ashamed of my paltry petulance at Eli , Jonah , and the rest , that I would not indulge in a peep at Sally 's letter , which come last of all , until I have starve myself for a day . that literary effort show so much improvement , both in writing and in spelling , that any critic would have endorse Mr. Huxtable 's conclusion that the gift must be in the family . a few word still there be of rather doubtful texture , but who can bind or bind the caprice and luxury of the english language ? moreover , Sally 's stop be leave once more to the discretion of the reader . but if Lord Byron could not grasp the mystery of punctuation , how could sally huxtable ? yet that eager little maid would have learn in half an hour the art which might have mellow the self - tormentor 's howling . Sally 's be a healthy , sweet , and wholesome nature . 
 Tossil 's Barton Farm , Trentisoe . 
 the tenth day of March A.D. 1851 . 
 " DEAR MISS CLARA DEAR , - if you please , father and mother and I and our little Jack hope this letter will find you in good health as it leave all of we at this present , or when it will be finish , thank God for the same , and hop no offence . the baby as be bear on the 20th day of October last be a very fine and lusty wench at this time of writing , and have get two tooth , and her hair come again , and answer to the name of Clara , as you know Miss you be so kind to give her leave and liberty , and father call Clara to she now , and so do I and Jack , but mother will call she Babby still , and so the chiller do . 
 Father often say , " Babby ! why there be a hundred babbie in the world , and a thousand either , for ought I know again it , but I reckon there be not   half a dozen Claras . " but mother say she can not   help it : she always do call they babbie till they be put into short - clothe , and long too , if so be there be not   another , and she feel a call on she to do it , and no offence Miss Clara for that same . if you please Miss , when the parson say " name this child , " and Aunt Muxworthy , from over to Rowley Mires , say , quite peart , " Clara , sir " - father have be learn she , you see Miss , all the morning - parson look , so mother say , the same as a skinned sheep all skivere out to dry ; and Tim Badcock go haw haw , till father be force to slip behind the godmother and fetch he a little clout on the side of his head . then parson say at last , " Clara maam ! there be no child of that name to this side of Coom , and it seem to I to go again the rub rick . " Father say the parson must be a high farmer , for none of we ever hear tell of that rick in this country . " now take my advice and think well of it Mrs. Muxworthy , " the parson say again . so she look to father , for you see Miss she be not edify about it be right , because she could not find it in the Bible nowhere . and she say , " think well of it farmer now ; if you want a handsome name , there be Tryphena and Tryphosa , and have be in the family afore . " " Mother , " say my father , and he look the way he do when he do not   intend to talk about a thing , " Mother , go home with the child , and I will take she to Parracombe Church next Sunday : and tell Suke not to put the goose down . " 
 you see , Miss , we be go to have a supper after church , and the good goose on the farm , and the parson be come too . " Sober now , " say the parson , " if so be now , farmer John , you have put your mind upon name this here infant Clara , why I will christen she so , only an under protestant , and with difference to the chapter . " Father only say " amen , so be it ; " and then parson do it , and do it uncommon well too , father say . and she only laugh when they give she the splash . Father tell we afterwards as he believe parson be fear he could not   spell Clara fitty ; but mother say he be wrong there , and all along of his pride , for parson be a college chap and so he can spell anything amost , in one way or another . 
 Miss Clara , all they beautiful thing as you send for we to Christmas time , with the forepart of all our name upon they , except Sally , be sink in the bottom of the brook in the hole below the stickle by the hollow ash , where the big trout hath his hover , all along of Joe the Queen 's boy ; and we never know ought about it till your after letter come . then our little Jack , who be quite a big boy now , and button his own corduroy , go down to the brook at once , and pull off all his thing , and there he rake and feel among the stone for the big part of a day , though the ice be on the edge but the water be quite clear ; and Tabby Badcock want to pull off her thing and go in too , but Jack would not let she , and be ashamed of herself , and I sit on the bank and Tabby , and Jack pull out nine beautiful thing , as be mean for father , and mother , and he , and Billy , and little Honor , and Bobby , and Peggy , and the two weanie , but he could not   find nothing as be mean for I Sally , unless Tabby steal it , and she be quite equal to it I be afeare : and we all return you many many kind thank and love , especially the one as have it , and I . our Jack say , no she would not   do it , he will go bail for that , no fie ! but I shake my head ; though perhaps she never have the chance , if so be there be not   none mark Sally , and thank you every bit the same , Miss , so long as there be not   none for Tabby . " 
 poor little Sally ! she must have cry bitterly to think of she be forget . but the good of all , next to the farmer 's , be for she , and there be one for Tabby too . 
 " Miss Clara dear , the thing be not hurt at all by be under water for a week , and father say they must be make of the very same gold as Queen Victoria 's crown and sceptre be , as never can rust with the briny wave ; and Beany Dawe feel cock sure as it be the fairy of the brook steal they from Joe 's breech pocket , and keep mine still he say because it be the prettiest . but there , he never know much , any more than Tabby do . 
 if you please Miss , ask your pardon , when Aunt Muxworthy be here , to the christening time , she say she never see such writing in her life as mine , and it be not   my good copy neither , and she say it be a sin to make a scholar of a honest wench like that , and I should want to be the parson next , and read the forty - two generation and play the fort piano ; and I do not   know , Miss , whether to laugh or cry , so I begin to eat an apple ; but father say quite slowly , " Sister Muxworthy , you be never gift with no eddication no more nor I Jan Uxtable , and how be we to know if it be good or bad ? once I have a horse , say father , as afore ever he go into the field , turn up his nose at the grass like , and with turn up so much he die at last of the glander . but I never see that there horse persuade the other to starve . " Aunt Muxworthy toss her head , and we think she would not   eat no goose , but the smell of the stuffing and the weather be too many for she ; and she eat a wing , and a leg , and one side of the breast , and it do she good . and afore she have have much brandy , " John , " she say , " you be right and I be wrong . let the little wench crack on , and some day they will hear of she to tother side of Hexmoor . " so father laugh and kiss she , and the chiller be put to bed , and we drink your health Miss , and Clara 's nine time nine , and father say he will learn himself some day , when he give up wrestling , only he fear it would make his hand shake terrible , and then some laugh and some of we cry , and they have more hot water , and Beany Dawe set to , and make so many poem he turn the stair somehow inside out , and Suke and Tim be force to heave he into the tallat , and keep he from go abroad by a rope of onion round he and two truss of hay on the top . next day , he make no poem at all till he drink more than a gallon of cider . 
 oh Miss Clara dear , what ever be the matter with you ? Father be in such a taking I never see . to - day your letter come about sell that knob - thing of Beany Dawe 's , and we know it must be all along of the crown jewel you buy for we , as we mean to keep in the family to the end of all our time . mother double up , and cry into the churn , and spoil all the butter ; and father be that upset he stamp out of the house a try hard to whistle , and he could not   see no one there to let it off on but Timothy Badcock , and he be a little saucy , so he toss Tim up on the linhay roof and his leg come through the thatch , and father be force to ease he out with the pitchfork . Tim be stiff a bit in the evening , and serve he right say mother , for laugh so at the Cornishers ; but father give he some neatsfoot oil and cider , and we know we could not   hurt he because he be double - jointed . 
 and if you please Miss Clara dear , we would not stoop to ask Beany Dawe and he nothing but a saw poet ; so father go to the old oak chest with the whitewash on it , and pull it open without the key , and take out some old rubbish he saith , and order mother to pack it without a word , and mother want to put in a pair of linen sheet and the good table - cloth , but father say quite crusty like , " do e take our Miss Clara for a common packman ? " and when I say , " please father what shall I say about it all ? " he answer I quite low , " how ever can I tell child ? ask your mother there . only give my good respect and most humble duty , and tell Miss Clara I wish I could find a man to throw I all four pin , for be such a drunken hosebird not to have more to send she . but I know she will not   take money from the like of we . stop , " father say , " ask she to please to lift our horn up as the horn of an unicorn . I know where to go for lot of money and all to be have for ask . I will go to Bodmin town next week , " say father , " and show they cornisher a trick of Abraham Cann . since honest Abraham take the sprain , he leave it all to I , though God know , and thank he for the same , I never want it yet . I should like to see the Cornisher as could stand my grip . " and then father pull both his hand out of his pocket . mother say he wear they out he do spraddle both his thumb so . 
 it seem a curious thing , Miss Clara dear , father never get vex or weist like , but what he want to wrestle , and other time he never think of it , unless it be to fair or revel time . 
 when I ask mother and say as father tell I to , the tear be in her eye , and she try to look angry with I , and then she break out cry as loud as Suke when the cow molly kick she . so between the both of they , Miss , I can not   know what to say , so please to make it yourself Miss , for I be sure I can not   find any thing only the good love of our heart and a side of bacon we would like to send , and the butter from my own little cow , all sweet hay and no turmot ; I be to sit in Coom market , all by myself , on Saturday , and mother not come nigh I , and I know you will let I send you the money , and I expect elevenpence a pound , because you never be proud with your love scholar ever to command and obey . SALLY HUXTABLE . 
 all this here underneath and over the leaf be go to be write after the rest of this here paper . 
 if you please Miss Clara dear , there come now just a very fine speak gentleman with a long coat the colour of udder , and blue flap , and blue at the hand , and ever so many great silver button with a print like pat of good butter , and gold ribbon round his hat . we seem at first he be an officer of dragoon , till we see'd the flour in his hair , and then we know he be the Queen 's miller . Father be a great mind to show he a forehip and send his button to you Miss , because he see they be worth ever so much more than these little thing all put together , only mother stop he . 
 then the gentleman say he know Mr. Henwood well , and respect he much , and he be send here by expression to discover where you be Miss Clara , and it be most particular , and if we wish you well , we would tell he to once . Father and mother and I put he in the parlour and give he a jug of the very good cider , and then we go and lay our head together about he in the cheese - room , and mother and I be for tell he , only father say no . you never give we leave , and we want to do what be right and upright , unless you order we contrary , and we have no right to tell without ask you , and you so full of enemy . 
 so father say , very grand for he : " honoured sir , we hop the honour of a papper scrawl from Miss Clara in ten day time , or may be a fortnight , accord to the weather please God , and we be satisfied too . my eld daughter here be write to Miss Clara for a week or more , and if so be she have get room leave on the papper scrawl she ask Miss Clara 's leave , and we shall have time enough to hear what she say in a fortnight , or mebbe three week . " 
 " oh then , she be go to Hitaly at the least . " the gentleman say . Father never hear tell of Hitaly whether it be in London town or no , but he look to mother and I to hold our noise . the gentleman say something sound very much like " Dang , " and father hop he would be saucy , because then he send his button in spite of mother and I ; but when he look at father he think well of it , and go off very civil in the carriage he come by , only say he would find out in spite of we . 
 and please Miss Clara dear , mother say she be ashamed to send you a parcel all rubbage , except the picture , but she do hope they will not cheat you about they there , for they be the fine ever come to these part , and warrant real London make . all the farmer hereaway want to buy they of we . and father say , " dang the picture , tell Miss Clara to come to we , and she shall not   want Beany Dawe 's thing , nor the Queen 's miller either . " oh do come , Miss Clara dear , the bank be yellow with primrose , and white and blue with violet , and I know three blackbird nest already and an ousel be down by the river . oh do come .  SALLY huxtable . " 
 CHAPTER XIV . 
 from Sally 's eager description of the coat and button , I conclude easily that a servant from Vaughan St. Mary have be send in quest of I . my father hat showy livery and loathe hair - powder , but Mr. Edgar Vaughan return to the family usage , or rather allow they to re - establish themselves ; for on such question he be wholly indifferent . now what could be his motive for send so expressly to discover I ? I know not , neither care very greatly , but write at once to Tossil 's Barton , first to return their love contribution , which consist mainly of ancestral relic prize for generation , secondly , to set free the secret of my address . 
 into my own self I return once more . somehow I seem to expand whenever I come in contact with the yeoman 's family , and their lowly greatness . I be like a worm when it rain , after the drought of summer . surely the God , who leave we to stifle ourselves with the dust of fashion and convention , have his own gracious time to breathe upon and scatter it . at interval we may see through the reek of our own exuding , and inhale a more brace air than sleep in mausoleum . but instead of be exalt and feed by the open breeze , we shudder at the draught and replace our respirator . 
 I return into myself , and find little comfort there . I do not live inside myself , as most people live in theirs . true , I be apt to resent any slight to it offer from the outside . true , I seek its keep and comfort in a mechanical sort of way . but as for crust in its bottle , ripen in its husk , rust in its watch - case , I have be too long the toy of wind and weather not to be turn inside out . never can I moulder into the fungoid nucleus the british taste admire . and yet there be about I , if I must not say within I , a stanch cleave , a cohesion , a concrete will , which be of genuine anglo - saxon fibre . so I thrust aside all dream of Tossil 's Barton and Vaughan Park , and certain wilder sweeter dream which have begin to flutter and thrill through I , and in earnest I return to my task of money - making . 
 Giudice still be faithful , and comfort much my solitude . he have never ask his master 's leave or mine , he have never receive any formal invitation , yet here he look all at home , sleek and unblushing , though long since quite convalescent and equal to livery stable diet . once indeed , as we pass the entrance , he pretend to I that his conscience prick he . to ease it , he sniff about , and halt just for a moment , then turn his nose up , recocke his tail , and trot jauntily on . since then he have always avoid that left side of the street . he be affable still to Isola , but clearly regard she as no more than a pleasant acquaintance . whenever she enter the room , he walk from his corner with a stretch and a yawn , sniff all round her dress , to learn where she have be , and what dog she have speak to ; then , in the absence of any striking discovery , he look into her face with a grave complacence , and bring I his conclusion . Tom , and the bird , the squirrel , and the little marmoset   Mrs. Shelfer 's new and dear pet , he gaze upon from a lofty standing as so many specimen of natural history , interesting so far , but otherwise contemptible . he be now allow free run of the house , understand all the lock , and present himself in every room at the proper meal - time . even the little dress - maker be then honour with his attention . everybody love he , he be so gentle and clever and true . back he come to I , with his mouth rather greasy I must admit , give I one kiss   as a form , I be afraid , and exclaim , " dear I ! what a life this be ! ' sit down on his rug to think . 
 no one can tempt he far than the corner of our street , except his master or myself . Miss Flounce , with my permission , grant not without jealousy , once aspire to the escort of Giudice . although she carry a bag of his favourite biscuit   make perhaps of bone - dust , and keep one of they in her hand , Judy flatter she only to the corner ; then he turn abruptly , and trot firmly   rudely she call it   home , with his eye upon my balcony . I give he more of his biscuit than he would have get from she . 
 all this be very delightful . but there be two sad drawback . in the first place , Giudice expect I to forego every other line of art , and devote all my time to portraiture of himself . this be unreasonable , and I could not do it . apart from other consideration , Mr. Oxgall , after buy three study of he , decline to take any more until those three should be sell . to Giudice himself I have base my refusal upon more delicate ground . I have quote to he , 
 " although , lest I profane your hallowed part , 
 Queen Nature chill the blood around my heart ; 
 sweet dog , permit I to indulge my dream 
 of country valley , and the mazy stream . " 
 but he take no heed , and never would permit I so to do , without the keen jealousy . 
 the other drawback be still more serious . either by maintain the dog , I place his owner under an obligation ; or by engross the dog 's society , I lay myself under obligation to his owner . either view of the case be unpleasant ; the latter , which I adopt , soon become intolerable . so I speak about it to Isola , for I could not well explain myself to her brother , who ought indeed to have perceive my dilemma . 
 " oh Donna , " she cry , " what nonsense you do talk ! oblige to we indeed ! I be sure we be all greatly oblige to you ; and many a stir it save we at home , for the dog detest papa so ; and when Conrad come to see we , he can not   bear to have Judy shut out like a thief , and he the most honourable dog that ever wag a tail . " 
 " to be sure he be . you know you be , do not   you , oh combination of Bayard and Aristides ? " 
 that union of justice and chivalry wag his tail to I , and nod gravely to Isola . 
 " but I have say all along that Conny should pay for his board , and he feel it too : but we could not tell how to propose it to you , dear Donna , you be so very outrageous . " 
 " I should hope so indeed . " 
 " and then I be sure it would break poor Judy 's heart to go . would not   it now , Judy ? " 
 Giudice do not answer she , but come and lay his great head on my lap , and look up at I as only a dog can look . in that wistful look he say as plainly as possible - 
 " you know I be only a dog . but you , Clara , happen to be a human being ; and so you know all we dog know , and ever so much besides . only you can not   smell . you can talk , as fast as you like , both to each other and to we , but we can talk to none except our fellow dog . now do not   take a mean advantage of I . I know that I be make only to be your servant , and I love you with all my heart , that I do . I can not   tell at all where I shall go when I die , or if I shall go anywhere ; and I be sure I shall die , if you cast I away like this . " 
 so I kiss his dotty whisker , and promise not to desert he , though I should go all the way to the stable twice a day to see he . 
 " and another thing , Clara dear , " resume his master 's sister , " I consider he now more my dog than Conny 's . you know he be give between we " - this be the first time I hear of it - " and I only lend Conrad my half as long as he like to pay for he . " 
 Lovely Isola , like most other lovely girl , be keen about money - matter . not that she be ungenerous . that impulsive little mortal would give away all her substance , the moment her heart be touch , and it be not hard to touch , despite all the quick suspicion which her London life and native shrewdness have now begin to produce . but as regard small dealing , she be thoroughly qualified to keep a meat - pie shop , or go upon board wage , or even to take furnished lodging : by which climax I mean no disrespect to Mrs. Shelfer , who   consider her temptation   be the very pink of honesty , especially since Giudice can . 
 as to these small matter , and as to many large one , I be dear Isola 's cardinal opposite . she would make , for most man , a far well wife than I should ; although she will never love with a tenth part of the intensity . she can not   even hate like I . when I hate , I loathe and abhor . I never hate any one lightly , and hardly ever be reconcile , or suppress it . Isola talk about hat , but have never learn what it mean . spite she can carry , and nurse like a doll , and count it a minor virtue , albeit she can not be sulky ; hate be too heavy a burden . Scorn , which be with woman the hate of thing beneath they , Isola hardly know . perhaps she will learn it when her knowledge of the world narrow and condense , as with most woman it do . 
 another great difference there be between Isola and I . although she never would think of deceive any one seriously , and would on no account tell a downright malicious lie , yet she be not so particular about tell little fib , or at any rate colouring matter so highly that other be mislead . this she can justify to herself in a charming warm - hearted way . and yet she rarely make mischief . her departure be half unconscious , and always arise from good will . 
 " and so now , Clara dear , " conclude the senior sophist , " as Conrad have own all the dog so long , it be my turn to own every bit of he for an equal period , and I must pay you half a crown a week for his keep , and half a guinea for doctor he so well . " 
 I be much inclined to take she at her word , it would have be such a surprise . but what a disgrace to Giudice and to I ! 
 " oh Donna , " she continue , " you have no idea how fond dear Conny be of you . I be get so jealous . he think much more of you than he do of I . " 
 I bent over my drawing with more carmine on my cheek than be on the palette . what folly to be sure ! and Isola would come round in front . 
 " why do not   you answer I , Clara ? do you ever know such a shame ? well , I do believe you like be admire every bit as much as I do , in spite of all your sublimity . why there come Conny himself ; " and to my great relief she step into the balcony . " I think so . I know the ring of his heel . he will wear such clumsy boot , though his foot be as pretty as mine . I always know his step , and so do Judy . " 
 alas ! and so do i. how weak and paltry of I , with a life like mine before I ! 
 " I will go and open the door , " cry his sister ; " how rude he be to come when you be so busy , Clara . " 
 away she run , then usher he grandly in , and away again to nurse the marmoset . I know that I look slightly discompose . there be a glow upon I as if I have step into sunlight . Conrad fail to notice it , or conceal the perception . he stand before my easel . how I long for his approbation ! that of course be only from his knowledge of art and his native taste . yet I fear to look at his face , but wait for he to speak . with a stretch like a windlass , and a cavernous yawn , up come Giudice , and poke himself right in front of my work . could I have foresee that effrontery and execrable taste , less bread and milk would he have have for breakfast . conrad perceive my vexation , and despite his good breeding be too natural not to smile . the smile be infectious , and I obtain no more than a look of commendation . but that be enough for I . I resolve to keep the drawing : Mr. Oxgall may bid what he like . 
 as our eye meet , Conrad 's and mine , I see that he be not in his usual spirit . something have happen to vex he . oh that I dare to ask what it be . I also be heavy at heart , and ill at ease with myself . be it any wonder ? my nature be true and straight - forward as well as proud and passionate . but here have I be , for week and week , stoop below its level . I have even be deceitful . perhaps there be no dishonour in my change of name , with such an object in view . perhaps there be good excuse for maintain disguise with Conrad , when first we meet in London . but be it right and honourable to persist in my alias , when I could not help suspect his grow attachment to I ? peradventure my conscience alone would not account for all the misery I feel about this . have I no selfish misgiving as well ? now as I stand before he , my breast begin to flutter with fear , not so acute , but deeply than my alarm in the dark , when I crouch from the conspirator . 
 " Miss Valence , " at last he begin , " I be grieve in my heart by hear that you be not treat at all politely last night . " he be greatly move , and begin to lose his command of colloquial English . I have spend an evening at the Professor 's house in Lucas Street , the second time only of my be there . now I come to recollect it , Dr. Ross have certainly be a little overbearing , but I do not feel hurt thereby , because I care not for he , and know it to be his manner . Isola have tell her brother , but without mean any harm . her father no doubt have be vex , because I could not sell he my gordit . 
 " oh , Mr. Ross , " I reply , " I think nothing at all of that . a learned man like your father can not be expect to bear with every ignorant girl 's curiosity . " 
 " to a lady 's love of knowledge every gentleman should administer and be gratify . all man of lofty science enjoy to meet with a gentle mind inquire . " 
 it be not the first time Master Conrad have disparage , by implication , his father 's great acquirement . to I it seem scarcely graceful , and very far from dutiful , but many of my sentiment be dreadfully old - fashioned . an awkward pause ensue ; how could I answer without condemn one or the other ? though I could not quite acquit Conrad , my heart be entirely with he , for I have long be aware that he be not happy at home . there he stand , with an angry countenance , have decline the chair I have offer he . suddenly he take both my hand and look I full in the face , though his eye be glisten . I gaze full at he , with vague apprehension rise . how or why , I know not , but at that very moment my hair , which be always a trouble to I , fall in a mass down my cheek and neck . he start back , but still hold my hand . 
 " I be make certain that I have see you long ago . I will think , I will think . " 
 I see at once how it be , the fear on my face remind he . I mean to tell he some day , but I never mean he to find out . scorn myself for a hypocrite , I look stedfastly at he and smile . 
 " you will forgive I , Miss Valence , you know that I would not use a freedom . " 
 he see in my eye that I know it , and drop my hand , and go on . 
 " you will think I the weak in mind and most wicked , but I be most unhappy . " 
 I start in turn , and how I long to console he . what use be pride if it can not even command one 's eye ? 
 " it be to I a disgrace to come to you with my trouble . but I do it from no unmanly temper . I do it alone for the sake of my precious sister Isola . I have no long any one whom I dare to love but she , and now I be compel to abandon she at the last . " 
 " do you mean to be long away ? " this I manage to ask pretty well , though it be sore work . 
 " I shall not be away from London , but I shall be depart from Isola . the house where she live I be no more to visit . a long time I have go there only a little , and alone to see she . she be order now to come no more to I . this day I speak very violently . but I will not detain you with that . I will confess I do wrong ; but I be richly provoke . my object in burden you be double : - first to implore you , if I may without use liberty , to endure well with the Professor , lest she should be interdict from come to visit you , and then she would have no one remain to love she . second to ask , a thing that I hesitate because I can not narrate to you all thing , whether you would indulge I , if there be no wrong , to come now and then to see my own and my only sister . " 
 " of course you do not mean without her father 's knowledge . " 
 " I would never insult you , Miss Valence , by ask a thing like that . I desire nothing of what you call clandestine . you be so free and open , you would never have to do with any sort of concealment . neither be I in the habit to do anything like that . it have only be command that I may not go there , or invite she to come to my house . the Professor have great power in the present , but he do not pretend to interdict I from my sister . " 
 his eye flash , as he speak , with an expression quite unfilial . remember how differently I have love my own dear father , I feel disappointed and grieve , but have no right to show it . 
 " only one more thing I will entreat of you , Miss Valence ; poor Isola have never learn what mean any grief . if she be vex by this , I pray you to sustain and comfort she ; for I shall never make a wrong advantage of your most kind permission , so as to see she very often . " 
 he raise my hand to his lip in gratitude for what he call a kindness beyond all value to he , and his voice be tremble as he turn away . but I have do no kindness , I have give no permission ; for I be not calm enough to distinguish right from wrong . strange indeed it seem to I that I , for the most part so decide , could not now determine , but be all perplexity . my great iceberg self - reliance , build in bleak and lonesome year , be now adrift and melt in the bright sun of friendship and the warm sea - depth of love . 
 CHAPTER XV . 
 Isola happen that day to leave I before the usual time , be afraid that her father , who be not in his sweetest mood , would be angry with she . she be grieve of course at the new dissension , and think I   her idea be of loose texture   somewhat to blame somehow . nevertheless she soon forgave I the crime I have not commit . 
 that day I could paint no more , but sit I down to meditate . suddenly a loud ring and a loud knock echo through the house . Quickly Mrs. Shelfer 's little foot come patter up the stair , and her grey eye actually seem to come in first at the door . on the crown of her head her black cap hung , like the top of a chaise double back . 
 " oh my good friend , look here ! I be never so frightened in all my life . " 
 she hold as far from she as she could reach a closed envelope , address " Miss Clara Vaughan . " I tear it open and read - " Mr. Vaughan be die , come instantly . send by Mrs. Fletcher . " 
 " Telegraph , my good soul , " cry Mrs. Shelfer , " Electric Telegraph Company , all scream the wire red hot , and you must sign the message he say . and be there any answer ? and they give he eighteen penny . oh dear , I shall never get over it . never have such a turn since my brother John go , and they tuck he up so bootiful , and I say to the clerk at Barbican - " 
 " out of my way if you please . let I sign the form , and leave I alone a minute . there be no answer . " 
 should I go or not ? bitterly as I dislike he , could I let he die among hireling and stranger - I , his brother 's daughter ! a year ago I would have do so and think it the judgment of God . now I remember my dear mother 's death , and doubt about go only because I know not how he would take it . my hesitation be very brief . a cab be order , Giudice entrust to Mrs. Shelfer 's care , a short note leave for Isola , a few thing put together anyhow , and I be ready to start . 
 even in this hurry a selfish terror smite I , and I caution Mrs. Shelfer strictly to conceal both name and destination . she have only to say that some relative be suddenly take ill , somewhere down in the country ; the country be to her mind a desert mark with milestone , my description do not seem unreasonably vague . 
 as I stand in the passage wait for the cab , the poor dog , who have be quite flurry , and scented indefinite evil , commenced , prolonged , and would not conclude a howl of pass sadness . 
 " oh , my good friend , " cry Mrs. Shelfer , " let I stop the cab . all waste of money to go . the good gentleman , whoever he be , be as dead as a crabshell now . there be a terri with a split ear , next door but one , when my poor brother John be ill ; his name be Jack , I think , no , Tom ; bless I , no , what be I think of , Bob - Charley know , I dare say - " 
 " well , send I his name by telegraph . here be the cab , Mrs. Shelfer . " 
 heavy thump of weary wambling foot , grating of wheel , a needless " whoa , " and we open the door . 
 giudice bolt first into the cab , and sit upright with his tongue out and a sprightly pant , occupy the whole . it take the united strength , address , and authority of cabman , landlady , and myself to get he out again . then he coil his tail to his stomach , droop his ear and eye , and receive two hot tear and a kiss be sidle and delude into the narrow passage . the last thing I hear be a howl that wind far round the corner and beyond the square .
