 in an hour and a half from the delivery of the message , I be in a second - class carriage , and we shriek away from Paddington . the hurry and rush overcome I for a while . soon the April evening be spread with shadowy gray , and we be rush past the woode wave of pangbourne , and cast silver ring of steam on the many - finger spruce , before I could collect and feel my thought again . as we glide through plantation and between the wind hill , with the partridge begin his twilight call , the pheasant come out of the coppice to feed , and the late rook ply his dusky wing , at length the dust and city turmoil lag round the corner mile away , and we sparkle in the dewy freshness of the silent moon . though all alone in the carriage , I vainly try for prudence ' sake to creep into the cloak of sleep . every vein and every pore be full of gush thrill electric life . the country , the country ! the heavenly country 's glory ! how have I breathe and grope in the city grave so long ? for every thought that dribble there and gutter in my brain , a hundred thousand now flow through I , not of brain , but soul . thought I can not call they , for there be no volition , neither have they sequence , impress , or see image : only a broad stream gliding , whence and whither I know not . how can I describe to other what I can not tell myself ? 
 " Glost ' ! Glost ' ! change here for Chelt'm ! "   c. break my dreaming suddenly . it be eleven at night . I have come unwrapped ; the heavenly country and nature 's tide forget to keep I warm . out I come upon the platform , and dreamily begin to seek my carpet - bag , for I have no heavy luggage . the moon be struggle with the gas - light , as nature in I fight with modern life . 
 " Fly , Miss , fly ? " the lonely porter ask . 
 " yes , please , " say I. 
 " where for , Miss ? " 
 " Vaughan St. Mary . " - at this part of my life , I drop the grand " Vaughan Park ; " it seem too fine for I , and I be well content to be of Conrad 's class in the world . 
 " oh , there be a carriage wait at every train , if you please , Miss. " 
 and with tenfold politeness the porter show I across the square to one of the family hearse , which my father and I so detest . it so happen that the driver and footman be take some light refreshment at the bar of a neighbouring edifice , while the horse champ their bit and whinny . the man come out against their will , and star at I in the broad moonshine . I be very simply , plainly , and cheaply dress , in deep mourning still for my darling mother ; but no servant of even slight experience could take I , I think , for anything but a lady ; little as it matter . the man be half - drunk , very surly at be disturb , and incline to form a low estimate of my dress and carpet - bag . 
 " you mean to say you be Miss Vaughan , young ' ooman ? " stutter the reel coachman , with his hand beneath his flap and a short pipe in his mouth , " now I tell you plainly , there be no mistake about I mind , I can not   noway credit it . it do not   seem likely , do it , Bob ? " 
 " likely , Jacob ? yes , like enough to a fool ; but nohow creditable to the like of we . think I do not   know now ? perhaps the young ' ooman will answer a few question , Jacob . " 
 " ah , let you alone ; let you alone , Bob ! specially for young woman ! " 
 " Porter , a cab at once , if you please ; or a fly I think you call it here . " oh my London impudence ! 
 " to be sure , Miss ; the good in Gloucester directly . and , Miss " - confidentially , " if I be in your shoe , I would walk they chap about their business to - morrow . how they have be carry on here , to be sure , ever since the six o'clock train come in . why , in the time of the old Squire Vaughan - " 
 " thank you , the fly , if you please . " 
 in two minute I be off for my father 's home with mighty rattle of glass , and many jerking noise . about three mile from Gloucester we be pass by Jacob and Robert , who be sit side by side and drive furiously . convince at last by the porter of my genuine Vaughanship , they have set off full speed to secure first audience . 
 at length we pass the lodge , where the gate creak as of yore , and dear old Whitehead tremble at my voice , and so along the great avenue where I have study the manner and way of every tree , and where Tulip   Nestor among deer   come to stare at we with his grey face silver in the moonlight . poor old friend , he know I as well as Giudice do , but I could not stop to talk to he . soon as the bell be ring the broad bolt of the great lock , which I be once so proud to draw , fly back with suspicious promptitude . 
 albeit he have change the cloth too ochrously describe by Sally , for a suit of gentle gray , and have draw out his face to a most unjovial length , and assume an attitude of very profound respect , there he be , quite unmistakeable to observant eye , the Bacchanalian Bob . 
 " and please , Miss " - after he have fuss awhile - " what train do you please to come by ? I understand that the carriage have be wait there all day ; indeed , I see it come back from the pantry window myself , and they say in the yard the last train be in afore they come away . " 
 " I come by the train that ought to be there at half - past ten o'clock . " 
 " well to be sure ! that must be the very train as Samuel and Humphry say they wait for ; but they never have much judgment , they two man . and to let you come in a common fly , Miss ! " 
 " I see my father 's carriage at the station , and two low - look servant quite tipsy . their name , however , be not Samuel and Humphry , but Jacob and Robert . " 
 strange servant now come throng round , with an obsequiousness so long unknown that it quite disgust I . no familiar face among they , none whom I could bring myself to ask how my guardian be . but from their servility to I I conclude that his time be short . 
 " will you step into the small drawing - room , if you be so kind , Miss ? there be a good fire there , Miss , and a lady wait for you . " 
 " thank you . take my thing to my own little room , if you please ; that be , if you know which room be call mine . " 
 " tilly know , Miss. I will run and fetch Tilly , " cry the officious Bob . 
 " if Matilda Jenkins be still here , let she answer my bell as long as I remain . " 
 and therewith I be show into the room where the lady be expect I . she sit with she back to the door , and I could only see that she be richly attire in full evening dress . there be a powerful smell of vinegar in the room , and two pastile be burn . as I walk round the table she rise with some reluctance , and I confront Mrs. Daldy . 
 CHAPTER XVI . 
 we stand for a moment , examine each other . she be fatten nicely on what she call " holy converse and spiritual outpouring at Cheltenham . " she rush forward with great enthusiasm . 
 " why , Clara , darling , be it possible ? can this be you - so grown , and improve in every way ? I never should have know you , I do declare ! why , you have quite a brilliant colour , and your eye , and your hair - oh dear , how proud your sweet mother would have be ! you lovely creature , I must have a kiss ! what , not even your pretty hand ? " 
 " no , Mrs. Daldy ; never more my hand to a person who dare to insult my father . I you might have insult a thousand time , and I would have forgive you . " 
 " come now , let bygone be bygone , that be a dear . oh for a little more of the essence of Christianity ! let we stoop to the hem of the garment of the meek and lowly " - I will not write the sacred name she use - " let we poor grovel fellow - sinner - " 
 " do not   couple I with yourself , I beg . " I be lose my temper , and she see her advantage . 
 " not even as a sinner , dear ? I think in my humility that we all be sinner . " 
 " so we be ; but not all hypocrite . " 
 she keep her temper wonderfully , in all except her eye . 
 " ah , you impetuous young people can not understand the chasten lowly heart , which nothing but heavy trial and the grace of God produce . you know , Clara , you never could . " 
 this last truth be put in the form of an exclamation , and in such a different tone from the rest , moreover it be so true , that I could hardly help smile . 
 " since last I see you , I have be try severely and chastise most heavily . I bow to the rod . all work together for our spiritual good . until that bless day , when all the sheaf - " 
 " Mrs. Daldy , I as well have see and suffer much since last we meet . if I could not be hoodwink then by this sham religion , be it likely that I can be now ? I wonder that you waste your time so . " 
 the truth be that she talk in this strain less from hope than habit . 
 " then if I must treat you , Miss Vaughan , but as a sister worldle , let we at least combine , for Providence have see fit to make our interest the same . " 
 " how so ? " I be do my utmost to bear with her awhile . 
 " first , before I tell you anything , have you as keen an eye for the perception of your own sweet interest as for the discovery of what you kindly call ' hypocrisy ? ' ah well , it be all for my good . " 
 her rolling compendious eye glisten at the thought that she be about to catch I here . I pretend to be catch already . 
 " what of it , if I have ? " 
 " then I will tell you something . sit down by I , Clara . " 
 " thank you , I will stand . " 
 " now first , before I tell you anything , we must make some little arrangement for our mutual benefit , and then resolve upon united action . you must give I one little pledge . that be do I will tell you everything , and it be of the last importance to you . " 
 " be it about my father ? ' 
 " no . it have nothing to do with he ; it be about your uncle , who now lie at the door of death . all , it be all for the good . there be , I fear , no chance of his recovery , and the disposal of this splendid property be in our hand , if we know how to play our card , and if we act together . but there be no time to be lose . only think , 15,000 l.   a year , for it be now worth every farthing of that , besides this beautiful place . why , Clara , all the pleasure of life will be at our foot ! " 
 in her greedy excitement , she forget all her piety ; but I like she well so . in a moment she see that she have lay her wicked heart too open . in my eye there be no co - partner flash of avarice . 
 " what be the matter with my poor uncle ? " 
 " first a paralytic stroke ; since that low gastric fever , and entire prostration . do you remember when you come to your dear mother 's funeral ? " 
 " of course , I do . " 
 " and could you help observe how altered he be even then ? the hour he hear of her death , he be seize with violent illness , yet he would go out of door alone , on the very day of the funeral . something then excite he ; he come home worse , and in the night be visit with a slight paralytic stroke . however , he quite recover the use of his limb for a time , though never his former spirit - if we can call they spirit . for several month he go about as usual , except that instead of a horse he ride a quiet pony . he see to the property , receive the Michaelmas rent , and invest large sum of money both in land and the fund ; he even commence some great improvement , for he have always be , as you know , a most skilful and liberal steward and manager . " 
 " that I never deny . there could not be a well one . " 
 " but suddenly , after no Christmas festivity   for he would hear of none , for the sake of your dear mother , he be find on the morning of the last day in the year bolt upright in his study chair , and fully dress , with two pistol , load and cock , on the table , no sign of life in his face or pulse , his body stiff yet limp , like a sand - bag tightly stuff . the man who find he describe it well than I can . ' poor master , whichever way I put he , there he stop , like a french dog do trick . " 
 " how terrible ! " 
 " yes , but it be true . at first they think it be catalepsy only ; but when that pass off , paralysis remain . I want to send for you at once . " 
 here she meet , for she could not help it , but do not answer , my gaze ; and I know it be a lie . 
 " however , I be over - rule ; and your poor uncle lie bed - ridden , but in no actual danger , until this horrid low fever come . he must have a frame of iron to have bear up as he have . the doctor say this fever be partly from the prostration of the nerve . " 
 " who be the doctor ? " I feel almost as if I could love my uncle . 
 " a very eminent man . his name be Churchyard . " 
 " that be not our old medical man . where do this gentleman come from ? " 
 " Cheltenham , I believe . " 
 " surely , you must know that , if he be an eminent man ; live there yourself ! " 
 I see that she have bring he . 
 " well , " she answer sharply , " it matter little where he come from , and I have not verify his residence . I fear all the doctor in Europe could not save your poor dear uncle . " and here   from habit when death be think of   she fall into the hypocritical vein once more - " ah , how true it be ! the thing that will most avail he now , when his poor sinful frame be perish , and the old man with all its work - " 
 " thank you . I know all that . which room do my uncle occupy ? " 
 " surely , you never would think of disturb he at midnight ! " 
 " do death look what o'clock it be ? if he be really die , I must see he at once . " 
 she seem resolve to prevent I . I be determined to do it . it be needless to tell all her stratagem , and needless to say   unless I have fail to depict myself   that they prove utterly vain . I be only surprised that she do not come with I . 
 CHAPTER XVII . 
 how vast the room appear to I , how endless the main passage , after the dimension long familiar at Tossil 's Barton , and Mrs. Shelfer 's . I even fear to lose the way , where my childish foot have measure every step . first I hurry to my own snug room , or room - for I have parlour and bedroom adjoining - in the western wing , where mother use to live . everything there be in beautiful order , a lamp and a good fire light ; and Matilda Jenkins meet I at the door . 
 directly after our departure for Devonshire , Mr. Vaughan have think fit to discharge all the old servant , except the housekeeper and Matilda . they be all in league against he , for they could not bear that the " rightful owner , " whom they have know so long , should be eject . moreover , his discipline be far more stern than our ; for my father and mother have always rule by love . the housekeeper , a great friend of mine , be retain from respect and policy , and poor Tilly   who enter life through a dust - bin   from contempt of her insignificance . by that time she have rise to the rank of scullery - maid and deputy dishwasher ; now she have climb in the social scale to the position of under - housemaid . 
 " why , Matilda , how well you look , and how smart ! I declare you be get quite tall . I suppose the new time agree with you well than the old . " 
 " oh do not   say that , Miss Clara , please do not ! I would tear the gownd off my back " - look savagely at the neat print - " if I think it make you think that . no , I get a little more wage , but a deal more work , and I never get a kind word . oh it do my heart good to see you here again , in your own house , Miss Clara dear , and evil to they as drive you out " - and she lift the corner of her new white muslin apron ; - " and I have tend your room all myself , though it be not   in my part , and never let no one else touch they , ever since I be take from the kitchen , and always a jug full of flower , Miss , because you be so fond of they . " 
 " thank you , Matilda . how kind of you , to be sure ! " 
 " many be the time I have   cry over they , Miss , and the new shilling you give I , when we be little girl together . but please to call I ' tilly , ' Miss , the same as you always use . " 
 " I can not   stop to talk to you now , Tilly ; how be Mrs. Fletcher ? " 
 " quite hearty , Miss , all but the rheumatic . ah , she do suffer terrible from they . Us both wait up , Miss , and I to and fro the door , till the carriage come home ; and then she go off to bed , and I be up with she , and never know when you come . but she be get up now , Miss , to come here to see you . " 
 " go and stop she , at once . I will see she to - morrow . stop , show I first your master 's room ; knock gently and bring out the nurse . the doctor be go I believe . " 
 " yes , Miss , he leave here at eight o'clock , for he have a long way to drive , and he could not   do nothing more . but you must not go , Miss , oh pray , Miss , do not   go there ! " 
 we go along the passage , until we come to the door . I be surprised to see a new door across the lobby , very closely fit . there be an inner door also , and the nurse do not seem very wakeful . instead of knock again , Matilda retreat hastily . at last the nurse appear , and I find she to be a very respectable woman , who have be with my mother , through several attack of illness . a dark suspicion , which I have scarcely confess to myself , be partly allay hereby . after whisper for a few moment , she lead I into the dimly light room , and to my uncle 's bed . 
 I start back in terror . prepare as I be for a very great change , what I see astound I . the face so draw and warp aside , wither and yet pulpy , with an undercast of blue ; the line of the mouth so trenched and livid , that the screw lip be like a bull' - eye in a blue diamond pane ; and the hair , so dark and curly when last I see he , now shred in patch of waxy gray . the only sign of life I see , be a feeble twitching of the bed - clothe , every now and then . the poor eye be closed , hard , and wrinkle round ; one waste arm lie on the quilt , the hand bend up at the wrist , the finger clutch yet flabby , and as cold as death . it be a sight for human pride to cower at , and be quell . 
 " be he like this always ? " 
 " no , " she reply , " but he have be so now for ten hour and more : generally he be take with pain and thirst , every six hour ; and it make my heart ache to hear he moan and cry . " 
 " do he say anything particular then ? " 
 God know I be not pursue my own fell purpose in ask this . thank he , I be not such a fiend as that . all I wish be to relieve he whom I pity so . 
 " yes , he open his eye and stare , and then he always say , and he try to shake his head only he be not   strong enough , ' my fault , ah I , my fault , and to rob they too ! if I could but see she , if I could but see she , and die ! ' he always say that first , and then that exhaust he so , he can hardly say ' water ' after , and then he moan so melancholy , and then he go off again . " 
 the tear stand in her eye , for she have a tender heart . I burst into my usual violent flood , for I never have any half - crying . 
 " have you any medicine to give he ? " 
 " no , Miss , no more ; he have take a shopful already , though he can only swallow at the time he wake up . the doctor say to - night he could do no more ; this awful black fever must end in mortification ; no medicine move it at all . " 
 " do the doctor call it black fever ? " 
 " yes , the very bad form of typhu of the real irish type , such as they have have once or twice in Manchester . it have settle most on the stomach , but all the blood be poison . " 
 and she sprinkle herself , and the bed again , with disinfect fluid , and throw some over I . 
 " excuse I , Miss , you would not   allow I , so I be bind not to ask you . you know you come in dead against my will , and dead against all order " - this be what the whispering have be about - " and if anything happen to you , Miss Vaughan , who be to have all the property , but that bad Mrs. Daldy ? " 
 oh ! in a moment I see the whole ; though it be too black for belief , blacker than any fever that fester the human heart . this be the purpose with which that woman have send for I . she have lie to I as to the character of the disease . she have oppose I , because she know it the sure way to urge I . she have bring I too at night , when fever be doubly infectious . 
 " you see , Miss , we be force to keep the three window open , and the passage door all close . it be a wonder I have any of the fluid left , for they never send it up this afternoon ; but I have a drop put by , no thank to they for the same . Mrs. Daldy bring the first nurse , but she run clean away when the fever take the turn ; and they be force to send for I , for nobody else would come near he . but my poor old man have no work , and I have   minded as bad a case as this , and they say I be fever - proof . but you , Miss , you ; I should never forgive myself , if anything happen to you , and in your youth and bloom . though I could not stop you , you know I do my good . and they say you catch thing most when you come off a journey . " 
 " Jane , whatever happen , you be not to blame . I have no fear whatever ; and now I be here , I will stay . it be safe so , both for myself and other . " 
 " well , Miss , so I have hear say . once in for it , keep to the air . but come into this little room , if you want to talk to I , Miss. we can hear the poor gentleman move , or even sigh ; and the air be a little fresher there . but we must keep the window open . " 
 she lead I into the dressing room ; but even there the same crawling creep smell pervade , as if a grave have be open , when the ground be full of gas . instead of talk to the nurse , I begin to think . it break upon I vaguely , that I have hear of some very simple remedy for a fever of this nature , and that my dear mother , who in her prosperous time be the village doctoress , have be acquaint with the case . but in the whirl of my brain , I could not bring to mind what it be . oh what would I give , only to think of it now ! though not , I be sorry to say , at all of a pious turn   at least if Mrs. Daldy be so , in the strong feeling of the moment , I fall upon my knee , and pray for help . so have my mother teach I , and Mother Nature teach I now . I will not be so daring as to say that my prayer be answer . perhaps it be only that it calm my mind . 
 " Jane , have they be brew lately ? " alas the bathos ! but I can not   help it . 
 " yes , Miss ; last Thursday and Friday . they will not   let I go near the kitchen part : but I know it all the same . " 
 " go and get I a nice jug of fresh yeast . I will watch your master . " 
 she star , and hesitate ; but see that I be in earnest . 
 " I do not   know where to find it , Miss ; and none of they will come near I ; and they will stop I too if they can . why they will not   bring my food to the door , but put it half - way down the passage . they want to lock I in , only I would not   stand that ; and they break all the plate and dish , and to - day they send word that my dinner must come in at the window to - morrow . " 
 " low coward and zany ! now find the yeast , Jane , if you have to search for an hour . they must all be go to bed now , except Matilda Jenkins ; and she dare not stop you if you say you have my order . " 
 " bless you , Miss ; she will run away as if I be a ghost . " 
 " then call to she , that I say she must go to bed directly . " 
 after a few more word , Jane go her way stealthily , like a thorough - breed thief ; and I be leave alone with my poor die uncle . wonderful as it seem to I , I feel now a tender affection for he , I the resolute , the consistent , the bitter Clara Vaughan . even if he have tell I that moment , that he have plot my father 's death , I would have perille my life for his ; because I should have know that he be sorry . yet I be full of cold fear , lest he should awake to consciousness , and utter that awful cry , while I alone be with he , in the dead hour of night . 
 soon than I expect , the nurse come back with a jug of beautiful yeast , smell as fresh as daybreak . we put it outside the window on the stone sill , to keep it cool and airy . she have see no one except Matilda , who be wait for I , and cry dreadfully , predict my certain death , and her own too ; if she should have to attend I . she keep at a most respectful distance from Jane ; and , with all her affection , be glad to be clear of I for the night . 
 for nearly two hour , the nurse and I sit watch , with hardly a speak word , except that I ask one question . 
 " how often have Mrs. Daldy be to see my uncle ? " 
 " she would hardly leave his bedside , until the fever declare itself . since then she have not be once . " 
 Broad awake at that strange hour , and in that strange way , I begin to pass through the stereoscope of my brain the many strange slide of my life . of all of these , the last for the moment seem the strange . suddenly we hear a low feeble moan . run into the bedroom , there we see the poor sick one with his eye wide open , vainly attempt to rise . I put my arm around he , and raise he on the pillow . he try to say ' thank you , ' for he be always a gentleman in his manner ; then he gaze at I with hazily wonder eye . then he open his mouth in a spasmodic way , and begin that bitter cry . 
 Ere he close his mouth again , I pour well into his throat a table - spoonful of yeast , hand to I by Jane . to my great pleasure , it glide beyond the black tongue ; and I give he two more spoonful , while he be star at I with a weak and rigid amazement . 
 " no water , Jane , not a drop of water ! it will work far well alone . he do not   know what it be , and he think he have have his water . keep he thirsty that he may take more . " 
 as he lie thus in my arm , I feel that one side be icily cold , and the other fiery hot . his face look most ghastly and livid , but there be not that mystical gray upon it , like the earth - shine on the moon , which show when the face of man be death 's mirror , and the knee of death on man 's heart . in a minute he slide from my grasp , down on the pillow again , and , with a long - draw sigh , become once more stiff and insensible . my hope be faint indeed , but still it be hope : if he have hope 's vitality , he might yet be save . 
 the rest of that night be pass by the nurse and myself in heavy yet break sleep . Jane assure I that there be no chance of my poor uncle become conscious again , for at least six hour . I be loth to forego my watch , and argue that the dose we have give might cut short this interval ; but lo - while I keep repeat at weary and weary period , that I could do no harm , since the physician give up , and I might do good - sleep , the lover of repetition , lay his hand alike on my formula and myself . Dear Judy 's howl be in my dream , and Mrs. Shelfer 's never cease prattle . 
 CHAPTER XVIII . 
 cold and fresh be the morning air , and the open window invite the sound of country life . who could think of fever with the bright dew sparkle on the lawn , the lilac bud grow fat enough to claim their right of shadow , the pleasant ring of the sharpen scythe , and the swish sweep of the swathe ? from the stable - yard , round the corner , come the soothe hiss of the groom , the short stamp of the lively steed   I fancy I know my own favourite " Lilla " , and the gruff " stand still , mare , wull'e ? " far down the avenue whistle the cowboy , waddle - footed , on his way to the clover ley , or the milkmaid sing with the pail on her hip , and the deer come troop and stoop their horn along . be it not one of my own pet robin , who hop on the window - sill , peer bravely at himself in the jug , and try to remember the last of his winter note ? 
 but it be cold , Jane , very cold indeed ; and we have never be to bed ; and now the mower have descry we , why do they stop their work , and shake their head together so , and keep outside the ranunculus bed , and agree that the grass beneath our window do not require cut ? why , if they be Papists , they would cross themselves , and that save many an oath . but the grass do want cutting , Jane . it can not have be cut for a week . I will call to they . no , it might disturb my uncle . 
 there be no sound from the bed - room yet : all deep and deadly silence . I will go and see . 
 there my patient lie , just as when I see he first , except that I have arrange the wreck of his hoary lock , and apply a lotion to his temple on the burn side . and yet , now I look close , the face be not quite so livid ; or be it the difference between the candle - light and the morning ray ? 
 even while I look , he start up , as if my eye revive he . he do not moan or cry ; but open wide his filmy eye , and gaze feebly and placidly at I . for a time he do not know I : then a great change gradually creep through his long faltering gaze . fear the effect of excitement upon he , I try to divert his attention by another good dose of yeast . three time he take it with resignation like a well - train child , but his eye all the time intent on I . presently they begin to swim and swerve ; the effort of the faint blood - tissue brain and the exertion of swallowing have be too much for his shattered power . he fall off again into the comatose state , but with a palpable difference . the pulse , which have throb on the hot side only , could now be feel most feebly move in the other wrist , and the tension of the muscle be relax : circulation be be restore and balanced , and the breathing could now be trace , short as it be and irregular . 
 I have not time to describe all the symptom of gradual improvement , and I have not the medical knowledge needful to do so clearly . enough that the six - hour interval be shorten that day by half , that the breathing become more regular , and a soft perspiration break through the clogged and clammy pore . Jane want to second this by an additional blanket , but I fear to allow it in a case of so utter prostration . when the perspiration be over , then I prescribe the blanket for fear of a chill reaction . 
 at every return of consciousness , our patient make an effort to speak , but I hush he with my hand on his lip , and he even manage to smile , when he find that I would be obey . in the evening he try to open his arm to I , and then try to push I away , in some faint recollection of the nature of his disorder . to I the interest be so intense , and the delight so deep , that if I have lose he now , it would surely have break my heart . 
 at sunset of that day , as nurse and I sit near the dressing - room window , watch the slant ray flicker on the sward , and the rook alight and swing over their noisy nest , a black cloud hang for a moment just above the sun , a black cloud with a vivid edge of gold . it temper the light in a peculiar manner , and seem to throw it downwards . peer through my finger at it , for it be very beautiful , I see a whitish mist or vapour steam and hover above the disk of the set sun , between my eye and that golden marge . I wonder what this could be ; there be no heat to cause strong evaporation , nor any mist or dewy haze about , nor be the sun " draw water . " but what I see be like that trembling twinkle of the air , which we often observe on a meadow footpath in the hot forenoon of July . I draw Jane 's attention to it , not expect any solution , but just for something to say . 
 " dear I , Miss , do not   you know what that be ? I see it every evening ; it will be twice as plain when the sun go down , and then it will be quite white . " 
 " well , what be it ? why can not   you tell I ? be everything here a secret ? " 
 I be rather irritable , but vex with myself for be so . too much excitement and too little sleep be the cause . 
 " no , Miss , there be no secret at all about that . every one know what that be . it be only the scum that rise through the grass from the arch pool that take all the drain of the house . some of the arch fall in they say , and the ground shake when they mow it ; they be afraid to roll there . " 
 " be it possible ? and you know it , a practised nurse like you ! do my uncle know it ? " 
 " I be sure , Miss , I can not   tell : most likely not , or he would have have it mend , he hat thing out of repair . but it can not   do any harm , with the mould and the grass above it . " 
 " can not   it indeed ? and you can see it rise . shut all the bedroom window in a moment , Jane . I will shut this . " 
 she think my wit be wander , from what I have go through ; nevertheless she obey I . 
 it happen that I have attend , at Isola 's urgent request , one lecture of the many deliver by Dr. Ross . she forget what the subject be to be . it prove to be an unsavoury and " unlady - like " one - Mephitis . Isola want to run away , but I have none of that nonsense about I , when human life be concern , and listen with great attention , and even admiration ; for he handle the matter eloquently and well . 
 " now , Jane , throw all the door open , and the lobby window that look in the other direction . when do you think it will be possible to move our poor patient from these room ? the air here be deadly poison . " 
 " well I be sure , Miss ! and he could not   have a nice nor a more airy room ; and all my thing in order too , and so handy , and so many cupboard ! " 
 " out of this poison he must go . when can he be move ? " 
 " well , Miss , he might be move to - morrow , if we could only get plenty of hand , and do it cleverly . " 
 " surely we can have plenty of hand . there use to be twenty - five servant here ; and I have not hear that my uncle have lessen the number . " 
 " no , Miss ; but save and keep we , we shall not   get one of they here . " 
 " Nonsense ! I will have they , or they leave the house . of course I will not   peril their life . we shall only want two or three ; and they may take a bath of disinfect stuff , with all their clothe on , before they come ; and they may smoke all the while . " 
 the nurse laugh grimly , and shake her gray head . 
 " and we will fumigate , Jane , fumigate tremendously . surely englishman have more self - respect than to be such baby , and you a woman , and I a girl , sham they out of face . " 
 " it do not   matter , Miss ; they will not   come . I know they well , the lot I mean that be in the house now . " 
 " very well , Jane , we will have Gamekeeper Hiatt , and his eld son ; they be man I know . and if that be not enough , we will send to Gloucester for Thomas Henwood . but why do not   you open the lobby door , as I tell you ? " 
 " if you please , Miss , I can not . they have fasten it outside . " 
 " do you mean to say that they have dare to lock we in ? " 
 " indeed I do , Miss ; we have be fasten in since the morning . " 
 " and pray , why do you not tell I ? ' 
 " because I fear to excite you , Miss. I know your temper when you be wrong , ever since you be that high ; and in this fever air , excitement be sure to kill you . brute ! but I suppose they do not   know it . " 
 " they know it well ; at least the master - spirit do . and for that very reason I will crush my indignation . since I be that high , Jane , I have pass through much tribulation , and have drop my lady - heiress tone . I can now command myself . " 
 " then , Miss , I will show you what they send this morning , round the handle of the coffee - jug . I be afraid to let you see it before . " she give I a twist of paper , on which be write as follow : - 
 " for the safety of the household , Mrs. Fletcher order that the person in the fever - room be allow no communication with the other servant . the intercept door be fasten , because a most sinful un - christian act be perpetrate last night . due supply will be deliver once a - day , at 10 A.M. no empty vessel and no correspondence receive . any attempt to break these rule will be punish by suspension of supply . servant be forbid to come beneath the sick - room window . may the Lord have you in his keeping , in his tender mercy , accord to his holy will . you be request to read Philippians i. 8 - 11 inclusive . there be three holy bible on the drawer and dressing - table . " 
 when I have read this , and perceive , by the blasphemy at the end , that it could proceed from no other than that awful woman , I confess that my spirit be cow within I . not from selfish fear , nor yet from the taming of passion , but from the lowering think that I belong to the same race of be as the author of such satanity . presently , I become too indignant to speak , or even think . it add , if that be possible , to my indignation , that I have see she leave the house , about nine o'clock that morning , in our good close carriage . she keep the window up until she be past the lawn and the light iron gate , beyond the arcade of rose ; then , at the first turn in the avenue , she let down the glass and gracefully kiss her hand to I . I do not believe , however , that she be go back to Cheltenham . with so much at stake in our house , and depend on her direction , she would surely stop in the neighbourhood , if only to watch the course of event . 
 soon than I dare to expect , I regain the command of myself ; horror within I be strong than wrath , and strong than either become the resolve to survive and win . " there can be no God , " I exclaim , in my presumptuous ignorance , " if this scheme of the devil be permit to triumph . " 
 first I try the door , which sever we from the rest of the house . my uncle 's room be in the western wing , very near those which my dear mother have occupy , and not very far from my own . they form one floor of the western gable ; the three bedroom window and that of the dressing - room look to the west , while the great lobby window , from which I have see Mrs. Daldy 's departure , look southward along the avenue , the curve of which could be see also from the bedroom window . an oaken door , at the end of the main passage , cut off the room in this storey of the gable from all the rest of the house . this door Jane have leave lock from the inside , fear lest other should lock she in , as they have threaten to do . but now we find that a strong iron bolt have be fix upon the outside , while we be asleep in the morning , and that we have no chance of force it . 
 next I ask Jane , whether she think that the house , now Mrs. Daldy be go , would be still in the hand of our enemy . would not Mrs. Fletcher at once re - assert her authority ? might not Matilda Jenkins be expect to fly to the rescue ? the nurse , know all the politic of the servant ' hall , assure I that there be no hope of either of these event . Robert , a drunken Wesleyan , turn out of the sect in Cheltenham , be Mrs. Daldy 's lieutenant , and would take all care of Matilda , to whose good grace he have be make overture . as for Mrs. Fletcher , she be probably in the same plight as ourselves . from what I hear about Robert , I begin to believe that he have private order to disown I at the station , for the double purpose of yield a tit - bit of insolence , and warning of my arrival . 
 however , that matter very little ; but out of those room I must get , either by door or by window ; and that , too , without delay . do they expect to triumph so easily over Clara Vaughan ? and in her father 's house ? the window be about twenty foot from the ground , as nearly as I could guess , and the room beneath be empty . at once I resolve to attempt an escape that way , and to do so before the moon , which be southe now , should shine on the western aspect . Good Jane be terrify at the thought ; and then , upon my persisting , implore I to let she make the attempt , if it must be make at all . 
 " now , Jane , no more , if you please . we can not   waste time about that . you have a husband partly dependent upon you , and several child to think of . for I nobody care . " but I hop somebody do . " and you know I be far more active and much light than you be . help I out with the feather bed . " 
 the little bed in the dressing - room , which she have to sleep on , be speedily bring to the window , and drop just underneath it . it fall upon the grass with a pleasing and quiet flop . then the two strong bell - rope , already cut down and plait together , be tie round the bar of the double window sash , the low sash be throw up to the full extent , the glass press quietly out with a pair of wet towel , and the splinter remove , so as not to cut the rope . the latter still fail to reach more than half - way to the ground , but I would venture the drop if I could only descend so far . after wind a linen sheet around my body and dress , with the end tie round one ankle , so as to leave I free use of my limb , I sit upon the window - sill in the broad shadow , and calculate my chance . should I begin the descent with face , or with back , to the wall ? face to the wall I resolve on , for though I should have to drop backward so , yet what I fear most of all be have the back of my head crush against the house . next to this I dread a sprain of the ankle , but all our family be well - knit and straight in the joint . 
 so I launch myself off , begin as gently as could be , Jane have firm hold of one hand , until I be well on the voyage . though not well verse in calisthenic art , I get on famously almost as far as the end of the rope , keep away from the wall by the over - saling of the window - sill , and the rapid use of my foot . then I rest a moment on a project ledge - call , I believe , a ' string - course " - and away hand below hand again . but I strike my knuckle terribly against that string - course , and very nearly lose hold from the pain of the blow ; then bend my body forward I give one good push at the wall , and shut both eye , I believe , let go the rope altogether . Backward I fall , and roll over upon the feather bed . I be not even stun , but fear for a moment to try if my limb be sound . 
 there I sit and strip off the wind sheet . presently , up I get , and , in my triumph , alas ! could not help cry " all right , Hurrah ! " like a foolish little child . in a moment I see that my cry have be hear , where it should not have be . a rapid flitting of light along the low window and in the stableyard , and I know that chase would be give . 
 but after leave my father 's house in such a dignified manner , be it likely that I would give in and be catch ? now , Clara , you could beat all your nurse in run , off and away like the wind ! away I go full speed towards the shade of the avenue , while Jane have the wit to scream out of the window , " help ! help ! here be the house on fire ! " this make some little diversion ; I have a capital start , and it be but half a mile to the lodge where old Whitehead live . once there , I should care for nobody . I must have escape very easily , for my foot seem as swift as a deer 's ; but , as my luck would have it , the light iron gate between the lawn and the park be fasten . what on earth should I do ? I see man run across the lawn , and , what be bad , they see I . in vain I pull at the gate ; they rattle , but would not yield . have I own true presence of mind , I should have walk boldly up to the man , and dare they to touch I fresh from the fever - room . in the flurry of the moment I never think of that , but dart into the shrubbery , and crouch among thick laurel . presently I hear they rush down the main drive and begin the search , with some heavy swearing . two of they come to the very clump I be hide in , and push a pitchfork almost into my side , but the stupid fellow have lantern , which blind they to the moonlight . on they go with grumbling and growling , which tell I exactly where to shun they . judge at length , from the silence , that the search have pass to the right , I slip from my tangled lair , and glide away to the left , beyond the shrubbery spring , where a little gate , as I know , lead to a glade in the park . the deep ha - ha which I have fear to jump in the dark , because of the loose stone at the bottom , be here succeed by a high oak pal , and probably through that gate have come the murderer of my father . 
 with a cold shudder at the remembrance , I steal along through the shadowy place , and have almost reach the little gate , when I see two of the searcher come straight towards I . to the right of I be the park - pal , on the leave a breastwork of sod , which I could not climb without be clearly see ; to fly be to meet the enemy ; should I yield , and be baffle after all ; insult too , most likely , for I know that the man be tipsy ? 
 in my hand be the tightly - wound sheet , use as a rope to confine my dress . I have fold it short and carry it , on the chance of its prove useful . in a moment I be under the paling in deep shadow , with the white sheet throw around I , fall from my forehead , and drape artistically over the right arm . stock still I stand against the black board , and two great coil of long black hair flow down the wind sheet . the man come up , tired of the chase , and grumble ; and by their voice I know they for my good friend Jacob and Bob . suddenly , they espy a tall , white figure , of tremendous aspect . they stop short , both tongue and foot , and I distinctly hear their tooth chatter . with a slow and spectral motion , I raise my drape white arm , and fetch a low , sepulchral moan . down fall the lantern , and , with a loud yell , away go the man , as hard as their leg could carry they . 
 laugh heartily , I refolde my sheet , and take the short cut across the park to the lodge where old Whitehead live , arrive , without have meet even my old friend " Tulip . " 
 the old man , in hot indignation , draw forth his battered musket - for he have once be in the militia - and swear that he would march upon the - - rogue at once . instead of that I send he for the two Hiatts , and the village constable ; and soon , without invitation , half the village attend . with my tear dress tuck up by good Mrs. Whitehead , and a hat on my head , newly buy for her clean little grandchild , I set forth again in the moonlight , at the head of a faithful army , to recover my native home . 
 Hiatt easily open the gate , which have defy my flurry effort , and we present ourselves at the main entrance , a force that would frighten a castle . it be needless to say that we carry all before we . the state of siege be rescind , Mrs. Fletcher and Tilly set free , all the ringleader turn away neck and crop , and what be far more important , my poor uncle remove , without be conscious of it , to a sweet and wholesome room . the sturdy Gloucestershire yeoman scorn all idea of danger . 
 tired with all my adventure , before I sleep that night - still near my uncle 's bed - two reflection come dreamily over my mind . 
 the first be a piece of vanity . " ah , Mrs. Daldy , you little know Clara Vaughan ! " 
 the second be , " dear I , how Conrad would be astonish at this ! and how strange that his father should thus have save my uncle 's life ! for he must have die , if leave in that noisome room . " 
 CLARA VAUGHAN 
 BOOK IV . 
 CHAPTER I. 
 before that week be over , my uncle could sit up in bed for a short time every day , be duly prop in a downy nest of pillow . one arm , however , remain quite impotent , and part of one side rigid and numb . his recovery be slow and tedious , as might well be expect with one who have be drag not from the jaw but the very throat of death . for a long time also his mind be feeble and dim , a mirror overcast by the vapour of the body . 
 to I , who be fond of observe , in my own little childish way , it be interesting as well as delightful to note how , day by day , the mind and body , hand in hand , rise strong . More than all be I teach , and humble in my own conceit , by take heed how tardily come back the power to guide and control the imagination . that object - glass of the mind - not achromatic even in first - rate intellect - have long be out of the focal distance from the lens of reason 's eye . upon it have be glance loose distorted image , render home imperfectly , if at all , to the retina of the brain . Herein its state be the very opposite to that of my own phrenoscope . I have no large imagination ; but the image it present be vivid , and I see well round they . every one of they be not cast , but cut , on my sensorium . whether I can strike they off in word - whether my telegraph can print its message - be quite another question , and beside its purpose . have render home to I the idol   oftentime invert , though distinct   it leave expression and judgment to do their good with the copyright . 
 now , both in fabric and in mould , my uncle 's mind be different . naturally his power be far superior , but he seem to take no pride in they . no dark and settle purpose have ever throw its shadow , and even its weight , upon they ; nor have they feel , so far as I know , the rough grasp of adversity . therefore they be long in recover from the blow , than I think my own would have be . 
 there be few thing , among the many desire by Mrs. Daldy , which she fail to reconcile with her strong sense of religion . " there be not one " - I have hear she say - " not one of the thing we believe to be for our good , which we should scruple to lay before the Throne of Grace . even the throbbing of that little unregenerate heart " - Clara Vaughan 's to wit , who have kick she that morning , quite by accident of course - " even they be know and sift there " - slight confusion of metaphor cause by strong conviction of sin - " infinite mercy know the thing that be for our edification , and confirmation in the faith . yes , backslide sinner , the want of real heart - feel spiritual life can be supply by prayer alone . be it not so in your experience , elder ? " " Prayer , my dear Madam , and search of the heart . oh the depth of the wickedness of the unconverted heart ! " and he take another glass of sherry . that night I remember she work very hard , for she ; and the next day she present I with marker the size of a gallow , progge with many hole ; on one be do in cross - stitch , " pray without cease , " and upon the other " wrestle thou in prayer . Gen. xxxii . 24 . " both of these I throw into the fire there before her eye . 
 from this it will be clear , that in her devotion she still remember I , and doubtless pray in good Scriptural phraseology for my release from this wicked world . Dr. Churchyard 's last report have raise her terror to the high pitch , and instead of wrestle in prayer , she have run away in high panic , upon hear that the fever - nurse be see at large the night before . " we must use the mean of grace , " she say to Mrs. Fletcher , before she lock she in , " and accept the mercy vouchsafe to we . and it would be sinful , dear Mrs. Fletcher , in I to neglect such a warning as this . " 
 it be wise , as well as righteous , in she to keep aloof for a time , while her device work their consummation . for the present it appear to I that they be fail signally . my uncle be regain strength of mind and body ; while native air , a sense of triumph , and daily exercise , keep I in bloom health . my patient , who otherwise could hardly bear I to leave he for an hour , insist upon my take a long ride every day . Lilla be charm , and so be I , with the sweet spring air , and the rich familiar scenery . and how it do make I eat ! thankful indeed I ought to be , and be , that it please God to spare I that awful and deadly pestilence . but the bad injury do by cant hypocrite be , that the repulsion they create drive away other from good . truly I may say , that for day after be in contact with that slimy sanctity , I could not say my own prayer , as a little child should do . 
 of that fever there have be three fatal case in the village , before it enter our house ; and I find that it be spread rapidly . with my uncle 's authority , I have the drainage survey and amend at once ; and so the p be stay . of course we do not neglect our own weak point ; and the crawl noisome smell be no long perceive in the room , nor the white vapour on the grass . 
 and so three week go by ; no news from London or Devonshire , no explanation between my uncle and myself , no arrangement as to my expectation in life . as yet my uncle be too weak to bear any sort of excitement , and seem desirous only to be passive in my hand . his eye always follow I to every part of the room , and he would even be prop on the sofa to see I ride down the avenue ; and there I always find he watch for my return . meanwhile I yearn to be once more in a certain little room with a north aspect , opposite a cheesemonger 's shop in an obscure street of London . nightly I dream of Giudice , and daily I dream of dear Isola and Conrad . the dog in the stableyard , who have hitherto own no especial attraction for I , suddenly find himself pet , and coax , and feed   which he think much more of   to the scandal of Mrs. Fletcher , and the great alarm of the groom , who would rather not have I there . moreover , the dog himself , though I strove to invest he with every chivalrous attribute , be of a low and ungenial order , adorn with no grace of mind , and little taste , except for bone and gravy . but perhaps my standard be too high : peradventure I even commence with more prejudice than a bulldog 's . be that as it may - and if I can see round thing , I ought to see round myself - every day fall heavier and heavy from the fair balance of time ; and every night the star - for there be now no moon - look weary in the heaven , and less incline for business . how long , how long shall you go round the pole in your steady pacing way , as if the sky be for auction , and you be pace the lot ; while I , with more fire in I than you can strike or steal , be ditch like a glow - worm kick under a dock - leaf , and see no polestar at all ? 
 here be May , the height of May : I be full of life and spirit : the garb of death , like an April cloud , blow over . let I see . last birthday I be eighteen : I have know more trouble than year , and enjoy no youth as yet . last year I spend in grow , and pining , and starve . now the Power , that balance earth and heaven , have fill I with joy and light . 
 neither be I renegade to my life , in open wide my heart to this flood of love and happiness . still be I set upon one strong purpose . still be I swear , and will not repent , that if filial duty demand it , I will trample love under my foot , and cut the throat of happiness . 
 during most of this time , I have no idea where the queen of hypocrite be ; though doubtless she know all that be happen to we . as soon as he hear of my uncle 's surprising rally , Dr. Churchyard come over , and claim all the merit for his own last prescription . bring face to face with the awkward fact that the medicine have not be procure , he be not in the least disconcert , but find that we have misunderstood he , the prescription to which he refer be the one before the last . at any rate , he enhance his own fame immensely , and become " instrumental under Providence " in kill more people than ever . in reply to Mrs. Fletcher , for I would not deign to ask he , he state that the excellent and devoted Mrs. Daldy have not be see lately in Cheltenham . her son , however , be there , and foremost in the rank of pump - room Lady - killers . just what he be fit for . 
 the doctor entertain a belief , and spread the report in Cheltenham , that Dorcas be lodge in a humble cot among the haunt of pestilence , imperille her life and lavish her substance to relieve the fever - stricken . this be more than I could stand , I ask the worthy doctor - who , after all , be a man of the world - what three wealthy person Dorcas have carry with she . at first he feign not to understand I , then look sly , and change the subject cleverly . of course I refer to the well - know fact , that she support her grandeur and her son 's extravagance by play an admirable rubber . she be play a well one now . 
 Dr. Churchyard finish by write another prescription , which , after his departure , I hand to the husband of Venus , legitimate disposer of mineral medicine . 
 CHAPTER II . 
 London ! London ! be still the cry of my heart ; and be I not summon thither by duty long ago ? what might become , during all this time , of the man whom I be bind to watch at every turn , and whom I be now in a well condition to deal with ? my first visit , every morning , be to my parent ' graf , and neither of they would be there but for his ruthless hand . as I sit there how lonely I feel ! how sadly forlorn in the world , be my lot wealth or poverty , victory or defeat ! 
 one morning as I sit there my spirit be move by dream of the night before , and I vow , in that bodily but invisible presence , that none , except one whose name I whisper , should ever kneel on that turf hand in hand with I . 
 Borne out of my usual vein by the deed myself have do , I enter the ancient church , always leave open for I , and , kneel at the altar - rail , with many a Vaughan supine in prayer , pennon , helm , and trophy round I , stately dame in marble white , and old crusader clutch still the cross - there I make my vow upon the knee - cupped stone , that if he claim I not , the race should end with I . 
 it be a presumptuous and unholy act , with all around I quell by time , with age lay aside in dust , with many a stouter heart and large mind than mine , helpless even to superintend the wasting of his tenement , with all his bygone bliss and woe , stanch love and deadly hate , less eloquent now than the fly whom the spider have catch in his skull . 
 return across the park , after a warm interview with " Tulip , " who insist mainly upon have his ear well scratch , I find my uncle in his snug wheel - chair , wait near the side - door for I to help and accompany he forth . this be our good way to take he out , because of the step at the front - door . he have not yet be in the open air since his terrible illness , but , judge by my own experience , I think that he pin for the breeze , and , after long council , it be resolve to trust he forth this day . with all his heart he be long to be out ; but , instead of express impatience , smile gratefully at I . I now observe that he have a sweet and win smile - a gift bestow not rarely on face of a sombre cast . 
 in return for it I kiss he , and we sail smoothly out . how he revel , to be sure , in the first clear breath from the lip of heaven ! stretch one poor arm forth - the other he could not move - he try to spread himself like a flower to the sun . then he draw long draught of liquid freedom , and be for a time as one intoxicate . in the glorious crystal bath he seem to float away from earth . come to himself at length , he look at I , and say , " now John may go , if he please . " a year ago he would have say , " go , John , " and no more . but illness be a great refiner . when John be out of sight he allow free vent to the tear of joy and gratitude , whereof , in my opinion , he have no call to be ashamed . I kiss he many time . my warm impassioned nature always feel for and delight in any touch like this . then he place his well hand on the cold and rigid one , lift this with that , and pour forth silent thank to the Giver of all thing . 
 " Clara , darling , " at length he say , " how can I ever show you a thousandth part of my gratitude for all the lovingkindness you have heap on I ? coal of fire , indeed ! and they have warm my selfish heart . with loathsome death before your face , in all the pride and bloom of early youth and rich - " 
 I will not repeat his word , because it would not become I ; but I be force by all that have happen to show what his feeling be . 
 " and all this for I - I who have be your bitter enemy , who have turn you out of your father 's house , and cause your mother 's death ! " here I stop he , lest he should be overcome . 
 " dear uncle , talk no more of this - never even think of it . the fault be all my own . you know I would not stop , often as you ask I . there always be a bar between we , and it be my obstinacy . " 
 " no , it be my pride . Clara , in my well mind I love you all along . how could I help admire your truth and courage and devotion to your father ? although I own that you be very bitter against I , yet , if I have only use the proper mean , I might have get the well of it . if I have tell you all my story , you would have pity more even than condemn I . but my pride forbade , and I make the common mistake of regard you as a child , because you be that in year . I forget to allow for the force power of grief . even now , pull down as I be , and humble by the wisdom of Heaven , I can not tell you my strange history without the acute pain . " 
 " then I be sure , uncle , I will never let you do it . " 
 " yes , it be my duty , and the soon do the well . rescue though I be , for the present , by your wonderful courage and skill , I feel that one more blow , even a slight one now , and time for I be end . but if it be God 's will to cut I off to - morrow , I should die in happiness , have make my peace , and win your kind forgiveness . "

 I begin to wheel he over the grass and gather flower   which " he play with like a child , to change , if possible , the current of his thought . stupid thing ! I take the wrong way to do it .

 he tremble so violently , that I fear the chair would be upset .

I can not tell , much less can you , what mischief may be do if you leave this house again . that low , crafty woman will be back again directly - she who make coward of all the household , and act the coward herself , who leave I to die in my lonely bed , while she take all my key . if her treachery succeed , I shall rise from my grave . and I know she will poison I yet , if she get the chance , and can make anything by it .  he fall back in the chair , from which , in his excitement , he have strive to rise , and for some minute there he lie insensible . when I have succeed in bring he to himself , he look at I so piteously , with so much death in his eye , that I promise , with a sink heart , never to leave he more , except upon absolute necessity , until he should be well , or need my care no long . 
 he even try to persuade I not to go to London for the thing I have leave there , but to send a trusty person to pack and bring they home . to this , however , I could not yield , feel , as I do , that , after all my love for Isola , and all her kindness to I , I be bind to see she and say farewell ; and what harm could there possibly be in so short an absence ? my uncle wish I to bring she down for a good long visit , but this at such a time could not be think of . moreover , lively , impulsive Idols would have grow very long - face in a dull sick house , which our must be for the present . it be settle at last that I should go to London the follow Monday , stay there one entire day , and come back the day after with all my trifling chattel . one thing more my uncle propose which I would not hear of . it be , that he should transfer to I , by deed of gift , all the estate , both real and personal , reserve only a small annuity for himself , and a sum of 10,000 l.   for some special purpose , which he would disclose to I at leisure . thus , he say , he should feel as if justice have be do , and there would be some security against Mrs. Daldy 's scheme . of the latter I feel no fear whatever , and think it the effect of a shaken mind that he attach so much importance to they . under no circumstance would I think , for a moment , of allow he so to divest himself . money , to any amount , I could have , though I want very little , see that now , once more , a solemn duty would withdraw I from my long pursuit , and from all the frivolity which many girl delight in . I beg my uncle to appoint an honest steward for the estate , and to assign I a moderate yearly allowance , which would save much trouble . to this he at last consent , and propose for I so large a revenue , that , after remove the last cipher , I have more than I know how to spend . the first thing I do be to send the kind farmer the residue of the sum he have lend I , together with interest at ten per cent . , which do not seem excessive , consider that he have no security . 
 and now , with the utmost anxiety , I look forward to the time when my poor uncle should be strong enough to tell I , without risk , that history of himself which he have distinctly promise I . surely it must shed some light on the mystery of my own . this thought , as well as the sense of duty , reconcile I in some measure to the suspension of my life - long search . he would have tell I everything then and there , in his warm gratitude for my undertaking ; but I durst not let he . he be already fatigue with so much talking , and when the stimulus of the fresh air be go , he suffer a serious relapse . 
 CHAPTER III . 
 on the following Monday , my poor uncle be rather well again , I set off for London , as have be determine , and arrive there late in the afternoon . it have be propose to send a servant with I , but I have be too long accustom to independence , and also have reason of my own for refuse . I be to receive , on the morrow , an account , by telegraph , of my patient 's health and spirit , and promise to give , in return , some tiding of myself . Mrs. Shelfer have not be apprise of my coming , because she would have be sure to tell Miss Isola , whom , as well as her brother , I wish to take by surprise . Dear Isola have often inquire about my family , but only know that I be an orphan , much reduce in the world , poor , and all alone . much as I love she , I know quite well that she could not keep a secret , and whenever she tease I about my " iron mask , " I retort upon she that she have first to discover the secret of her own home . 
 as we rush towards the mighty city , what a flush be in my cheek , what a flutter in my heart ! whom might I not see even upon the platform , or , at any rate , in the street , and , poverty be remove , what obstacle could there be between we ? not that I intend to resign myself to affection , and lead a life of softness , until I have discharge to the utmost my duty to the dead . yet some sort of pledge might pass - some surety there might be , that neither of we would feel thereafter quite alone in the world . but how could I tell that he even care about I ? well , I have a strong suspicion . in some thing the eye be the good detective police . only I have always be so unlucky . be it not too good luck for I ever to be true ?

I be bother if it be all lie , after all . never dream the old gal could tell a word of truth . have a high opinion of she . blow if the young woman herself be not   come ! "

" out come another man with a like appearance and air , and a blade - bone in his hand , whereat he continue to gnaw throughout the interview . it be indeed a squint with which he favour I , and neither of they would move for I to pass .

 instead of answer I , they close the door enough to put the slide - chain on , leave I still outside , where , with boiling indignation , I hear myself discuss ; the cabman look on with an experienced grin . 
 " well , Balaam , now , and what do you think of that party ? " 
 " Uncommon fine young gal , and doose mannersome too ; but it do not   follow , for all that , that the thing be on the square , you know . have she get any luggage , Balak ? " 
 " no , mate . and that look fishy , now one come to think on it . stop , let I have another look . " 
 " no ; leave that to I . slip the chain out , Balak : and keep your foot behind the door . she can not   push we both in without assault and battery . " 
 to my shame and indignation , I be subject to another critical cross - fire from half drunken eye . I turn my back and stamp in my vexation ; the cabman give I an approve nod . this little act of mine be so unmistakably genuine , and display such very nice embroidery - I do like a tasteful petticoat - that the hard heart of Balaam be soften ; at the same moment a brilliant idea steal through his cautious mind . 
 " stop now , Balak , put your foot there . she can not   push we both in , I believe ; leastway not without battery and contempt of court . now what do you think of this ? " - and he whisper to his grimy friend . 
 " well , that beat all I ever heer'd on . let you alone for brain , Balaam , and I for muscle and pluck ! " 
 " now , young lady , " begin Balaam in a diplomatic tone , " I and my mate here be in a constitution of trust , or else you may take your oath , and never a pervarication , we never would keep an agreeable young female " - here he give I two ogle intend for one - " on the flinty stone so long ; only we can not   say if you mean honest , and there be such a many bad one go , and we have   get a leary file inside . now listen to what I say . there be a dog as big as a lion in the room as you call yourn ; and he do show his tooth , and no mistake . we be afeare to show our nose there , even at the command of dooty . you can hear he growl now like all the Strand and Fleet Street ; and my mate Balak here leave half his breech behind he , save your presence , Miss , and lucky to get off so . now if so be you undertake , honour bright , to march straight into that front room , my mate and self have conclude to let you in . " 
 " of course I will , " say I , smile at their terror . so I pay the cabman , take up my little bag , and run right up the stair . Balaam and Balak fear to come round the corner . " you must unlock the door , Miss , " cry one of they , " we be force to lock he in . " 
 " oh Judy , my darling Judy , my own pet love of a Judy . " he let I say no more ; his paw be on my shoulder , and I be in a shower - bath of kiss . in the ecstasy of my joy , I forget all about the two man and their mysterious doing , and fling myself down on a chair , while Judy , out of his mind with delight , even try to sit on my lap . he whinny , and cry , and laugh , and yell , and could find no vent for his feeling , until he throw his great head back and tell all in a wow - wow , that must have be hear in Oxford Street . a little familiar knock , and Mrs. Shelfer appear , look rather well than ever . 
 " why , dear Mrs. Shelfer , how glad I be to see you ! and you look much young , I declare ! " 
 " and , Miss , you do look bootiful , bootiful , my good friend ! splendid thing , " - I be dress a little well , but still in sombre colour - ' splendid , Miss Vaughan , and how you become them to be sure ! talk of Miss Idols after that , why it be the Queen to a gipsy ! and pray , Miss , if I may make so bold , what do you give for this ? it beat my sarcenet dress , I do believe . "

 " gracious I , Miss , then you have   be and get engage , and to a lord at least . I hear you be come into your great fortune at last ; more than all Middlesex they tell I , Regency Park and all ! and that poor straight - legged young man , as come here every day to see Judy , and to ask for you . "

 she jump with pleasure ; she be so fond of knick - knack : then she put her finger on her lip and go to the door and listen . presently she come back with a mysterious air .
