 " oh , it be nothing , Miss ; nothing at all to speak of : only they knock my stick so in make the inwentory , and the one they make last time , and the time before , would do every bit as well . but they charge for it , every time , the rogue - and they dare to put the chair down lackered and american cloth , good , morocco as ever be , and as if Miss Minto - " 
 " now , Mrs. Shelfer , tell I in two word what it mean . be it a sale ? " 
 " no , no , Miss , I should hope not ; only an execution , and they two man be the bailiff ; civil tongue enough , and very good judge of porter and periwinkle . they be the one as come last time ; but I would soon have the old one , jolly fellow they be , and know how to wink both eye . but that cross - eyed thief - " 
 " and have they get my thing , Mrs. Shelfer ? " 
 " no , Miss ; only what few be in the bedroom ; they dare not   come here for Judy . it be as much as their life be worth . if I have know they be come , I would have have he at the front door , but they lock he in as soon as he get a piece out of the other fellow 's leg . bless I , how he do holloa ! " 
 " do you mean to say , Mrs. Shelfer , that they have take possession of my thing in my bedroom ? " 
 " to be sure , Miss. I say they be your , and of course they would not   believe I , and the folding door be shut , but Judy would have break it down only they put the bedstead again it . gracious I ! I never see a dog take on so in all my life ! he be like a roar lion . " 
 " I should rather hope so . Giudice , I commend you ; and I have   a great mind to let you out , and what be more , I will if they do not   give I back my thing . surely , Mrs. Shelfer , they have no right to my property . " 
 " well , so I say , Miss ; because it be not   for the landlord ; but they will not   believe they be your . " 
 " if they do not   believe I pretty soon , Giudice shall convince they . he be a judge you know , and I have   no idea of robbery any more than he have . but who be do all this , and why do you seem so unconcerned about it ? I should cry my eye out , I be sure . " 
 " bless your pretty heart , Miss ; this make the fifteenth time I have   have they here in the last four year . at first I be terribly put out , and make myself a figure cry ; but now I only think it be company , and they drink as if they be , that be certain . you must have see the inwentorie , Miss , round the candle lot of time . only one thing they do that do not   strike I as wery honourable , though it be law I b'lieve ; they charge I , and wery high too , for eat up my victual , and they will have meat four time a day . why , that Balak , he with his breech go - " 
 " who put they in , Mrs. Shelfer , and how much be it for ? " 
 " oh , it be one of Charley 's bill or note , of course . Quinlan hold it , he as keep " the little dust - pan , " down Maiden Lane , and Charley say that all he get for it be ten shilling and a waggon - load of water - cress . now they will be here directly , Miss , with you to keep the dog in . excuse I , Miss , I see you have get one of they new wide thing as go all round and up - capital thing , I must have one before they come again . and could you manage to sit upon the sofa , Miss , and the three good chair in your petticoat , with the tea - poy on your lap ? " 
 " what on earth do you mean , Mrs. Shelfer ? " 
 " why , Miss , they can not   lay hold of any article in use , I believe , and you have get so much room in your thing . " 
 " do you suppose I intend to let they come here , for a moment ? now let I look at my bedroom . come , Judy . " 
 " oh , Miss , they do have such a hunt here for Charley 's double - barrel gun ; a regular beauty it be , and that big rogue Quinlan be after it . they know it all round this neighbourhood , it be make by a famous maker , Joe something , I b'lieve , and the good he ever make ; it be poor Miss Minto 's brother 's ; and they shall not   have it , not one of them . I would soon shoot they with it . I keep it always in the safe place I know on , and twice a year I see that it do not   get rusty . " 
 " what safe place do you keep it in ? " 
 she put she little mouth up to my ear , and her little hand up to her mouth , and whisper - 
 " at the broker 's , Miss , in Barbican . he have have it now six year . it be in for a quarter its value , but that be all the well for I : I have less to pay for keep it , and I carry the ticket night and day in my bosom . and do you know , my good friend , they think they have get it just now ; they get a key that fit that box of your , that you always lock so carefully , and they make sure that be it ; ha , ha , how I laugh at they when they open it ! " 
 " what ! have they dare to open my mahogany box ? " it be the repository of my precious relic . 
 " to be sure they do , Miss , and they find such curious thing there ! a lovely thing all set with jewel , they say , a baggonet fit for the Duke of Wellington , and plaster shape like a cobbler 's last , and colour paper with queer letter on it , and a piece of long black hair , and a plan with distance on it - Lor , Miss , what on earth be the matter ? water ! water ! you be like death - Balaam ! balak ! " 
 " Stop , Mrs. Shelfer " - I have fall on the bed - " I would not for ten thousand pound have have that box expose to those low ruffian , ransack , and even catalogue . if I can punish they I will ; and you too , you low , miserly , meddling , inquisitive old crone . " 
 she care for nothing - though afterwards she tell I she never see such eye in her life - until I luckily call she an " old crone . " at that , she fall back upon the towel - horse , and sob with both hand over her eye , as if her heart would break . I have pierce she in the tender point - her age . 
 I do not feel sorry for she at all for at least two minute , but let she cry away . " serve she right , " I think . even if she could not have stop they from open that box of mine , at any rate she have no right to gossip about it , and enjoy it all , as she evidently have do . furthermore , I know well that she have always be on the tingle to learn the content of that box , and many a time I have baffle she . now she have triumph thoroughly , and I should not have be female if I have calmly allow it . but see she great distress   through all of which she talk , with sob for affirmation , I begin to think what a pity it be ; then to wonder whether she deserve it all ; next , to believe that she have do no harm ; lastly , to feel that I have be a brute . thereupon I rush to coax and kiss she , wipe away her tear with my own lawn handkerchief - the feel of which console she , for the edge be lace - and beg her pardon fifty time in a thousand foolish word . finally she be quite set up again by this : 
 " I tell you , my dear Patty , when I come to your age , when I be five and thirty " - she be fifty - two at least - " I shall fully deserve to be call an old woman for this ; and much old I shall look , there be no doubt , than you do . " 
 " right , my good friend , you be quite right there " - this expression show I that she herself be right . - " why the young man from the butcher 's , he say to I this morning , and beautiful black hair remind I of your , Miss , all stick together with the fat from off the kidney - " 
 " come , Mrs. Shelfer , let I see about my box . " 
 " to be sure , to be sure , my dear Miss Vaughan ; but what do you think he say ? ' now , William John , ' say I , ' a good steak mind , a tender juicy steak , for the gentleman visitor here ' - Balaam , Miss , and Balak , if you please , - ' do like good juicy meat . ' ' Mrs. Shelfer , ma'am , ' he say , a bow with his tray like that , ' you shall have a steak , ma'am , as fresh and as juicy as yourself . ' now be not   that pretty , my good friend ? " 
 " beautiful , Mrs. Shelfer . but see about my box . " 
 " surely , surely , Miss Vaughan . but it be very pretty , like a valentine , do not   you think it be now ? " 
 " where be it ? " 
 " Downstairs , Miss , in my little parlour . " 
 " then send it up at once , by one of the man . " 
 presently Balaam come up , look askance at Judy , and with the mahogany box under his right arm . he touch his dirty hat , for Mrs. Shelfer have fill he by this time with the wonder of my wealth , and then he look doubtfully , and with sorrow , at his burden . 
 " put it here if you please , " and I point to some chair , " the dog will not touch you while I be here . now what be the amount of this execution ? " 
 " debt fifteen pound , Miss ; expense up to five o'clock , four pound ten . " 
 " here be the money . now give I a receipt . " 
 " no , Miss ! you do not   mean to pay all ! " 
 " of course , I do . " 
 " then , Miss , I beg your pardon , but I can not   allow you . I have a duty to my employer , and I have a duty to the public too , not forget Mrs. Shelfer , and Charley an old friend , and all so handsome in the way of victual . and I be sure she would not   wish you to be cheat , Miss. pay ten pound for the debt , Miss , and that be a deal more than it cost they or they expect to get . ' twixt you and I , Miss , every stick of this here furniture be in a dozen bill of sale already ; and we come here more for practice like , than for anything else . " 
 in short , I pay 10 l.   for the debt , and 4 l.   for the expense : whereupon Balaam look at I with a most impressive and confidential glance . 
 " now , Miss , you will not   think I rude ; but you have come down so handsome , I can tell you something as you may like to know . I have   seed the very moral of that sword of yours before . " 
 " be you certain ? pray where be it ? " I tremble with excitement . 
 " it be in a place in Somers - town , Miss ; where I make a levy , some eight year agone . " 
 " what be the name of the people ? " 
 " Dallyhorse , or Jellycorse , or something of the sort . foreigner they be , and they have only just come to this country . but I can tell you the name more shipshape from the book . ah , the very moral of it ; only there warn't   no serpent . " 
 " do you know what have become of they ? " 
 " no that I do not , and do not   want to come across they again . a mean set of mongrel parlywoo ; I get starve amost . but I do hear they be rid the high horse now , and something about court . " 
 " be you quite sure that the weapon be exactly like this ? look at this again . " 
 " Miss , I can take my oath it be the fellow pea , all but the little snake , and he be not   a fixture , I do not   believe . I would have swear it be the very same , only you tell I not . I notice it most particular ; for I never see one like it , though I have have a sight of foreign weapon in my hand ere now . and the gent have get it put away so ; we come across it only through a cat as happen to be confine - " 
 " and what become of it ? do your employer have it ? " 
 " not he , Miss. when the gent find we have get it , he be put out and no mistake ; though he sham not . away he go and get the money somehow , and have we all away in no time . " 
 " how many be there in the family ? " 
 " well , let I see . they be only live in lodging , and have but half the house . there be Dallyhorse himself , and a queer - look lady , and some child , I do not   know how many child , for they keep they out of the way ; and a nice young woman as do the cooking for they , and precious little it be . " 
 " what be his profession ? and who be his creditor ? ' 
 " I do not   know . they call he an artist I think , but he look to I more like a sailor . it be a boarding - house bill , as I be on he for . Rum - temper fellow . I think he would have stick I when I get his sword thing . a tallish man he be , slight build , and active , and such black eye . " 
 " now , Balaam , if you can trace that man , and find out where he be live now , I will give you two hundred pound . here be ten pound for you as an earnest . " 
 Balaam be so amazed , that he almost look straight at I . 
 " please , Miss , may I tell Balak ? I shall not   be happy if I do not . we always work together , and it would not   be on the square like . " 
 " be he with you then ? and can he keep a secret ? " 
 " yes , Miss , he be with I , and I would trust he with a gallow secret . I can not   do no good without he . " 
 " then , certainly you may tell he ; but not while in this house . here be my country address , that you may know who you act for . keep clear of the Police . keep the whole matter to yourself . in two day , I leave London ; if you discover nothing in that time , write to I here , and I will take good care to have the letter forward . do nothing , but find out that one thing , and when I have verify it , I will pay you the two hundred pound . " 
 " would you mind , Miss , put it on paper ? " 
 " yes : for many reason , I will not write it down . but you be at liberty to inquire who I be , and whether I be likely to disgrace my word . " 
 after take his address , " Balaam Levison , Dove Court , Chancery Lane , " I allow he to depart , and hear he pause on every stair , to ponder this strange matter . 
 presently Mr. Shelfer come home , and be delighted to see the bailiff ; and the pleasure be mutual , and my cash burn to be quench , a most hilarious evening be the natural result . my health be drunk , as I could hear too plainly , to unfathomable depth : and comic song from three loud organ , provide with patent nasal stop , with even Patty 's treble pipe audible in the chorus , break from time to time the tenour of my sad and lonely thought . 
 CHAPTER IV . 
 the bailiff 's discovery , and the pursuit commence thereon , appear to I so important , that in reply to the message receive the next morning - that my uncle be much the same , and longing for my return - I send word that my journey be put off until the day after the morrow . this allow I one day more for tiding from my new scout , as to the success of their effort . I be very sorry to disappoint my poor sick uncle , but it seem still bad to run away all in the dark . 
 the next thing I do be to arrange with Mrs. Shelfer about the money I have pay for she . it be not the money I care for , but I have other view . although she be politely thankful , I perceive that she think it a very bad job indeed , and a most romantic transaction . thirty per cent . be the very large dividend she have ever intend to pay . but the plan which I propose be so much for her benefit , while it suit I , who otherwise must have lose the money , that it almost recover she from the shock of have pay a debt . the plan be simply this , that she should reserve my room for I , air and clean they duly , and always keep the bed in a fit state to be sleep on at an hour 's notice . my previous rent have be twelve shilling a week , the utmost I could afford out of my narrow income ; attendance , and linen , and other trouble be now dispense with , I think it fair to allow her ten shilling off her debt to I , for every week I should so retain the room . the 4 l.   for the expense of the execution I forgive she altogether ; inasmuch as I have pay without consult she . directly my payment should be exhaust , to wit in twenty week , I would send she a further sum , if I still require the room . 
 she be delighted with this arrangement , which in fact enable she to have she ' stick " all to herself , to pet they and talk to they every day , and even to clean they , if such a freak of destruction ever should enter her brain . she could use the sitting - room for her own pleasure and pride , as much as ever she choose , so long as it always be ready for I ; and already vision be pass before her mind 's eye , of let the parlour downstair with the onion - room for its dormitory . to I the arrangement be very convenient , as afford a fixed and familiar resort in London , and a pivot of ready communication . nor be it a small consolation to feel that I still retain a stronghold in the neighbourhood of dear friend . 
 all this be comfortably settle , Giudice and I go forth to pay our visit in Lucas Street . the whole of that street we find so utterly change in appearance by a vigorous onset of painter , grainer , and decorator , that it be not easy to know the house we be in quest of . even the number on the door , which have be illegible , or very nearly so , have now be re - arrange and paint over again upon the fashionable and very sensible mode of mark odd numeral on one side , and even one on the other . find myself in a difficulty , and the house all alike as the central pea of a pod , I trust to Judy 's delicate nose , and ring the bell of the door at which he halt . then he draw back , and tremble , and crouch upon the pavement , to wait for my return . as I hear the tinkle , my heart begin to flutter : who could tell what new phase of my life might begin with that little pull ? after some delay , poor old Cora come , look as weird and woebegone as ever - fierce would have be that look to any one but I . I know that I hold she by my magic gordit , like the slave of the lamp . after implore in some mumble word   which I interpret only by knowledge of her desire   gracious leave to kiss that potent charm , she lead I into the breakfast - parlour , where I find sweet Isola in a passionate flood of tear . 
 at sight of I , her beautiful smile break through they , and her quick deep sob spend themselves in kiss . 
 " oh , I be so gug - gug - glad , my own dear Cla - Cla - Clara ; and I will not   cuc - cuc - cry one bit more , the moment I can stop . " 
 she put her arm around I , and her head upon my breast , as if I have be , at the very least , her brother . 
 " my pretty dear , what be it all about ? " 
 I have never see she look so lovely as now , her violet eye brim with liquid brightness , the velvet of her cheek deepen to rich carmine , and the only thing that sweet face ever want , the expression of earnest feeling , now radiant through the whole . 
 " why , dear , I ought not to tell you ; but I must tell somebody , or my heart will break . " 
 here she press her little hand on that pure unfissured casket , where sorrow be as yet an undreame - of robber . 
 " you know , dear , it be all about papa and my darling Conny . the only trouble I ever have , but a very great one , big enough and too big for two little folk of my size . half an hour ago , I go in suddenly to get a book upon the politico - economical science , the very one papa be lecture about so beautifully ; and I do not even know that Conny be in the house . there papa be , white as death with passion ; and Conrad with his eye like coal of sparkle fire ; and what do you suppose my papa call his own son Conny ? " 
 " do not   tell I , if it be anything bad . I can not   bear it , Isola . " 
 " oh , I know you be fond of he , and I be so glad ! " 
 this she say in such an artless way - as if Conrad and I be two doll which she mean to put in one doll 's house - that instead of colour , I actually laugh . 
 " oh , but I must tell you , Clara : it be right for you to know ; one of the lead principle of political economy - " 
 " do not   talk to I of that stuff . " 
 " well , I will not ; because I see that you do not   understand it . but he actually call he - and his voice come from a depth , like an artesian well - he call our darling Conny - " 
 " what ? " and in my passion , I fling off her hand , and stand up . 
 " a low bastard , a renegade hound , a scandal to his country - and then he even say Rimbecco . " 
 she pronounce the last word almost with a scream , as an insult beyond forgiveness . what it mean I do not ask , I have hear enough already . 
 " I must leave this house . where be your brother Conrad ? " 
 " Gone , I believe , to inquire for you . nothing but that compose he . I wish he would never come here . and he be order not to . but it be about some business . oh , he never will come again . " and she begin to cry at the thought of the very thing she have wish for . 
 " neither will I come again . where be your father now ? " 
 " up at his lumber cabinet , where he always console himself , whenever he be put out . but if you be go , dear child , do let I come with you . I shall cry till I die here , all by myself : and Pappy never care about I , when he be in his black dudgeon . " 
 in a few minute we leave the rude unpleasant house , and even Judy seem relieve to get away from the door . by the time we reach Mrs. Shelfer 's , Idols be in capital spirit again , and press I for some account of the wonderful wealth , and the grand house she have hear of . no doubt this rumour have find its way through Ann Maples . 
 " and the great Lord - what be his name , dear Donna ? I would not   believe a word of it ; though I be sure you be a deal too good for all the house of peer . but Conny do ; and be not   he in a way ? but he ought to be very glad you know - wish you every blessing , as they say in the play ; and a peer be the very high blessing to an Englishwoman . but one thing I be quite resolve on : Judy belong to I now , do not   you , lovely Judy ? " 
 " no , " say the judicious , " I belong to Clara . " 
 " though Conny pretend , since he be leave at your place , that he belong to he . now I will give he to you ; and so will Conny too . you can afford to keep he now , and I can not , he do eat such a lot ; and he do not care a pin for I , but he love you with all his heart . " 
 " how do you know he do ? " I be not attend much , but think of some one else . 
 " why , can not   you see that he do , how he wag his tail every time you even look at he ? but I hope poor Conny be here . I should think he would stop , when he find darling Clara come back . " 
 I have jump to that hope long ago , before we even leave Lucas Street , and that have something to do with my walking so fast . 
 no , he be not there , he have not be there to - day . it be my turn now to cry ; what might he not have do , after that fearful insult , and from his own father too ? 
 the tear , which I confide to no one except the wooden - legged blackbird - for Giudice would have make such a fuss about they - be still upon my cheek , when I hear the well - know step - not half so elastic as usual . I flee into my bedroom , and push the box about , to make a goodly noise , and to account for the colour in my face . then out I come at the side - door , and run downstairs perversely , though I know that Conrad and Isola be in my sitting - room . 
 but this first - rate manoeuvre only outwit its author , for Isola run down after I , and send I upstairs alone . all my little nonsense vanish the moment I look in Conrad 's face . his healthy brown complexion be fade to an opal white ; beneath his eye such dark blue rim , that I think he have spectacle on ; and on either cheek a round red spot be burn . so shocked I be , that when he take my hand , I turn my face away and smother down a sob . I feel that I have no right to be so fresh and blooming . nor be it only in health that the contrast between we lay . I be dress with unusual care , have fidget all the morning , and with my utmost taste . poor Conrad be in his work clothe , full of marble dust , tumble , threadbare , and even in need of mending ; his hair sweep anyhow , and his hand not over - lately wash . yet , for all that , he be as clearly a gentleman , as I be a lady . 
 not so would he have be array , I fancy , have he think to see neat Clara . and yet , who know ? " I trust that you will excuse I , " he begin to say , " but such thing have happen lately - you will not account I rude - I have no sense at all of this great pleasure . " 
 " I fear you have not be very happy . " I know not what to say , or how to keep my voice clear . 
 " yes , " he reply , " as happy as I deserve . it serve I aright for esteem so much of myself , before that I do anything . but I will win my way " - and his own proud glance flash out - " and we shall see how many will scorn I then . " 
 " no one in the world can scorn you , " I say very softly , and my voice thrill through he . 
 " ah , you be always kind and gentle : " - be I though , think I - " but I will no more fatigue you with my different lot in life . I be tell that some great nobleman have win you for his own . perhaps you will give I an order . " 
 his throat be swell with these bitter word , and he look at his dusty clothe . somewhat rude I think he , but I know not half his trouble . 
 " whoever tell you that , have make a great mistake . I be engaged to no one . your sister know I well . " and I turn away to the window . for a minute he say nothing ; but I could hear his heart beat . stedfastly I look at the cheesemonger 's shop . oh for a flower , or something on the balcony ! 
 presently he come round the corner of the sofa . without be rude , I could not help turn round . 
 his face be much , much , bright , and his eye more kind . 
 " have I say any harm - I would not for the world - I know not it be harm . " 
 " no harm , " I say , " to think so ill of I ! to believe , for a single moment , that because I be not so poor , I would go and forsake - at least , I mean , forget - any one I care for ! " 
 " can I ever hope , if I serve you all my life , that you will ever care for I ? " 
 " do not   you know I do ? " and I burst into my violent flood . 
 when I come to myself , both his arm be round I , and I be look up at his poor sick face , my hair quite full of marble chip , and he be tell I with glad tear in his eye , which he never take from mine , how he care for nothing now , not for all the world , not for glory or fur shame , so long as I only love he . 
 " with all my heart and soul , " I whisper , " he and no one else whatever , whether in life or death . " 
 all the folly we go through I be not go to repeat , though I remember well every atom of it . let the wise their wisdom keep , we be babe and suckling . neither of we have ever love before , or ever mean to love again , except of course each other , and that should be for ever . 
 " one thing I must tell you , my own sweet love , and yet I fear to do it . but you be not like other girl . there be no one like you , nor have there ever be . I think you will not scorn I for another 's fault . " 
 " of course I will not , my own pet Conny . what be this awful thing ? " 
 " I be an illegitimate son . " 
 one moment I spring from he ; the next I despise myself . but in spite of all my trouble , there still lurk in my heart the narrow pride of birth . down to the earth it fall beneath the foot of true love , and I kiss away from his eye the mingled reproach and sorrow , assure he that at least he should have a legitimate wife . 
 to make amend , I lean upon he one moment , and put my hand on his shoulder , and let he play awhile with the dark shower of my hair . 
 " Darling Conny , you have tell I your , now you shall hear my secret . only promise I you will give tit for tat . you say you love I ever since you see I first ; then you must have love your Clara when you save her life . " 
 " what do you mean , my Clara ? those low ruffian in the Park be not go to kill you . " 
 " no , dearest ; I do not   mean that at all . but there be a kiss for that , I have owe it you ever since . but what I mean no kiss can repay ; no , nor a life of love . you save a life worth fifty of my own . " 
 some dark alarm be grow in his eye , on which I gaze with vague increase terror . 
 " why , dearest , it be nothing . only your own Clara be not Clara valence ; you must call she ' Clara Vaughan . ' " 
 with actual violence he thrust I from his arm , and stand star at I , while I tremble from head to foot ; his face be one scarlet flame . 
 " and pray , Sir , what harm have I do ? be I to suppose that you " - special emphasis mean for illegitimacy - " that you be ashamed of my father and I ? " 
 " yes , I be . accurse low licentious race ! if you know what you have do , you would tear your heart out rather than give it to I . " 
 " thank you - I feel obliged - my heart indeed - to a bastard . take back your ring if you please ; kindly restore I mine . may I trouble you for room enough to go by ? " 
 and I sweep out of the room , and through the side - door into my bed - room , where I crouch in a corner , with both hand on my heart , and the whole world go away . " mad ! " I hear he cry , " yes , I must go mad at last ! " away he rush from the house , and I fall upon the bed , and lie in fit till midnight . 
 CHAPTER V. 
 I believe that my heart would have burst , if they have not cut my stay ; and how I wish it have . when I come back to my unlucky self , there be something shivery cold in the forehead wave of my hair . be it Conrad 's finger ? I put up my hand to dash it away , and catch a fine fat leech . Dr. Franks be sit by I , hold a basin and a sponge . 
 " that be the last of they , my dear child . do not   disturb he . he be do his duty by you . " 
 " his duty ! be it his duty to say such fearful thing ? to break my heart with every word ! ashamed of I - ashamed of my darling father ! low and licentious ! what have I do ? what have I do ? oh , it I only know what harm I have ever do ! " 
 " no harm , my poor dear , no harm in the world ; let I bathe your pretty face . come now , you shall not   cry another drop . what be to become of the beautiful eye I be so proud of save ? " 
 " oh , I wish you have not , how I wish you have not . Dr. Franks , I have no father , and no mother , and no one in all the world to love I , and I be just get so nice and happy again , so proud of myself , and so much prouder of he , and I begin to think how glad my own dear father would be ; and , Dr. Franks , I do love he so , with all my heart , perhaps it be not very large , but with every morsel and atom of my heart - and now , now I must hate he as much as ever I can . oh let I go home , do let I go home , where my father and mother be bury . " and I rise in the bed to start , and the candle glimmer in my eye . 
 " please to go out of the room , every one please to go ; and do not   let Isola come . I can not   bear the sight of she now . it will not   take I long to dress , and I do not   want any luggage ; and , Mrs. Shelfer , please to go for a cab : and I shall not   want the room any more , and it do not matter a bit about any letter . I will tell my father everything when I see he , and then perhaps he will tell I what harm it be I have do . why do not   you go , when you see I want to get up ? ' 
 " do not   you see , my dear child , we be go ? only you must take this glass of wine first , to prepare you for your long journey . will you take it now , while we fetch the cab ? " 
 " yes , anything , anything : I do not   care what it be . only let I get ready . " 
 and I drank , without even taste it , a glass of some dark liquid , which save I from wander far either in mind or body . 
 when I awake , it be broad noon once more , and Dr. Franks be sit by I with one of my hand in his . " magnificent constitution , " I think I hear he mutter , " glorious constitution . " what good be it to I ? at the foot of the bed , sit Isola cry terribly . slowly I remember all my great disaster , but see it only through a dull gray veil . the power of the opiate be still upon my brain . but a cold dead pain lie heavy on my heart , and always seem to want a heavy hand upon it . after he have give I a revive draught , Dr. Franks perceive that I wish to speak to Isola , and accordingly withdraw . 
 Poor Isola come slowly and sit beside my pillow , doubt whether she should dare to take my hand . therefore I take her , draw her face towards I , and cover it with kiss .  CHAPTER VI . 
 Annie Franks be exactly as Isola have describe she , ' such a nice girl .  an hour of deep sleep from pure weariness both of mind and body , and I awake with every fibre full of nervous life . the moon be high in the south - east , and three narrow stripe of lozenge light fall upon the old oak floor . although my uncle have leave the gable where the window face the set sun , he still keep to the western wing . the house , which be build in the reign of Henry the Eighth , cover the site and in some part embody the relic of a much more ancient structure . the plan be very simple , at least as regard the upstair room . from east to west run one long corridor , cross at right angle , in the centre and near the end , by three gable passage . although there be so many servant , not half the room be occupy : all the good bedroom have be empty many a year . no festivity have fill they since my father 's day . gloom and terror still hang over the eastern part , where he have be so foully murder . in most of the downstair window along the front of the house , the rickety lattice of diamond pane have be replace by clear plate - glass , but the old hall , and the corridor , and some of the gable window still retain their gorgeous tint and heraldry .

 " what , Clara Vaughan ! be it possible ? I think you be in London . " 
 " be it possible that I have find a Christian , so truly earnest about her soul , so yearn over the unregenerate , commit a black robbery in the dead of night ? be this what you call a wholesome conviction of sin ? " 
 low exultation I confess : but the high blood in the land , if it be blood , could scarcely have forbear it : for how I abhor that hypocrite !

 " perhaps you be young enough to imagine , that because appearance be at this moment peculiar - "

" and I give a peculiar whistle thoroughly know to my dog . he leap against the door , force the worn catch from the guard , and stand at my side , with his great eye flash and his fang lay bare . Mrs. Daldy jump to the other side of the table , and seize a heavy chair . 
 " my dear child , my dear girl , I believe you be right after all . it be so hard to judge - for God 's sake keep he back - so hard to judge when one 's own right be in question . the old unregenerate tendency - " 
 " will lodge you in Gloucester jail to - morrow . once more those paper - or - " and I look at Giudice and begin to raise my hand . his eye be on it , and he gather himself for the spring like a cannon recoiling . in the height of her terror , she tear her dress open and fling I the packet across the end of the table . I only ask if she could reveal the mystery of my father 's death . she could not tell I anything , or she would have jump at the chance . at last I promise to let she go , if she would show I the secret entrance under the oriel window . it be not for her own sake I release she , but to avoid the scandal and painful excitement which her trial must have create . when she depart , now thoroughly crestfallen , I follow she out of the house by the secret passage , wherein she have store a few of her stage - property . Giudice , whom , for fear of treachery , I keep at my side all the time , show his great tooth in the moonlight , and almost challenge my right to let she go . after take the packet from he , I give he a sheepskin mat under the window there , and leave he on guard ; although there be little chance of another attempt be make , while the paper be in my keeping . her mask and spectral drapery remain with I , as trophy of this my ghostly adventure . 
 CHAPTER VII . 
 next day when I show my uncle the two seal packet which I have rescue , and tell he all that have happen , at first he be overcome with terror and amazement . his illness seem to have banish all his satirical humour , and that disdainful apathy which be the negative form of philosophy . he take the parcel with a trembling hand , and begin to examine the seal . 
 " all safe , " he say at last , " all safe , to my surprise . dear child , I owe you more than life this time . you have defeat my bad enemy . to your care only will I commit these paper , one of which , I hope will soon be of little value . it be my will ; and by it your father 's estate be restore to you , while the money which I have save by my own care and frugality be divide into two portion , one for you , and the other , upon certain event , for that worthless Mrs. Daldy . this must be alter at once . when you have hear my story , you may read the will , if you like . indeed I wish you to do so , because it will prove that in spite of all our estrangement , I have mean all along to act justly towards you . but that you may understand thing properly , I will tell you my strange history . only one thing you must promise before I begin . " 
 " what be it , uncle dear ? " 
 " that you will forgive I for my one great error . although it be the cause of your dear father 's death . " 
 I could not answer , for a minute . then I take his hand and kiss it , as he turn his face away . 
 " my darling , I be not quite strong enough now after all you have be tell I . although I have dark suspicion yesterday that some plot be in action ; for I have observe that thing in the study be not as I have leave they ; and I have other reason too . but take I , my precious child , to the sunny bank this afternoon , and please God , I will at least begin my tale . " 
 I beg he in vain to defer it : there be a weight upon his mind , he say , which he must unload . so in the early afternoon , I wheel he gently to the sheltered nook . there , with the breeze way - lose among new street of verdure , tall laburnum dangle chain of gold around we , and Giudice stretch out his paw in sunny yawn of glory , I listen to my uncle 's tale , and be too young to understand the sigh which introduce it . how few may tell the story of their life without remember how they play with life ! alas the die throw once for all , but leave to roll unwatched , and lie uncounted ! 
 though I can not tell the story in his impressive way , I will try to repeat it , so far as my memory serve , in his word , and with his feeling . solemnly and sadly fall the history from his lip , for his mind from first to last be burden with the knowledge that the end be nigh at hand , that nothing now remain , except to wait with resignation the impending blow . 
 STORY of EDGAR VAUGHAN . 
 " I have always be , as you know , of a rove unsocial nature . my father be dead before I be bear , and my mother have marry again before I could walk , there be little to counteract my centrifugal tendency . I seem to belong to neither family ; though I always clung to the Vaughans , and dislike the Daldys . the trustee of my mother 's settlement be my virtual guardian ; for all the Vaughan estate be most strictly entail , my father have nothing to dispose of , and therefore have make no will . my mother 's settlement comprise only personal estate , for no power have be reserve under the entail to create any charge upon the land . the mortgage , of which no doubt you have hear , as pay off by your father , be encumbrance of long standing . 
 " the estate , I need not tell you , be shamefully mismanage , during your father 's long minority . an agent be appoint under the Court of Chancery , and an indolent rogue he be . meanwhile your father and myself go through the usual course of education , no difference be make in that respect between we . although we be only half - brother , we be strongly attach to each other , especially after a thorough drubbing which your good papa find it his duty to administer to I at Eton . it do I a world of good ; before that , I have rather despise he for the gentleness of his nature . at Oxford , after your father have leave , I keep aloof both from the great convivial and from the thinly people reading set , and live very much by myself . soon as the humorous doing , whose humour culminate in the title ' lecture , ' soon as these be over , I be away from the freckle stone , punt lazily on the Cherwell , with french and italian novel ; or lounge among the gipsey on the steppe of Cowley . Hall I never frequent , but din at some distant tavern , and spend the evening , and often the night till Tom - curfew , in rid through the lonely lane towards Otmoor , Aston Common , or Stanlake . it be strange that I never fall in love , for I have plenty of small adventure , and fall in with several pretty girl , but never one I care for . gaze on the wreck I be , it be no conceit to say that in those time I be consider remarkably good - look . of course I be not popular ; that I never care for ; but nobody have reason to dislike I . I affect no peculiarity , give myself no air , behave politely to all who take the trouble to address I ; and the world , which I neither defy nor court , follow its custom in such case , and let I have my way . 
 " at Lincoln 's Inn , my life be much the same , except that wherry succeed punt , and evening ride be exchange for moonlight walk in the park . it be report at home , as it be of most man who be call to the Bar , that I be likely to do great thing . there never be a chance of it . set aside the question of ability , I have no application , no love of the law , no idea whatever of touting ; and still more fatal defect , my lonely habit be darken into a shy dislike of my specie . 
 " you have hear that I be extravagant . as regard my early career , the charge be quite untrue . money , I confess , be never much in my thought , nor do I ever attempt to buy thing below their value ; but my want be so few , and my mode of life so ungenial , that I never exceed the moderate sum allot to I as a young son . afterwards this be otherwise , and for excellent reason . 
 " during the height of the London season I be always most restless and misanthropic . not that I look with envy on the frivolous dust of fashion , and cloud of sham around I ; but that I feel myself lower as an Englishman by the cringing , the falsehood , the small baboonerie , which we call ' society . ' I long to be , if I could but afford it , where man have more manly self - respect , and woman more true womanhood . 
 " your parent be marry , my darling Clara , at the end of December , 1826 , six year before your birth . upon that occasion , your dear father , the only man in the world for whom I care a fig , make I a very handsome present . in fact he give I a thousand pound . he would have give I a much large sum , for he be a most liberal man , but the estate have suffer from long mismanagement , and be seriously encumber . I do not hesitate to say that the gross income of this property be now double what it be when your father succeed to it , and the net income more than quadruple . during the four year which elapse between that event and his marriage , he have devote all he could spare to the clearance of encumbrance and therefore , as I say , the present he make I be a most generous one . More than this , he invite and press I to come and live on the estate , and offer to set I up in a farm which I might hold from he on most advantageous term . upon my refusal , he even beg I to accept , at a most liberal salary , the stewardship of the property , and the superintendence of great improvement , which he mean to effect . I remember , as if it be yesterday , the very word he use . he take my hand in his , and with that genial racy smile , which very few could resist , 
 " ' come , Ned , ' he cry , ' there be but two of we ; there be room for both in the old nest ; and you be big enough to thrash I now . ' " 
 at the sweet recollection of his Eton drubbing , as he call it , my poor uncle 's eye grow moist . 
 " so you see , my child , instead of grudge your father the property , I have every reason to love and revere he . however , I refuse this as well as the other offer ; but I accept his present , and invest it rather luckily . after spend a pleasant month at home - as I always call it - I return to London early in April , 1827 . there be no two mind alike , any more than there be two body ; and yet how little variety exist in polite society ! surely it be more reasonable to wedge the infant face into a jelly - mould , to flute its ear and cheek like collared head , and grow the nose and lip and eyebrow into rosette and grape and acorn , than to bow and cramp and squeeze a million mind into one set model . yet here I find man all alike , Dane and Saxon , Celt and Norman , like those who walk where snow be deep , or Alpine traveller lash to a rope , trudge each in other 's footprint , swing all their arm in time , look neither right nor leave , and so on through life 's pilgrimage , a file some million deep . who go first they do not know , why they follow they can not tell , what it lead to they never ask . I be mark and scorn at once , because I dare to adopt a hat that do not scalp I in half - an hour , and a cravat that do not throttle I ; and even have the hardihood to dine when I feel hungry . how often I long for a land of freedom and common sense , where it be no disgrace to carry a barrel of oyster , or shake hand with a tradesman . I know what you be smile at , Clara . you be think to yourself , ' how different you be now , my good uncle ; and wern't   you a little inconsistent in sanction all this livery humbug here ? ' yes , I be different now . I be old and wise than to expect to wipe away with my coat - sleeve the oxide of many century . as for the livery , it make they happy : it be an Englishman 's uniform . and I have see and suffer so bitterly from the violence of an untamed race , that I admire less what I use to call the unlassoed arch of the human neck . I have see a coarse line somewhere , 
 " ' and freedom make a deal too free with I , ' 
 which express briefly the moral of my life . however , at the time I speak of , nurse perhaps a young son 's bias against the social law , and fresh from the true simplicity and unaffected warmth of your father 's character and the gentle sweetness of your mother 's , I could not sit on the spike of fashion 's hackney coach , as become a poor Briton , till the driver whip behind . finding of course that no one care whether I sit there or not , and that all I get at the side of the road be pea - shot from cad in the dickey , I do what thousand have do before I , and will probably do again , I vote my fellow - Britons a parcel of drivel slave , and long to be out of the gang . perhaps I should never have make my escape , for like most of my class , I spend all my energy in small eccentricity , if it have not be for what we idler entitle the force of circumstance . at a time when my life be flow on calmly enough though babble against its bank , it come suddenly on an event which drive it into another and rougher channel . 
 " early one afternoon in the month of April , 1829 , I launch my little boat from the Temple - stair , where I keep it , and feel more than usually saturnine and moody , resolve on a long expedition . so I victual my ship like Robinson Crusoe , and take some wrap and covering . it be then slack water , just at the height of the flood . I mean to have go to Richmond , but be far too indolent to struggle against the tide , I yield to nature 's good pleasure , and pull away down stream . in a few minute a rapid ebb tide be run , and I make up my mind to go with it as far as ever it choose , and to return with the flood whenever that please to meet I . 
 " after row steadily for several hour , I find myself a long way past my customary Cape turn - again . with a strong ebb tide as well as a land - fresh in the river , I have get beyond Barking Reach , and as far as the Dagenham marsh . here some muddy creek , pill , and sluggish channel wind and welter among the ooze - land on the north side of the Thames . all around they stretch and fade away a dreary flat monotonous waste ; no dot of a house , no jot of a tree , to vary the dead expanse ; except that by the river - side one or two low caboose , more like hoy than house , be ground among the slime . this , so far as my memory serve , be the state of these Essex marsh in the year 1829 : how it be now I can not say . 
 " it be high time for I to turn : row as I would , I could hardly get back to my haven by midnight . Outrigger skiff be not yet know ; and an oarsman could not glide along at the rate of ten mile an hour . just as I be work round , a steam packet , which have be moor a short way below , cripple perhaps in her engine , now at the turn of the tide pass up , and be quickly out of sight . as she pass I I hail for a tow - rope ; but either they could not hear , or they do not choose to notice I . there be nothing for it but to bend my back to the oar , and keep a sharp look out . presently the flood begin to make strongly up the river , and I give way with a will , my paddle bend and the water gleam in the early starlight . it be a lonely and melancholy scene . the gray mist return from some marshy excursion , and hug the warm sea - water , float along in dull fold , with a white flaw of steam here and there curdle over the current . not a ship , not a barge be in sight ; no voice of man or low of cattle break the foggy silence : but the wash of the stream on its sludgy marge , or on some honey - comb mooring - post , surge every now and then betwixt the jerk of my rowlock . the loneliness and the sadness harmonise with my sombre mind . all be transient , all be selfish , all be a flux of melancholy . if we toss and dance we be only boat adrift ; we be nothing more than crazy tide - post , if we be philosopher . 
 " suddenly a clear loud cry break my vacant musing . it startle I so that I catch a crab , cease rowing , and gaze around . at first I could not tell whence it come , till my boat , with the way she have on she , shoot round a low spit of the Essex shore , which from the curve of the river I be near rapidly . louder and louder the cry be twice repeat , and I hear in the still spring evening the oath of man and the scuffling of foot . within fifty yard of I be an ill - look house , make of batten , and raise on pile above high water mark . a tattered sign hang on a pole , and a causeway lead to the step . while I be hesitate , two figure cross a lattice window , as if in violent struggle , and a heavy crash resound . three strong stroke of my oar , and the keel grate on the causeway . out I leap with the boat - hook , throw the painter over a post , and rush up the slimy jetty , and the narrow wooden step . the door be fasten , I push it with all my force , but in vain . one faint scream reach my ear , as of some one at length overpower . swing the boat - hook with both hand , I strike the old door with the butt , and break it open . in the low room there be no one , but a moaning and trampling sound over head . Upstairs I run , and into the room where the villany be do . a poor girl lie on the floor at the last gasp of exhaustion . two ruffian with a rope be bend over she . down go one , at a blow of my boat - hook , flat beside his victim : the other leap at my throat . I see and soon feel that he be a powerful man , but in those day I be no cripple . we be most evenly match . I wrench his hand from my throat , but twice he get I under he , twice I writhe from his grasp like a python from a tiger 's jaw . clutched and lock in each other 's arm , in vain we tug to get room for a blow . throttle , and gripe , and roll - which should be first insensible ? an accident give I the mastery . for a moment we lay face to face , glare at each other , draw the strangle breath , loose the deadly grip , panting , throbbing , and watch . my boat - hook lie on the floor , my enemy spy and make a sudden dash at it . instead of withholding , I job he towards it with all my might , and as he raise it , the point enter one of his eye . with a yell of pain and fury , he sink beneath I insensible . shake and quake all over after the desperate struggle , I bind he and his mate , hand and foot , with the twisted tarry junk , which they have mean for the maiden . 
 " at length I have time to look round . on a low truckle bed at the end of the long dark room , in which a ship - lamp be burn , there lie an elderly lady in a perfect stupor of fright and illness . upon the floor with her head throw back against the timber , and her black eye wide open and fix on I , sit a girl of remarkable beauty , though her cheek be as white as death . a magnificent ring , for which she have fight most desperately , be wrench from its place on her finger and hang over the opal nail , for her hand be clench , and her arm quite stiff , in the swoon of utter exhaustion . both lady be in deep mourning . 
 " for the rest a few word will suffice . the poor lady revive at last , after chafing of hand and sprinkling , and tell I where to find the woman of the house , who have be lock up in another room by her husband and brother . there be no one else on the premise . how come the lady there , what be their destination , and why be they so outraged ? they be on their return to London from the continent , be call home by tiding of death , and have sail from Antwerp two day and a - half before , in the steamer which I have see lie to . steamer be then heavy lumber thing , and all that time Mrs. Green and her daughter have be knock about on a pecky sea . no wonder that the poor mother have cry out feebly , to be land anywhere , anywhere in the world , where thing would leave off go round . and before they come to that tedious halt in the river , fair Adelaide , who have enjoy her meal throughout , renew and complete her poor mamma 's excavation , by inquire calmly with her mouth full of pickled pork , where the peas - pudding be . now too Miss Adelaide soon recover from her fearful battle for honour and life . she be what be call now - a - day a girl of ' splendid organisation . " if she have not be so , she would have lain ere now with her mother at the bottom of Barking Reach . the two scoundrel of that lonesome hostelry have be order to send to Barking for a conveyance . but they only pretend to do so ; for they have cast foul covetous eye on the wealth of their unknown guest and on brave Adelaide 's beauty . beyond a doubt both lady would have be murder , but for the gallant resistance , the vigour , and presence of mind of Adelaide . 
 " have restore their watch , and scatter trinket , and lead the poor thing from the scene of the combat , I be quite at a loss for mean to convey they home . barking be a long way off , and the marshy track unknown to I , and not likely to be find in the dark . moreover , there must be some hazard in leave they still in that villanous den , no matter how their cowardly foe might be bind . at last , and with great difficulty , I embark the two lady in my shallop , and wrap they warmly from the night air ; then after relashe my prisoner , and lock they up in separate room , and the woman downstairs , I pull away stoutly for Woolwich . here I obtain a carriage , and start my convoy for London , and then return with two policeman to the " Old row Barge , " as the low caboose be call . but both our bird be fly , as I be inclined to expect . most likely the woman have contrive to get out , and release they . at any rate the " Old Row Barge " have no crew , and the deserter have set it on fire . the flame , as we row away , after vainly search the marsh , cast a lurid glow on the mud - bank , and on the slackening tide ; a true type it be of what soon befell I - the burning of my caboose . the two man be catch long afterwards by the Thames Police , and transport for life on a conviction for river piracy . at least , I be tell that they be the man . " 
 " and of course , dear uncle , you fall deeply in love with the beautiful Adelaide Green . " 
 " of course , my dear , a young lady would conclude so . but at present I must not talk any more . " I have several time try to stop he . " and what I have next to relate be matter of deep feeling . by Jove , to think how I battle with that strong man ! and now your little fist , Clara , would floor I altogether . " 
 he sigh , and I sigh for he . then I think of Mr. Shelfer , and glory in my prowess , as I wheel my uncle home . 
 CHAPTER VIII . 
 my uncle 's tale , as repeat here , will no more be break either by my interruption , which be frequent enough , or by his own pause , but will be present in a continuous form . 
 STORY of EDGAR VAUGHAN . 
 " on the following day , when I call at the house in Bloomsbury - then a fashionable neighbourhood - to which I have be direct , I be meet at the threshold , with power and warmth , by Peter Green himself , an old acquaintance of mine , who prove to be Adelaide 's brother . my nature have be too reserved for I to be friendly with he at College , but I have like he much well than any one else , because he be so decide and straight - forward . the meeting rather surprise I , for Green be not a rare name , and so it have never occur to I to ask the weary Adelaide whether she know one Peter Green , a first - class man of Christchurch . Peter , who be a most hearty fellow , and full , like his sister , of animal life , overpower I with the weight of his gratitude , which I do not at all desire or deserve . as , in spite of your rash conclusion , my romantic Clara , I do not fall in love with Adelaide , who besides her pithsome health and vigour be in many respect astray from my fair ideal , and more than all , be engage long ago to the giver of the sapphire ring , I need not enlarge upon my friendship with Peter Green , whom I now begin to like in real earnest . 
 Young as he be , his father 's recent death have place he at the head of a lead mercantile house , Green , Vowler , and Green , of Little Distaff Lane . and young as he be - not more than seven - and - twenty - his manner be form , and his character and opinion fix , as if he have see all the way , and take stock of the sentiment of all the civilised world . present to he any complexity , any conflict of probability , any maze whose in and out be abroad half over the universe , and if the question be practical , he would see what to do in a moment ; if it be theoretical , he would quietly move it aside . I have know many learn judge sum up a case most lucidly , blow away all the verbiage , sweep aside all the false issue , balance the contradiction , illuminate all the obscurity , and finally lift from its matrix , and lie in the colourless sunlight the virgin truth , without either dross or polish . all this Peter Green seem to have do in a moment , without any effort , without any reasoning process ; not jump at his conclusion , but make it fly to he . he possess what an ancient writer , once highly esteemed at Oxford , entitle the " wit universal , " which confer and comprise the " wit of detail . " for this power when apply to a practical purpose , a great historian employ a happy expression not welcome by our language ; he call it the power to " pontoon the emergency . " excuse my harsh translation , perhaps it be well than paraphrase . 
 with all these business quality , my friend be as merry and unpretentious a man as ever make a bad joke , or laugh at another fellow 's ; liberal also , warm - hearted , and not sarcastic . in a word , he be a genuine speciman of the noble english merchant , who have do more to raise this country in the esteem of the world than would a thousand Nelsons or Wellingtons . 
 now this man discern at a glance the wretched defect of my nature and position . an active mind like his could never believe in the possibility of be happy without occupation . and by occupation he mean , not the chasing of butterfly , or maunder after fox , but real honest anglo - saxon work ; work that string the muscle , or knit the hemisphere of the brain . and work he would himself , ay , and with all his energy . not the man be he to tap the table with his pipe , and cry out , " Bravo , Altiora ! a little more gin if you please , and chalk it down to the strike ; " but he be the man to throw off his coat , and pitch into the matter before he without many word , though with plenty of thought . now , this man , feel deeply indebted to I , and begin to like I as my apathy and reserve go to piece before his energy , this man , I say , cast about for some method of make I useful and happy . wonderfully swift as he be in pounce upon the right thing , I believe it take he at least five minute to find out the proper course for an impracticable fellow like I . and when he have find this out , it take even he a week to draw the snail out of his hole . year of agreeable indolence , and calm objective indifference , seldom ruffle except at fashionable snobbery , have make of I not a Sybarite , or a supercilious censor , much less a waiter on fortune , but a contemplative islander , a Haytian who have be once to Spain , and would henceforth be satisfied with the view of her caravel . but my Adelantado be a man of gold and iron . Green , Vowler , and Green be largely concerned in the oil and dry fruit business . they have ransack the olive district of continental Europe , and find the price go up and the quality go down , so they want now to open another oil vein . 
 Peter Green observe my love of uncultured freedom , the only subject on which I ever grow warm and rapturous , espy the way to relieve I of some nonsense , give my slow life a fillip , and perhaps - oh climax - open a lucrative connexion . he know , for he seem to know everything do or undo by commerce , that there be a glorious island rich in jewel and marble and every dower of nature , and above all teem with olive , lemon , and grape , and citron ; and that this gifted island still remain a stranger , through french and genoese ignorance , to our London trade . this be the island libel by Seneca , idolise by its native , drench with more blood than all the plain of Emathia , yet mother of hero and conqueror of the world - if that be any credit - in a single word , Corsica . once or twice indeed our countryman have attempt to shake hand with this noble race , so ruin by narrow tradition ; and in the end we shall doubtless succeed , as we always do ; but the grain of the Corsican be almost as stubborn as our own . in fact the staple be much the same , the fabric be very different . bold they be , and manly , simple , generous , and most hospitable , lover too of their country beyond all other nation ; but - oh fatal ignorance - industry to they be drudgery ; and labour be an outrage . bad than all be the fiend of the island , the cursed blood - revenge . 
 " just the place for you , Vaughan , " say the indomitable Peter , " every one there as dignified as an eagle after steal a lamb . no institution to speak of , but the natural one of Vendetta , splendid equality , majestic manhood , lot of true womanhood , and it do all the work that be do , which be not   say much . why , my dear Quixotic , the land of Sampiero and Paoli , and where Rousseau be to legislate , only he prove too lazy , - be not that the jockey for you ? after all these levee and masquerade that you so much delight in - you need not scowl like a bandit ; it be only because they do not   want you , you be just the same as the rest , or why do you notice the nonsense ? - after all this London frippery , Monte Kotondo will be a fresh oyster after devil'd biscuit . " 
 " true enough , my friend : but an oyster to be swallow shell and all . " 
 " well , be not that just what you want ? Lime be good for squeamishness . and more than that , you be just the man we want . you can talk italian with excellent opera style and sentiment ; and you will not   be long till you fraternise with the Corsicans . perhaps they will drive out the French , who do not   know what to do with it , and make you their king like Theodore of Neuhoff ; and then you proclaim free trade restrict to the navy of Green , Vowler , and Green . but in sober earnest , think of it , my dear Vaughan . anything be well than this cynic indolence . some of your view will be correct , and all enlarge by travel . a common sentiment . yes , the very thing you be short of . all your expense we pay of course , and give you an honest salary ; and all we ask of you be to explore more than a tourist would ; and to send we a plain description of everything . you have plenty of observation ; make it useful instead of a torment to you . we know well enough the great gift of that island , but we want to know how they lie , and how we may well get at they . " 
 " then you would expect I to make commercial arrangement ? " 
 Peter laugh outright . " I should rather fancy not . somewhat queer one they would be . platonic no doubt , and panisic , but not altogether adapt to double entry . " 
 " then in fact I be to go as a committee of inquiry . " 
 " I have tell you all we want . if you make any friend all the well ; but that we leave to yourself . perhaps you will grow sociable there . though the Corsican do not sing , ' we will not   go home till morning , ' and be go home all the time . " 
 " and how long would my engagement last ? " 
 " till you have thoroughly traverse the country , if you stick to it so long ; and then if you quit yourself well , we should commission you for Sardinia . what an opening for an idle man , though it would soon kill I - so little to do . but you may cut it short when you like . plenty of our people would jump at such an offer ; but for a country like that we must have a thorough gentleman . a coarse - mannered bagman would very soon secure the content of a fusil . he would be kiss the corsican girl , who be wonderfully lovely they say , and their lover amazingly jealous ; and every man carry a gun . a timid man they despise , an insolent man they shoot ; and most of our fellow be one or the other , or both . but will you undertake it ? yes , or no , on the spot . and I ask you to say ' yes ' as a special favour to I . " 
 " then of course I say yes . when shall I go ? " 
 " to - morrow , if you like . next month if you prefer it . we can give you introduction . there be no real danger for a thorough gentleman , or you should not go for all the olive in Europe . mind we want a particular sort , very long and taper - Virgil 's ' Ray , ' in fact . you shall have a sample of it . as yet we know but one district of Italy where it grow , but have get scent of it in Corsica . glorious fellow they be , if half that I hear be true , glorious fellow but for their laziness , and that - - Vendetta . " 
 to be brief , I receive very clear instruction in writing , and be off for Bonifazio the follow week , in a small swift yacht of my own , a luxury to which I have always aspire , and which I could now for a time afford . but before I go , your poor father , Clara , protest most strongly against the scheme , and even come to London in the vain hope of dissuade I . he have some deep presentiment that it would end darkly , and so indeed it do . 
 " Ned , " say he once more , " there be only two of we , and my dear wife be very delicate . I have be at Genoa , where those islander be well know , and even there they be rarely speak of but with a cold shudder . they be a splendid race , I believe , great hero and all that , but they shoot a man with no more compunction than they shoot a muffro . I implore you , my dear brother , not to risk the last of our family , where blood flow as freely as water . and your temper , you know , be not the good in the world . do not   go , my dear fellow , do not   go . I shall have to come and avenge you , and I do not   understand Vendetta . " 
 ah , I ! if I have only listen to he . and yet , I do not   know . after a pleasant voyage we reach the magnificent island , about the middle of May . my intention be to skirt round it from the southern extremity , take the western side first , and touch at every anchorage , whence I would make incursion , and return to my little cutter , as the most convenient head - quarter . of course I should have to rough it ; but what young man would think twice of that , with an adventurous life before he ? 
 I will not weary you , my dear child , with a long description of Corsica . it be a land which combine all the softness and the majesty , all the wealth and barrenness , all the smile and menace of all the world beside . I could talk of it by the hour ; but you want to know what I do , and be do to , more than what I see . from the awful rock of Bonifazio , the street where man should have no elbow , and the tower of Torrione , along the fantastic coast which look as if time be a giant rabbit , we trace the blue and spur - vex sea , now edge with white , and now with gray , and now with glow red , until we reach that paradise of heaven , the garden of Balagna . 
 CHAPTER IX . 
 STORY of EDGAR VAUGHAN . 
 let I hold myself . weak as I be and cripple by premature old age , not the shortness of my breath , not the numbness of my heart , not even the palsy of my frame , can quench or check the fire rekindle by the mere name of that heavenly valley . to live there only half a minute be worth a day of english life . life - it be a space to measure , not by pendulum or clock - hand , not by our own stride to and fro   the ordnance scale of the million , not even by the rolling sun , and nature 's hail and farewell ; but by the well - spring of ourselves , the fount of thought and feeling . every single breath I draw of this live air - air the bride of earth our sire , wed to he by God Creator , air whose mother - milk we fight for in cluster baulk one another - every breath I draw dance with a buoyant virtue , suck , in any other land , but from mountain nipple . bright air of a rosy blue , where northern eye be daze with beauty , where every flower cut star of light , and every cloud be sunshine 's step ; can even lover part thus believe themselves divide ? every rock have its myrtle favour , every tree its clematis wreath ; under the cistus and oleander hide the pink to lace its bodice , watch by the pansy 's sprightly eye . lavishly , as child 's bubble , hover overhead orange , and citron , lemon , almond , fig , vary by the blushing peach and the purpling grape . far behind , and lean forth the swarthy bosom of the mountain , whose white head lean on the heaven , be rank on rank of glaucous olive , giant of a green old age dash with silver gray . and oh , the fragrance under foot , the tribute of the ground , which Corsica 's great son - as we man measure greatness - pin for in the barren isle , where the iron of his selfishness enter his own soul . 
 these be say to be the large olive - tree in the world , and of the very good variety . heap upon heap the rich fruit lie at the foot of the glorious tree ; nature be too bountiful for man to heed her gift . for this district of Balagna , and that of Nebbio far north , my attention have be especially bespeak by my shrewd and sagacious friend . therefore and by reason of the charm around I , here I resolve to pass the summer ; so my vessel be lay up at Calvi , and be quarter in Belgodere at a little Inn - " locanda " it should be call , but I hate interlard - I address myself right heartily to business and to pleasure . 
 first I have to study the grand palladian gift . unless old Seneca be , as the Corsicans say , a great liar , he can not have be the author of that epigram which declare this land a stranger to the peaceful boon . it be impossible to believe that a country so adapt to that tree , so often colonize by cultured race , can have be so long ungifted with its staff of life . the island itself in that same epigram be utterly mis - describe . 
 as regard the inhabitant , the first line of the well - know couplet be verify by age ; to the second it do not plead guilty now , and probably never do . 
 " Law the first revenge . law the second to live by robbery . 
 Law the third to lie . the fourth to deny any Gods . " 
 
 the Corsicans , on the contrary , have always be famous for candour , whose very soul be truth , and for superstition , the wen or hump of religion . for my own part , love not that unprinciple     fellow hard labour , towards whom these noble islander entertain a like antipathy , and love much any freedom not hostile to my own , I get on with the native admirably , for a certain time . Time have reconcile I to their custom of carry , instead of cane or umbrella , long double - barrel gun , whose muzzle they afford the stranger full opportunity of inspect . first - rate marksman be they , but they sle their gun at hap - hazard on their back , and cheek to jowl we come upon the cold metal at the corner of the narrow street . tall and powerful man they be , especially the mountaineer ; with all the Spaniard 's dignity and the Italian 's native grace . the woman be lithe , erect , and beautifully form , with a swan - like carriage , and a free and courteous bearing , such as very few of our high - bear damsel own . 
     " Labor improbus " of Virgil . 
 the olive - grower frankly give I all their little information about that tree whose typical virtue they have never care to learn . the variety chiefly grow , or rather which chiefly grow itself , be one they call the Genoese . the owner afford they very little culture , and many be too idle even to collect the fruit . there be say to be ten million olive - tree in the island ; at least they be reckon up to that number by order of the government ; then the enumerator grow tired , and leave off counting . whatever number there be might easily be triple , if any one have the energy to graft the oleaster , with which the hill be cover . there be also the Saracen olive , and the Sabine , the latter perhaps the Regia of Columella , Raggiaria of Casalpinus , and Radius of Virgil . however , though not unlike my sample fruit , it be not quite identical , and as my employer want a very special sort for very special quality , I be as far from my object as ever . 
 one magnificent summer evening , as I ride along the mountain side near the village of Speloncato , suddenly the track turn sharply into a woode dingle . steep in the dream of nature 's beauty , I be think of nothing at all , as become a true Corsican , when I receive a sharpish knock in the eye . something fall and lodge in my capacious beard . smart from the pain . I catch it , and not be able to see clearly , take it at first for a spent and drop bullet . but when my eye have cease to water , I find in my hand a half - grow olive of the very kind I have so long be seek . I draw forth some of my London specimen which have be chemically treat to prevent their shrivelling , and compare it narrowly . yes , there could be no doubt ; the same pyriform curve , the same bulge near the peduncle , the same violet line in the skin , and when cut open , the same granulation and nucleus . I be truly delighted , at length I should be of some real service ; at least if there be many tree here of this most rare variety . by rid up the dingle , I soon ascertain that it be plant with tree of this sort only , gray old tree of a different habit from any other olive . afterwards I find that it require a different soil , and a different aspect . full speed I gallop back to the hamlet of Speloncato , and inquire for the owner of this olive Eldorado . signor Dezio Della Croce , owner of all this lovely slope , and of large estate extend as far as the road to Corte ; in fact the chief proprietor of the neighbourhood . he be , say the peasant with some pride , a true descendant of the great race of Cinarca , foremost in the island annal for a thousand year , and of whom be the famous Giudice Della Rocca , Count and Judge of Corsica , six hundred year ago . " 
 at the sound of his name , Giudice open his great sleepy eye , and prick his ear : I promise not to interrupt , but he give no such pledge . 
 " let the Cinarchesi blood go for its full value ; but it be worth something to the Della Croce to be descend also from the Tuscan Malaspina ; for the land of those great Marquises be now in the possession of the Signor Dezio . and the Signor have such a daughter , a young maiden . ah , Madonna ! the lovely girl in Corsica . and the vine - dresser cross himself . as I listen to all this information , I begin to look through my unused credential , which I always carry . it strike I that this name of Della Croce be quite familiar to I , though I know not how , until a letter in the sprawl hand of young Laurence Daldy fall out from among Peter 's crab character . Laurence Daldy , my mother 's young son , be now in full career , as a pigeon and a Guardsman , spending at full gallop his dead father 's money . these Daldys be of italian origin , the true name be D'Aldis , which after some year of english life they have naturalise into Daldy . and now I recollect that when we Vaughan boy scorn they as ignoble son of commerce , they use to brag about their kinship to the ancient Della Croce . 
 rid up the forest hill , on whose western bluff stand boldly the gray old tower of the Malaspinas , I begin of course to make forecast about the character of my host . my host I know he need must be , for Corsica be of all the world the most hospitable spot . although by this time well acquainted with the simple island habit , I could not but expect to find a man of stateliness and surrounding , of stiffness and some arrogance . now the sun be set , and the western fire from off the sea glance in spear of redden gold into the solemn timeworn keep . all thing look majestic , but a deal too lonely . where be I to apply , how be I to get in ? the narrow doorway overhung with the wreck of some portculli , be block instead with a sort of mantlet like the Roman Vinea ; the loophole on the ground - tier be board almost to the top , the high window , such as they be , have their rough shutter close . everything betoken a state of siege and fear . two or three magnificent chesnut , which must have command the front of the tower , have be cut down and add to the defence of the approach . over these I manage at last to leap my horse , who be by no mean a perfect hunter ; and there I halt at a loss how to proceed . I have be long enough in Corsica to know , even without a certain ominous gleam from a loophole , and the view in transverse section of a large double - barrel gun , that the owner of this old mansion be now in the pleasant state of Vendetta . 
 expect every moment to be shoot , and nothing say about it , I wave my letter , as a white flag , furiously above my head . presently that frightful muzzle be withdraw , and the slide push back , to reconnoitre I at leisure . I try , for the first time in my life , to look like a real Briton ; my corsican ambition be already on the wane . so I sit my horse , and wait ; and what come be worth a thousand year of wait . 
 round the bastion of the tower , under the rich magnolia bloom , towards I glide through the rosy shadow the lovely being that ever move outside the gate of heaven . she seem not to walk but waft along , like the pearly Nautilus . a pink mandile of light gauze light the sable of her clustering hair , and wreathe round her graceful head deepen the tinge of the nestling cheek . the lithe faldetta of white cashmere , throw hastily over the shoulder , half conceal the flow curve of the slender supple form , half betray they as it follow every facile motion . but when she smile - oh , Clara , I would have leap from her father 's tower , or into the black cave of the Restonica , for one smile of her . the dark - fringe lustre of her eye seem to dance with golden joy , trusting , hop , love all thing , pleasure please at pleasing . and the gleesome arch of her laugh lip , that never shape evil word ! oh , my Lily , my own Lily , I shall see you soon again .
