' you see , Miss , he have get his hat off , and he stand out of door , on the shady side of the street ! so what I say seem to stop he altogether , and he look as if he want to think about it ; and I be just a slapping the door in their face , when the other man , the queer guy I ever see , a hanging in his clothe like a skiver in a dish - clout , he look full in my face as grave as a heretic parson , and stretch out his skinny arm , and keep time with one foot , while he say or sing , 
 " ' Ma'am , we be here now in this Lunnon town , 
 and it bain't   likely as we be go down , 
 till we see every mortal thing as there be for to see , 
 and take all the change out in a thorough - go spree . ' 
 
 then the big man laugh and clap he on the back ; and the little one wink both his eye , and look to see what I think of it . then when he see I laugh , he make I such a coorous bow , that what with his - what do they call the plaister , Miss ? " 
 " Diachylon , perhaps you mean , Mrs. Shelfer ? " 
 " ah , that be the word . what with his strange diaculum , and his dancing altitude , I declare I be a most a go to invite they in : but I recollect , no , no : if Charley get along of such Reginalds as these , I may stand at the bed - room door and whistle for a week . there be nothing Charley love so much as a downright Reginald . " 
 poor simple - minded woman ; how little she perceive that she of all the number be by far the most original ! and , like most of those who be truly so , she would have take the imputation as an outrageous insult . only the sham original glory in be think queer . 
 " well , Mrs. Shelfer , I want to hear the end of it . " 
 " just what I say , Miss. yes , yes , no time to spare , and the pudding boiling . so I say , quite sharp , ' what name , my good sir , and will you leave a message ? Miss Vaughan be out of town . ' ' wull , ' say he , just as I tell you , Miss , ' ony plase you say , ma'am , as Jan Uxtable , and Beany Dawe , and the two beggest of the chiller have do theirselve the honour of come to lave their dooty . ' then the little girl look up and she flash her ribbon and say , ' Mr. Huxtable , if you please , ma'am , and Mr. Ebenezer Dawe , and Miss Huxtable , and Master John , have call . ' ' had not   you well write it down , Miss ? ' say I , as innocent as possible . ' do you suppose I can not   then ? ' say she , with such a spitting out of her eye , and she swing a new parry sole . ' just give I a sheet of papper , if you keep such a thing in the house . ' ' plase to excuse the little wanch , ma'am , ' say the big man , quite humble , ' we can not   hardly make head nor tail of she , since she come to this here Lunnon . if I have only know it I would have have her mother along of I , that I would ee fai , and the coo be her own midwaife . but ony plase you say Jan Uxtable come if they count it dacent hereaway . Threescore acre and five , ma'am , without reckon the Cleeve , and no man have a call , to my mind , to christen himself " Mister " on less than a hundred acre , in Lunnon or out of it . ' ' very well , sir , ' I say , for I take to the big man somehow , ' I will deliver your message . Miss Vaughan only go from here of middle day on Wednesday . ' ' and tell she please , if she do come back , ' say spirity Miss Parrysole , with the tear in her great blue eye , ' that Sally Huxtable leave she very good love and duty , and hope so much Miss Clara will come to see the great wrestling to - morrow , twelve o'clock , and be early . and they be bet now two to one on the other man , ma'am . but he have no chance , no more than Tim Badcock with father . ' ' I be much afeare , ma'am , ' say the deep - voice man , as soft as any bell , ' I be afeare our Sally will be begger by a lanyard nor ever her daddy or her mammy be . but likely it be all for the good . ' and with that all four of they crook their leg to I most polite , and go on round the corner ; and after they go a score of boy , that seem to follow they everywhere . the boy know all about it , and so do I at last , that it be the great champion wrestling , that be to be to - morrow . Charley have be mad about it go on now two month . and can you please to tell he , Miss , which way to lay his money ? " 
 " to be sure , I can . let he take every offer of two to one against the Devonshire champion ; and if he lose I will make it good to he , upon condition that he give you everything he win . now please to let I have a cup of strong tea . " 
 have thus get rid of my most talkative friend , and Mrs. Fletcher have start off to buy something , I have time to think a little . 
 it be nearly two o'clock on the Friday afternoon . nothing more could be do at present towards recover Conrad , for he have not even leave at his lodging any Continental address . possibly his place of sojourn might be reveal in the letter to his sister , post by my hand : but it be far more likely that he himself know not , at the time of writing , where he should find quarter . I must have be beside myself with worry and disappointment , when I drop that letter into her Majesty 's box ; for if I return , as have be arrange , by the express at five o'clock , several hour would be save in the delivery of its tiding . and , as yet , I little dream where I should be at five P.M. 
 in that little room , whose wall be more relieved than decorate by certain daub of mine , which even in my narrow strait I could not bear to part with , because an indulgent critic have find merit in they - a discovery require much acuman - here I now sit , gaze fondly , dream hazily , yearn strongly for the day go by , yet only three month old , when I have not a crust or dress till I earn it by my labour . how that pinch enlarge my heart , God only know , not i. ah , then I be a happy girl , though I never guess it . how proudly I walk down the Square , with my black straw bonnet on - which Idols call the Dowdy , - and my dark plaid shawl around I , the plainest of the plain , yet not prepared to confess myself so quotidian as my dress . who could tell , in those happy day , who might come , or round what corner , and who could say whether of the twain would look the more accidental ? and then the doubt - shall I look or not , well perhaps be intent on the fire - plug , and make he come round again ? 
 but now . ah I , they have heap up rich for I , and who shall come to enjoy they ? 
 just as I be warm to this subject , gush along in a fine vein of that compassion which alone of soft emotion we find it no duty to wrestle with , I mean of course self - pity - in come Mrs. Fletcher , suddenly , and in anger . 
 " well , Miss Clara , " she exclaim , throw down her parcel , ' so this be London , be it ? " 
 " to be sure , Mrs. Fletcher . what objection have you to make to it ? " 
 " no objection , Miss , only this , that if ever I see a set of countrifie folk , the Londoners be they . why the commonest of our kitchen - maid would be ashamed to talk so broad , and to dress so contemptuous . and here I go half a mile to buy boot , real London - make ; and tree all along by the side of the road , and pot on the shelf of the window . I never , if Gloucester do not   look much more like a town . " 
 as Mrs. Fletcher do not tell a story with the herodotean vivacity of Tim Badcock , I will render her fact in my own unpretending version , premise only that she have take the farmer and Sally for specimen of the true Cockney ; a bit of saltatory reasoning of which she have not hear   and perhaps never will hear   the last . while then the worthy housekeeper be drive a slow but shrewd bargain , in a smart shop by the Broadway , take the boot to the sunshine , to pick clever hole in the stitching , she observe a diminutive boy , of the genuine shoe - black order , encamp in a bight or back - eddy of pavement , just at the side of the door . this little fellow be uniformed , or rather multi - colour , in gold , and red , and green . his cap be scarlet , and edge with gold twist ; his tunic red , and his apron of very bright green baize . on his cap , and on one shoulder , appear his number , 32 , in figure of brass , an inch and a half in length . strap on his back he carry an oblong block of wood , like a great club - foot , and nearly as large as himself . this he deposit , with elaborate fuss , on the curb of the inner pavement , which terrace some inch above the true thoroughfare . a black - jar hang at one end of his block ; from a drawer below he pull out three well - wear brush , and begin to hiss and to work away , in double quick time , with both hand , at some boot project towards he on the delicate foot of fancy . as he grow warm at his work , with one sharp eye all the while look out for a genial passenger , there slowly come straggle towards he a bevy quite fresh from Arcadia . first , in treble importance walk , impressively roll and leer around , Hermes , Pan , and the owl of Pallas , combine in one Ebenezer Dawe . his eye , never too co - operative , rove away upon either side , in quest of intelligence , which they receive with a blink that mean , " Pooh , do not   I know it ? " with occasional jerk of his lank right arm , he be drag along , like a saw through a knot , the sturdy , tight - button , and close - pronged form of our little Jack . Jack be array in a black wide - awake , with blue ribbon , and a bran - new suit of broad - furrow corduroy , make of night by his mother and Suke , and turn out with countless pocket , each have three broad button , to foil the London thief . in one of these pocket , the trouser one I do believe , in spite of all Sally have teach he , he be now chink , to the creak of the corduroys , his last - abide halfpence , and lag heavily on the poet 's arm , he cast fond glance at a pile of glorious peg - top . stick her toe into little Jack 's heel , to kick anybody that dare to steal he , come my little Sally , all fire , and wonder , and self - assertion , tow her mighty father along , like a grasshopper lead an ox . at time she strove to drag he towards the finery of the window , and pay very little heed to his placid protestation . " walk fitty , my dear ; walk as you ought to do , my dear . oh fai ! oh fai ! whatever wull they lunnoner think of Davonsheer , if they zee you agooin on laike this here ? there , dang that Beany Dawe ; bl if I baint a toornin Poüt too . Coomth of larnin to wraite , I reckon . " the farmer 's pocket be cram with circular , handbill , and puff of every description , which he receive from all who offer , and be save they all for his wife . 
 " clean your boot , my gentleman , " cry a little shrill voice ; " clean both your boot for a halfpenny . never say die , Sir ; polish them bright till the cat at home will not   know they . three - farding - worth of blacking , and a penny in skill and labour , and all for the laughable sum of one half - penny . pure satisfaction guarantee , or the whole of the money return . up with your foot , my gentleman ! " 
 the farmer pull up suddenly , for fear of walk over he , as the boy , despise Beany Dawe , have dash in between Jack and Sally , and dance before Mr. Huxtable . his brush be whisk about , like bumble - bee roughly disturb , and already menace the drab of the Sunday fustian gaiter . 
 " Zober now , " cry the farmer , who could not believe that he be address , have never dream , in his most ambitious moment   if any such he have , of ever be call a gentleman , " zober now , wull'e . where bee'st gooin to , thou little hosebird ; be they your lunnon - town manner ? Lat alo - un , I zay ; lat alo - un now , wull ' e ? " - as the boy get more and more tentative - " Heart alaive , can not e zee , they be my Zunday gaiter ? oh , if my missus wor here ! and ' e bain't   more nor naine year old ! wull , wull , where ever do ' e goo to schoüll ? " 
 " Hinstitooshun 66 . no child or female admit . up with your foot , old bloke ! do the young uns and tootor half - price . just two minute to spare , till the Dook of Cambridge 's turn . great Exhibition polish , and all to encourage the fine art . " 
 the good farmer be lose beyond hope , in the multitude of subject press all of a pulp on his slow understanding ; nevertheless , he have presence of mind to feel first for his watch and his money , and then for the good pocket - handkerchief stitch into the crown of his hat ; meanwhile the boy get hold of one foot , and begin to turn up his gaiter . then Sally and little Jack rush to the rescue , and Jack punch the boy in the face , while Beany Dawe look on with a grin of broad experience . but in spite of all aid , the farmer begin to collapse before his mosquito enemy ; when luckily three giant life - guard   for a crowd be now collect   open their mouth , like the end of a monkey - fur muff , in a round and loud guffaw , with a very coarse sneer at poor Sally . the farmer look at they in much amazement ; then his perplexity go like a cloud , and his face shine with something to do , as he give Sally his hat to hold . till now all the mocker have be too small for he anyhow to fall foul of . Ere the echo of laughter be over , the three dandy Lifeguards lie on their back in the mud , with their strip leg erect in the air , like the rod of a railway surveyor . the crowd fall back headlong , as if from a plunge horse , then laugh at the fallen and with the conqueror . even the boy be humility multiply into servility . 
 " Wutt be up to , arl on ' e ? " ask the farmer , replace his hat ; " cas'n none on ' e lat a pacible chap alo - un ? and wutt will they chiller think as coom here to get example ? why , Beany , if we have know this , we would have bring Bill constable with we , ees fai . now ' e do not   know nothing about it " - he remonstrate with the admire multitude - " one o ' they dree worn't   throw handsome laike , ony dree pin , I tull ' e. but us'll do it over again , if he claimeth it . can not   do nothing vitty , zin I lave my missus at home . but she wadn't   coom , God know . " these last two remark be address to himself , but the crowd have full benefit of they . " worn't   ' e ax of lave , two or dree minute agone , little chap with the brisk there , to tend my butt , and tuk it amost wiout ax ? Us be bind laike to stap here now till we zee if they ' lisher man feel up for any moor plai . do as ' e plase , little chap , zoon as Sally hath toorne my good gaiter up , if she bain't   too grand in Lunnon . " 
 with bright ribbon flutter and finery flap about she , poor Sally kneel down in a moment to work at the muddy fustian : but her father would not allow it , he have only wish to try she ; so he catch she up with one hand , and kiss she , and I think , from what Mrs. Fletcher say , he must have give her sixpence at least . 
 it be needless to say that , although the boy work with both hand in the most conscientious manner , the farmer 's boot defy he . Neats'-foot oil , and tallow , and beeswax hold their own against Day and Martin . " Coom , little chap , " say Mr. Huxtable , kindly , " thee hast dooe thy very good , but our Zuke will have the laugh of thee . tache thee perhaps it wull to be zoberer next taime , and not be quite so peart to do a dale more nor thee can do . but thee hast use more ink than ai wud over two copy . here be a groat for the Exhibition polish . " 
 in this little episode , as will be manifest , Sally have help I more than Mrs. Fletcher . but now , to return to my narrative . 
 almost directly after the housekeeper leave I , Patty come trot in with a large white breakfast - cup full of most powerful tea . I can not help think that the little woman put some brandy in it , or allow Mrs. Fletcher , who trust much in that cordial , to do so ; but they stoutly deny the charge , and declare that there be only a pinch of gunpowder . whatever it be , be parch with thirst , I swallow without taste it , and the effect upon my jade brain be immediate and amazing . all self - pity be go ; and self - admiration , and haughty courage succeed . be I , Clara Vaughan , who have grope and grub for year to find the hole of a blast snake , and have now get my hand upon it , be I to start back and turn pale at his hiss , and say , " God speed you and polish your skin . give I your slough for a keepsake ? " would I not rather seize the incarnate devil , trample his spine , and make his tongue sputter in dust ? in a moment my cloak and hat be on again ; I scarcely look at the glass , but feel the hot flush on my cheek , as I lightly skip down the stair , and silently leave the house . what to do next I know not , nor ask , but fly headlong before the impulse , to lift and confront - as be my nature - the danger that lie before I . as I glide along , I be conscious of one thing , the people in the street turn in surprise to watch I . as if by instinct , I hurry straight to Lucas Street , my courage mount high and high as I near the accursed threshold . Balaam and Balak stand at the bar of a tavern which command a view of the street , but be much too busy with beer to see I pass so swiftly . loudly I ring the bell of No . 37 ; the figure be bright on the door , and look narrowly , I perceive the old no . 19 , more by the line than the colour . 
 Old Cora come as usual ; but start at see I , and turn as pale as death . 
 " be your master within ? " I could not use his false name . 
 " yes , Meesa , but you not see he now . " 
 " dare you to disobey our Lady 's heart ? " and I hold my gordit before she . she cower with one knee on the mat and kiss it ; then lead I into the presence of Lepardo Della Croce . 
 CHAPTER VIII . 
 it be a dark and gloomy room , with three high , narrow window . Cora depart hastily , frighten at what she have do . in a recess at the farth end , before a chest of black bog - oak , sit the man I seek . the crown moment of my life be come . all rehearsal go for nothing : the strong feeling of my heart be scorn , cold , unfathomable scorn . to show myself well , I take off my hat , and advance in my haughty manner . 
 as he turn his head , I see that his mood be black than the oak before he . some dark memorial perhaps be there ; hastily and heavily he fling down the lid , as I walk with even step towards he . 
 " ah ! Miss Valence ! the young lady that paint . I fear that you be lose to London ; for now - a - day the pursuit of the fine art require either genius , or fashion , at any rate the latter most , to be at all remunerative . may I show you the way to the drawing - room ? I have not often the honour of receive visitor here . but I think you know how entirely I be the slave of young lady , Miss Valence . " and he hold out his delicate hand . 
 " Lepardo Della Croce , my name be not valence . I be Clara Vaughan , the only child of he whom in his sleep you murder . " 
 he turn not pale , but livid . his jaunty nonsense be go in a moment . he quail from my dark eye , and fall upon a chair . for one minute there he crouch , and dare not meet my gaze ; every fibre of his flesh be quiver . it be not shame that cow he , but the prostration of amazement . 
 suddenly he leap upright , and meet I eye to eye . then I see that his pupil turn towards each other , as my uncle have describe . I neither speak , nor allow my gaze to falter . every nerve and cord of my frame be tense , and rigid , and root . to he I must have seem the embodiment of revenge . 
 at last he speak , very slowly , and in word that tremble . 
 " you have no right to judge I by your english notion . you do not understand I . " 
 " I judge you not at all . God shall judge and smite you . in cold blood you murder a man who never wrong you . " 
 " what ! " he burst forth in a blaze of triumph , " no wrong to steal my lovely bride , and my noble inheritance , to debauch the pure blood of Corsica by a prostitute wedding ; no wrong to strike I senseless ! even your nation of policeman would call this rather initiative . " 
 " the man you steal upon in his sleep have never see or hear of you , have never be in Corsica . " 
 " what ? " his tooth strike together like fire - tong badly joint , and he could not part they . 
 " it be true . I regret to inform you that you must go to hell for nothing . you could not even murder the right man . " 
 " tell I . " 
 " like a coward as you be , you crawl , and lurk , and lie ; you spend what little mind you have in secure a baby 's blow , you crouch among old clothe and bed - tick , and behind the housemaid 's flask ; and you go away exult in your bloody soul , over what ? the wrong man 's murder . " 
 " can it be ? " 
 " not only this , but you enrich and bring into high position the man you mean to kill . he become the lord of his half - brother 's land , and now be wealthy and happy , and the child you stole will help he to laugh at your Vendetta . " 
 " wait a little . " 
 " cat and small dog you can carve alive , when a woman have strap they down for you , and the poor thing be try to lick you . but as for midnight murder , however sound your victim sleep , you have not nerve enough . you quake and quiver so that you know not a dark man from a fair . clever , do not   you think ? particularly for a Professor . " 
 I see that my contempt be curl round he like a knout ; so I give he a little more of it . 
 " of course we could not expect you to meet your foe like a man . even be you a worthy sample of your sneaking race , you never could do that . too wholesome memory of the english blow between your quail eye . I be pleased to see you fumble clumsily for your dagger . who know but what you be fool enough even to have some self - respect ? " 
 a black tint dart beneath his skin , as if his heart be a cuttle - fish . have I take my eye from he , he would have stab I . he fall back against the oak chest . my madness grow with my triumph . 
 " no . you dare not do it , because I be not asleep . come , I will give you every chance , Lepardo Della Croce . if you be brave enough to shoot a white - haired man at dinner , surely you have the courage to stab a young girl on the sofa . here I lie . I will not move . and I defy you to do it . " 
 quietly I lay and watch he ; but as if he be scarcely worth it . he could not take his eye from mine . he be like a rat before a snake . and all the while , his hand be work on the cross haft of a poniard . 
 " what more can I do to encourage you ? would you like the curtain to skulk behind ? " 
 and I throw the window - hanging over the foot of the sofa , but so that I hold he still in view . calm as I be , I must have be mad to play with my life so contemptuously . presently I rise , put back my hair and turn away , as in weariness . 
 " I fear your appetite be cloy with the writhing of cat and dog . or have murder no relish for you , unless it be in cold blood ? but there , I be tired of you : you have so little variety . we will send you back to Corsica , and write ' Rimbecco ' on you . " 
 he spring at I madly , gnash his tooth , and whirl his stiletto . I face he just in time , with both hand by my side . have I raise they , or show the least sign of fear , my life would have follow my father 's then and there . 
 " yes , " I say , while he pause , with the weapon not a yard from I , " a spirited attempt , consider what you be . but waste of time and trouble . however , I have hit the word which seem to suit your view . allow I to repeat the agreeable term , ' Rimbecco . ' " 
 I see in his eye the flash which show the momentum give , but his arm fall powerless . he look even humbly at I . 
 " Clara Vaughan - " 
 " be kind enough to address I properly . " 
 " Miss Vaughan , you must have some powerful reason for wish to be rid of life . " he try to look piercingly at I . 
 " you be quite mistaken . it be nothing more than contempt of an abject coward and murderer . " 
 " to you I will make no attempt to justify myself . you could not understand I . your way of thought be wholly different . " 
 " I beg leave to hope so . do not   come near I , if you please . " 
 " if I have injure you in ignorance , I will do my good to make amend . what course do you propose ? " 
 " to let you go free , in pity for your abject nature and cowardice . we scorn you too much for anything else . " 
 this seem to amaze he more than all before . it be plain that he could not believe I . a long silence ensue . look at the wily wretch , I begin unwittingly to compare , or rather to contrast his noble victim with he . I think of the deep affliction and misery work by his despicable revenge . I think of his brutal cruelty to the poor creature God have give we ; and a rancour like his own begin to move in my troubled heart . it have be there all the while , no doubt , but a large pressure have still it . watch I intently , he see the change in my countenance , and as cold disdain grow flush with anger , my power over he depart . but he do not let I perceive it . I be sure that I might have go whither and when I please , and he would have fear to follow I , if I have only regard he to the end with no other emotion than scorn . 
 " be I to understand , " he say at last , " that you intend to do nothing to I ? " 
 " it be not worth our while to hang you . for such a crime any other punishment would be an outrage and a jest . you slew a good and a gentle man ; one as brave as you be cowardly . by the same blow you destroy his wife , who linger for a few year , pin till she die . both of these be dear to God . he will avenge they in his good time . only one thing we shall insist on , that you leave this country immediately , and under a solemn oath never to return to it . one good point you have , I be tell - fidelity to your word . " 
 " and if I refuse , what then ? " 
 " then you die a murderer 's death . we have evidence you little dream of . " 
 he have now recover his presence of mind , and his scoff manner ; and all his plan be form . 
 " what a brave young lady you be to come here all alone , and entertain so low an opinion of the poor Professor . " 
 " the very reason why I scorn precaution . " a deep gleam shoot through the darkness of his eye . 
 " you must indeed despise I , to come here without tell any one ! " 
 " of course . but I do not mean to come , till my father 's spirit lead I . " 
 with a shudder he glance all round the room . Lily be not mistaken when she call he superstitious . then he try to sneer it off . 
 " and do the good Papa , dear to God , undertake to escort you back ? " see that I disdain to answer , he continue thus : " you have display much graceful and highly - become scorn . I , in turn , will exhibit some little contempt of you . you be pleased to say , if my memory serve I , that you have some wonderful evidence . I will furnish you with more , and perhaps what you little dream of . approach , and examine this box . " 
 he raise the lid of the oaken chest , and prop it with a staple . quite throw off my guard for the moment , I begin to devour the content with my eye . not many thing be in it ; but all of they be remarkable . to I they look like theatrical property , or material for disguise . some of they be fade and tarnish ; some be set with a silver cross . my gaze be rivette on a pair of boot , fix in a ledge with horse - shoe bay ; on the sole of one I perceive a cross of metal inlay ; I draw near to see it more closely , when something fall over my head . all down I , and round I , and twist behind in a tight tourniquet , before I could guess what it be . I be not weak , for a girl ; but I could no more lift my arm than a swathe mummy can . neither could I kick , although as a child I have be famous for that accomplishment ; if I lift either foot , I must tumble head - foremost into the box , which be large enough for I to live in . Scream I could , and do , in spite of all my valour , not only from fright , but from pain , for my chest be dreadfully tighten ; but before I could scream more than twice , a cloth be pass over my mouth , and knot behind my neck . so there I stand , a helpless prisoner , in the recess at the end of the oaken ark . a low laugh thrill in my ear , but the hand on my spine relax not ; I turn my neck by a violent effort and meet the demon 's eye . 
 " very pretty you look , young lady , very pretty indeed . I must have a kiss before I have do with you , in spite of all indignation . there be a dress resemble this among the Tartar tribe . do I hurt your proud , straight nose ? if so , accept most humble apology . I would not injure it for the world ; it do express so much scorn . take care , my child , your eyelash be come through the worsted . " 
 yes . ignoble confession ! I , for whose disdain the world have be too small , be prisone and helpless in an " anti - macassar , " like a fly in a paper cage - trap . the sofa , on which I have lie so grandly defy my enemy , be cover with a stout worst net , long and very strong : this he have double end to end , and fling over my haughty head . I have not patience to recount his paltry , banter jeer . Contempt be a tool I be use to grasp by the handle only . be it enough to say that , without release I , he ring the bell for Cora , whose greedy eye glisten when she see my gordit loose from my bosom , and tangle in the net . her master allow she to disengage , and , for the time at least , appropriate it . in return for this , she be , at his pleasure , to stab I if he should order it . by his direction , she tie my ankle together , while he lash my arm anew , and tighten the muffler over my bleed lip . I close my eye , and pray ; then I make up my mind to die , as many a Vaughan have do , at the hand of a brutal enemy . my last thought be of Conrad , and then my sens forsook I . 
 CHAPTER IX . 
 I have a faint recollection of feel myself swung , and jolt down a number of stair , and of a cold breeze strike on my face . and doubtless they carry I down ; for the room in which I have find my enemy be two floor above the cellarage . when I come to myself , I have no idea where in the world I be . the air be heavy with a most powerful and oppressive smell , a reek and taint as of death and corruption . it make I faint , and I think I must have go off again . lift my head at last , I begin to look languidly around . the table , or working - bench , on which I lay , be near the centre of a long and narrow room , gloomy and cold , even in the dog - day , floor with moss - green stone , and far below the ground - level . those flag - stone , I suppose , be bed immediately upon the tough blue London clay , that most unconquerable stratum , sullen , damp , and barren . I could only see two window in the long low room , both upon the same side , horizontally fix , and several foot from the floor . heavy iron bar , perpendicularly set , cross they at narrow interval , as if it have be the condemn cell in a prison . one of these window be already darken with a truss of straw , and sack over it , place outside the glass ; as be do in Corsica , during Vendetta siege . the technical term be " inceppar le fenestre . " through the other window   which look up a slide or scoop of brickwork , like a malt - shovel , to the flabby garden behind the house , I see an arm , the colour and shape of an american herring , very active with a hammer . 
 I know that arm at once . stick out at the joint , like the spur of a pear - tree , welt and wire with muscle between they , like the drumstick of a turkey , but flat as if plait of hide , no friend of mine could claim it , except the Corsican Cora . deliberately she drive the nail , like a gardener train a tree , pay undue attention to her skinny knuckle ; then she lift the sack , stoop down and look in , grimly reconnoitre I . by the slanting light I see what a horrible place I lay in . around and under I , on the furrowed timber , be dull plum - colour blotch , where the slowly trickle blood of many an unlucky dog and cat have curdle ; even if there be not any shed from nobler vein . reach in a back - handed way towards the jagged margin , I grasp a cold hard cylinder . it be an iron hold - fast , like , but large than the instrument to be see in every carpenter 's bench , which work in a collared hole , and have a claw for clutch . under it , no doubt , many a poor live victim have quiver and sob in vain . at my head be two square slide , fit with strap of stout unyielding web . near they be a rasped iron plane work along a metal bed or groove , with a solid t piece , and a winch to adjust it . 
 as with morbid observation I survey these fiendish device , and many other which I can not stop to tell of , I who love almost every creature make by our own maker , especially those to whom we be lend as Gods , my flesh , I say , begin to creep , and my blood to curdle , as if the dissect knife be already in my diaphragm . surely those who in full manhood torture his innocent creature - poor thing that can not plead or weep , but worship the foot that kick they - surely these , if any , we may without presumption say that he who make will judge . four brief line by a modern poet , too well know for I to quote they , express a grand and simple truth , seldom deny , more seldom feel . 
 but here be I , lay out in this fearful place , perhaps myself a subject for vivisection . no , I be not strapped ; even my foot be free . off the grouted and grimy table I roll with all possible speed , the table where even strong Judy must have lain still as a skeleton . of skeleton there be plenty range around the wall , and other hideous thing which I can not bear to think of . one be a monstrous crocodile , with scale like a shed fir - cone , all reflexed and dry , and ringent lip of leather , and tooth that seem to look the wrong way , like a daisy - rake over - work . another be some pull - out beast , that never could hit his own joint again - plesiosauri , deinosauri , marsupial , proboscidian - I be sure I can not tell , have never be at college . I only know that at every one of they I shudder , and shrug my shoulder , and wish that he smell rather nice . then there be number of thing always go up and down , in stuff like clarify syrup , accord to the change of temperature , just as leech do in a pickle - bottle . snake as well , and other reptile streak like stick of peppermint , and centipede , and Rio wrigglers , call I think La Croya . it be enough in that vault - like room , which feel like the scooping of an August iceberg ; it be more than enough to strike a chill to the marrow , as of one who sleep in a bed newly bring from the cellar . but the bad and most horrible thing of all be the core and nucleus of the smell that might be feel , the half - dissect body of a porpoise , lean on a dozen stout cross - pole . it be enough to make the blood of a dog run cold . 
 overpower by sight and smell , and the fear of mingle with they , I huddle away in a corner , and try in vain to take my eye from the only sign of life yet leave , the motion of Cora 's club - like arm . the poor old woman enjoy my interest in her work , and when she have finish , she make I a mock salaam , and kiss the pixie 's heart . then , with a grin , she drop the rough hanging , and leave I in ghastly twilight . 
 as the sack fall over the window - frame , I lose all presence of mind , all honest indignation , everything but a coward horror , and the shrinking of life from death . with all the strength of my chest and throat , I cast forth , as a cannon discharge , one long , volleyed , agonising shriek . as it ring among the skeleton , and rattle their tissue - less joint , a small square grating in the upper panel of the heavy door swing back , and in the opening appear the face of Lepardo Della Croce . he lift his hat with a pleasant air , and address I with a smile , 
 " ah ! now , this I call a pity , a great pity , indeed , Miss Vaughan ; but that I always fear the imputation of pedantry , I should call it a bathos . you can hardly be aware that since you make that dreadful noise , you have fall in my opinion from a Porcia , or an Arria , to a common maid Marian . Fie , fie , it be too disappointing . it sap one 's candid faith in the nobility of human nature . but , as I can no long appeal to your courage or spirit , I must , it appear , address myself to your reason ; if , as I be fain to hope , your nerve have not impair it . be assure , then , once for all , that it be a vulgar error to exert your sweet voice in so high a key . my little dissect theatre , though not so perfect as I could wish , particularly in ventilation , be nevertheless so secured from erroneous plebeian sympathy , that all the cat in London might squall away their fabulous nine life without affect the tea and muffin of the excellent old lady who live on either side of we . that noble tabby , on the third shelf right , be a household god at No . 39 , until he have the honour of attract my attention . breathe not a word about he , if you ever come out . twice a day , I send to inquire , with my kind compliment , whether poor Miss Jenkinson have recover her darling cat . meanwhile , by inanition scientifically graduate , I succeed in absorb his adipose deposit , and find he one of the kind subject I have have the pleasure of manipulating . be not alarmed , Miss Vaughan ; I have no intention of starve you ; neither , if you behave with courtesy , will I even dissect you . I only mention these little fact to convince you of our pleasing retirement . the ceiling of your room be six foot below the level of the street , the wall be three foot thick and felt , and the brick set all as header , which make a great difference in conduct power . the window , as perhaps you have already observe , be seclude from vulgar eye , and command a very partial view of our own little Eden . moreover , if by exert your nobly - develop chest , to an extent which for your sake I affectionately deprecate , you even succeed at last in produce an undulation - do you remember my lecture upon the conflict theory of sound ? - or a vibration in the tympanum of a neighbour , I fear you would be regard - it shock I greatly to think of it - as a cat of rare vocal power , unduly agitated by my feeble pursuit of science . therefore , let I conclude my friendly counsel in the language of all your theatre - ah ! you have no drama now in this country , such poverty of invention - but in the word , which I regret to say , appear from six to a dozen time in every british trugody , Miss Vaughan , ' be calm . ' " 
 through all this brutal sneering , I stand resolutely with my back turn to he . perhaps he think that I would stoop to supplication . I could have bite my tongue off for that contemptible shriek ; it be such a triumph to he . 
 " ah ! sulky , I fear ; young lady sulky with the poor Professor , who try to develop her mind . Fie , fie , very small and ungrateful , and not half so grand a study as the attitude of contempt . what a pity poor Conrad be not present an hour ago ! how he might have enrich his little book of schemata . several most magnificent pose . but I fear the poor fellow have take his last chip . a sad thing , be it not ? why , how you start , Miss Vaughan ! oh , you can show your face at last ! and how pale ! well , if eye could only kill - " 
 " what be it - I mean be good enough just to go away . " 
 " to be sure I will . I have a little matter on hand which must not be delay ; to leave my carte de visite upon the right man , this time . I can not sufficiently thank you for your invaluable information . be that snug little entrance practicable still ? very hospitable people they use to be at Vaughan Park . fare you well , young lady ; I will not keep you in any unnecessary suspense . after my return , I shall arrange for your release ; if it can be make compatible with my safety . you will have plenty of food , and much time for meditation . let your thought of I be liberal and kindly . I never injure any one , when I can avoid it . I only regret that the air you breathe will impair , for the while , your rose . but what an opportunity of analyse the gas ! carbonic acid predominant . do you gratify I by bear in mind a lecture , at which you be very attentive , on Malaria and Miasma ? " 
 taunt to the last , and sneer even at himself , as man of the black dye of wickedness be very apt to do , he close the grating carefully , and I hear the ring of the metal cross on the rough stone step . he have the boot of vengeance on ; his errand be stealthy and cold - blooded murder ; I , who have never harm he , he be abandon perhaps to death , certainly to madness - and yet to his own idea , all he be do be right . 
 Frantic at the horror around I , and still more so at those impend through my own rash folly , I tear and scratch at the solid door , and fling myself against it , till my nail be break , and my finger bleed , and all my body palpitate with impotent mad fury . in weariness at last and shame at this wild outburst , I sit upon the floor , for I could not touch the operator 's stool , and try to collect my thought . be there any possibility of save my poor Uncle ? it must now be nearly four o'clock on the Friday afternoon , or at least I so compute it . the beautiful watch give I by my Uncle have stop through my reckless violence , and the breaking of the glass . the hand , as I could barely perceive , stand at a quarter to four . the express - train , by which Mrs. Fletcher and I be to have return , would leave Paddington at five P.M. and reach Gloucester soon after eight . Lepardo Della Croce would catch it easily , and perhaps would accomplish his foul design that night . my only hope of prevent he lie in his own tenacity of usage . from my Uncle 's account , I know , that on their cursed Vendetta enterprise , a certain pilgrimage on foot be , in many family , regard as a matter of honour . this usage owe its origin perhaps to some faint trace of mercy , some wish to afford the evil passion one more chance of relent to the milder reflection of weariness , and the influence of the air . be that as it may , I believe that the custom be hereditary in the Della Croce family ; and if so , the enemy would finish his journey on foot , quit the train some distance on this side of Gloucester . therefore if I could contrive to escape in the course of the night , I might yet be in time . 
 all the rest of the daylight , such as it be , I spend in examining , inch by inch , every part of the loathsome chamber , which be now my dungeon . by this time all my patience , habitual more than natural , have return , and all my really inborn determination and hope . surely I have be every bit as badly off before , and have struggle through quite as hopeless a difficulty . if arduous courage and tough perseverance be of any avail , those four wall should not hold I , though they might be three foot thick . so stop both my nostril with cotton - wool from a speciman   for the smell be most insufferable , and pin up my dress , I set to work in earnest . first , I examine the window : there be nothing to hope from they ; I could never loosen a bar , and even if I could , I should only escape from one prison to another , for the garden behind the house be surround with high dead wall . fireplace there be none ; the door have already baffle I ; could I dig through the party wall , and into the adjoining house ? most likely it be all a falsehood and boast about the thickness , intend perhaps to discourage I from attempt the easy way . and in so damp a place , the mortar probably would be soft . 
 so , after search and groping , ever so long , to find , if possible , one loose brick to begin with , I draw from my pocket a knife , of which I be very proud , " because my father have give it I ; and I look at it wistfully in the dusk , because I fear so to break it . nothing but the thought that life itself be at stake would ever have induce I to use that beloved knife for work so very unsuitable . 
 it be a knife of strong but by no mean elegant make , short in the handle , and squarer in the joint , than the rise generation of knife . very likely Sheffield of the present day would laugh at it ; but like most who laugh , it could not produce the fellow . my father himself have own it for nearly thirty year , and have treat it with the high respect which an honest knife deserve . from this due regard his daughter have not derogate , and the knife be now as good as when it leave the maker 's hand . it have never be hone in utter ignorance of proper plane and angle , as nearly all knife be , and by none so often as the professional knife - grinder . I never dare to meddle with it , except on a very mild razor - strap ; and all it be allow to do be to mend my pen - I , Clara Vaughan , hate steel paper - stabber - and sometimes to cut my pencil . 
 now , this true and worshipful knife be to cut brick and mortar ! in my natural affection for it , I hesitate and tremble , and know what be to come it close upon my finger . oh , ruthless Atta Navia ! trusty knife , fall to ! 
 meanwhile old Cora show at the heavy grating her countenance demiss ; to all my eager adjuration , promise , and prayer , she answer not a word , but grimly smile , like an ancient bird , beyond the reach of chaff . she hand I in a pint of milk , and a loaf of the variety term in London a " twopenny brick . " a red herring on the toasting - fork , drip with its own unction , be hastily show , and then withdraw , and the gordit appear in its stead ; which be do , the experienced dame wink , and regard I deeply . this mean , " surrender your legal right in Our Lady 's heart , without which I shall have no luck , and I will give you this beautiful fish , hard - roed , and do to a nicety . " ah no , sweet Cora , a good red herring be not to be despise ; but who could eat in a reeking hole like this ? once I go , for Judy 's sake , be rash and light of step , into the back premise of a highly respectable butcher . Woe be I , what I see and smell there be Muscat grape compare to this . 
 when Cora have depart , after hand I in a pillow and a blanket of the true work - house texture , and cross herself with a strange expression , meaning , as I interpret , " now keep alive if possible till breakfast time , young woman , " I sit I down upon the floor at one end of the room , and begin my labour . first . I put on a pair of tan - leather glove ; for small as my vanity be , I do not like my hand to look altogether like a hodman 's . then I remove a strip of the feel with which the wall be cover . it be nearly dark , but I could easily feel the joint between the brick . the mortar be not very good , but my work be render doubly difficult by the brick be all set cross - wise to the line of the wall ; this , I suppose , be what he mean when he describe they as " header . " by reason of this arrangement , I have to dig and dig for hour , before I could loosen a single brick ; and work all in the dark as I be , I fear every moment to break the stick - blade of my knife . the finger of my glove be very soon wear away , and even the palm where the heel of the knife be chafing ; nor be it long before my skin be full of weal , and raspy , like the knob I have see inside the leg of a horse . at last , to my wonderful delight , one brick begin to tremble . in another half - hour , I ease it out most carefully , kiss my trusty blade , now wear almost to a skewer , and with stiff and aching muscle , and the trophy brick upon my lap , fall off into as sound a sleep as ever I be bl with . 
 CHAPTER X. 
 when I awake , the summer dawn be steal faintly through the barricade window . oh ! how I long for one draught of air , even as London import it ! my head be burn and my eye distend from the tainted stuff around I , and my hand , and arm , and even shoulder be stiff from over exertion . languidly regard the brick I have work so hard for , and commiserate much the plight of my tender hand , I feel inclined to give it up , till I think of all at stake . my poor Uncle in deadly peril through my desperate folly ; Conrad too , as that murderer imply , in a critical position . my own life also - it might be a week before the monster return ; and I feel sure that I could not live more than three day in that corruption . the oppression be so horrible , especially when I stand up , that I resolve at all hazard to break one of the window . I have try to do so the night before , but they be beyond my reach , and I have no stick , for I durst not touch the pole that prop the unlucky porpoise . now , I have a good missile , and after two or three vain attempt from the closeness of the bar , I hurl the brick - bat through the glass ; and , as it raise the sack a little , I obtain more light , as well as a breath of air . the taint upon the glass , the reek of the deadly gas , even clear away for a short distance round the fracture . 
 Cora be fast asleep no doubt , and the crash of the glass do not disturb she ; so I fall to again , and work very hard till breakfast time . if I could only get out by noon , in time for the two o'clock train ! when I expect my jailor , I hide away under the porpoise the seven brick I have remove since daylight - for I could work much fast as the aperture increase - and then I fasten my blanket over the hole . after drink the milk with some relish - eat I could not in that pestilential den - I return to my labour , and prepare to attack the second course in the thickness of the wall . by this time I have contrive , with the help of a brick , to extract the hold - fast from the bench , which I could not do the night before ; and very useful I find it , both as a hammer and lever . so with rise hope , I resume . 
 oh , cruel disappointment ! the second course be bed in cement hard than the brick themselves . most likely they have form the outside of the wall , until Lepardo add the nine - inch lining of header . I be utterly dismayed ; and now my beloved knife , which have stand like a hero - martyr all its grind indignity , break off short at the haft , and leave I helpless and hopeless . and I be get on so well , and so proud of all I have do . there be nothing for it but a storm of cry . it serve I right for ill - treat my dear father 's knife so shockingly . 
 I cry for at least a quarter of an hour , before it occur to I what a great baby I be . then , with the tear in my swollen eye , and sob that make my net - press bosom sore , I begin to grope and peer again along the side of my prison . there be more light now than have hitherto enter , since Cora drop the curtain . this be partly owe to the position of the sun , and partly to the interposition of the brick . just opposite that window , on a shelf where lie an old Penguin look very bilious , I spy the corner of a little box , half cover with tow and moth - eat feather . snatch it eagerly , I find it to be a match - box . but alas , how light ! with tremble finger I pull it open , for it be one of those that slide . there be three , and only three , fine stout lucifer match , with the precious blue still on they . but even if they should prove dry enough to kindle , what good would they be to I ? 
 " all the good in the world , " say hope , look towards the door , " if you have show sense enough , Clara , to fall to at that door , before your knife be break , you might have cut through it by this time . now you can not , that be certain ; but why should not   you burn it down ? " 
 at any rate , I would try ; that be , if my match would only strike fire . I have feel last night a piece of candle on the floor near the crocodile . this I soon lay hand upon ; and now for operation . no fear of old Cora smell the smoke , for she spend all the forenoon , as I know well , in a little chapel she have establish quite at the top of the house ; and this be the festival of St. Bottle - imp , she would be twice as devout as usual . as for suffocate myself , that I must take the chance of . much well to die of curl wood smoke than of these crawl odour . 
 to give the wood , which be hard and solid , every inclination to burn , I channel it first in a fan from the bottom with my little pen - blade . then I cut off the low half of my precious candle , and smear the tallow in the shallow groove I have make . this be do , I break , with as little noise as possible , some other pane of glass , to admit the air to my fire , procure all the wool and tow that I could reach , and a pile of paper , and steep they , though it sicken I to do it , in the rank oil from some of the specimen . 
 all this be ready at hand , I prepare , with a beat heart , to try the match , on which the whole depend . I have take the precaution of slip they just inside my frock , hop that the warmth of my body might serve to dry they a little . the first , as I rub it on the sandpaper , flash for a moment , but do not kindle ; the second just kindle with a sputter , but do not ignite its stick : the third - I be so nervous that I durst not attempt it then ; but tremble as I look at it . I would not even breathe for fear of damp the phosphorus . perhaps three life depend on the behaviour of that match . in desperation at last I strike boldly ! a broad blue flame leap upon the air , and in a moment my candle be light . in the hollow of my hand I carry it round the room , to search for anything likely to be of service to I . oh ! grand discovery - behind a great tabby cat , I find a bottle contain nearly a pint of naphtha , use , I suppose , for singe some of the hair off . now I need not fear , but what I could burn the door down ; the only thing to fear be that I should burn myself as well , use the naphtha very cautiously , keep most of it as a last resource . 
 then commend the result to God , I set my candle carefully at the foot of the door , just below the spot where all my little groove converge . at once the flame run up they , the naphtha kindle angrily with a spatter and a hiss . the blue light show in livid ghastliness all the horror of the chamber . the naphtha be burn in a moment , it seem to go off like gunpowder ; from a prudent distance I throw more upon it , and soon I have the delight of see a steady flame establish . the lump of tallow be burn now , and the wood begin to smoulder . several time I think that I must be choke by the smoke , till it go in a cloud to the window , and stream away under the sack . 
 as the fire grow and grow , and require no more feeding , I lay on my face , to get all the air possible , at the further end of the room , where my loose mortar be scatter . I could feel my heart thump heavily on the pavement , and my breath be short and short , as much from fear as from smoke . if once I become insensible , or even if I retain my sens but fail to extinguish the fire , nothing more would ever be know or hear of Clara Vaughan ; there would be nothing even to hold an inquest upon . I must burn ignobly , in the fat of that dreadful porpoise , and with the crocodile , and all those grinning beast , so awful in the firelight , make face at I ! surely it must be time , high time to put it out ; that be to say if I could . once let the flame gather head on the other side of the door , and with my scanty mean I never could hope to quench it . 
 at last , I become so frightened , that I hardly let it burn long enough . it be flare beautifully , and lick deeply and deep   with ductile wreathe tongue and jet like a pushing crocus , the channel prepare to tempt it ; and now the black wood be redden , and a strong heat be give out , and the blaze begin to roar ; when I cast on the centre suddenly my doubled blanket , and prop it there with the pillow . after a few vain effort , the flame , deprive of air , expire in gray smoke ; then I remove the scorch blanket , and let the smouldering proceed . 
 the charring go on nicely for perhaps a quarter of an hour , and the smell make I think of bonfire and roast potato ; and I gouge away with the claw of the holdfast , until I see that , by a vigorous onset , a large piece might be detach ; so I step back and run at it with a mighty kick , and with a shower of dust and spark , a great triangle fly out before my " military heel . " 
 at the risk of set myself on fire , though gather in the small possible compass for a girl rather full in the chest , I squeeze through the hole in the door , and meet face to face old Cora . 
 she could not speak , but fall back upon the step , and roll in fit of terror . I think her black eye would have leap from their socket ; they come out like hat - pegs japanned . press as I be for time , I could not leave she so . I run up to the pump - trough for water , and put out the fire first , and then poor Cora 's hysteric . 
 I can not repeat her exclamation , to our ear they be so impious ; but the mild of they be these , as render weakly into English . 
 " Holy Madonna , most sacred mother , take back your blessed heart . take it back , for the sake of the God that love you , take it back , and trample on the wicked stomach of she who dare to steal it . you have come through the fire of hell to fetch it , mother of the beloved one , lo I hold it out to you . " 
 I gladly receive my poor gordit , and leave the old lady , as there be now no danger , to recover her wit at leisure ; for I have not a moment to spare . 
 as I enter Mrs. Shelfer 's door , the church clock at the top of the Square be strike twelve . by the two o'clock train I must go , or I might as well have stop in my dungeon . though the smoke have purify I a little , I still feel conscious of a nasty clinging smell ; but it would have surprise I , if there have be time , when the little woman cry , 
 " Lor bless my soul , Miss Vaughan , where ever have you be ? why , Mr. Chumps the butcher - " 
 " the bath in one moment , and all the water in the house . and as I throw my thing out , burn they in the garden . " 
 in twenty minute I be reclad from head to foot , and as sweet as any girl in Gloucestershire ; my eye be bright with energy , and my dripping hair in billow , like a rapid under the pine - tree . I have no time to tell Mrs. Shelfer , who be off her leg with excitement , one word of what have happen , or what I be go to do ; but fling on myself another hat and cloak , then her old bonnet and little green shawl on she , drag she out of the house , and lock the door behind we ; for Mrs. Fletcher , after wait and wonder long about I , be go to consult Ann Maples . if Mrs. Shelfer 's good bonnet be twenty - two year old , her second - good must have be forty - four ; at any rate it appear coeval with herself . 
 Patty trot along at my side , wonder what would come next . her thin little lip be work , and her face be like a kaleidoscope of expression ; but whenever I glance toward she , she cast her eye up , with a scared weird look , as if she be watch a ghost through a skylight , and trudge still fast , and mutter , " yes , yes , Miss Vaughan . quite right , my good friend ; not a moment to lose . " 
 " and pray , Mrs. Shelfer , where do you suppose we be go ? " 
 " oh , I know well enough " - with her eye like cork draw by distance - " I know it all the time . yes , yes . let I alone for that . Patty Shelfer be not   bear yesterday . why only Tuesday be a week - " 
 " if you guess right , I will tell you . " 
 " why go to Charley , Miss Vaughan , to be sure . go for Charley 's opinion . and very wise of you too ; and what a most every one do ; particular when he have money . but how you know he be there - " 
 " where ? " 
 " at the great wrestling match to be sure . and he want to take I ; a thing he be not   offer to do fifteen year next oyster - day . no , no , say I , with Miss Vaughan away , and most likely among they resurrectioner - " 
 here she cast at I a glance , like a flash of lightning , to see if the hit have tell . in a moment I understand all that I have not care to ask about ; why she tremble and shrink from my hand , why she fear to look at I , and fix her eye away so . she believe that I have be burke , and that what she see walk beside be my spirit come to claim burial . I could not stop to disprove it , any more than I could stop to laugh . 
 " and his grandfather be a sexton , Miss ; and our Charley himself a first - rate hand at the spade . " 
 " Mrs. Shelfer , we be close to the place . now , listen to what I say . it be not your husband I want , but Farmer Huxtable , whom you see at the door . nothing but a question of life and death would bring I among this rabble . no doubt there be many respectable man , but it be no place for a lady . the farmer himself know that , and have never dare to ask I ; though his wife and daughter , in ignorance , have . it be half - past twelve exactly ; in a quarter of an hour at the utmost , I must speak to , and what be more , carry off the Devonshire competitor . your husband be here , and on the Committee , you tell I . I expect you to manage it . go in at once and find he . stop , here be plenty of money . " 
 in her supreme astonishment , she even dare to look at I . but she fear to take the money , although her eye glisten at it , for I offer more gold than silver . 
 " come back to I at once ; I shall not move from here . mind , if the farmer lose the match through I , I will pay all , and give the money for another . " 
 for once the little woman obey I , without discussion . she push through a canvass door into the vast marquee , or whatever it ought to be call , and be admit readily on give her husband 's name . I hang back , but with a sense of the urgency of my case , which turn my shame into pride . many eye be on I already of lounger and outsider . in two or three minute poor Patty come back , bring Mr. Shelfer himself , who ever since his ducking have show I the rose and pink of respect . he even go the length now of remove his pipe from his mouth . 
 " very sorry indeed , Miss Vaughan , very sorry , you know . but we darrn't   interrupt the man now . our life would not   be worth it , and they would kill both the umpire and the referee too you know . why it be fall for fall , only think of that , Miss Vaughan , it be fall for fall ! " and the perspiration stand upon his forehead , and he want to run back . 
 " what do you mean ? " in spite of my hurry , I feel deeply interested . how could I help it , love the farmer so ? 
 " why , the Great Northern win the first throw by a bit of foul play , a foul stroke altogether , and no back at all , say I , and my eye be pretty good ; however , the umpire give it , and you should see John Huxtable 's face , the colour of a scythe - stone ; he know it be unfair you know . and you should see he go in again for the second fall . ' I could ha dooe it , ' I hear he say , ' I could ha dooe it aisy , only I wudn't   try Abraham , and I wun't   nother if can help it now . ' none of we know what he mean , but in he go again , Miss , and three time he throw Sam Richardson clean over his shoulder , and one as fair a back as ever be in sawdust . but the umpire would not   give it , till just now he turn he over straight for'ard , just the same as a sod in a spade , and they could not   get out of that . and now they be just in for the finishing bout , and if you want he , your only way be to come . may be , he will try Abraham , when he see you . ah they have   catch . " 
 a shout inside proclaim some crisis ; Mr. Shelfer , in his excitement , actually pull I in without know it . once there , I could not go back ; and the scene be a grand and thrill one . 
 in the centre of a rope arena , hedge by countless face , all rigid , flushed , and strain with suspense , stand two mighty form ; the strong man in England and perhaps in all the world . a loose sack , or jerkin , of the tough canvass , throw back clear of the throat , half - sleeve , and open in front , show the bole of the pollard neck , the solid brawn of the chest , and the cord of the outstretched arm . stout fustian breech , belt at waist , and strap at knee , case their vast limb so exactly , yet so easily , that every curve be thew , and every wrinkle sinew . thin white stocking , flake with sawdust and look rather wet , roll and stand out , like the loop of a mace , with the rampant muscle of the huge calf , and the bulge of the broad foreleg . 
 as the shout proclaim , they have catch or clutch ; a thing which be do with much fencing and feint , each foining to get the good grasp . where I go , or what happen to I , I never notice at all , so absorb at once I become in this rare and noble probation of glorious strength , train skill , and emulous manhood . 
 round and round the ring they go , as in musical measure , hold each other at arm ' length , pace warily and in distance , skilfully poise to throw the weight for either attack or defence . each with his left hand clutch the jerkin of the other , between the neck and shoulder , each keep his right arm lightly bent , and the palm like a butterfly quivering . neither dare to move his eye from the pupil of the other ; for though they be not build alike , each know the strength of his fellow . the Northern Champion be at least three inch tall than the Son of Devon , quite as broad in the shoulder and large of limb , but not so thick - set and close - joint , not quite so stanch in the loin and quarter . but he be long in the reach , and make the most of that advantage . on his breast he bear the mark of a hug as hard as a bear 's ; and his face , though a fine and manly one , look rather savage and spiteful . 
 the farmer be smile pleasantly , an honest but anxious smile . for the first time he have meet with a man of almost his own power ; and on a turn of the heel depend at least four hundred pound , and what be more than four million to he , the fame of the county that nurse he . above they hang the champion 's belt , not of the west or north , but of England and of the world . 
 suddenly , ere I could see how they do it , they have close in the crown struggle . breast to breast , and thigh to thigh , they tug , and strain , and pant . nothing though I know of the matter , I see that the north - man have win the good hold , and as his huge arm enwrap my friend , a tremble go through my own frame . the man of the North and their backer see it , and a loud hurrah peal forth ; deep silence ensue , and every eye be intent . though giant arm be round he and Titan leg inlocke , never a foot he budge . John Huxtable stand like a buttress . he try not to throw the other ; place as he be , he durst not ; but he make up his mind to stand , and stand he do with a vengeance . in vain the giant jerk and twisted , lever , heave , and labour , till his very eyeball strain ; all the result be rope and bunch in the wide - spread Devonshire calf , and a tightening of the clench that threaten to crush the northern rib . as well might a coil snake expect to uproot an oak . 
 as this exertion of grand stability last and outlasted , shout arise and ring alike from friend and foe , from north , and west , and east ; even I could not help clap my feeble hand . but the trial be nearly over . the assailant 's strength be ebb ; I could hear he gasp for breath under the fearful pressure . by great address he have win that hold , and make sure of victory from it , it have never fail before ; but to use a devonshire word , the farmer be too ' stuggy . " now , the latter watch his time , and his motive power wax as the other 's wan . at length he lift he bodily off his leg , and cast he flat on his back . a flat and perfectly level cast , as ever pancake crackle at . thunder of applause break forth , and scarcely could I keep quiet . 
 with amazement the farmer espy I as he be bow on all side , and amid the tumult and uproar that shake the canvass like a lark 's wing , he run across the ring full speed . then he stop short , remember his laboured and unpresentable plight , and he would have blush , if he have not be as red as fire already . none of such nonsense for I . I call he by name , take his hand , and with all my heart congratulate . 
 " but , farmer , I want you immediately , on a matter of life and death . " Beany Dawe and the child come , but I only stop to kiss Sally , and motion they all away . " if you remember your promise to I , get ready for a journey in a moment , and run all the way to my lodging . we must leave London , at two o'clock , to save my Uncle 's life . " 
 Mr. Huxtable look astounded , and his understanding , unlike his leg , for the moment be carry away . meanwhile up come Sally again , catch hold of my hand , and silently implore for some little notice , if only of her costume , violet , indigo , blue , green , yellow , orange and red . I could only kiss she again . 
 " oh do come , farmer Huxtable , do come at once , I entreat you ; or I must go alone and helpless . " 
 " that you shall not , my dearie , dang Jan Uxtable for a girt lout . " 
 " please , sir , I be send to tell you that the umpire give it no fall , and you must play again . " 
 the man look abase by his errand ; even he know well . in my hurry I have pay no attention to the ominous hissing and hoot around a knot of man on the bench at the end . 
 the farmer 's face I shall never forget ; as he slowly gather the truth , it become majestic with honest indignation . a strong man 's wrath at deceit and foul play sit upon it , like a king on his throne . 
 " for the chiller - " he stammer at last - " ony for the poor chiller 's sake - else I would never stand it , dange if I wud , Miss Clara ; it make a man feel like a rogue and a cheat himself . " 
 then , with all the power of his mighty voice he shout , so that every fold of the canvass shake , and every heart thrill fearfully : 
 " Men of Lunnon , if man you be , no chap can have fair play with you . it be all along of your swindle bet about thing you do not   know nothing of . you offer I five hunder pound , afore ever here I come , to sell my back to the Northman . a good honest man he be , and the good cross - buttock as ever I meet with ; but a set of rogue and coward that be what you be ; and no sport can live with you . as for your danged belt , I wun't   have it , no tino , it wud be a disgrace to the family ; it shall not   never go along side the Devonshire and Cornwall leather . but I will throw your man over again , and any six of you to once as plase . " 
 then , thorough gentleman as he be , he apologize to I for his honest anger , and for have draw all eye upon I , as there I stand at his side . 
 " but never fear about the time , Miss Clara , I will not   kape you two minute . I will give he Abraham 's staylace this time . they have a drive I to it , as we have not   a moment to spare . " 
 proudly he step into the ring again , and again the North Country giant , look rather ashamed , confront he . no fencing or feint this time ; but the Devonshire wrestler , appeal thus to the public , 
 " now look here , lunnoner , wull e , and zee if this here be a back , " rush straight at his antagonist , grapple he in some peculiar manner , seem to get round his back , and then spin he up over his own left shoulder , in such a way that he twirl in the air and come down dead on his spine . dead indeed he appear to be , and a dozen surgeon come forward , in the midst of a horrible silence , and some be prepare to bleed he , when the farmer move they aside ; he know that the poor man be only stun by concussion of the spine . awhile he kneel over he sadly , with the tear in his own brave eye : 
 " I wudn't   have do it , lad ; indade and indade I wudn't , ony they force I to it ; and you do not   say nought agin they . it be all fair enough , but it do hoort so tarble . that there trick be invent by a well man nor I be , and it be karle ' Abraham Cann 's staylace .
