" perhaps he mean skirmish and manoeuvre , all in one . Sam Richardson , slowly recover , put out his great hand , all white and clammy , and John Huxtable take it tenderly , amid such uproarious cheering , that I expect the tent on our head . even Shelfer 's sharp eye have a drop of moisture in they . as for Beany Dawe , he fling to the wind all dithyrambic gravity , and chant and dance incoherently , Cassandra and Chorus in one ; while Sally Huxtable blot all her rainbow in heavy drop . 
 hundred of pipe be smash , even the Stoic Shelfer 's , in the rush to get at the farmer ; but he part the crowd right and leave , as I might part willow - spray , and come at once to I . whether by his aid , or by the sympathy of the multitude , I be sure I can not tell , but I find myself in a cab , with Sally at my side , and Mrs. Shelfer on the box , and the farmer 's face at the window . 
 " twenty minute , Miss , I will be there , raddy to go where you plase . it bain't   quite one o'clock yet . I must put myself dacent like , avore I can go with you , Miss ; and git the money for the sake of they poor chiller , if so be they lunnoner be honest enough to pai . Jan Uxtable never come to Lunnon town no more . " 
 with thousand of people hurrae , we set off full gallop for Albert Street . 
 CHAPTER XI . 
 at the door we find Mrs. Fletcher just return from Lady Cranberry 's , and eager to say a great deal which could not now be listen to . have prove the speed of our horse , I beg the cabman to wait for a quarter of an hour , and then take we to Paddington at any fare he please , so long as he drive full gallop . this suit his view very nicely , and know Mr. Shelfer , as every one in London do - so at least I be force to believe - he fain would have keep I ten minute of the fifteen , to tell of Charley 's knowingness , how he have keep it all dark as could be , you see , Miss , and have win three hundred and twenty - five pound , without reckon the odd money , Miss - 
 " reckon it then , Mr. Cabman , " and I run upstairs full speed , after tell Mrs. Shelfer the sum , lest she should be cheat . 
 in five minute I be ready , and come out of my bedroom into the sitting - room , with my hat in one hand , and a little bag in the other ; and there , instead of Mrs. Fletcher , I find , whom ? - Conrad ! 
 very pale and ill he look , so unlike himself that I be shocked , and instead of leap to he , fall upon a chair . he mistake I , and approach very slowly , but with his dear old smile : how my heart beat , how I long to be in his arm ; but they look too weak to hold I . 
 " oh , Miss Vaughan , I know everything . will you ever forgive I ? " 
 " never , my own darling , while you call I that . forgive you indeed ! can I ever forgive myself , for the evil I have think of you ? how very ill you look ! come and let I kiss you well . " 
 but instead of my do that , he have to do it for I ; for I be quite beat at last , and faint away in his arm . by this folly five minute be lose ; and I have so much to say to he , and more to think of than twenty such head could hold . but he seem to think that it must be all right , so long as he have I there . 
 " oh , Conny , " I say through my tear at last , " my own pet Conny , come with I . your father be in such danger . " 
 " life of my heart , I will follow you by the very next train . this one I can not go by . " 
 I could wait for no explanation , and he seem inclined to give none . perhaps this be the reason that he spend all the time in kiss I ; which , much as I enjoy it , would have do quite as well at leisure . be that as it may , there be no time to talk about it ; he say it do his lip good , and I believe it do , they be so pale at first , and now so fine a red . suddenly in the midst of it , a great voice be hear from the passage : 
 " why now , what ever be we to do with the chiller ? " 
 out I run , with my hair down as usual , and a great flush in my cheek , but I do not let any one see I . 
 " leave they here , to be sure , leave they here , Mr. Huxtable . they shall have my room ; and in all London they would not find such a hostess as Mrs. Shelfer . " 
 there be no time to consider it . the throat of hurry be large , and gulp almost any suggestion . away we go full gallop ; the farmer be on the box , - how the driver find room I can not   say , - Mrs. Fletcher and I inside , all consult she watch every minute . across the Regent 's Park , scatter the tame wild duck , past Marylebone Church , and the Yorkshire Stingo , and Edgware Road - we save it by just two minute . although I have take his ticket , the farmer would not come with we , but go in a second - class carriage . 
 " they blue featherbed trim with pig 's tail , be too good for the like of I , Miss Clara ; and I should be afeare all the wai that the Missus be rating of I for my leg - room . I pay parlour price come up , and go in the kitchen waggon , because it zim'd only fair , as I take such a dale of room . " 
 I know that none ever could turn he from what he consider just , and therefore allow he to ride where he please . but a dozen time I think we should have lose he on the way ; for at every station , where the train stop , he make a point of come to our window , which he have mark with a piece of chalk , and " humbly ax our pardon , but be we all right and no fire ? he could not   think what they want , not he , with tempt God Almighty fast . " not fast enough for I , I tell he every time ; whereupon he put on his hat with a sigh , and say he suppose I be bear to it . and yet all the time he seem to consider that he be protect I somehow , and once he call I his dearie , to the great surprise of the other passenger , and the horror of Mrs. Fletcher ; see which he repent hastily , and " Miss Vaughan'd " I three time in a sentence , with a hot flush on his forehead . at Swindon , where we change carriage , he pull out very mysteriously from an inner breast - pocket a little sack tie with whipcord , and in which , I do believe , the simple soul have deposit all his hard - earn prize - money . then he lead we to the counter , proud to show that he have be there before , and earnestly beg for the honour of treat we to a drop of somewhat . his countenance fall so on my refusal , that I be fain to cancel it , and to drink at his expense a glass of iced sherry and water ; while Mrs. Fletcher , with much persuasion and simpering , and for the sake of her poor inside , that have be so long her enemy , venture on a " wee wee thimbleful of Cognac . " the farmer himself , much abash at the splendour around he , which he tell I , in a whisper , beat Pewter Will 's out and out , and even the " Fortescue arm , " would not call for anything , until I insist upon it ; be hard press he ask at last , hop no offence of the lady , for a pint of second cider . the young woman turn up her nose , but I soon make she turn it down again , and fetch he , as the near thing , a bottle of sparkle perry . 
 as always happen , when one be in a great hurry , the train be an hour behind its time , and the set sun be cast gold upon the old cathedral - to my mind one of the light and grand building in England , though the farmer prefer that squat and heavy Norman thing at Exeter - when we glide smoothly and swiftly into the Gloucester Station . I fully intend to have send an electric message from London , not for the sake of the carriage , which matter nothing , but to warn my dear uncle ; at Paddington , however , we find no time to do it , and so stupid I be that I never once think of telegraph from Swindon . to make up by over alacrity , in a case of far less importance , I go to the office at Gloucester , and send this message to Tiverton , then the near station to Exmoor - " Farmer have win , and get the money . Clara Vaughan to Mrs. Huxtable . " the amazement of the farmer , I can not stop to describe . 
 no time be lose by do this , for I have order a pair of horse , and they be be put to . then , stimulate the driver , we dash off for Vaughan St. Mary . anxious as I be , and wretche at the thought of what we might find , so exhausted be my frame by the thaumatrope of the last six - and - thirty hour , that I fall fast asleep , and wake not until we come to the lodge . Old Whitehead come out , hat in hand , and whisper something into Mrs. Fletcher 's ear . that good old lady have be worry I dreadfully about her jam , for the weather be so hot , she be sure all the fruit would be over ,   c. , none of which could I listen to now . as Whitehead speak , I see through my half - open lash that she start violently ; but she would not tell I what it be , and I do not want to intrude on secret that might be between they . the farmer also divert attention by call from the box , as we wound into the avenue , " dear heart alaive ; this bate all the sojer as ever I see , Miss Clara , or even the melisher to Coom . why , arl thiccy treese must a grow so a puppose , just over again one another , and arl of a bigness too . wull , wull ! Coachman , be ever you to Davonsheer ? " 
 I do believe those man of Devon see nothing they admire , without think at once of their county . 
 at the front door , the butler meet we , which surprise I rather , as be below his dignity . he be a trusty old servant , who have be under Thomas Henwood , and have come back to his place since the general turn - out of the household . now he look very grave and sad , and instead of lead I on , draw I aside in the hall . it be get dark , and the fire in the west be die . great plume of asparagus - shame it be to cut they - wave under the ancient mantel - piece . 
 " bad news it be , Miss Clara " - they all seem to call I that - " very bad news indeed , Miss. but I hope you be prepare for it . " 
 " what do you mean ? " 
 " why , have not   you hear about poor master 's death ? " 
 " dead , my dear uncle dead ! do you mean to say " - I could not finish the sentence . 
 " no , Miss , only to - day , and not as you think ; no fit at all , nor paralyatic stroke . he go off quiet as a lamb , as near as could be three o'clock . he be very poorly before ; but he have a deal to do , and would not give in on no account . he be sit by himself in the study after breakfast , and at last he ring the bell , and tell they to send I up . when I go in , he be bolt upright in his chair , with a beautiful smile on his face , but so pale , white I ought to say , Miss , and so weak he could hardly move . ' John , ' he say , ' yes , Sir , ' say I ; ' John , ' he say again , ' you be a most respectable man , and I can trust you with anything in the world , John . take this letter for Miss Vaughan , and put it with your own hand into her own , directly the moment she come back . I would like to die in my bed , and it will save some trouble . and let I look out of the window ; what a lovely day it be , it remind I quite of the South . so I set he up in the bed , Miss , handy altogether , and beautiful , and he could see two lark on the lawn , and he ask I what they be . then he say , ' thank you , John , you have do it wonderful well , and I hope they will not   speak evil of I round this place , after I be go .  ah , John Hoxton , do you say that to he , I wonder , or interpolate , ex post facto ? 
 " so he look very pleased at that , Miss , and he say again , ' John , let all that love she know that she be the live image of her mother . now go and send she quickly ; but John , take care not to frighten my little darling . " 
 " no . he mean her mother . where be my cousin now ? " 
 " in your own room , Miss , lie down , they tell I . she do take on so awful , Jane think she would have die . but at last she bring she round a little , and persuade she to lie down . she call for you , Miss , every time she come to herself . " 
 I go straightway to the poor little dear , without even stop to read the letter place in my hand . the room in which she lie be dark ; for Jane , who be watch in my little parlour , whisper to I that the poor child could not bear the lamp - light , her eye be so weak and sore . 
 at first Lily do not know I ; and it go to my heart , after all my own great sorrow , to hear the sad low moaning . she lie on my own little bed , with her pale face turn to the wall , her thick hair all over her shoulder , and both hand press to her heart . Annie Franks have be many time to ask for she , but Lily would not let she come in . bend over I lay my cheek on Lily 's , and softly whisper her name . at last she know I , and take my hand , and turn her sweet lip to kiss I . then she sob and cry most bitterly ; but I see that it do she good . by and by she say , with her finger among my hair : 
 " oh , Clara , be not   it hard to find he at last , and love he so , and only for three day , and then , and then - " 
 " and then , my pet , to let he go where his heart have be nearly twenty year . would you be so selfish as to rob your mother of he ? and to go so happy . I be sure he have . come with I and see . " 
 " oh no , oh no . I can not . " and her lovely young form tremble , at the thought of visit death . 
 " yes , you can , if you only try , and I be sure that he would wish it . that you and I should kneel hand in hand and bless he , as other shall kneel some day by we . what , Lily afraid of her father ! then I have no fear of my Uncle . " 
 God know that I speak so , not from harshness , only in the hope to do she good . 
 " if you really think he would wish it , dear - " 
 " yes . it be a duty I owe he . he would be disappoint in I , if I fail . " 
 " oh , how he long to see you once more , dear Clara , but he feel that you be safe , and he say you would come to see he , though he could not see you . he talk of you quite to the last ; you and darle Conny . " 
 " Conny will be here to - night . " 
 " no ! oh I be so glad ! " and a bright flash of joy shine forth from the eye that be red with weeping . something cold push quietly in between we , and then give a sniff and a sigh . it be darle Judy 's nose . he have learn in the low region , where he always dwell in my absence , that Miss Clara be come home ; and know my name as well as his own , he have set off at once in quest of I . after offer I his good love and respect , with the tip of his tongue , as he always do , he look from one to the other of we , with his eyebrow raise in surprise , and the deep sorrow and sympathy in his beautiful soft - brown pupil . I declare it make we cry more than ever .

 his eye drop , as she be tell I . they always do , when he think he have be a bad dog . 
 " now go down , Judy ; good little Judy , go to Mrs. Fletcher . a great friend of mine be with she . " 
 away he trot obediently , and his tail recover its flourish before he have get to the corner . 
 " now , darling , let we go there , " say the poor child , tremble again . " I would go anywhere with you . " 
 Hand in hand we walk into my Uncle 's chamber . young as I be , and still thoughtless in many way , twice before now have I gaze on the solemn face of death ; but never , not even in my mother 's holy countenance , see I such perfect peace and bliss as dwelt in and seem to smile from my dear Uncle 's lineament . the life , in youth puff here and there by every captious breeze of pride , in its prime becalm awhile on the halycon deep of love , then tempest - toss through the lonely dark , and shatter of late by blow from God , that life whose flaw of misanthropy and waste of high ability have be redeem , ennoble even , by a pure and perfect love - now it have bid farewell to all below the cloud , calmly , happily , good of all - in faith . 
 we kneel beside the bed and pray - Lily as a Catholic , Clara as a Protestant - that we , and all we love , might have so bl an end . then we both sit peacefully , with a happy awe upon we , in the dark recess behind the velvet curtain . two wax candle be burn on the table towards the door , and by their light the face we love , look not wan , but glorious , as with a silver glory . 
 clasp each the other 's waist , and kiss away each other 's tranquil tear , how long we sit there I know not , neither what high flutter thought , thought or angel , which be they - stealthily a door be open , not the door of heaven , not even the main door of the room we sit in , but a narrow side - door . through it creep , with crawl caution , he whom most of all man I now despise and pity . Lily do not hear his entrance , neither do she see he ; but my eye and ear be keen from many a call of danger . stun for a while by the heavy blow , that meet I on my return , I have forget all about he ; I mean , at least , all about his present design . I have indeed tell the farmer , for it be only fair to do so , my object in bring he down ; and how I rely on his wonderful strength and courage , have then no other to help I ; but since I get home , and hear the sad tiding , it seem a mere thing for contempt . not even Lepardo Della Croce could catch a depart spirit . so , and in the landslip of the mind , sap by its own , and slide swiftly into another 's sorrow , I have not even order that the house should be watch at all ; I have not even post Giudice , who have a vendetta of his own , anywhere on guard . 
 with a stiletto still conceal , all but the handle on which the light fall , he approach the bed , wriggle along and crouch , as a cat or leopard would . then he rise and stand upright at the side of the bed , not our side but the other , and glare upon his intended victim 's face . I push Lily back behind the curtain as if with the weight of my bosom , while I watch the whole . never in all my tempestuous life , of all the horrible thing I have see , and hear , and shudder at , see I anything so awful , so utterly beyond not only description , but conception , as that disdainful , arrogant face , when the truth burst on he . not the body only , but the mind and soul - if God have curse he with one - be smite back all of a lump , as if he have leap from a train at full speed into a firing cannon 's mouth . before he have time to recover , I advance and face he . all dress in white I be , with my black hair below my waist , for I have throw off my travel frock , and take what first come to hand . they tell I I look well in white , it show my hair and eye so . 
 he believe that it be a spirit , the Vendetta spirit of the other side ; and he cower from I . I be the first to speak . " Lepardo Della Croce , it be the rebuke of heaven . dust upon ash ; such be man 's revenge . I have nurse , but scorn it now . go in peace , and pray the Almighty that he be not like you . stop ; I will show you forth . you have a vindictive foe here , who would tear you to atom . " 
 I lead the way , tremble at every corner lest we should meet Giudice ; for I know he would not obey I , if he once catch sight of this hat one . after stand silently , unable to take his eye from the placid face of the dead , Lepardo begin to follow I , walk as if in a dream . meet none , I lead he forth along the corridor , down the end staircase , and out on the eastern terrace . there I wave he off , and point to the dark refuge of the shrubbery , beyond the mineral spring . the moonlight sleep upon the black water narrowly thread the grass . over our head droop the ivy , the creeper of oblivion . the murderer turn and look at I ; hitherto he have glide along with his head down , as in bewilderment . oh that he have say one word of sorrow or repentance ! he speak not at all ; but shudder , as the ivy rustle above we . his face be pale as the moonlight . do he see in I something high than the spirit of Vendetta ? 
 I point again to the tree , and urge he away from the house . he have two strong enemy there ; a minute might make all the difference . break as if from a spell , he wave his italian cap , and his lithe strong figure be lose among the Portugal laurel . for a minute I stand there , wonder ; then slowly go round the house - corner , and gaze at the grey stone mullion of the room which have be my father 's . 
 I be still in the anguish of doubt and misgive - what right have an ignorant girl like I to play judge and jury , or more , to absolve and release a crime against all humanity ? - when a mighty form stand beside I , and Giudice , all bristle and fire , dash forth from the door in the gable . with command and entreaty I call he , but he hear I not , neither look at I ; but scour the ground like a shadow , quarter it as a pointer do , only he carry his nose down . 
 " dang my slow bone , " say the farmer , " but I will have he yet , Miss. I seed he go , I will soon find he . " 
 " no , no . I will not   have he stop . he shall go free , and repent . " 
 " by your lave , Miss , it can not   be . a man as have do what he have , we have no right to play buff with . never before do I go again your will , Miss ; but ax your pardon , I must now . look , the girt dog know well . " 
 as the dog find the track and give tongue , the farmer rush from I and follow he , dash headlong into the shrubbery , after leap the mineral spring , at the very spot where the footprint have be . Judy and Farmer Huxtable be fast friend already ; for that dog always make up his mind in a moment on the question of like and dislike . 
 for a time I be so horror - strike , that no power of motion be leave . I know that the farmer be quite unarmed , he carry not even a stick . even with the great dog to help he , what could he do against fire - arm , which lepardo be sure to have ? what should I say to his wife and child , what should I say to myself , if John Huxtable fall a victim to that wily and desperate criminal ? 
 resolve to be present , if possible , I rush down the narrow path which lead to the little park - gate , where probably they would pass . I be right : they have pass , and fling it wide open . breathless I look around , for hence several track diverge . no live thing could I see or hear , but the beating of my heart , which seem to be in my throat , and the hooting of an owl from the hollow elm at the corner . I fling myself down on the dewy grass , and strain my eye in vain ; until by some silver birch - tree on which the moonlight be glance , I see first a gliding figure that look like a deer in the distance , then a tall man run rapidly . away I make by a short cut for the " witch ' grave , " as the end of the lake be call , for I know that the path they be on lead thither . quite out of breath I be , for I have run more than half a mile , when I come full upon a scene , which would have rob I of breath if I have any . at the end of a little dingle , under a willow - tree , and within a few foot of the water , stand Lepardo Della Croce , bring to bay at last . a few yard from he , Giudice be struggle furiously to escape the farmer 's grasp ; perhaps no other hand in England could have hold he . his eye kindle in the moonlight , like the red star of a rocket , and a deep roar of baffle rage come from the surge of his chest , as he champ his monstrous fang , and volley all the spring of his loin . the farmer lean backward to hold he , and stay himself by a tree - stump . 
 " sharp now , surrender , wull e , man . in the name of of the Quane and the Lord Chafe Justice , and the High Shariff of Devon , I tell e surrender - dang this here dog - surrender , and I ' ont hoort e ; and I ' ont let the girt dog . " 
 Lepardo answer calmly , in a voice that make my blood cold : 
 " do you value your life ? if so , stand out of my way . I have death here for you , and five other dog . " 
 I see the barrel of a large revolver , with a stream of light upon it . he hold it steadily as a tobacco - pipe . I be glad he own some courage . for my life , I could not stir . all the breath in my body be go . 
 " dear heart alaive . Thiccy man must be a fule , " say the farmer quite contemplatively . " do not   e know who I be ? do e reckon they peppermint twistesse can hurt Jan Uxtable ? I seed ever so many in a smarl shop window to Lunnon . Surrender now wull e , thou shalt have fair traial to Hexeter , as a Davonshire man have take e , and a dale more nor e desarve . sharp now : I be afeare of the girt dog get loose . dang you dog . Ston up a bit . " and the farmer approach he coolly , trail the dog along ; as if what the murderer hold in his hand be a stick of spanish liquorice . 
 " Fool , if you pass that stump , your great carcase shall lie on it . " 
 " fire away , " say the farmer , " I know you be a coward , and I be glad it be so . now mind , if so be you shut , I let the dog go , honour braight , because e dunno what fair play be . but if e harken to rason , I will give e one chance more . I will tie up the dog with my brace to thiccy tree - aller wear cart rope I do - and I will tak e Quane 's prisoner , with my left hond , and t'other never out of my breech pocket ; look e , zee , laike thiccy . " 
 and the farmer bury his right hand in his capacious trowsery . the Corsican seem astonish . 
 " fool - hardy clown , worthy son of a bull - head country , stop at the stump - then , take that . " 
 out blaze the pistol with a loud ring , and I see that the farmer be strike . he let go the dog , and leap up ; his right hand fall on Lepardo 's temple , and seem to crush the skull in , - another shot at the same instant and down fall the farmer heavily . " Great God , " I scream , and leap forward . but Giudice be loose to avenge he , though I could swear that it be on a corpse . corpse or living body , over and over it roll , with the dog 's fang in its throat . I hear a gurgle , a tearing , and grind , and then a loud splash in the water . the dog , and the murderer , both of man and dog , sink in the lake together . twenty foot out from the shore rise above water one moment , draw ghastly white in the moonbeam , the last view see till the judgment - day of the face of Lepardo Della Croce . 
 almost drown himself - for he would not release his father 's murderer , while a gasp be in he - stagger at last to the shore my noble and true dog Giudice . he fall down awhile , to recover his breath , then shake himself gratefully , totter to I , where I kneel at the farmer 's side , and wag his tail for approval . the water from his chest and stomach drip on the farmer 's upturned face , and for a moment revive he . 
 " no belt , no tino lad , I ' ont tak ' it . Zimth laike a ticket for chat . I dunno as I would tak ' the mony , if it warn't   for the poor chiller , naine chiller now , and anither a - coomin . must not   drink no more beer , but beany shall have his'n . " and his head fall back on my lap , and I feel sure that he be dead . how I scream and shriek , till I lay beside he , with Judy lick my face , none can tell but the gamekeeper , who have hear the shot , and come hurry . 
 of this low end of the lake they happen to be most jealous ; for a brood of pintail duck , very rare I believe in England , have be hatch here this summer , and no one be allow to go near they . poor Judy keep all the man aloof , till I be able to speak to he . then I perceive that he as well be bleed , wound perhaps by the poniard as he leap on his enemy 's breast . it have enter just under the shoulder , and narrowly miss the heart . 
 they take we at once towards the house , carry the farmer and Judy on the wooden floodgate of the stream call the " Witches ' brook , " which here fall into the lake . as we enter the avenue , be oblige to take the broad way , though much further round , we hear a carriage come . it be the one I have send for Conrad , with a hurried note to break the sad news of his father 's death . he have be detain in London by a challenge he find from Lepardo ; which be of course a stratagem to keep he out of the way . how delighted I be to see his calm brave face again , as he leap down , and take my tottering form in his arm . in a minute he understand everything , and know what be good to be do . he would not allow they to place the poor farmer in the carriage , as they foolishly want to do ; but lay the rude litter down , examine the wound by the lamplight , and bind they up most cleverly with the appliance of the moment . 
 " oh , Conrad , will he die ? " 
 " no , my darling , I hope not ; but he must if they have let he bleed so much long . " 
 " I never hear that you be a surgeon , Conny . " 
 " could I call myself a sculptor , without have study anatomy ? my dearest one , how you tremble ! go home in the carriage , and give direction for we . a room downstairs , with a wide doorway , and plenty of air . I will stay with they , and see that they bear he gently . poor Judy may go with you . " 
 thus Conrad see for the first time the hearth and home of his ancestor , with his father lie dead there , and his avenger carry helpless . but I meet he at the door . do that comfort you just a little , my darling ? 
 CHAPTER XII . 
 the lake be drag that night , and all the follow day , in spite of the gamekeeper 's strong remonstrance for the sake of the tender pintail . but nothing whatever be find , except the italian cap . the " Witches ' grave , " invisible I be glad to say from the house , be more than forty foot deep , when the water be at its low . three or four year afterwards young William Hiatt catch a monstrous pike in the lake , and send he , with our permission , to be stuff at Gloucester . like the famous fish of Samos , this pike have swallow a ring , which be send to Conrad by the Gloucester gun - maker . it be Lepardo 's seal - ring , the cross of the family engrave on a bloodstone , with L.D.C. below it . 
 whether the midnight stabber die by the blow of an english fist , or suffer vivisection through a dog 's vendetta - an institution more excusable and dignified than man 's - be know to he , and he alone , who hold the scale of retribution , and laugh in scorn as well as wrath at our attempt to swing they . for be we not therein ourselves ; and how shall the good and strong of we carry the thing he be carry in ? right glad I be , and ever shall be , that I move not in the awful scene , which close my father 's tragedy . 
 through Conrad 's skill and presence of mind , the dear farmer 's life be save . we send to Gloucester immediately for the clever surgeon there ; and he own that he could not have fix the ligature well , though he do what Conny durst not attempt , he extract the murderer 's bullet . it be the first shot that do all the mischief , be aim deliberately at the large and tender heart . thank to the waving of the willow - tree , for Lepardo be a know marksman , it have miss by about two inch . the second shot , fire quite close and wildly , have groove the left temple , and stricken the farmer senseless . 
 for six week now our dear friend , whose patience amaze all but I , be keep from his Devonshire home . to London I send at once for the two child and Mr. Dawe , and would have send to Devon as well , for kind and good Mrs. Huxtable , but her husband would not hear of it . by Ann Maples , who have leave Lady Cranberry ' shockingly , " on hear from Mrs. Fletcher that I would take she again , he send to his wife " kind love and good duty , and for goodness ' sake , stop at home now . no call to make a fule of yourself , and the farm go to rack and ruin . there be fuss enough 'bout I already , and never I brag no more , when a pill like thiccy upsot I . but Miss Clara , God bless her bootiful eye , she nurse I , just as if she wor my own darter , with the apron on as you give she . and you should see the kitchen , Honor , you love a kitchen so ; they be a bilin and roastin arl day , and they be vorce to swape the chimbley three time in a vortnight " - the rest of this glorious message , about three page long , I be " vorced " to suppress ; I only hope Ann Maples remember a quarter of it . 
 but his wonderful Miss Clara do not nurse he long . hear from the surgeon that all the danger be over by the end of the following week - so strong be the constitution - Conrad , Lily , and I set sail for Corsica on our melancholy errand . in that letter , which seem to come to I from the grave , my poor Uncle after express his joy and deep gratitude at so happy a close to his life , continue thus : - 
 " yes , my dear child , the close of my wasted and weary life . you may be surprised and perplex at what I be about to tell you ; but you be not one of those low - minded one , who condemn as superstition all beyond their philosophy . the very night after you bring I my new Lily , a sweet thing just like her mother , I lay for some hour awake , broad awake as I be now . I be think of my two Lilies , the lovely and love creature . I be not in the least excited , but calm , reflective , and happy . soon after the clock strike two , at the time when our life burn low , I hear a soft voice , sweet as the music of heaven , call I by name three time . of course I know whose it be : too often that voice have murmur upon my bosom , for I not to know it now . not rashly , but with a mind long since resolve , I answer : ' sweetest mine ' - her own artless and young endearment - ' Sweetest mine , no long will I keep you lonely . ' no answer come in word ; but the light , the golden light of my own love 's smile , as I have see it in Corsica , when she come from the grave to comfort I . and now , as after that visit , I fall into deep and perfect rest , such rest as come but rarely until the sleep of all . no wonder you and Lily think I so strong next day . in the morning I know and rejoice in my quick departure . this cold obstruction be to be cast aside , this palsied frame to release the wing soul . on the third day I be to find and dwell with my Lily for ever . so on the first day I enjoy the harmless pleasure of life , and could not bear you to leave I , because that would have turn they to pain . the second day I get through all the business that still remain , refresh its dryness often with my sweet child 's society . on this , the third , I write to you , and be , through the grace of God , as calm and content , nay more content than if I be go to bed . 
 " beloved daughter both , and my dear son as well , I implore you not to grieve painfully for I . too well I know the weight of excessive sorrow , and how it oppress the lost one , even more than the loser . since the parting be so brief , the reunion so eternal , why make the interval long and dreary by count every footstep ? 
 alas , it be easy to talk and think so , but very hard to feel it . time demand his walk with sorrow , and will not have his arm dispense with . then think of my happiness , darling , and how your own will increase it . 
 only one more request , which after ciceronian sentiment - which Cicero could not practise - you be all too young not to wonder at . if you , my three child , can manage it , without any heavy expense , or much trouble to yourself , it be my last wish as regard the body , that it should lie by the side of my wife 's . the name of the little church , St. Katharine 's on the Cliff , can scarcely have escape my Clara 's excellent memory . Lily lie beside her father , in the right - hand corner towards the sea . each of they have a cross of the Signor 's alabaster , make from my own design . Lily 's be enough for I : put my name with her . " 
 not only do we look upon his last fond wish as sacred , but we accomplish it in the manner that be likely to please he most . we put his own " Lilyflower , " the little love - boat as they call it , into commission again , engage a good captain and crew , and take old Cora with we , set sail from Gloucester for the Mediterranean . Poor Cora be now all devotion to Conrad and Lily , ever since she have find that they be lawful blood and direct heir of the Della Croce . the more recent part of the family story she have know only from her master 's version , and have set little store by the child as bear the stamp of disgrace ; though she could not help love sweet Lily . now , by her evidence , couple with my dear Uncle 's deposition , his relic , and document , and my own testimony , confirm by Balaam and Balak , we establish very easily the birth and the claim of my Uncle Edgar 's child ; and the old Count Gaffori , most venerable of signor , would have keep we a month at least to go through all his account . he be entreat to retain his position as the guardian of our Lily . 
 so far as our recent sorrow permit enjoyment of scenery , we be all enchant with the Balagna . at the funeral of " Signor Valentine , " whose name be still remember and love , nearly all the commune be present ; and many a dignified matron shed tear , who have smile as a graceful girl , and strown flower , at his wedding . they be burn with curiosity to see our beautiful Lily , for the tender tale have move they , as southern nature be move ; and many of they have love and glory in her mother . 
 but in spite of all this desire , not a pry glance fall on she , as she bow in the hooded robe , and weep to the mournful vocero . foremost of all stand old Petro and Marcantonia , who have find out and kiss with sob of delight their beloved master 's daughter . for my part , I love the Corsicans ; there be something so noble and simple about the man , so graceful , warm - hearte , and lady - like in the woman ; and in a very short time I could understand more than half they say . the black Vendetta , they tell I , be die out among they , and in a few year would be but a wonder of the past . God in his mercy grant it . 
 there must have be something surely in my Uncle Edgar 's nature , which win the southern heart , as my father win british affection . such thing I can not explain , or account for . I only know and feel they . 
 we be all back at Vaughan St. Mary before the end of August , and find the farmer , the two chiller , and Beany Dawe as happy as if they be bear and rear there . Old Cora be leave at Veduta Tower ; and have obtain Mr. Dawe 's permission I present she once and for all with the whole treasure of the gordit . she intend , however , to bequeath it to I in her will . soon afterwards Conrad give she a more substantial blessing ; for he sell the thing leave in Lucas Street , under letter of administration , as be the next of kin . all the proceed he hand over to Cora , except one - tenth , which he present to the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to animal . as many of the specimen , iguanodon , and other monster , fetched price as hard to explain away as themselves , poor Cora be amply provide for : all which of course she attribute to the holy Madonna 's heart . and now at last I understand how 19 , Grove Street have become No . 37 , Lucas Street . the change of number I have already explain ; the change of name be on this wise : - the builder , a rise man , who have buy the old part of the street , and build thereto the new one , have a son , a fine undergraduate , well skilled in the boat than in the book of Oxford . read hard one day , after his third pluck , this young man discover that lucus be the Latin for grove . he smite his hand on his forehead , and a great idea present itself . have there not be both nymph and philosopher of the grove ? the street that be his inheritance should be distinguish by nomenclature from the thousand grove of London , wherein the nightingale pipeth not , neither - but I be get poetical , and do not   understand the Gradus . enough , that he write at once and earnestly to his father , forget the vivid description , which be now grow stale , of his pluck - a result secure , as the Winchester gentleman tell I , by learn too solid to carry - but beg that his Oxford career might at least be commemorate in and by the street that pay his bill there . " Lucus " he write plainly enough , and in very large letter , but the father read it " Lucks . " no , say the mother , she be sure Alexander never mean such a low thing as that , it be " Lucas " of course ; why the Lucases be her own cousin , and Rosa such a nice girl , she see how it be , that she do , and Alexander might have do bad . and so it be paint most bravely " Lucas Street , " and the builder be not   go to make a fool of himself , when Alexander protest . 
 when John Huxtable set off for home , just in time to see to his harvest , which be always late round Exmoor , I kiss he - ay , Conny , you see it - and thrust , during his amazement , something far down into his mighty pocket , which something he be not upon any account to look at until he get home . it be a deed , prepare by our solicitor , present he with the fee simple of Tossil 's Barton farm . true , I be not of age , but I sign it as if I have be , and Conny and I again sign it , when we pay our first visit there . perhaps , in strict law , it bind not my interest even now ; but if ever any one claim " by , from , through , under , or in trust for " I , forget the Vaughan honour and dare to dream of that farm , I will be at he as sure as a ghost ; and I trust before that time come , the farmer will have sound title by immemorial year of possession . he be now a prosperous man ; and have never find it necessary to give up his beer , as he threaten Young John , who be just like his father , cleave fast to Tabby Badcock , now a bloom maiden ; but my Sally have more than balanced that imminent loss of caste , by fix the eye and transfix the heart of George Tamlin , the son of our principal tenant , and himself of Devonshire origin . the young lady come to and fro every six week , and be to be marry from our house , when her father consider she " zober enough . " Beany Dawe , who do not like work , still live at Tossil 's Barton , and be in receipt of a pension of sixpence a day from Government , as a bard at last appreciate . 
 as for I , Clara Vaughan , on the very day after that which release I from my teen   count forward , as we do , till we count recede year , to wit on the 31st of December , 1851 , I do not change my name , but write it in the old church register , half an inch below a well and firm hand . there be no fuss or frippery ; no four clergyman and ten bridesmaid simper at one another . our good vicar represent the one class , dear Lily and Annie Franks the other . my godfather , newly disclose for the purpose , give I away very gracefully , and young Peter Green help Conrad . Lily Vaughan look so exquisite , so deliciously lovely , that nobody in the whole world - now Conny , hold your tongue , I never fish for compliment , do not   degrade yourself so for a kiss , of course I know all my perfection , but how can I care about they , when you say they belong to you ? - Lily Vaughan , I say once more , be such a sunrise of loveliness , that young Peter Green , just new from his Oxford honour , collapse , and fall over the railing , and wedge his head in the " piscina , " or whatever those nice young gentleman , who see the duty of wear strait waistcoat , be pleased to denominate it . 
 ah , Little Distaff Lane , most unconnubial title , ah firm of Green , Vowler , and Green , your Hercules hold the distaff , and hold it , alas , in his heart ! from that shock he never recover , until we have at Vaughan Park a really merry wedding ; and I , ah I , I could not dance just then , but I shower rose upon they , for the shadow of death be past . old Mr. Green , - nay , nay , not fifty yet , by our Lady , - Mr. Peter Green the elder , come down here for the occasion , and I hardly ever take such a fancy to any man before . he seem to know almost everything , not by the skin , as Dr. Ross seem to hold thing , but by the marrow and fibrine of their alimentary part . and withal such a perfect gentleman : he keep in the horn of his knowledge , instead of exalt they , and make we wish for hay on they , while toss in headlong ignorance . 
 Scant as I be of space , I must tell how he behave , when his son reveal his attachment . 
 " be it a lady , Peter ? " " I should rather think she be , father . " " do you love she with all your heart ? " " of course I do , every bit . I be tough , but I know I shall die , unless - " " that will do , my son . you have my full consent , and your mother 's be sure to follow . most likely you get it beforehand . you young fellow be so deep . let I kiss your forehead , my boy , although I be not dramatic . " 
 have behave so nobly , for this boy be his only hope , he deserve to find , as he do , that if he have search the world he could not have hit upon any other so desirable for his son , as the daughter of his old friend . the only mistake he have make be that he so adore she , he can not bear she to be in Corsica ; though the trade they conduct be worth at least fifty thousand a year . when Lily fall in love , I tell she that it be because she have an eye for the olive ; and olive enough the darling have , I trow , and olive branch too . the eld be call Clara . " Clara Green ! " I do not   like the sound altogether ; but the substance be something beautiful , and the fresh of all Spring verdure . nevertheless , my Clara be an inch large round the calf , and I think her eyelash be long . her hair weigh more , that be certain . we compare they very often ; for they live only half the year at Veduta Tower . in the summer heat they be here , and the child between they , my own every bit as bad , leave dear Annie Elton   Annie Franks of old , uncommonly few British Queens . it be all Mr. Shelfer 's fault . what be the use of a gardener , if he allow dessert all the day long ? 
 every autumn we go to Corsica to help at the olive harvest , and rarely we enjoy it . the Old Veduta Tower be like a nest in the ivy , chirrup with young voice ; and the happy sleep of the two who love so well be dream , if dream it can or care to do , of the fair flower in Europe , scatter there by little soft hand . Conny be wild every time about the Rogliano and Luri ; and if Peter Green listen to he - which every one do , except I - he will introduce , very slowly of course , those fine - body yet aerial wine to the noble british public , that love not even intoxication , unless it be adulterate . 
 oh , queer Mrs. Shelfer , oh Balaam and Balak , shall I pretermit your annal ? the two Sheriff 's officer , have secure their reward , set up therewith a public - house call the " Posse - Comitatus , " which soon become the head quarter of all who be agent or patient in the machinery of levy . as at such time all people drink and pay more than double , the public - house have already a Queensbench - ful of good - will . 
 Poor Mrs. Shelfer and Charley do not invest the 325 l.   altogether judiciously : at least , it go mainly to purchase " eternal gratitude , " whose time do not begin to run till the purchaser 's be over . but Patty , I be glad to say , have still that 30 l.   a year of her own , leave to she in the fund by good and grateful Miss Minto . " can not   touch it , my good friend , not the Queen , the Lord Mayor , and all the royal family . government give their bond for it , on parchment make of their skin , and the ink come out of their gall . " be this as it may , what be much more to the purpose be that Mr. Shelfer can not touch it . and now I have pride in announce , for I never expect such glory , that all the cat and bird , squirrel , mouse , and monkey , live , like the happy family , in our northern lodge , where Patty be most useful and happy as the Queen of the poultry . in a word , they keep the gate , not of their enemy , but of old and grateful friend . I expect to see at least a lead article in the " Times , " when Mr. Shelfer leave the metropolis ; but they let he go very easily for the sake of the discount market . they give he only two - and - twenty dinner ; but when he first come to Vaughan Park , how he want country air ! now he attend to the wall - tree , and the avenue , and I hope find harmony there . at any rate , he never break it by any undue exertion . nevertheless , his very long pipe be of some account with the green fly , which have be very bad on our peach , ever since they repeal the corn law . Mr. Shelfer , accordingly , be compel to spend half his time in smoke they . " wonderful nice they do taste , Miss Clara ; you would be quite surprised , you know . wonderful good , Miss , and werry high - flavour you know , when they begin to fry . " 
 " come , come , Mr. Shelfer , I fear you cultivate they for their flavour . there be ten time as many of they , I see , as of peach on the tree . and you charge I every week five shilling for tobacco . " 
 " to be sure , Miss Clara . show a fine constitooshun , you know . and dreadful hard work it be to have to smoke so much , you know . and then the sun will come on the wall , and only a quart of beer allow all the afternoon . and sometimes they make I go for it myself , you know ! indeed they do , Miss , they have such cheek here in Gloucestershire ! " 
 Patty bring all her stick of course , in spite of the twenty - five bill of sale , which by this time have grow upon they . one whole roomful be pack in the duplicate inventory . the law on this subject she contemplate from a peculiar point of view . 
 " Lor , Miss , I never grudge them . they do cost a bit at the time ; but see how safe they make they . if it be not   for they I should be frighten out of my wit of thief , down here where the tree and all the green grocery be , bad than the Regency Park . bless I , I never should have go out of door , Miss , if you have not   pull I . and to see the flower here all a - grow with their head up as bootiful as a bonnet . pray , my good friend , be that what they be make for , if I may be so bold ? " 
 " no , Patty , not for bonnet . they be make for the bee and the butterfly , and for we to enjoy they , while they enjoy themselves . " 
 " well , I never . pray , Miss , do I tell you Uncle John 's come home , and they only eat a piece of his shoulder for they find his belt be tenderer ; and he put the glazing on it the same as they wear on their hat , and three cork pin to hold it , and he find it werry convenient , it save so much rheumatism : and he will be here next week to convict the man that make his wife swallow the tea - pot . dear , dear , what thing they do do in the country . not a bit like Christians . and so , Miss Clara , the old man will not   drop off after all ; and Uncle John a - come , how nice it would have be . " 
 the old man be poor Whitehead , whose lodge Mrs. Shelfer covet , as it be large and lively than her own . 
 " no , Mrs. Shelfer , I think he will get over it . surely you would not wish to hurry he . " 
 " to be sure , my good friend ; no , no : let he have his time , I say . but he would have have it long ago , if he have any reason in he . what good can he do now , hold on with his eyebrow ? please God to let he go in peace ; and so much happy for we all . " 
 when Uncle John appear , he scold I for my want of intelligence on the night when I be blind . of the four man in that room , the one whom I have notice least be the very one whom he have mean I especially to observe . at least , so he say ; but I fully believe , and do not scruple to tell he , that he have discover little beyond the information and description give at the time by Mr. Edgar Vaughan . these he have disinter from the archive of Bow Street and Whitehall , and be then try to apply they . however , I forgive he freely ; inasmuch as , but for my blindness , even blind love would have know I as an objectionable being . 
 and now I come to a real grievance . when there be another Miss Clara - such a beauty ! I can not   tell you - and a little Harry , for whose sake this tale be tell - why will every one on these premise , even the under - gardener 's boy , persist in call I " Miss Clara ? " it make I stamp sometimes , and such a bad example that be for my child . dear I , if either of my duckling be to carry on as I do at their age , I would cut down immediately the large birch - tree on the property , and order a hogshead of salt . but , to return to that contumely - be it to be suspect that I be more forcible and pronounce , in the day of my trial and misery , than now when I be the happy of all the young mother of England ? " come , Conny , tell the truth now , do not   I keep you in order ? " 
 " my own delight , I should think you do . I be nearly as much afraid of you as I be of little Clary . clary ride on Judy now , and Harry on pup Sampiero , and come and see papa go chip , chip , chip ? " 
 " no , clary stop and see mamma go scratch , scratch , scratch , like Cooky at the pie - crust . clary love mamma to - day , and papa to - morrow . " 
 and the lovely dear jump on the stool , to pull the top of my pen . Harry pop out from under the table , and prepare himself for onset . my husband come and lift my hair , and throw his arm around I . it be all up now with writing . 
 " darling , I love all three of you , to - day , to - morrow , and for ever . only do not   pull I to piece . " 
 the END .
